nodes.title: Cognitive Expectations of Homophily in Village Social Networks
nodes.authors: Feltham, Eric*
nodes.abstract: Homophily, the tendency for individuals to associate with similar others, is a central principle of social organization. Here, we investigate individuals’ expectations of homophily and compare these expectations to actual homophily among 10,072 adults in 82 isolated Honduras villages. We elicited subjects’ beliefs about the existence of social ties between pairs of people within their village-wide social networks. We show that people deploy cognitive heuristics that substantially overestimate the level of homophily, including based on wealth, ethnicity, gender, and religion. We also find that people exploit network structure when predicting ties between others. Understanding cognitive homophily enhances theoretical models of network formation; has implications for interventions aiming to influence social behavior and information diffusion; and has relevance for the maintenance of social inequality.

nodes.title: Disentangling Communities and Factions in Signed Networks through Gremban Expansions
nodes.authors: Diaz-Diaz, Fernando*; Devriendt, Karel; Lambiotte, Renaud
nodes.abstract:
Signed networks capture both cooperative and antagonistic interactions in complex systems: friendships and rivalries in social networks, activations and inhibitions in biology, or positive and negative correlations in finance. A central challenge is to identify and classify mesoscale structures in these networks. In particular, communities are groups of nodes with dense internal connectivity, while factions are characterized by predominantly positive ties within and negative ties across groups. Standard tools from network science rarely separate these two forms of organization, leading to ambiguous results.

We introduce a new approach based on the Gremban expansion, a transformation that maps a signed network into a larger unsigned one without losing information. This expanded network possesses a natural symmetry that makes it possible to apply well-established spectral clustering techniques while still accounting for the role of negative ties. Our main result is a rigorous way to distinguish between communities and factions: symmetric eigenvectors of the expanded Laplacian correspond to community partitions, while antisymmetric ones reveal factional divisions. This provides the first method that jointly captures and separates both structures in a principled way.

We test our framework on synthetic networks generated with stochastic block models, showing that the Gremban approach successfully identifies the different mesoscale structures across regimes where communities, factions, or mixtures of both prevail. The method also generalizes naturally to multi-group settings and connects to dynamical processes on networks, highlighting its potential for broad applications. Beyond offering a mathematically robust foundation, the framework is simple to implement, interpretable, and applicable to diverse empirical systems where cooperation and conflict coexist.

nodes.title: Learning Social Mechanisms for Link Prediction in Signed Social Networks
nodes.authors: Brázia, João*; González-Casado, Miguel; Sánchez, Angel; Francisco, Alexandre; Teixeira, Andreia Sofia
nodes.abstract:
Signed networks have emerged as a powerful framework in social network analysis to capture friendships (positive) or
antagonistic relationships (negative). Structural balance theory and status theory have become the most used frameworks
to study these relationships. Structural balance postulates that relationships follow patterns such as ”an enemy of my
friend is my enemy” and ”an enemy of my enemy is my friend,” to reduce social tension. Status theory interprets directed
signed links as reflecting hierarchical relationships. However, such theories fail to explain the existence of imbalanced
triads in social networks, and do not incorporate competing structures or patterns including homophily, reciprocity, and
popularity. To try to understand better the interconnectedness of these different mechanisms, we use a logistic regression
classifier to combine node and edge features into an edge sign prediction task. Our aim is to uncover how the different
signed edges and meso-scale structures contribute to a better signed link prediction (see Figure 1 (a)). For this purpose,
we use a temporal high school network dataset among 888 students [1]. We apply inductive learning, partitioning the
network by course, and training the model on one course while testing and validating on the others to avoid data leakage.
Our results show strong inductive capability, with high balanced accuracy, AUC and F1-scores when predicting link signs
in held-out courses, for different training courses and snapshots (see Figure 1 (b)). Additionally, by analyzing the top 10
most important feature coefficients across training courses, we identify consistent predictors: reciprocal tie strength, friend
influence, and positive out-degree predict positive edges, while negative out-degree and negative reciprocal links predict
negative edges (see Figure 1 (c)). Finally, some triad based features predict edge signs that violate both structural balance
and/or status theory (BBpp and FFpp), suggesting that other mechanisms may contribute to signed tie creation. Ongoing
work focuses on how these analysis may differ from using state of the art signed graph embeddings that incorporate both
nodes and edge features, and whether the same social patterns or discover complementary structures are captured. Our
preliminary findings identify the most significant social pattern features, offering guidance for improving the performance
and explainability of current machine and deep learning models for link sign prediction, which typically rely solely on
structural balance and status theory.

nodes.title: Measuring Social Media Network Effects
nodes.authors: Nicolaides, Christos*; Benzell, Seth ; Collis, Avinash; Aral, Sinan
nodes.abstract:
Network effects, which dictate that the utility from consuming a good increases with the total number of other consumers consuming it, are widely regarded as critical to the digital economy. But recent theory and evidence suggest that local network effects—the economic value created by specific social network connections—are what drive value in networked online platforms. Here, we use representative, incentive-compatible online choice experiments involving 19,923 Facebook, Instagram, LinkedIn, and X users in the United States to provide the first large-scale, empirical measurement of local network
effects in the digital economy and to measure heterogeneity in the value of network connections across social media platforms. Our analysis reveals that social media platform
value ranges from $78 to $101 per consumer, per month, on average, and that 20-34% of that value is explained by local network effects. We also find that 1) stronger ties are more
valuable on Facebook and Instagram, while weaker ties are more valuable on LinkedIn and X; 2) connections known through work are most valuable on LinkedIn and least valuable on Facebook, and people who are looking for work value LinkedIn significantly more and Facebook significantly less than people who are not looking for work; 3) men value connections to women on social media significantly more than they value connections to other men, particularly on Instagram, Facebook and X, while women value connections to men and women equally across platforms; 4) consumers value network connections on any platform more if they are also connected with them on other platforms, suggesting that social media platforms are complements, not substitutes; 5) white consumers value relationships with other white consumers significantly more than they value relationships with non-white consumers on Facebook while, on Instagram, connections to alters eighteen years old or younger are valued significantly more than any other age group—two patterns not seen on any other platforms. The social media platforms we studied each
individually generate between $53B and $215B in consumer surplus per year in the US alone. These results suggest that social media generates significant value, that local network effects drive a substantial fraction of that value and that the sources and contours of these effects vary across platforms, consumers, and connections.

nodes.title: The structure and function of antagonistic ties in village social networks
nodes.authors: Ghasemian, Amir*; Christakis, Nicholas
nodes.abstract:
Negative or antagonistic relationships are common in human social networks, but they are less often studied
than positive or friendly relationships. The existence of a capacity to have and to track antagonistic ties raises the
possibility that they may serve a useful function in human groups. Here, we analyze empirical data gathered from
24,770 and 22,513 individuals in 176 rural villages in Honduras in two survey waves 2.5 y apart in order to evaluate
the possible relevance of antagonistic relationships for broader network phenomena. We find that the small-world
effect is more significant in a positive world with negative ties compared to an otherwise similar hypothetical positive
world without them. Additionally, we observe that nodes with more negative ties tend to be located near network
bridges, with lower clustering coefficients, higher betweenness centralities, and shorter average distances to other nodes
in the network. Positive connections tend to have a more localized distribution, while negative connections
are more globally dispersed within the networks. Analysis of the possible impact of such negative ties on dynamic
processes reveals that, remarkably, negative connections can facilitate the dissemination of information (including
novel information experimentally introduced into these villages) to the same degree as positive connections, and that
they can also play a role in mitigating idea polarization within village networks. Antagonistic ties hold considerable
importance in shaping the structure and function of social networks.

nodes.title: Correlated heterogeneity in simple contagions mimics complex contagions
nodes.authors: Kaiser, Daniel*; Tang, Katerina; Thompson, William; Young, Jean-Gabriel; Hébert-Dufresne, Laurent; Landry, Nicholas
nodes.abstract:
The study of epidemic spreading in structured populations has been an impactful domain of network science since its inception.
Recent work has found that a variety of infection kernels---functions specifying infection likelihood given exposure to infected individuals---underlie spreading phenomena in networks, from the ``simple" contagion kernels characteristic of infectious diseases to ``complex" contagion kernels commonly associated with the spread of social behaviors~[1].

However, determining which type of contagion is spreading in a population is difficult. For example, recent studies have shown that interacting simple contagions [2] and uncertain transmission settings [3] may be confounded with complex contagions.
We show that heterogeneity in simple contagion kernels, when correlated with population structure, can produce spreading dynamics resembling complex contagions. 
In particular, when subpopulations---each with its own simple infection kernel---are organized into communities, the resulting infection patterns can mimic complex contagion dynamics.
Using recent nonparametric contagion models [4], we infer infection kernels from time-series data on nodal states.
We find that assuming a single infection kernel for an entire population can yield an inferred kernel displaying the nonlinear behavior commonly associated with complex contagions, even when only simple contagion kernels are present.

nodes.title: Hierarchy and scarcity: How territory quality and environmental variability shape a social network of dispersal
nodes.authors: Swain, Anshuman*; Klein, Brennan; Kawakatsu, Mari; Barve, Sahas; Koenig, Walt ; Walters, Eric
nodes.abstract: Dispersal is a fundamental process that shapes the social, genetic, and demographic structure of animal populations. In cooperatively breeding species, decisions to disperse are governed by a complex trade-off between remaining in a natal territory and seeking independent breeding opportunities. The factors driving these decisions remain a central question in behavioral ecology. Here, we utilize a social network framework to disentangle the roles of static spatial constraints, intrinsic territory quality, and dynamic environmental variability on dispersal in the cooperatively breeding Acorn Woodpecker (Melanerpes formicivorus). Analyzing over 40 years of transition data from a long-term study population, we model territories as nodes and dispersal events as directed links in a social network. We show that dispersal is structured by a stable social hierarchy determined by territory quality, a heritable resource. High-quality territories, characterized by extensive acorn granaries, function as ‘sources’ in a source-sink system, disproportionately exporting individuals and serving as the preferred destinations for dispersers. This pattern, consistent with an ideal despotic distribution, is largely independent of annual fluctuations in resource availability. However, while the volume of dispersal is governed by this static landscape of territory quality, the structure of the dispersal network is modulated by temporal resource scarcity. Years with low acorn abundance are correlated with a more heterogeneous, exploratory network structure, as measured by network entropy. Our findings reveal a decoupling of the drivers of dispersal volume and pattern, demonstrating how a stable social hierarchy and dynamic environmental pressures interact to shape the social landscape.

nodes.title: Attraction to politically extreme users on social media
nodes.authors: Zimmerman, Federico*; Goldenberg, Amit
nodes.abstract:
Political segregation can be seen in almost any facet of social interactions, from the choice of social partners (Nicholson et al., 2016) to the composition of neighborhoods and cities (Brown & Enos, 2021; Motyl et al., 2020). Political segregation is associated with attitudinal polarization, intergroup hostility, and an increased spread of misinformation (Iyengar et al., 2019; Stein et al., 2023). Prior work has proposed that a key driver of political segregation is political homophily, which is the tendency to affiliate with others who have similar political views (Halberstam & Knight, 2016; Kossinets & Watts, 2009; McPherson et al., 2001). Recently, however, it has been proposed that tie-selection decisions in the context of politics are also impacted by political acrophily, the tendency to prefer to affiliate with others who hold more extreme (as opposed to more moderate) political views in the direction of one’s political leaning (Goldenberg et al., 2022; Zimmerman et al., 2022). However, there is no direct evidence for the occurrence of acrophily in social media interactions.

To address this issue, we conducted two studies. First, we performed an online preregistered experiment in which we created fictional social media users and modified their degree of political extremity. Participants (N=400; 200 Democrats, 200 Republicans) were presented with these profiles and were asked whether they would follow them. Participants were more likely to follow an extreme copartisan rather than a moderate one (Fig. 1A). We also explored individual-level predictors of this preference and found that it was associated with negative feelings toward the opposite party.

In the second study, we analyzed real-world tie connections on social media using a dataset (Chen et al., 2022) related to the 2020 U.S. presidential election. This dataset comprises tweets from 1.9 million users. We evaluated users’ political affiliations based on their media consumption (Badawy et al., 2019; Bovet & Makse, 2019; Ferrara et al., 2020), and we examined retweet connections between users because they reflect how information spreads across the network. Notably, simply analyzing users’ retweets does not necessarily indicate a preference for more extreme political views, as user behavior might reflect the political distribution of their network rather than individual preferences. Accurate detection of acrophily, therefore, necessitates contrasting observed behaviors with those predicted by a hypothetical simulation to understand the influence of network characteristics on user interactions. We conducted two simulations. In the homophily simulation, we examined the case in which users were more likely to retweet users whose political views were closer to their own, regardless of whether they were more or less extreme (Fig. 1B). In the acrophily simulation, we considered users to be more likely to interact with others who were politically more extreme than themselves (Fig. 1B). Our results showed that Twitter/X users were more inclined to retweet individuals with more extreme views compared to the homophily simulation, suggesting that users' tie-selection decisions are shaped by both homophily and acrophily. Moreover, we analyzed the sentiment of users’ tweets using VADER (Hutto & Gilbert, 2014) and found that users who were more likely to retweet others with more extreme views also tended to write and retweet more negative tweets.

nodes.title: Demographic Dependence of Vaccine Adoption under Opinion Persuasion
nodes.authors: Mitchell, Lewis*
nodes.abstract:
Inspired by contagion models of social belief formation, we develop an \emph{epistemically-informed} modeling framework, SIS-Vo [1], in which vaccine-related information propagates on a signed opinion network. Our model allows for heterogeneous treatment effects of policy messages across subpopulations through demographic-specific responses. We derive fixed-point characterizations of the healthy (disease-free) and endemic equilibria of this model, and obtain conditions for local stability of the healthy state in terms of the contact network and opinion-dependent vaccination capacities. 
We derive fixed-point characterizations of the healthy (disease-free) and endemic equilibria of the SIS-Vo model and obtain a sufficient condition for local stability of the healthy state in terms of the contact network and the vaccination carrying capacities. 
Using numerical simulations, we illustrate how suitably targeted policy interventions, acting through opinion dynamics, can stabilize the epidemic process by moving the system towards the healthy regime. The SIS-Vo framework thus provides a natural basis for \emph{control-theoretic} analysis of vaccination policies that remain robust even when misinformation targets specific subgroups.

nodes.title: How Out-Group Animosity Shapes Partisan Divisions: A Dynamical Model of Affective Polarization on Social Networks
nodes.authors: Nettasinghe, Buddhika*
nodes.abstract: Politically divided societies are also often divided emotionally: people like and trust those with similar political views (in-group favoritism) while disliking and distrusting those with different views (out-group animosity). This phenomenon, called affective polarization, influences individual decisions, including seemingly apolitical choices such as whether to wear a mask or what car to buy. We present a dynamical model of decision-making in an affectively polarized social network, identifying three potential global outcomes separated by a sharp boundary in the parameter space: consensus, partisan polarization, and nonpartisan polarization. Analysis reveals that larger out-group animosity compared to in-group favoritism, i.e., more hate than love, is sufficient for polarization, while larger in-group favoritism compared to out-group animosity, i.e. more love than hate, is necessary for consensus. We also show that, counterintuitively, increasing cross-party connections facilitates polarization, and that by emphasizing partisan differences, mass media creates self-fulfilling prophecies that lead to polarization. Affective polarization also creates tipping points in the opinion landscape where one group suddenly reverses their trends. Our findings aid in understanding and addressing the cascading effects of affective polarization, offering insights for strategies to mitigate polarization.

nodes.title: Manipulating Collective Opinion through Network Intervention
nodes.authors: Hata, Shigefumi; Lambiotte, Renaud; Nakao, Hiroya; Kobayashi, Ryota*
nodes.abstract:
Political polarization—where opinions on political issues diverge into opposing camps—has intensified worldwide, posing a serious threat to the effective functioning of democratic institutions. Because large-scale empirical studies of opinion formation are often impractical, mathematical modeling plays a crucial role in understanding these dynamics. Although existing models successfully reproduce polarization phenomena [1], the effects of structural interventions by social network providers remain poorly understood. Developing a rigorous understanding of these effects is essential for evaluating the risks and unintended consequences of interventions aimed at shaping public discourse, such as efforts to mitigate polarization. 

In this study, we investigate whether collective opinion can be manipulated by temporarily altering the social network structure, which is a feasible action for platform providers [2]. We consider a simple mathematical model for opinion formation within a social network with $N$ individuals (users) who exchange opinions on a binary issue such as gun control or abortion:  
$ \dot{x_i} = -x_i + 
   \kappa \sum_{j=1}^N  a_{ij} g( |x_i-x_j| )  h(x_j)$, 
where $x_i$ is the opinion of a user $i$, $\dot{x_i}$ is the time derivative of $x_i$, $\kappa$ is the strength of social interaction between users, and $a_{ij}$ represents the entries of the adjacency matrix of the social network. The sign and magnitude of $x_i$ indicate the user’s stance, i.e., agreement or disagreement, on the issue and the strength of the opinion, respectively. The homophily function $g( |x_i-x_j| )$ describes the tendency of individuals to interact with people who have similar opinions, and the controversy function $h(x_j)$ describes the social reinforcement regarding the issue. Using analytical tools such as mean-field approximation and linear stability analysis, we can analytically identify conditions and tipping points for abrupt opinion shifts. Numerical simulations show that reducing network density---an intervention intended to mitigate polarization---can unintentionally amplify radicalized states. Moreover, network interventions by inferring user stance can drive transitions from polarized states to a targeted radicalized opinion state. Our results highlight the potential risks of large-scale network interventions and underscore the need for theoretically informed design to foster resilient and inclusive public debate in an increasingly networked world.

nodes.title: Peer Influence Under Platform Control
nodes.authors: Wu, Jiduan*; Abebe, Rediet; Mendler-Dünner, Celestine
nodes.abstract:
The study of opinion formation through peer interactions has a long history in the social networks community. With the rise of digital platforms, these peer interactions are increasingly mediated and influenced by algorithmic systems. 
In this work, we are interested in characterizing the complex dynamics that emerge between populations and platforms when algorithmic systems are repeatedly optimized based on observed data about expressed opinions. To this end, we combine concepts and insights from performative prediction, a growing line of work in the machine learning community, with established models and equilibrium concepts of opinions dynamics.  

In particular, we propose a model where a platform provides predictions to individuals based on observed data about their past expressed opinions and these predictions influence individuals' future opinions which then spread across the network through peer interactions. In our formulation, we build on the classical Friedkin-Johnsen model (FJ) to characterize how individuals update their beliefs through peer interactions, and we let predictions influence individuals' initial opinions. The equilibrium of interest is the state where peer dynamics are at equilibrium and the platform has no reason to change its predictions based on observed data. This long-term equilibrium state, termed performative stability, depends on the choice of learning policy and platform susceptibility of individuals, as well as network structure and peer susceptibility.

We characterize performative stability under varying assumptions on the platform's learning policy. A key insight is that platforms that provide faithful predictions have a homogenizing effect on the equilibrium opinions. In particular, in the limit of high platform susceptibility, the dynamics lead to consensus under the minimal and standard assumption of connectivity. This stands in contrast with the heterogeneous equilibrium emerging from the FJ model in isolation. Another insight is that when a platform systematically deviates from perfect prediction, qualitatively different equilibria emerge. We analyze how the platform actions affect individuals even if those individuals are not at all susceptible to platform influence, and we show how this indirect influence through peer interactions can be exploited by platforms to steer collective opinions. We analyze the convergence rate and show that the most persistent individuals in the population dominate the convergence where the persistence is determined by the platform susceptibility and the strength of platform policies. In other words, the individuals who are least influenced by the platform bottleneck the convergence. Our theoretical results are supported by simulations and highlight the important role of the platform's learning policy when characterizing equilibria in social networks.

nodes.title: Simple contagion drives population-scale platform migration
nodes.authors: Quelle, Dorian*; Denker, Frederic; Garg, Prashant; Bovet, Alexandre
nodes.abstract:
Social media platforms exhibit strong network effects that raise switching costs and entrench incumbents [2]. Theory predicts that costly, identity-laden behaviors like platform migration require complex contagion, meaning social reinforcement from multiple peers before adoption [3,4]. Because network effects stabilize incumbents, mass exits are rarely observed, and the mechanisms driving them remain poorly understood. We study this using the Twitter/X to Bluesky migration, linking 276,431 academics to their profiles among 16.7M Bluesky accounts through a two-stage matching pipeline [1].

Overall, 18% of academics transitioned, ranging from 13% in Medicine to 31% in Arts \& Humanities. Contrary to lock-in predictions, users deeply embedded in Twitter's social graph are more likely to transition, while academic metrics add little predictive power. Political expression also predicts migration. Early reconstruction of one's network on Bluesky predicts sustained engagement, with day-one reconnection associated with over 10% increases in active days, and posting.

We establish causal peer influence through two strategies. First, we exploit Brazil's court-ordered suspension of Twitter/X (August 2024) as a natural experiment. Non-Brazilian academics with Brazilian followees experienced sharp increases in transition probability post-ban. Second, we implement dynamic propensity-score matching on 225 pre-treatment covariates [5]. Transition risk doubles within one day of a peer's departure and returns toward baseline within a week. Naive comparisons inflate effects dramatically, with 62 to 93% of apparent clustering reflecting homophily. Among exposed users, 56% of adoptions reflect peer influence and 44% homophily.

Training a classifier on synthetic cascades [6], we find simple contagion dominates (64%) while complex contagion is rare (4%). This contradicts theoretical expectations but admits a resolution through multi-homing (being present on multiple platforms). Our results suggest that because users multi-home the trial is nearly costless. Mechanism heterogeneity supports this interpretation. Heavily invested users transition via simple contagion, while peripheral users require reinforcement or external shocks. Our work reveals how multi-homing transforms a collective action problem into a set of independent decisions, suggesting that platform competition is more contestable than theory implies.

nodes.title: A comprehensive characterisation of the landscape of Partial Information Decomposition applied to synthetic and real-world networks
nodes.authors: Liardi, Alberto*
nodes.abstract:
Information theory is a powerful framework for investigating higher-order interdependencies in complex networks. Recent advances have led to a variety of tools capable of detecting, characterising, and quantifying multivariate information relationships on graphs, with applications ranging from neural and brain systems to social and technological networks [1].

Among these frameworks, Partial Information Decomposition (PID) has emerged as a particularly influential approach [2]. PID seeks to explain statistical dependencies among multiple variables by decomposing the information that a set of sources provides about a target into redundant, unique, and synergistic components. 
However, while PID offers a conceptually powerful formalism that has been successfully applied in a wide range of settings in network science [3], it does not prescribe a unique functional form for its atomic components. 
This lack of a canonical definition has led to the proposal of an increasing number of desiderata that a redundancy function should satisfy, resulting in the introduction of many different PID measures. 
Moreover, several of the suggested properties were also shown to be mutually incompatible, implying that no single redundancy measure can satisfy all of them, and hindering the search of a universal PID definition. 
Hence, the field now comprises a vast collection of measures grounded in different, and often irreconcilable, axiomatic choices, challenging both the theoretical interpretation and empirical application of PID.

Thus, our work provides a unified and comprehensive perspective on the PID landscape, both mathematically and empirically, clarifying the suitability of different PID measures in specific scenarios and offering practical guidance for their application. 
We first perform an exhaustive theoretical analysis of all PID measures existing in the literature, compiling the full set of axioms each measure satisfies and classifying them according to their similarity distance (Fig.~1a). 
We then study the logical dependencies and mutual (in)compatibility of the axioms themselves, establishing a series of theorems and no-go results that elucidate their interrelations (Fig.~1b). 
Finally, we conduct an large-scale empirical analysis by computing all PID measures on the pyspi dataset, a rich collection of synthetic and real-world networks [4].
A hierarchical clustering of the results shows that the measures group into a small number of large clusters (Fig.~1c), which can be explained by a limited set of mathematical properties. 
Interestingly, this structure cannot be predicted from standard time-series statistical estimators, thus hinting that it reflects genuinely different modes of information flow. 

Overall, this study reconciles the growing diversity of PID measures and axioms by organising them into coherent clusters. By highlighting a small number of measures representative of each cluster and proposing a unified open codebase, we explain the practical consequences of specific axiomatic choices and provide concrete guidance for the application of PID. This enables more interpretable and reproducible investigations of higher-order structures in complex networks.

[1] Timme, Nicholas, et al. Journal of Computational Neuroscience 36.2 (2014): 119-140. 
[2] Williams, Paul L., and Randall D. Beer. arXiv preprint arXiv:1004.2515 (2010). 
[3] Milzman, Jesse, and Vince Lyzinski. Journal of Complex Networks 10.5 (2022): cnac026. 
[4] Cliff, Oliver M., et al. Nature Computational Science 3.10 (2023): 883-893.

nodes.title: Asymmetric Feedback Loops in Cross-Partisan Interaction Networks
nodes.authors: Biswas, Ahana*; Lin, Yu-Ru
nodes.abstract:
Cross-cutting interactions provide critical pathways for information flow across polarized social networks, yet how such interactions shape and are shaped by network dynamics remains poorly understood. In particular, little is known about how different \emph{types} of cross-boundary interactions generate asymmetric visibility responses and how these responses feed back into future network behavior. We address this question using a large-scale longitudinal interaction network of U.S.\ state legislators and their audiences on Twitter/X, comprising over 1.1M interactions from 2020-21.

We model elite-audience communication as a temporal, directed, and \emph{typed} network, where nodes represent legislators and audience members, and edges represent cross-partisan interactions (retweets, replies, and mentions). Interaction edges are characterized by content attributes (posting styles), and audience engagement induces a node-level visibility signal measured relative to each legislator’s historical baseline. For legislator $u$ and post $i$, visibility is defined as $V_{ui} = E_{ui}/(\tilde{E}_u + b_0)$,
% \[
% V_{ui} = \frac{E_{ui}}{\tilde{E}_u + b_0},
% \]
where $E_{ui}$ is observed engagement and $\tilde{E}_u$ is the median engagement of $u$ over a sliding temporal window, and $b_0$ is a small stabilizing constant. This normalization isolates changes in prominence attributable to interaction structure rather than global activity fluctuations. Figure~1 shows the study design and observed feedback loop dynamics.

Our analysis reveals pronounced \emph{asymmetries} in visibility dynamics across interaction layers. Cross-partisan retweets are associated with higher visibility for Republican legislators but lower visibility for Democrats, whereas cross-partisan replies and mentions exhibit the opposite pattern. These patterns indicate that distinct audience communities reward different network actions—amplification versus direct engagement—despite operating within the same platform.

Crucially, we demonstrate that visibility serves as a feedback signal within the network. Let $\rho_{u,t}$ denote the rate of cross-partisan interactions by legislator $u$ at time $t$, and let $\delta_{u,t}$ denote the difference in visibility between cross-partisan and non-cross-partisan interactions. We model the feedback process as,
\[
\rho_{u,t+1} = \beta_0 + \beta_1 \delta_{u,t} + \beta_2 \rho_{u,t} + \mathbf{X}_u + \epsilon_{u,t},
\]
where $\mathbf{X}_u$ captures node-level attributes and $\beta_1$ quantifies reinforcement from audience response. We find that increased visibility on cross-partisan retweets and mentions is positively associated with higher rates of future cross-partisan interactions, revealing a reinforcement loop between audience response and elite behavior.

Together, our findings characterize cross-partisan engagement as a \emph{typed, asymmetric diffusion process} embedded in a polarized temporal network, where visibility mediates feedback between local interaction structure and future edge formation. More broadly, this work highlights how heterogeneous interaction modes shape information flow and behavioral adaptation in socio-technical networks.

nodes.title: Concentration in Digital Information Ecosystems: From Sharing to Exposure
nodes.authors: Ozturan, Burak*
nodes.abstract:
The internet was expected to democratize information access by weakening traditional gatekeepers and expanding choice. Yet online attention remains highly concentrated, consistent with a network science account in which information visibility emerges from heavy-tailed, power law-like degree and popularity distributions [1–4].

We analyze concentration using two complementary datasets: Twitter/X URL-sharing by approximately 1.6 million U.S. registered voters (2017–2022) and Facebook URL-sharing data (2020–2022). We measure concentration across domains, URLs, users, and exposure, and we assess how these patterns vary by content type, demographics (age, gender, party), and time.

Concentration is pervasive and multi-layered. On Twitter, the top 10 domains account for 24% of shares, and news concentrates about 10% more intensely than general content. Exposure is more concentrated than sharing: the top 0.1% of domains account for 50% of shares but 65% of exposure, indicating that network structure reconcentrates attention even when sharing is moderately diversified. User activity is even more skewed, with 1% of users generating 40% of shares and the top 10% producing 75–80% of content. These patterns are broadly similar across demographic groups, pointing to structural rather than population-specific drivers. Over time, concentration remains high but fluctuates with major events, including diverging trends during COVID-19, and Facebook shows similar though slightly lower concentration than Twitter. Together, the results indicate that extreme attention inequality reflects robust properties of digital information systems, with direct implications for diversity in online information exposure.

nodes.title: Modeling diffusion in networks with communities: a multitype branching process approach
nodes.authors: O'Sullivan, David *; Pena, Caroline; Dubovskaya, Alina
nodes.abstract:
The dynamics of diffusion in complex networks are widely studied to understand how entities, such as information, diseases, or behaviors, spread in an interconnected environment. Complex networks often present community structure, and tools to analyze diffusion processes on networks with communities are needed. We present our recent work showing how a multitype branching process model can be used to shed light on the interplay between community structure and disease (or information) spread [1]. We model the diffusion of information using the popular independent cascade model (ICM), where node infections occur in discrete time. Nodes can be in three states: inactive, active, or removed. Active nodes attempt to activate their network neighbors once, before becoming removed themselves in the next time step. The process continues until there are no active nodes to carry on the process. We show how, by using limited information about the network---the degree distribution within and between communities (see Fig.~\ref{fig:model}a)) --- we can use probability-generating functions (PGFs) to track the number of active nodes in a two-community network $\G{N}{t}(s_1,s_2) = \sum_{n=0}^\infty \sum_{m=0}^\infty P[N_1(t)=n,N_2(t)=m] s_1^n s_2^m$, where $N_1(t)$ and $N_2(t)$ are the random variables for the number of active nodes in communities 1 and 2 by generation $t$. This PGF is particularly useful in deriving expressions for the probability of extinction. Additionally, we are able to derive new quantities specific to spreading processes in networks with communities, such as the the probability of (re)introduction of a disease into a community where it either has never spread before or stopped spreading in the past, which would be hard to analytically calculate otherwise. 

Furthermore, these tools alow us to probe the impact of the community structure on spreading behavior. Figure~\ref{fig:model}b) and c) show the impact of community structure for a two-community network. Community 1 and 2 have average degrees of $3$ and $7$, respectively, with their degree distributions following an approximately log-normal distribution. We can easily isolate the effect of communities on the cascade size distribution by removing the inter-community links (confining the spread to the seed community) and comparing this to the case where we allow spreading between communities. We can note from Fig.~\ref{fig:model}b) that it is indeed due to the community structure that community 1 sees a change in its cascade size distribution, where the inter community links provide a means of linking to the higher, on average, degree nodes in community 2. However, if we seed activations in community 2 (Fig.~\ref{fig:model}c)) we see no such discernible benefit for community 2 from being connected to community 1, where the inter community links provide a means of linking to the lower, on average, degree nodes in community 1, which does not have a qualitative effect on the cascade size distributions shape.

nodes.title: The Emergence of Causal Curiosity from Prior Causal Belief Networks
nodes.authors: Shi, Zhuoyu*; Jiang, Xintong; Jiang, Bohan; Morstatter, Fred
nodes.abstract: Causal curiosity is foundational to human cognition. It is the desire to understand why events happen, what mechanisms underlie them, and how outcomes can be explained or anticipated. It motivates exploration, sustains attention, and fuels the search for new knowledge. However, despite consensus on the importance of causal curiosity, little is known about how causal curiosity arises from existing causal belief systems. In our work, we examine how causal curiosity emerges from prior causal belief systems. With Reddit data from 2020 to 2023, and leveraging language models to extract cause-and-effect relationship pairs and identify causal curiosity-driven questions, our findings reveal that concepts that serve more as causes are more likely to appear in causal curiosity than those that serve more as effects.  Moreover, our findings indicate that positive nouns are more likely to be the object of causal curiosity. Lastly, our results also suggest that causal curiosity emerges from the central of the prior causal belief network. These novel insights reveal that causal curiosity is not random but systematically grounded in prior knowledge structures, and suggest their implication to facilitate the human learning process by motivating people to actively explore and construct deeper understandings rather than passively receiving information.

nodes.title: Overstating Attitudes, Ignoring Networks: Large Language Model Biases in Simulating Misinformation Susceptibility
nodes.authors: Choi, Eun Cheol*; Young, Lindsay; Ferrara, Emilio
nodes.abstract:
Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly used as proxies for human judgment in computational social science, yet their capacity to reproduce misinformation susceptibility remains uncertain. We examine whether LLM-based “synthetic survey respondents,” prompted with structured profiles that include egocentric personal network features, demographic attributes, attitudes, and behaviors can simulate human patterns of misinformation belief and sharing. This setting is important as personal network composition has been shown to predict both belief in and sharing of false headlines [1], while such features are largely neglected in LLM social survey simulations [2].

Using three online survey datasets as ground truth spanning distinct contexts including public health, climate change, and politics, we evaluate LLM simulations along three criteria: (1) whether simulated belief and sharing outcomes approximate empirical distributions, (2) whether models recover observed predictor–outcome relationships, and (3) whether they preserve the estimated effects of features relative to human data. Across models and prompting settings, LLMs can capture broad distributional tendencies and produce simulated responses that are modestly aligned with human responses. However, they systematically misrepresent key relationships that matter for social inference. In particular, predictive models estimated on simulated outcomes exaggerate explained variance, and rely disproportionately on attitudinal and behavioral predictors while underutilizing network predictors, when compared to models estimated on human responses.

Taken together, these findings suggest that LLM-based survey simulations, even when they resemble human distributions at a surface level, can yield systematically “cleaner” and more attitude-centric mappings than those observed in real survey responses, while neglecting network-based social context. We therefore argue that current LLM-based simulations are useful for identifying where model-generated judgments diverge from human patterns [3], rather than serving as substitutes for human data when modeling misinformation susceptibility with personal network features.

[1] Facciani, M. \& Steenbuch-Traberg, C. (2024). Personal Network Composition and Cognitive Reflection Predict Susceptibility to Different Types of Misinformation. Connections, 44(1), 2024. 165-180. https://doi.org/10.21307/connections-2019.044
[2] Ma, W., Deng, C., Moossavi, A., Wang, L., Vosoughi, S., \& Yang, D. (2024, August). Simulated Misinformation Susceptibility (SMISTS): Enhancing Misinformation Research with Large Language Model Simulations. In Findings of the Association for Computational Linguistics ACL 2024 (pp. 2774-2788). https://aclanthology.org/2024.findings-acl.162.pdf \\
[3] Anthis, J. R., Liu, R., Richardson, S. M., Kozlowski, A. C., Koch, B., Brynjolfsson, E., Evans, J. \& Bernstein, M. S. Position: LLM Social Simulations Are a Promising Research Method. In Forty-second International Conference on Machine Learning Position Paper Track. https://openreview.net/pdf?id=cRBg1dtj7o

nodes.title: Cyberbullying Interaction Networks on Instagram
nodes.authors: Sikdar, Satyaki*; Sandoval, Manuel; Hales, Taylor; Kilroy, Chloe; Juarez, Maddie; Rosario, Tyler; Rosendo, Juan J.; Hall, Deborah L.; Silva, Yasin N.
nodes.abstract:
Cyberbullying continues to grow in prevalence and its impact is felt by thousands worldwide. This study seeks a Network Science perspective on cyberbullying interaction patterns on the popular social media platform, Instagram. Using a human-annotated cyberbullying dataset of 411 Instagram posts and 35,364 comments, we outline a set of heuristics for building Session Graphs, where nodes represent users and their cyberbullying roles, and edges represent their exchanged communications via comments. Over these graphs, we compute the Bully Score, a measure of the net malice introduced by Bullies as they attack Victims (attacks minus pushback), and the Victim Score, a measure of the net support Victims receive from their Defenders (support minus attacks). In conjunction, these scores quantify the balance of power within each post. We find that a majority of posts have negative Victim Scores (attacks outweighing support), while the Bully Score distribution has a slight positive skew (attacks outweighing pushback). 

Utilizing small subgraph (motif) enumeration, our analysis uncovers the most common interaction patterns over all cyberbullying posts. The most prevalent structure that emerges has Bullies repeatedly attacking the Victims, with some variation introduced through the intensity of the edge weights. The presence of Defenders in high-ranking motifs with light edges reflects quick attempts to counteract bullying. We believe this is the first study to explore granular cyberbullying network interactions using human annotations on Instagram at the post and comment levels. Our findings can help develop proactive moderation techniques to better tackle cyberbullying on social media.

nodes.title: Relation Classification for Social Network Analysis in Data-Scarce Scenarios
nodes.authors: Alemany, Sheila*; Sullivan-Pao, Danielle
nodes.abstract:
Social network analysis allows us to understand how entities (e.g., people, organizations, or locations) are connected and how those connections influence social behavior and outcomes. Relation classification (RC) plays a crucial role in transforming unstructured text into structured knowledge. However, the volume of data is increasing rapidly, making it increasingly challenging to manually extract knowledge from the available unstructured data. As a result, the automatic extraction of named entities and relationships is key. Recent advances in RC have been driven by the use of pre-trained language models (PLMs) such as RoBERTa and large generative language models (LLMs) such as the LLaMA family. PLMs have demonstrated state-of-the-art capabilities but still require substantial amounts of high-quality labeled data [1]. Due to their large-scale pretraining, LLMs provide a zero- and few-shot approach to RC, reducing the need for time-consuming manual annotations. However, existing LLM-based approaches still struggle due to a low representation of RC tasks in instruction-tuning datasets and observed biases towards varying relation types.

We present two state-of-the-art pipelines designed for data-scarce RC scenarios. In the zero-shot setting, we propose a true/false prompting framework that queries an LLM for each possible logical relation between two identified entities drawn from a closed set of relation types. This approach incorporates relevance elicitation to determine the most likely relation among those marked as "true." In the few-shot setting, we introduce a two-stage RC pipeline: (1) prompt the LLM to predict the most probable relation type, and (2) verify this prediction to mitigate over-prediction errors. This few-shot pipeline is fine-tuned using the parameter-efficient technique LoRA within a causal language modeling framework, enabling the model to jointly learn to predict both the relation type and its accompanying chain-of-thought (CoT) explanation. The CoT rationales used for fine-tuning are generated by a teacher model, explicitly guiding the student model to learn broader relational reasoning patterns beyond dataset-specific cues [2]. Across both settings, our models outperform existing techniques in their respective data regimes on the TACRED family of datasets. For instance, the zero-shot true/false prompting with relevance elicitation performance using larger models (e.g., models with more than 30B parameters) is comparable to the leadership benchmarks that require full training datasets. The fine-tuned pipeline shows that the confirmation model improves precision at the cost of recall, but greatly improves the micro-F1 scores and reaches state-of-the-art RC performance over multiple k-shot training samples from the ReTACRED dataset.

nodes.title: Tracing Influence Across Interacting Networks
nodes.authors: Zhang, Zonghan*; Benbrahim, Marouane; Rashme, Tamanna; Zhang, Lei; Chen, Zhiqian
nodes.abstract:
Propagation processes in interconnected systems are rarely confined to a single network layer. For example, social interactions influence epidemiological exposure, and failures in one infrastructure network can cascade into others. Modeling these phenomena requires representations that capture not only the internal structure of each layer but also the directional and heterogeneous couplings among layers. However, widely used multilayer representations, most notably supra-matrices, collapse these interactions into a single aggregated operator, discarding alignment structure, blurring directional effects, and masking multiway relationships that govern cross-layer influence.
Although tensor representations are a natural alternative, direct tensorization of adjacency matrices or time slices typically stacks layers as independent modes, without enforcing directional alignment or a shared latent geometry across layers, which obscures cross-layer propagation structure and offers limited interpretability beyond matrix-based formulations. To address these limitations, we introduce a tensorized embedding framework designed to preserve the geometry, directionality, and coupling mechanisms that shape cross-layer propagation in complex interconnected networks.

We study the problem of learning structured representations of propagation dynamics in interconnected networks to characterize how influence flows within and across layers. Our approach proceeds in three steps. (\textbf{Step~1}) We construct \textbf{directional embeddings} for each network layer via spectral decompositions that preserve orientation, flow asymmetry, and local structural organization. These embeddings define layer-specific latent spaces in which heterogeneous networks can be compared without suppressing intrinsic structural differences.  (\textbf{Step~2}) We learn \textbf{alignment operators} that transform activity between embedding spaces, capturing how propagation effects transfer across layers. (\textbf{Step~3}) The resulting intra-layer embeddings and inter-layer alignments are organized into a \textbf{six-mode tensor representation}, which retains the native multiway structure of interconnected networks beyond matrix-based formulations. We apply \textbf{low-rank CP decomposition} to this tensor to identify dominant propagation components, enabling structured analysis of cross-layer influence patterns and their relative contributions. \textbf{Figure~1} summarizes this three-step pipeline.

Across synthetic benchmarks and co-simulated multilayer systems, our tensorized representation achieves \textbf{28 - 35\% lower reconstruction error} and \textbf{19 - 26\% higher classification accuracy} than supra-matrix and naive concatenation baselines while producing more stable propagation-mode signatures. Conceptually, it offers a principled perspective on multilayer diffusion by treating interconnected networks as genuinely multiway systems rather than flattened composites. This approach provides new analytical tools for understanding how dynamics propagate across interacting networks and opens opportunities for developing control strategies that leverage layer-specific and cross-layer structure.

nodes.title: Intersectional inequalities in social ties
nodes.authors: Cartier van Dissel, Mauritz*; Martin-Gutierrez, Samuel; Karimi, Fariba
nodes.abstract:
Social categories, such as race, gender, or socioeconomic status, are core driving forces of social tie formation. They shape our identities, determining our social behavior and our connection preferences. Homophily, the preference for connecting with similar others, is one of the most common and widely studied interaction patterns; however, the dynamics driving multidimensional connection preferences remain largely unexplored. Furthermore, while homophily in social networks leads to smoother coordination and enhanced trust between individuals, it also reduces diversity in knowledge, exacerbates segregation, and breeds inequality. As complex social beings, we belong to multiple groups, so individuals can experience marginalization in several dimensions simultaneously, suffering intersectional inequalities.

In this work, we develop a network model of homo / heterophilic interactions with multidimensional attribute vectors. We use the model to tackle two crucial questions: How do we integrate information from our multidimensional identities to form connections with each other, and how do multidimensional connection preferences impact intersectional inequalities of social capital? To answer the first question, we systematically model group-preference aggregation mechanisms and compare them using Bayesian model selection [1]. We test which aggregation mechanisms best describe empirical data from high school friendship networks [3] and marriages [4], finding that a simple aggregation mechanism consistently outperforms more complex alternatives. By controlling for relative group sizes in the population distribution and for attribute correlation, our model also enables us to measure multidimensional group connection preferences (e.g., of Hispanic girls towards Asian boys). 

We use the model to operationalize intersectional inequalities of social capital and derive analytical closed-form expressions for the predicted inter-group degree inequalities [2]. We rigorously demonstrate that when attributes are uncorrelated, multidimensional degree disparities mirror one-dimensional systems: majorities gain the advantage in homophilic regimes while minorities benefit in heterophilic ones. Attribute correlation induces counterintuitive patterns of \emph{emergent intersectionality}. For example, individuals belonging to one minority group in a two-dimensional system may be privileged, but those belonging to both minorities simultaneously can be severely disadvantaged. We verify the model's predictions with real-world network data, finding a remarkable alignment.

nodes.title: Network Dynamics of Interacting Contagions on X: From Semantic Barriers to Temporal Spikes in COVID-19 Conspiracy Theories
nodes.authors: Tschofenig, Fabian*; Rao, Hayagreeva; Greve, Henrich; Guilbeault, Douglas
nodes.abstract:
The COVID-19 pandemic triggered a wave of conspiracy theories on social media, undermining public health efforts. Despite contagion literature's emphasis on viewing such phenomena through an ecology of contagions encompassing intercontagion interactions, the network dynamics of their spread and interactions remain underexplored. Absent before the virus, COVID-19 conspiracies make the pandemic's quick onset a natural experiment ideally positioned for investigating the ecology of contagions.
We analyzed a dataset of 7,616,554 tweets from 7,416 users on Twitter/X, spanning from the outbreak's onset to July 2020, originally collected by Greve et al.. We reconstruct the social network based on retweets and replies, treating the diffusion of conspiracies as interacting complex contagions. Conspiracies were detected in tweets using a prompt-tuned BLOOMZ-1.7B large language model (LLM) from Vicinanza et al..
Using Cox proportional hazards models with time-varying covariates, we measured peer influence and adoption dynamics at the individual level, accounting for multiple exposures from neighbors and cross-conspiracy effects (e.g., how adopting one conspiracy affects the hazard for others). Results show hallmarks of complex contagion: the hazard of sharing increases with repeated exposures from multiple peers.
At the individual level, adopting one conspiracy theory temporarily elevates the hazard for adopting others, followed by an exponential decay back to baseline rates seen in non-adopters (Figure 1a). Moreover, not all theories serve equally as gateways: common hoax theories—often the first adopted—exert a weaker influence, whereas extreme bioweapon theories have the strongest effect, despite spreading more slowly overall compared to the faster diffusion of hoaxes.
We further explored the effect of semantic distances between the 13 identified COVID-19 conspiracies via hierarchical clustering. As visualized in Figure 1b, adoption speeds up within semantic clusters but initially slows when crossing to a different cluster, with speeds rebounding to match intra-cluster rates in the new group, suggesting semantic barriers form multiple "rabbit holes" that accelerate intra-cluster but slow cross-cluster adoption.
We employ the hazard model within agent-based simulations (ABM) on the empirical network to explore counterfactual scenarios, examining the impacts of various intervention strategies and network topologies on contagion dynamics. Our results demonstrate that models overlooking inter-contagion interactions substantially underestimate the diffusion speed of conspiracy theories. Notably, the simulations reveal that mitigating early "gateway" hoaxes proves to be more effective than selectively blocking extreme narratives. In summary, this study advances network science by illuminating how semantic and temporal interactions drive conspiracy theories, with implications for mitigating harmful content online.

nodes.title: Mapping inequities in global vaccine sentiment research
nodes.authors: Balsamo, Duilio*; Offeddu, Vittoria; Aghamohammadi, Zhina; Chiavenna, Chiara; Leone, Laura Pasqua; D’Agnese, Elena; Sharma, Deepak; Torbica, Aleksandra; Shayegh, Soheil; Andreu-Perez, Javier; Melegaro, Alessia
nodes.abstract:
Negative public sentiment towards vaccination (PSV) poses significant challenges to the effectiveness of immunization
programs, with dramatic effects on morbidity and mortality for vaccine-preventable diseases [1]. Yet, global health
initiatives are heavily influenced by economic and geopolitical factors [2] and by disruptive events, such as the COVID-19
pandemic [3], rather than countries’ epidemiological or healthcare needs. This study examines global patterns and drivers
of PSV research on five vaccines -polio, measles, human papillomavirus, influenza, and SARS-CoV-2- and evaluates the
COVID-19 pandemic’s impact on research volume, focus, and distribution. We conducted a machine-learning-assisted
global literature search on PSV with no geographic, language, or time restrictions (Figure 1). Using NLP and network
analysis, we constructed vaccine-specific, directed weighted country networks (5-year intervals) linking authors’ countries
to studied countries. From these networks, we derived node-level indicators of domestic, inbound, and outbound research.
These network-based measures were integrated into regression models to assess how economic, epidemiological, and
temporal factors shape the global production and direction of PSV research. We analysed 13,287 articles and detected
consistent literature growth from 1980 onwards, with vaccine-specific peaks following key licensing events. After 2020,
publication volumes rose above projections for influenza (32%; 95%CI = 20%, 46%) but declined for polio (-56%;
95%CI = -68%, -26%) and measles (-17%; 95%CI = -33%, 9%). Although PSV research had global coverage, its
distribution was markedly imbalanced, largely shaped by country-specific economic factors. A few high-income countries
(HIC) produced 72% of publications and the likelihood of a country being studied varied by income and vaccine. Foreign
authorship also increased as the income of the studied country decreased (over 75% in LMIC vs. below 50% in HIC).
PSV research reveals persistent inequities, with a misalignment between countries leading research and populations most
in need of its outcomes. These inequities, further exacerbated by COVID-19 disruptions, reflect systematic imbalances
in global health. Our findings underscore the need to decolonise research by fostering leadership, agenda-setting, and
accountability that centres on the necessities of affected communities. Achieving this will require funding and publication
reforms that promote equitable collaborations and elevate local priorities alongside long-standing global health objectives.

nodes.title: Leveraging Social Networks to Improve Adherence to a Prescribed Diet
nodes.authors: de la Haye, Kayla*
nodes.abstract:
Dietary recommendations are typically prescribed at the individual level, despite strong evidence that eating behaviors are socially embedded and shaped by social networks. Even emerging precision nutrition approaches—that tailor dietary guidance based on individual biology and health—rarely incorporate social or structural environments, potentially contributing to persistent challenges in diet adherence. We address this gap by developing a computational model to examine how social network characteristics influence adherence to a prescribed healthy dietary pattern.
We constructed an agent-based model representing adults in Los Angeles County, where each agent is embedded in a multi-layered social network spanning household, workplace, and community contexts. Agents choose between a Mediterranean–DASH (MED-DASH)–aligned diet and a Typical American Diet (TAD), with baseline preferences shaped by normative influence from close and weak social ties and by event-based social influence during shared meals, capturing mechanisms of social modeling and mimicry. At each eating occasion, agents probabilistically select a diet based on personal preferences, individual constraints (e.g., food budget, foods available at home or other contexts), and both longer-term social norms and immediate social cues. Using simulation studies, we examined how diet adherence varies with social network characteristics and evaluated social network–based intervention strategies that increase MED-DASH adoption among socially connected individuals and co-eaters.
Results indicate that diet adherence is highly sensitive to social network context. Household networks exerted the strongest influence on adherence, followed by workplace and community settings. Across contexts, greater prevalence of healthy eating among social ties substantially improved adherence, including among individuals whose baseline norms favored unhealthy diets. Notably, multiple weak ties adhering to a healthy diet could outweigh the influence of a single strong tie adhering to an unhealthy diet, highlighting the importance of network composition over isolated relationships. These findings frame diet adherence as a socially distributed process and offer a computational foundation for extending precision nutrition to households and social groups.

nodes.title: Beyond Dyads: The Roles of Triadic Closure and Real-Time Co-Presence in the Transmission of Healthy Eating Habits
nodes.authors: Li, Yifan*
nodes.abstract:
While social influence is a known driver of behavior change, the precise structural and temporal mechanisms governing this transmission remain under-explored, especially the involvement of social closure in small groups. This study utilizes real-time data from a randomized controlled trial among more than 8000 hospital employees conducted in the workplace over four years between 2016-20 [1] to investigate how stimulus-driven healthy eating habits spill over through social networks [2,3]. Specifically, we move beyond dyadic analysis to examine the roles of triadic structure and physical co-presence in mediating the spread of Healthy Purchasing Scores (HPS), a measure of healthy eating scaled between 0(less healthy) - 100(healthier) derived from traffic-light labeling of each item purchased in the workplace. Drawing on theories of network closure and transitivity, we hypothesize that tendencies towards homophily within closed triads (where ”a friend of a friend is also a friend”) create stronger pressures for conformity in eating behaviors than open triads or dyads, thereby accelerating behavior adoption. To further understand the mechanism, we use more than 29 million continuous-time cafeteria purchase records to construct undirected “real-time” triads—instances where all three actors were physically co-present and interacting during a meal, as well as ”artificial” triads where closure is induced over multiple occasions between three dyads during the purchasing year. This approach disentangles two distinct mechanisms: 1. Structural Transmission: Sequential, one-on-one influence occurring across the broader network over time. 2. Realtime Conformity: Immediate social modeling and peer influence occurring during shared eating occasions in small groups. This allows us to test the hypothesis that real-time co-presence in closed triads exerts a significantly stronger influence on HPS adoption than non-co-present (i.e. artificial) closed triads.
Our preliminary findings suggest that within-closed-triad HPS (µ= 68.7; SD=8.6) is higher (healthier) than dyadic HPS (µ= 67.1; SD=10.5), and that HPS within triads rises over the 4 years of the study, increasing from µ= 66.6 (SD= 8.6) in 2016 to µ= 72.6 (SD=7.9) in 2019. To investigate these trends, we employ Separable Temporal Exponential Random Graph Models (STERGMs) to decompose network evolution into distinct formation and persistence processes. By adding real-time co-presence as a time-varying covariate, we further isolate immediate conformity from long-term structural transmission. By identifying where and when influence occurs, this research provides a mechanistic foundation for optimizing the impact, equity, and cost-effectiveness of network-based intervention strategies regarding public health and potentially other subjects.

nodes.title: Social environment dynamics exhibit distinguishable trajectories, distinct mental health outcomes, and dissociable brain network connectivity
nodes.authors: Merritt, Haily*; Gonzalez, Marlen; Teixeira, Andreia Sofia
nodes.abstract: Better social support is associated with lower morbidity and mortality, better mental health, better cognitive performance, greater life satisfaction, and differences in brain network organization[1-3]. Beyond just the quality, instability in the social environment, especially during adolescence, has been linked to greater risk of psychopathology later in life[3]. To assess the intersecting associations between social environment dynamics, mental health, and brain network organization, we cluster thousands of adolescents (N=4664) from the ABCD Dataset by applying the Louvain algorithm for modularity maximization to a subject x subject similarity matrix defined by 10 measures of their social environment recorded over four years (see Figure 1a). The resulting three clusters represent social envirotype trajectories[2], i.e., distinct patterns of change in the social environment. At all time points and across all but two measures, Cluster 1 exhibits a worse social envirotype than both Clusters 2 and 3 (all ps for this and all subsequent tests <0.04 using ANOVA and post-hoc Tukey's test, corrected for multiple comparisons); Cluster 3 has the best social envirotype, scoring better than both Cluster 1 and 2 for at least three time points across all but two measures (see Figure 1a). Next, we evaluated potentially confounding demographic features of the clusters, finding that Cluster 3 had higher income and education and less pubertal maturation than the other two clusters, although there were no differences in age. These social envirotype trajectories are associated with both distinct mental health outcomes as well as distinct changes in mental health over adolescence, with Cluster 1 having worse and Cluster 3 having better mental health. Changes in Cluster 1's mental health exhibit a trade-off, while Cluster 2's mental health has the largest improvement and Cluster 3 sees the largest decline (see Figure 1b for composite measures). We then assessed whether these social envirotype trajectories exhibit dissociable patterns of brain network connectivity by performing edge-wise t-tests, correcting for multiple comparisons, then using a space-preserving `spin' test to summarize results at the level of brain system. We found that Cluster 1 displayed greater connectivity of Somatosensory regions (e.g. SMh, Aud, Vis) while Cluster 3 was distinguishable by more extreme connectivity in transmodal systems (e.g. CO, CP, DMN, DAN; see Figure 1c for group-level summary and d for pairwise comparisons). Additionally, we leverage the longitudinality of the data to perform a pseudo-intervention analysis to identify which kinds of changes in the social environment are associated with the largest changes in mental health. Finally, we assess how the patterns of brain network connectivity associated with better mental health look different for different social environment dynamics. This work points towards opportunities for interventions on the social environment to promote better mental health and wellbeing as well as a richer understanding of normative network neurodevelopment.

nodes.title: From Molecular Networks to Diet: Identifying Synergistic Vitamin Effects in Myocardial Infarction
nodes.authors: Aldana, Andres*
nodes.abstract:
Diet is a major modifiable determinant of cardiovascular disease risk, yet nutritional research has largely relied on reductionist paradigms that evaluate individual nutrients in isolation. Such approaches overlook the inherently networked nature of biological systems and the fact that dietary components act as coordinated, multi-target perturbations of molecular pathways. Here, we present a network medicine framework that integrates molecular interaction networks with population-level dietary networks to identify synergistic combinations of vitamins modulating myocardial infarction (MI) risk.

Our central hypothesis is that cardiovascular pathophysiology is organized into localized disease modules within the human protein–protein interaction network, and that vitamins influence disease risk by targeting proteins within or proximal to these modules. To test this hypothesis, we construct tissue-contextualized protein interaction networks and identify disease modules associated with key cardiovascular phenotypes, including thrombosis, inflammation, fibrosis, and vascular remodeling. We quantify the mechanistic relevance of individual vitamins by computing network proximity between vitamin target sets and disease modules, benchmarking observed distances against degree-matched null models to prioritize vitamins whose molecular targets are topologically aligned with disease-relevant neighborhoods.

Because vitamins are consumed as part of complex dietary patterns, we next model vitamin co-consumption using Gaussian graphical models applied to large-scale population dietary and biomarker data. These probabilistic networks capture conditional dependencies among vitamins, disentangling direct nutrient relationships from spurious correlations driven by overall diet quality or shared food sources. Community detection on the inferred vitamin networks reveals synergistic vitamin groups that co-occur in real diets and are jointly associated with reduced myocardial infarction prevalence through modulation of disease-relevant phenotypes. These communities define network-informed dietary scores that provide a data-driven alternative to expert-defined nutrient indices commonly used in public health.

Finally, we connect molecular and population-scale networks by mapping vitamin communities onto food composition data and performing in silico food substitution analyses constrained within consumption categories. This approach enables targeted increases in protective vitamin communities while preserving the overall structure of food macrogroups that reflect behavioral and cultural dietary habits. Together, these results illustrate how network science can translate molecular mechanisms into realistic, population-level dietary interventions, providing a scalable and mechanism-driven framework for identifying synergistic nutritional strategies to reduce chronic disease risk.

nodes.title: Network-driven discovery of food compounds for aging therapeutics
nodes.authors: Kerdelian, Garo*; Gross, Bnaya; Barabási, Albert-László
nodes.abstract:
Network medicine has become an effective tool for disease classification and drug design through mapping the protein-protein interaction network, identifying disease modules, and uncovering complex network relationships among gene expressions and disease phenotypes [1]. Although aging is not a disease, a recent study found that the hallmarks of aging - biological processes causing functional decline and vulnerability to disease - also form modules in the human interactome that can be leveraged for repurposing drugs to slow aging [2]. However, despite the essential role of food in human longevity, the impact of food compounds on aging remains largely unexplored in network medicine.

In this work, we developed a novel systematic pipeline to identify food compounds conducive to longevity, essentially treating food chemicals as drugs. We combine data on the protein-protein interaction network, the targets of food compounds, and expression data for aging genes to determine a list of food chemicals with statistically significant impact on aging hallmark genes in the positive (pro-longevity) direction, leveraging network proximity and a transcription-based metric called pAGE (pro-age).

We first validate that a significant portion of the compounds obtained from the pipeline have literature evidence for extending lifespan and propose others as candidates for cell line experiments. Second, we conduct an enrichment analysis of the compounds between pro-longevity and unhealthy diets. Third, we find that pro-longevity compounds have structural similarity through a learned embedding of the food compound database. Finally, we construct the mechanism of action for food compounds, illustrating that the network medicine pipeline uncovers biological network pathways that cannot be retrieved through compound targets alone.

[1] Barabási, A. L., Gulbahce, N., & Loscalzo, J. (2011). Network medicine: a network-based approach to human disease. Nature reviews genetics, 12(1), 56-68. 
[2] Gross, B., Ehlert, J., Gladyshev, V. N., Loscalzo, J., & Barabási, A. L. (2025). Network-driven discovery of repurposable drugs targeting hallmarks of aging. ArXiv, arXiv-2509.

nodes.title: Optimal prevention strategies for chronic diseases in a compartmental disease trajectory model
nodes.authors: Ledebur, Katharina*
nodes.abstract: In countries with growing elderly populations, multimorbidity poses a significant healthcare challenge. Despite this pressing development, important questions remain on how to model the development of multimorbidity. Leveraging a comprehensive dataset from approximately 45 million hospital stays spanning 17 years in Austria, we propose a compartmental model, traditionally used in infectious diseases, describing chronic disease trajectories across 132 distinct multimorbidity patterns (compartments). Our compartmental disease trajectory model (CDTM) forecasts changes in the incidence of 131 diagnostic groups and their combinations until 2030, highlighting patterns involving hypertensive diseases with cardiovascular diseases and metabolic disorders. We additionally use the model to pinpoint specific diagnoses with the greatest potential for preventive interventions to promote healthy aging. According to our model, a 5% reduction in new cases of hypertensive disease (I10--I15) leads to a 0.57 (SD 0.06)% reduction in all-cause mortality over a 15-year period, and a 0.57 (SD 0.07)% reduction in mortality for malignant neoplasms (C00--C97). Furthermore, we use the model to assess the long-term consequences of the Covid-19 pandemic on hospitalizations, revealing earlier and more frequent hospitalizations across multiple diagnoses. Our fully data-driven approach identifies leverage points for proactive preparation by physicians and policymakers to reduce the overall disease burden in the population, emphasizing a shift toward patient-centered care.

nodes.title: Network Resilience Theory of Aging
nodes.authors: Gross, Bnaya*; Barabási, Albert-László
nodes.abstract:
Two major theories compete to explain the origin of aging. The first, proposed in 1959 by Leo Szilard, attributes aging to the accumulation of DNA damage. The second, articulated by Robin Holliday in the 1980s, emphasizes the role of epigenetic alterations. While they reveal potential molecular origins of aging, both theories leave important puzzles unresolved. First, mutation and epimutation burdens increase linearly with age. In contrast, aging phenotypes follow markedly nonlinear trajectories: mortality risk rises exponentially with age (Gompertz law), and the onset of age-related diseases increases nonlinearly with age. Second, key aging phenotypes such as cell-to-cell variability, inflammaging, and immunosenescence cannot be linked to specific genetic or epigenetic alterations. Instead, they appear as collective phenomena emerging from the cumulative impact of these alterations on cellular function. 

Here, we present a network resilience theory of aging to resolve these puzzles.
