Network resilience is formalized as the ability of a network to sustain its basic functions under changes in its topology (mutations) and dynamical variables (epigenetics). Our theory conceptualizes aging as the progressive loss of network resilience as cells approach a novel critical mutation-epigenetic line. We characterize the changes in cellular susceptibility over time, identifying two fundamental regimes of cellular stability. Away from the critical line (young age), cells remain stable despite the accumulation of mutations and epimutations. In contrast, cells approaching the critical line (old age) exhibit a marked nonlinear increase in cellular susceptibility. 

Using both GTEx data and numerical simulations, we formalize a fluctuation–dissipation relation linking increased transcriptional noise to elevated cellular susceptibility with age. We also predict a nonlinear temporal delay in gene regulatory network (GRN) dynamics as the cell approaches the critical line, analogous to critical slowing down in statistical physics. Simulations of the mouse lung GRN reproduce this behavior and match experimental observations of delayed immune activation in aged mice.

Our framework also predicts cellular lifespan and rejuvenation potential across different tissues and links the accumulation of mutations and epimutations to aging phenotypes through resilience and critical phenomena, offering a unified perspective on the aging process.

nodes.title: Enhancing structural resilience in healthcare through patient flow network
nodes.authors: Zhong, Lu*
nodes.abstract: Large-scale disasters, such as pandemics and climate-related events, test the resilience of healthcare systems, that is, their ability to ensure patient care under extreme strain. Most existing studies measure resilience using broad, system-wide outcomes, but often overlook how coordination between regions helps manage stress and keep care accessible. In this study, we analyzed billions of electronic medical records to construct cross-regional patient flow networks in the U.S., mapping how patients moved across healthcare facilities. During the COVID-19 pandemic, we observed that cross-regional flow rose to 4.59\%, compared to pre-pandemic level of 3.53\%. This redistribution absorbed, on average, 63\% of the excess stress on facilities, meaning nearly two-thirds of surging demand was handled by shifting patients to less burdened regions, an absolute 32 percentage point improvement from the pre-pandemic baseline of 31\%. Further analysis suggests that strengthening cross-regional coordination could allow the healthcare system to absorb even more stress, reduce clinicians' demand, and reduce excess deaths. These findings show that structural strategies, like reinforcing patient flow between regions, can substantially enhance resilience, providing critical insights for future pandemic preparedness and disaster response planning, and ultimately improving patient care.

nodes.title: Sic Transit Imperium: A Metapopulation-Network Reconstruction of the Antonine Plague
nodes.authors: Riso, Biagio; Chinazzi, Matteo; Davis, Jessica; Urbinati, Alessandra; Gozzi, Nicolò*; Vespignani, Alessandro
nodes.abstract:
In the winter of 165 AD, during the Parthian Wars, a group of Roman legionaries stormed the sacred temple of Seleucia. Their impiety did not go unanswered: they carried away not treasure, but a curse of a deadly disease. From that moment, according to ancient Roman lore, began the chain of contagion we now call the Antonine Plague, an epidemic that swept across the Roman Empire for more than fifteen years, claiming numerous lives, including the co-emperor Lucius Verus [1]. Despite its vast impact, the plague remains shrouded in mystery. We still lack molecular evidence of its causative agent, and much of its natural history continues to elude us. Previous studies have used mathematical epidemic models to reconstruct the plague’s dynamics and to infer likely causative agents, comparing alternative model hypotheses against the limited evidence available on its impact [2]. However, these studies have typically relied on single-population approaches, overlooking the spatial spread of infection along the Empire’s travel routes. As a result, comparisons are often limited to totals of cases and deaths, rather than leveraging the spatio-temporal timeline of the epidemic across the Empire as an additional clue to its origin and underlying characteristics. In this work, we aim to close this gap by using a metapopulation network model to reconstruct the spread of the Antonine Plague. We draw on ORBIS data, which provide detailed, multimodal travel routes between major urban centres of the Roman Empire in the 2nd century [3]. After data cleaning, our mobility network comprises 677 nodes and 1104 edges. We simulate mobility flows using a gravity model that incorporates travel costs between nodes, calibrated to historical sources. We then couple this mobility layer to a compartmental epidemic model to simulate disease spread. Preliminary results suggest that a model parameterised on modern smallpox, when run on the reconstructed network, generates dynamics compatible with reported historical timelines—both the estimated time to reach Rome from the spillover site and the subsequent epidemic waves described in contemporary accounts. A representative simulation is shown in Fig. 1, illustrating the spatio-temporal diffusion of the epidemic across the ORBIS mobility network, with early transmission along major travel corridors and a progression consistent with historically attested spread patterns. Additionally, we extend the modeling framework to evaluate alternative transmission models, test competing hypotheses on the causative agent (e.g., measles and bubonic plague), and conduct extensive sensitivity analyses to assess the robustness of our findings. In conclusion, our work shows how computational, network-based epidemic models can support the exploration ancient pandemics by unifying fragmentary historical evidence into quantitative, testable hypotheses about their spread and underlying drivers.

[1] Duncan-Jones, Richard P. "The impact of the Antonine plague." Journal of Roman Archaeology 9 (1996)
[2] Karasaridis, Anestis, and Aleš Chalupa. "Comparative SIR/SEIR modeling of the Antonine Plague in Rome." PloS one 20.2 (2025)
[3] Scheidel, Walter. "ORBIS: The Stanford geospatial network model of the Roman world." (2015)

nodes.title: Host-specific recovery of the healthy human gut microbiota after antibiotics
nodes.authors: Kahn, Shaya*; Bashan, Amir
nodes.abstract:
Following disruption by antibiotic treatment, the human gut microbiota undergoes a recovery process that can extend over several weeks
to months, typically restoring broad community features such as species diversity and converging toward a general healthy state.
However, it remains unclear whether these recovery trajectories reflect a host-specific process where each microbiome tends to re-
establish its own original pre-treatment composition (“host-specific recovery”), or instead represent the assembly and engraftment of
taxa commonly found in healthy individuals, leading to a more host-independent post-treatment configuration. Because the microbiome
plays a critical role in health and disease, understanding the natural dynamics determine when it can be shifted versus when it remains
host-specific resilient is essential for developing effective interventions.
Here, we quantify the extent to which each individual’s post-antibiotic state resembles their own baseline compared to alternative
baselines and to randomized assemblages, and found that approximately 70% of individuals exhibit clear host-specific recovery. Moreover,
we show that the recovery process unfolds in two distinct phases: a rapid rebound of species that survive antibiotic exposure and thrive
again within days, and a slower reassembly phase involving the gradual colonization of new taxa over weeks to months. Yet even
when focusing solely on the slower reassembly phase, a substantial host-specific signal remains: about 45% of individuals still display
host-specific recovery, reflecting the higher likelihood of reassembling returning baseline taxa than of incorporating taxa not previously
present.
Taken together, these results suggest that post-antibiotic community assembly and recolonization are not random processes, but are
structured by ecological and biological selection mechanisms. One plausible interpretation is that historical contingency contributes to
this structure: following antibiotic perturbation, the resulting community composition may not be determined solely by contemporary
environmental conditions. Rather, it may be constrained by the pre-existing ecological context, defined by the interaction network linking
antibiotic-surviving species with both recolonizing taxa that were present prior to perturbation and genuinely novel colonizers.
To examine how historical contingency could enhance the colonization probability of both newly introduced species and taxa lost
during antibiotic perturbation, we developed a numerical simulation based on the generalized Lotka–Volterra model. Using a top-down
approach that does not require explicit knowledge of the underlying interaction network, we show that certain species are preferentially
able to engraft after antibiotics, relative to others. This preferential engraftment arises from their interactions with species that survive
the perturbation. Importantly, when the interactions between candidate colonizing species and antibiotic-surviving taxa are removed, this
advantage is abolished, indicating that network-mediated interactions are necessary for the observed effect.
Using our method, we detect a significant positive effect in more than 40% of individuals across two independent studies. These
results are consistent with the hypothesis that historical contingency contributes to host-specific microbiome recovery following antibiotic
perturbation.

nodes.title: Prioritizing the disease multiome with variant-coupled multilayer quantitative trait loci networks
nodes.authors: Halu, Arda*
nodes.abstract:
Background
The influence of genetic variants on molecular phenotypes, measured by molecular quantitative trait loci (molQTLs), offer invaluable insights into the functional consequences of genetics. While molQTLs have been used extensively in genetic epidemiology, no studies have so far attempted to analyze these relationships at the systems level by mapping trait-specific variant-omic associations onto large-scale multilayer networks. We hypothesize that deploying diffusion-based multilayer network prioritization algorithms on multilayer molQTL networks coupled by single nucleotide polymorphisms (SNPs) can provide unique mechanistic insights into the long-range and coordinated effects of genetic variants on multiple types of molecular traits.

Methods
We obtained blood- and plasma-based summary-level molQTL data for gene expression (eQTL), DNA methylation (meQTL), and protein abundance (pQTL) from the eQTLGen, EPIGen, and UK Biobank Pharma Proteomics consortia, respectively. We built a trait-agnostic heterogeneous multilayer network in which each network layer is an undirected bipartite molQTL network, with cis- and trans-QTLs represented as edges between SNPs and transcripts, CpG sites or proteins, interconnected by shared SNPs. We identified differentially expressed transcripts, CpG sites, and proteins in multiple cancer types in The Cancer Genome Atlas (TCGA). We used the resulting differentially expressed omics as seed nodes in a random walk with restarts on universal heterogeneous and multiplex networks (RWR-MH) algorithm to jointly prioritize transcripts, CpG sites, and proteins for each cancer type. We developed mixed-seed and leave-one-omic modality-out benchmarking approaches, and used the area under the receiver operating characteristic (ROC) and precision-recall (PR) curves to assess prediction performance.

Results
The resulting multilayer molQTL network included an eQTL layer with 725,803 SNPs and 9,929 transcripts, a meQTL layer with 209,138 SNPs and 247,429 CpG sites, and a pQTL layer with 11,624 SNPs and 2,525 proteins. All layers had a significant proportion of nodes in the largest connected component (73.6% for eQTL, 73.7% for meQTL, and 95.1% for pQTL). The mixed-seed benchmark, which used differentially expressed transcripts and proteins and differentially methylated CpG sites (|FC|>1; FDR<0.05) as seeds, achieved an AUPRC of 0.877 for breast cancer, identifying a balanced mix of omics, including non-seeds. In the leave-one-omic modality-out benchmark, which used disease-associated omics from one modality as seeds and aimed to identify disease-associated omics from the remaining two modalities, using CpGs, transcripts and proteins as seeds resulted in an increased prediction performance with AUROCs of 0.578, 0.657 and 0.731, respectively, for identifying disease-associated transcript/protein, CpG/protein, and transcript/CpG sets in breast cancer. 

Conclusions
We propose a novel approach to integrate molQTLs using multilayer networks and prioritize disease-associated transcripts, CpG sites and proteins using a multilayer network diffusion algorithm. Our preliminary findings suggest the potential utility of our method to uncover the coordinated effects of molQTLs at the global level in a trait-specific way, paving the way to identify multi-omic diagnostic and prognostic biomarkers that work in tandem.

nodes.title: Challenges and Opportunities in Single-Sample Network Modeling
nodes.authors: Glass, Kimberly*
nodes.abstract:
Analysis of biological networks can provide unprecedented insight into the mechanisms underlying disease. Although many methods have been developed to estimate biological networks, these approaches typically use multiple experimental samples to estimate a single overall network which does not capture population-level heterogeneity. To overcome this limitation, several methods have been proposed to infer single-sample networks, i.e. one network for each sample in an input data set. However, despite their common goal, the mathematics of these methods has been formulated differently, with different variable names used to represent similar concepts. To address this issue, we re-cast the mathematics of several common single-sample network methods, including LIONESS, SSN, SWEET, and BONOBO, using common variables. We then systematically explore the parameters, caveats, and underlying assumptions made by each method and examine how these differences impact single-sample network prediction.

Our results demonstrate the importance of developing an intuition for these methods grounded in their mathematical formulation. For example, both SWEET and BONOBO include "other" scale factors that strongly impact edge weights in the predicted single-sample networks, driving them toward the "background" network. Our analysis also points to a critical trade-off when trying to simultaneously predict network edges that are both shared across samples as well as those that are specific to a given sample. In particular, we found that BONOBO and SWEET consistently estimate the most accurate but least specific networks, while SSN estimates the most specific but least accurate networks. LIONESS straddles these domains, with an accuracy almost as high as SWEET and BONOBO and a specificity almost as high as SSN. In other words, although methods for which the predicted single-sample networks are highly similar to the "background" network tend to have the highest accuracy, this often comes at the cost of significantly lower specificity. This is important since the primary goal of sample-specific network modeling is to obtain networks that are specific to each input sample.

Overall, our analyses highlight some of the broader challenges in single-sample network inference, but also point to important methodological synergies. Code and resources supporting this work can be found at: https://github.com/kimberlyglass/single-sample-networks.

nodes.title: Beyond Proximity: Rethinking Accessibility Through Human Mobility Patterns in US Cities
nodes.authors: Kulkarni, Saumitra*; Moro, Esteban
nodes.abstract:
Traditional measures of urban accessibility rely on proximity to home, either in distance or travel time. Planning paradigms such as the "15-minute city" assume that nearby access ensures use. However, empirical evidence suggests otherwise: only 14% of daily consumption trips in the U.S. occur within a 15-minute walk from home, and residents of large U.S. cities travel a median of 6.7 km to consume fast food. These patterns indicate that people organize daily necessities across multiple urban locations, not just around their homes. We analyze individual-level mobility data from 1.86 million anonymized smartphones across 11 U.S. metropolitan areas to examine how people access daily necessities and which locations anchor their routines. We identify visits to daily-necessity points of interest (POIs)—Banks, Coffee Shops, Grocery Stores, Gyms, Laundries, Pharmacies, Post Offices, and Salons—using Foursquare data. 

For each individual, we define "offers" as the k-nearest POIs (k=10) around specific frequently visited locations, called containers. Containers are ranked by the total time spent there, with home always defined as the primary container. We introduce the offer-meets-demand ratio, phi^n, which measures the fraction of an individual’s visits to POIs that fall within the combined offers of their first n containers. Although the median distance from home to the nearest POI is only 700 meters, the median distance traveled to visit a POI is about 7 km, showing that people often bypass nearby options. Consistent with this, phi^1 (offers around home only) reveals three groups: individuals who exclusively use nearby POIs, those who never do, and many in between. Adding the second container—the most time-consuming location outside home—substantially increases phi^n for many individuals, indicating that daily necessities are often accessed near other routine locations. As more containers are included, the share of individuals with phi^n = 1 increases, though at different rates across POI categories. Importantly, locations serving as second containers tend to cluster in areas with high job density, highlighting workplaces as key anchors for accessing daily necessities beyond home. 

Overall, our findings reveal a dual nature of accessibility shaped by both residential and routine activity locations. Accessibility measures based solely on home location miss this behavioral dimension. Identifying where routine containers cluster can inform more effective and equitable placement of services and policy interventions, moving toward accessibility metrics grounded in how people actually use cities.

nodes.title: Causal discovery of housing-mobility relations in urban residential migration networks
nodes.authors: Yang, Xiyu*; Gibbs, Hamish; Santillana, Mauricio; Moro, Esteban
nodes.abstract:
Urban life is increasingly understood as the result of complex processes where neighborhood characteristic such as housing, physical infrastructure, employment opportunities, amenity access, and neighborhood context shape individuals’ social connections, access to opportunity, and patterns of residential mobility. A growing body of work adopts a network science perspective by representing cities as graphs whose nodes are neighborhoods and whose links encode flows of people, capital, businesses, or information [1]. However, most empirical data on urban networks are constructed from mobile devices and IoT streams, which emphasize high-frequency mobility dynamics over long-term residential change. Existing research therefore offers limited insight into how residential mobility networks are influenced by contextual factors at fine spatial scales, and largely relies on correlation-based analyses instead of explicit causal inference [2]. To address these gaps, this project develops a causal study of how housing, amenities, and demographics shape annual residential migration across census tracts in two large US cities.

This work investigates the causal relations between neighborhood’s housing, amenity, and demographic characteristics and tract-level residential migration, under the hypothesis that housing conditions exert a causal influence on migration while population demographics and spatial location patterns act as confounders. This study is informed by prior work [3] that uses a reinforcement learning–based causal computing framework to reveal causal and confounding relations among urban elements; adopting a similar causal perspective and data setup, we extend the focus to annual residential mobility in large U.S. cities. We construct four classes of urban elements: housing conditions and population demographics from the U.S. Census Bureau, amenities from OpenStreetMap, and annual residential migration from MIGRATE [4]. Census tracts define the nodes of a migration network, edges connect tracts with nonzero annual flows, and tracts are stratified by income level to aggregate inflow and outflow volumes into tract-level migration variables.

Using Greedy Equivalence Search with expert constraints, followed by Double Machine Learning for causal effect estimation, we infer causal diagrams among the four urban elements (i.e., demographics, amenities, housing conditions, and residential migration) for New York City, NY, and Houston, TX (2015–2019). Our method reveals consistent patterns
where median household income drives migration inflow from high-income census tracts. Yet the strength and direction of causal effects vary across cities and time periods. For instance, in New York City, a higher share of residents receiving SNAP benefits leads to reduced migration inflows from mid- to high-income tracts, a pattern absent in Houston. These
findings highlight both the promise of causal discovery for uncovering systematic forms of socioeconomic segregation and the sensitivity of inferred relationships to underlying urban conditions and data aggregation.

nodes.title: Daily mobility contributes to urban heat exposure inequalities across income and age groups
nodes.authors: Duran Sala, Marc*
nodes.abstract:
Segregation has long been recognized as a driver of environmental inequalities, concentrating disadvantaged groups in hotter neighborhoods. Yet the interplay between daily mobility patterns and intra-urban temperature variability remains poorly understood. Using anonymized mobile phone records and high-resolution temperature fields for 23 major Spanish cities, we quantify how residents from different income and age groups experience heat through their daily movements. We adopt a network-based framework where nodes represent districts and weighted directed edges represent trips between them. Each node is classified by its average temperature using deciles, allowing us to study how groups distribute their trips across the urban temperature field.

We find robust and systematic disparities: income-related exposure inequalities are substantially larger in magnitude, while age-related inequalities are smaller but highly consistent in direction across cities. To assess whether widely used population-based mobility models can capture these inequalities, we evaluate the gravity and radiation models. The radiation model reproduces most of the observed income-exposure gap, whereas the gravity model systematically underestimates it, showing that a large share of mobility-driven inequality can be explained by the spatial distribution of people and opportunities alone. Although more complex models could improve predictive accuracy, they would lose the generality and interpretability — key advantages of parsimonious mobility models. Overall, our results reveal systematic mobility-driven exposure inequalities within cities and highlight both the potential and the limitations of parsimonious mobility models for characterizing socio-environmental risk.

nodes.title: Urban semantic: detecting dynamic land use through human mobility patterns
nodes.authors: Iyer, Nandini*; Luca, Massimiliano; Langle Chimal, Ollin; Gonzalez, Marta; Di Clemente, Riccardo
nodes.abstract:
Human mobility research has leveraged large-scale digital traces to reveal hidden urban structures, with recent advances applying embedding techniques like Word2Vec to POI sequences, Place2Vec for location co-occurrence, and neural network embeddings to define urban functions. However, these methods treat space as static, either ignoring or aggregating temporal information. Place2Vec captures spatial co-occurrence but cannot encode how a location's function changes throughout the day, preventing understanding of how urban spaces transform functionally across time. We build on these foundations by proposing a novel embedding framework that captures the dynamic interplay between space and time in human mobility, expanding analysis beyond single-city studies to diverse urban contexts. Using mobility data from Cuebiq, we apply our framework across European cities (London, Turin, Rome, Cardiff), Latin American cities (Buenos Aires, Bogotá, Mexico City, Rio de Janeiro), and US cities (Boston, Bay Area, Chicago, Dallas), enabling cross-cultural comparison of temporal urban dynamics. Our methodology treats each trajectory as a sentence where tokens represent administrative zones visited at specific hours (e.g., "A\_8 B\_12 C\_18"). Applying Word2Vec learns vector representations for each place-hour combination based on co-occurrence patterns across trajectories. We cluster these embeddings using spherical K-Means to identify groups of place-hour combinations appearing in similar mobility contexts, revealing how zones shift between different functional contexts throughout the day (Figure 1A).

To validate that clusters correspond to real-world urban functions, we integrate Global Human Settlement Layer satellite imagery, classifying areas as Residential, Non-Residential, and Open Space at 10m resolution. We compute relative land use by dividing each zone's proportions by city-wide distributions (Figure 1B), then characterise each embedding cluster through three metrics per land-use type: average relative land use, tendency to be over-represented (relative land use $>$ 1), and consistency (variance), creating 9-dimensional vectors. We analyse how zones transition through mobility contexts by tracking cluster assignments hourly across weekdays and weekends. Clustering these temporal profiles identifies groups following similar daily activation patterns: some maintain high residential character with near-parity non-residential use on weekends, while others shift from weekday work activity to weekend residential use (Figure 1C). We distinguish embedding clusters (place-time combinations with similar mobility contexts) from zone clusters (zones based on temporal transitions). To examine spatial inequality, we incorporate socioeconomic indicators like the Index of Concentration at the Extremes for US cities and equivalent measures internationally. By analysing trajectory sequences, we track how different socioeconomic groups navigate urban contexts over time. Even when different groups visit the same zone, they may do so at different times when it serves different functions: residential during hours dominated by low-income visitors but shifting to commercial functions for high-income groups. This cross-city analysis reveals how temporal accessibility patterns vary across cultural and infrastructural contexts, exposing universal versus city-specific disparities in urban space experience beyond static land-use categories.

nodes.title: Dynamics between air transport network structure and urban economic activity
nodes.authors: Sugishita, Kashin*; Kajikawa, Shunsuke; Hanaoka, Shinya
nodes.abstract:
Air transport networks provide long-distance connectivity between cities, yet their dynamic relationship with urban economic activity remains insufficiently understood from a network-structural perspective. 
This study examines the temporal interactions between air transport network structure and urban economic activity using a global panel covering the world’s 50 major airports between 2014 and 2024.
Air transport network structure is characterised using the Global Airport Connectivity Index (GACI), which captures multiple dimensions of the structure of air transport network.
Urban economic activity is proxied by satellite-derived night-time light (NTL) intensity, which has been widely used in the literature as a consistent and spatially comparable proxy for economic activity. 

To disentangle interaction directions and temporal scales, we employ a dynamic time-series framework combining seasonal adjustment, unit-root tests, short-run causality analysis based on the Toda–Yamamoto procedure, and long-run relationship analysis using autoregressive distributed lag (ARDL) models. 
Here, short-run dynamics refer to month-scale fluctuations, whereas long-run relationships capture equilibrium adjustments over multi-year horizons.
The results reveal a clear contrast across time scales.
In the short run, causal relationship is rarely detected in either direction, indicating strong inertia and limited immediate responses between the two systems. 
In the long run, however, we find robust evidence of stable co-evolution between air transport connectivity and urban economic activity.

The spatial distribution of long-run relationships highlights pronounced regional heterogeneity.
While bidirectional co-evolution dominates globally, cities in China exhibit a distinct network-led pattern in which changes in air transport network structure precede long-term adjustments in urban economic activity.
These findings suggest that the interaction between aviation networks and urban economies is governed by slow-moving structural adjustment processes rather than immediate demand-driven responses.
By integrating network connectivity measures with satellite-based economic proxies in a multi-scale dynamic framework, this study contributes to network science by clarifying how infrastructure networks and urban systems co-evolve across space and time.

nodes.title: Reconstructing the Worldwide Airport Network using Open Source Data
nodes.authors: Santagata, Luca*; Urbinati, Alessandra ; Davis, Jessica ; Tizzoni, Michele; Srivastava, Ajitesh; Chinazzi, Matteo ; Vespignani, Alessandro
nodes.abstract:
While airport networks have often been studied using commercial datasets, reliance on proprietary sources limits the accessibility of air transportation research. Here, we introduce an open-source framework that reconstructs and analyzes the global airport network using only publicly available data, offering a fully reproducible alternative to commercial datasets. The methodology integrates heterogeneous open sources, including Wikipedia for route connectivity and passenger flows, OpenStreetMap for urban points of interest, and World Bank Open Data for demographic and economic indicators.

The framework follows a two-step approach. First, we reconstruct the worldwide air transportation network from the Wikipedia Airlines and destinations tables (which list the possible destinations reachable from the airport described on each page), achieving approximately 94% coverage of global airports. Validation against the OAG commercial reference dataset reveals strong correlations across multiple network centrality measures, indicating a high level of topological fidelity in the reconstructed network. Second, leveraging the validated network structure, we introduce a predictive framework to estimate annual passenger volumes on individual routes. We evaluate several machine learning models, including Decision Tree, Random Forest, and XGBoost regressors, using a multiscale feature set that combines country-level attributes (e.g., language, currency, and economic indicators), local characteristics such as population within airport catchment areas and the density of nearby points of interest (e.g., hotels and museums), network structural features (e.g., node degree), and geographical descriptors, including inter-airport distance and indicators of whether two airports lie in the same hemisphere or on the same continent. An ensemble stacking model, trained and tested using Wikipedia passenger volumes, achieves R^2 values of 0.67–0.70 at the global scale and 0.88–0.90 for domestic U.S.   routes.

Our results show that open data can simultaneously reproduce the structural properties of a large-scale transportation network and support accurate edge-weight prediction. Future steps include testing the fully reconstructed passenger flow against OAG data and adding additional predictive features, such as the number of hospitals and universities.

nodes.title: Emergent Scaling Laws in Drainage Networks Shaped by Probabilistic Growth and Landscape Boundaries
nodes.authors: Borse, Dnyanesh*; Biswal, Basudev; Singh, Arvind
nodes.abstract:
Drainage networks formed by natural landscape evolution processes self-organize to exhibit universal scaling laws. However, the underlying mechanisms behind these emergent patterns remain unclear. We introduce a probabilistic model that simulates the headward growth of drainage networks through probabilistic decisions based on local network properties such as drainage area and channel length. The resulting networks robustly reproduce key scaling relationships observed in natural drainage systems, including power-law distributions of contributing area and upstream length, as well as Hack’s law, which describes the scaling between channel length and drainage basin area.

Furthermore, the distribution of individual basin areas across a landscape is observed to follow a power law, with the scaling exponent increasing with boundary complexity. Our analysis of 25 island landscapes reveals a systematic increase in the basin area distribution exponent with increasing boundary complexity, quantified using fractal dimension and compactness metrics. To explain this relationship, we apply our probabilistic network evolution model to landscapes with varying boundary complexities and demonstrate that more complex boundaries influence network competition during growth, leading to basin size distributions with higher scaling exponents. Thus, the headward growth mechanism in the proposed model provides a unified explanation for both power-law scaling in basin areas and the positive correlation between the scaling exponent and the fractal dimension of landscape boundaries. 

These findings highlight the active role of boundaries in shaping basin area distributions and demonstrate how stochastic network growth mechanisms grounded in key physical processes can generate the universal scaling patterns observed in drainage networks.

nodes.title: Correcting temporal bias in mobility data using time-use surveys
nodes.authors: Sanchez, Sarah*; Gibbs, Hamish; Yabe, Takahiro; O'Brien, Daniel; Moro, Esteban
nodes.abstract:
Mobility data generated by mobile phones have been routinely used to measure patterns of social and economic activity in cities [1]. Despite its potential, mobility data suffers from well-documented limitations that can reduce the accuracy of these insights. These include {\bf demographic biases}, where certain sub-populations are over- or under-represented in mobility data, and {\bf temporal biases}, such as sample ‘drift’ over time or changes in individuals’ mobile device usage, which may affect the behaviors captured in these datasets. 

In this paper, we address temporal bias by benchmarking the American Time Use Survey (ATUS) [2], a high-quality, demographically representative survey of self-reported activities in the U.S. (Figure 1a) against observed behavior recorded by mobility data (Figure 1b). Using this approach, we quantify the mismatch between activities recorded in mobility data with those reported in ATUS across time of day and income groups (Figure 1c). We then apply these differences to re-weight mobility data in a process similar to post-stratification, which is commonly used to address demographic bias. Specifically, we examine the extent to which reweighting mobility data according to the ATUS impacts estimates of experienced income segregation at thousands of points of interest (POIs) in 11 metro-areas in the U.S., estimated via the method proposed by Moro, et al. [3]. We show that, while overall levels of segregation remain similar after adjusting for the temporal bias, there are still some significant differences at specific types of POIs and times of day. 

Our results show that, although aggregate measures of income segregation are relatively stable, re-weighting mobility data using external benchmarks, such as the ATUS, can reveal significant shifts in specific places and income groups, underscoring the value of correcting for behavioral and temporal bias. Our work highlights the importance of correcting for temporal bias when using mobility data to measure urban inequality, which has the potential to skew understanding of large-scale patterns of human behavior in cities.


[1] F. Xu, Q. Wang, E. Moro, et al., “Using human mobility data to quantify
experienced urban inequalities,” Nat. Hum. Behav. pp. 1–11 (2025).
[2] U.S. Bureau of Labor and Statistics, American Time Use Survey (2017).
[3] E. Moro, D. Calacci, X. Dong, and A. Pentland, “Mobility patterns are
associated with experienced income segregation in large US cities,” Nat. Commun. 12, 4633 (2021).

nodes.title: Mobility Networks Make Up for Missing Local Data in Epidemic Forecasting
nodes.authors: Ilami, Sepehr*
nodes.abstract: Human mobility networks—capturing how people move between places and interact across communities—have become central to epidemic modeling [1, 2]. Passively collected smartphone traces now allow researchers to construct dynamic, weighted networks where nodes represent geographic units and edges encode population flows, enabling improved epidemic forecasting. A key question for public health practice is: \textit{when does investing in mobility data infrastructure and network-based modeling pay off for epidemic forecasting?} We address this question using COVID-19 data from Massachusetts towns (April 2020--April 2021), constructing weekly directed mobility networks from anonymized smartphone traces and linking them to official case reports. We compute dynamic network measures, both holistic—such as betweenness centrality of each town in the network—and local—such as within-town mobility—and evaluate their out-of-sample predictive value for one- to four-week-ahead forecasts. Our central finding is that the value of mobility network structure is \textbf{conditional on data availability}. When high-resolution local case histories are unavailable or delayed, incorporating network topology dramatically improves forecasts: out-of-sample Predict-$R^2$ rises from 0.60 (using only statewide incidence with town and time fixed effects) to 0.83--0.89 when adding network position interacted with macro-level prevalence. The largest gains occur during epidemic waves and rising phases, precisely when connectivity patterns shift rapidly, and bridge-like towns become conduits for spread. However, once granular town-level case data become available, simple autoregressive models—capturing current cases in each town—already achieve Predict-$R^2 \approx 0.90$, and network features add only marginal improvement ($\sim$0.5 percentage points). These patterns are reproduced in agent-based simulations where infections propagate on mobility-like networks, confirming that the conditional value of topology reflects a general mechanism rather than data idiosyncrasies: macro-scale prevalence activates the predictive salience of a place's position in the human mobility network. Our results offer actionable guidance: invest in mobility network infrastructure and real-time network computation early in an outbreak or when local surveillance is sparse; once high-frequency case reporting is established, rely on autoregressive baselines and use network metrics primarily as diagnostic tools to identify potential spillover pathways [3].

nodes.title: Network Resilience under Chronic Disruption: Contested Road Networks in Ukraine
nodes.authors: Chung, Sukhwan*; Sardak, Daniel; Jin, Andrew; Linkov, Igor
nodes.abstract:
Modern transportation networks supporting military and civilian logistics increasingly operate under persistent adver-
sarial disruption. In contested environments, infrastructure undergoes continuous degradation, intermittently punctuated
by acute disruption surges. As full recovery may not be achievable, understanding resilience in this context requires
network models that capture the temporal dynamics of both functional loss and restoration.

We present a framework that integrates network science with GIS to analyze the resilience of transportation systems
under chronic stress, with application to military logistics. Using real-world armed conflict data, we model the degradation
and repair of Ukraine’s major road network over the three years of the 2022 Russo-Ukrainian war shown in Fig. 1. The
network is constructed from GIS road data, with origin–destination pairs representing logistical entry points and active
war fronts. Georeferenced conflict events are translated into road disruptions that evolve across both time and space.

Distinct from traditional percolation models that treat edges as strictly connected or disconnected, our approach
assigns paths a continuous functionality score based on travel-time increases. In this formulation, routes transition from
fully functional to delayed and eventually disconnected as disruptions accumulate. Network performance is quantified
by aggregating functionality scores across all origin–destination paths, generalizing binary percolation into a weighted,
time-dependent framework suited to logistics applications.

Through simulations of disruption and repair, we observe a transition from a collapsed to a functioning network state
as repair capacity increases. Below a critical threshold, ongoing disruptions overwhelm restoration efforts and the network
remains fragmented. Above this threshold, functionality recovers rapidly before entering a saturation regime in which
additional repair capacity yields diminishing returns, consistent with a second-order phase transition show in Fig. 2.

Furthermore, we examine restoration strategies under varying resource constraints. Priority-based repair, guided by
network-science metrics such as betweenness centrality, consistently outperforms first-in-first-out restoration at equal
capacity as shown in Fig. 3. Prioritization shifts the critical threshold, enabling recovery with fewer resources and
enhancing robustness against cascading failures. We also find that temporary surges in repair capacity can efficiently
move the system across the collapse–recovery boundary when applied at appropriate phases. However, poorly timed surges
may degrade performance, underscoring the nontrivial interactions between network structure and recovery dynamics.

These findings show that resilience in contested logistics depends not only on the amount of repair resources, but on
phase-aware deployment and strategic prioritization. Maintaining the minimum repair capacity is essential, while effective
prioritization of repairs provides the greatest performance gains. When prioritization is limited, resource surging can
serve as a complementary strategy, but must be applied carefully to avoid negative effects. This framework provides a
quantitative basis for designing resilient military and civilian critical infrastructure under persistent attack.

nodes.title: Reconstructing the early spatial spread of pandemic respiratory viruses in the United States
nodes.authors: Pei, Sen*
nodes.abstract:
Background & aims of study: Understanding the geographic spread of emerging respiratory viruses is critical for pandemic preparedness and response, yet the early spatiotemporal dynamics of SARS-CoV-2 and the 2009 H1N1 pandemic influenza (H1N1pdm influenza) in the United States (US) remain unclear. Here, we aim to reconstruct the early spatial spread of the last two pandemics and compare their transmission pathways across the US. We further simulate the effect of aircraft-based wastewater surveillance and subsequent local transmission control on curbing the initial spatial spread of future novel respiratory viruses.

Methods & results: We developed and validated an ensemble inference framework integrating airline travel, commuting flows, and pathogen superspreading potential to reconstruct transmission networks of novel respiratory pathogens, with associated uncertainty for inferred transmission pathways. Leveraging city-level influenza-like illness (ILI) records from medical claims and estimated county-level COVID-19 infections accounting for underreporting, we inferred the transmission pathways of SARS-CoV-2 and H1N1pdm influenza across US Metropolitan Statistical Areas (MSAs). The two viruses exhibited distinct spatial spread networks; however, both pandemics established local transmission in most MSAs within weeks, driven by several common hubs. The inference results for SARS-CoV-2 agreed with several findings from independent genomic studies in the US. While mobility flows partially explain the inferred transmission pathways, stochastic dynamics introduce substantial uncertainty in transmission routes, undermining the inference accuracy of heuristics based solely on mobility. Simulations suggest that both sufficient coverage of wastewater surveillance at transmission hubs and effective local interventions are needed to curtail the initial spatial spread of future outbreaks.

Implications: Our study provides a generalizable framework to infer early epidemic dynamics that may be applied to other pathogens. By combining mechanistic modeling, mobility integration, and ensemble-based inference, this approach provides a scalable, data-efficient method to resolve spatial transmission patterns in the critical early window. We find that stochastic dynamics play an important role during the early spatial spread of emerging respiratory viruses. Our findings highlight the rapid spread of pandemic respiratory pathogens and the challenges of early-stage containment.

nodes.title: How spatial proximity affects urban activities resilience: the case of COVID-19
nodes.authors: Desiderio, Antonio*
nodes.abstract: The COVID-19 pandemic disrupted urban mobility and economic activity, highlighting the role of city structure in resilience. Using anonymized GPS data from 181k users in Italy (Sept 2019–Sept 2020), we mapped visits to Points of Interest (POIs) and identified visitation motifs with Self-Organizing Maps, revealing six pre-lockdown patterns converging into three post-lockdown trajectories. Gradient Boosting models predicted POI recovery with up to 87% accuracy, with contextual urban features alone achieving 84%. SHAP analysis and street-network modeling demonstrated that POIs in densely connected, diverse clusters were more resilient, while isolated POIs were vulnerable. Our framework shows that both local POI characteristics and broader urban topology shape post-disaster recovery, offering metrics to guide resilient urban planning and targeted interventions.

nodes.title: Road Network Renormalization with Preserved Higher-Order Dynamics
nodes.authors: Zhang, Chen*; Hackl, Jürgen
nodes.abstract: Renormalization of road networks is traditionally approached as a problem of topological simplification. However, mobility on transportation systems is fundamentally shaped by path-dependent dynamics, arising from sequential decision-making rather than static connectivity alone. Using the widely adopted Sioux Falls benchmark, we show that different network representations, while exhibiting similar large-scale structure, can induce different behavioral and visitation patterns. This highlights a key limitation of topology-driven simplification: reducing network complexity without accounting for memory aware movement (i.e. higher-order dependencies) can suppress essential behavioral structure, thereby compromising the fidelity of models used for decision making and policy analysis. To address this, we propose a renormalization framework that operates across network orders and induced spatial distributions, enabling both the principled evaluation and the construction of coarse-grained network representations that preserve higher-order mobility dynamics.

nodes.title: Causal inference in the city: Improving Urban Policy Evaluation Through Mobility-Aware Methods
nodes.authors: Joseph, Bijin*; Gibbs, Hamish; Yabe, Takahiro; Moro, Esteban
nodes.abstract: Evaluating urban policy interventions is difficult because cities are highly interconnected and policy effects can spill beyond geographic boundaries, violating the no interference assumptions behind standard quasi experimental designs. We introduce a mobility aware causal inference framework that explicitly accounts for behavioral connectivity by constructing a mobility flow network from large scale, anonymized location data. We use community structure in this network to redefine exposure, assigning treatment and control groups by behavioral proximity rather than physical distance. We then apply the Synthetic Control Method to build counterfactual trajectories and estimate effects in the presence of overlapping citywide changes. Across multiple interventions, this approach improves placebo performance and produces more robust and interpretable impact estimates than conventional distance based designs.

nodes.title: Quantifying the Human Mobility Shift with Remote Work
nodes.authors: Gu, Francois*; Zhao, Pei
nodes.abstract:
The widespread adoption of remote work has profoundly reshaped mobility patterns following the COVID-19 pandemic,
yet there existed limited quantitative results for these mobility changes. In this study, we analyze large-scale mobility
trajectories from over seven million individuals across the United States and develop an activity-based–inspired model to
provide a comprehensive understanding of mobility changes induced by remote work [1].
Based on large-scale mobility data in 2019 (pre-remote work) and 2022 (post-remote work), we find people have
reduced traveling distance, extended stay duration at home, and higher flexibility regarding home departure time. The
average radius of gyration was approximately 5.8 km in 2019 and decreased to about 5.5 km in 2022, reflecting the
overall reduced need for daily commuting. Besides, we noticed an increase in the duration of time people spent in their
home census block groups (see Figure 1b).
The overall mobility shift comes from hybrid and remote workers in 2022. Based on weekly office visits, we classified
workers into three categories: remote, hybrid and on-site. As shown in Fig. 1a, weekly travel distances for hybrid and
remote workers are reduced by 22.3% and 59.6%, respectively, compared to on-site workers. Remote workers also exhibit
greater temporal flexibility, with home departure times more evenly distributed throughout the day, in contrast to the
pronounced peak hours observed for on-site workers (Fig. 1c).
Motivated by these observations, we introduce a statistical-physics framework for daily human mobility in which
complete daily activity sequences are treated as microstates weighted by a day-level energy (see Fig. 1d). The energy
rewards activity durations (Home, Work, Other) and penalizes travel costs between successive activities, while the
configuration space is restricted by simple behavioral constraints such as a minimum sleep duration. Remote workers
work from home with zero commuting cost, resulting in feasible sets of daily sequences that differ fundamentally from
those of on-site workers. This framework accounts for key mobility changes associated with remote work through feasibility
constraints rather than explicit behavioral optimization.
[1] J.L Bowman, M.E Ben-Akiva, Transportation Research Part A: Policy and Practice, Volume 35, Issue 1, 2001.

nodes.title: Exploring the Relationship Between Human Mobility and Inter-Firm Economic Networks
nodes.authors: Chen, Ketian; Yabe, Takahiro*
nodes.abstract:
Recent urban planning policies in Japan stress the need to create people-centered public spaces that foster social interaction among diverse agents and support productive economic activities. However, it remains unclear how everyday encounters in cities translate into formation of local economic networks. Previous studies have revealed how urban environments shape opportunities for physical encounters and generate patterns of segregation by using large-scale mobility data. In parallel, recent research has highlighted the critical role of face-to-face interactions in driving knowledge spillovers and economic productivity. Together, these findings suggest physical encounters may reflect the underlying social interactions patterns through which cities generate economic value.

Focusing on the Tokyo metropolitan area, we leverage mobility data and inter-firm transaction records to examine how mobility-driven social interaction relates to economic link formation. Using an anonymized mobility data set of 2.68 million mobile phone users and geolocated information for 196,317 firms, we construct meeting probabilities between firms by aggregating the physical encounters of their workers across space and time. We then empirically test the relationship between the worker mobility and inter-firm transaction network, which records annual buyer–supplier relationships among firms in Tokyo. Cross-firm meeting probabilities exhibit only a weak distance-decay pattern in Tokyo, suggesting that physical distance imposes limited constraints on workers’ encounters in dense metropolitan areas. We model inter-firm transactions with a linear regression model with meeting probability, physical distance, firm size, and transaction volume as covariates. The results show that higher meeting probabilities are associated with a greater likelihood of inter-firm economic ties. These findings underscore the role of everyday social interactions in shaping urban economic connectivity and provide insights for planning cities that facilitate productive economic linkages.

nodes.title: The Parenthood Effect in Urban Mobility
nodes.authors: Macedo, Mariana*; Menezes, Ronaldo; Cardillo, Alessio
nodes.abstract: We investigate how parenthood and marriage (two major life events) reshape urban mobility patterns, an aspect overlooked in traditional `average citizen' mobility models. Leveraging US census data, we analyse whether these life transitions create distinct urban experiences. Parenthood introduces new priorities including caregiving responsibilities, work-life balance adjustments, and access to family-friendly environments. Similarly, marriage introduces new dynamics including shared household decision-making, potential dual-income benefits, combined residential preferences, and shifts in social networks and lifestyle patterns. Our analysis demonstrates that cities vary significantly in how mobility can be accommodated by different household arrangements: some better accommodate either single individuals (Houston, Virginia Beach) or married people (Atlanta, Baltimore), whereas others favour parents (Cincinnati, Chicago). This classification becomes increasingly relevant for individuals and families as remote work expands relocation possibilities. We find that parents and married individuals face different mobility costs and amenity access patterns compared to their counterparts, with variations consistent across multiple null model tests. This research advances urban planning discourse by advocating for tailored design strategies addressing diverse demographic needs rather than one-size-fits-all approaches.

nodes.title: Women’s mobility networks enable efficient travel
nodes.authors: De Sojo, Sílvia*; Lehmann, Sune; Alessandretti, Laura
nodes.abstract: Gender differences in mobility are well documented: women tend to travel shorter distances and engage in less physical activity, yet surveys describe their travel as more complex and trip-chained, in contrast to digital-trace studies that find lower diversity and greater regularity. Using smartphone data from 543,155 individuals across 10 countries, we reconcile these perspectives by representing monthly mobility as networks of sequential visits. We compare repertoire size, activity, clustering, cycles, degree centrality of key locations, and the structure of “tours.” After matching individuals on baseline mobility, we show that men exhibit greater activity and variability, while women’s networks are denser, more cycle-rich, and more strongly anchored at home. Women more often form two-stop tours and achieve higher tour efficiency, whereas men rely more on back-and-forth trips, a pattern that persists up to 150 km. These structural insights align survey-based and digital-trace findings, revealing women’s mobility as both more interconnected and more efficient, and advancing understanding of gendered mobility inequalities.

nodes.title: Money does not buy time: Time-richness predicts ability to access and allocate time across urban life
nodes.authors: Rosen, Joshua*; Weis, Erik; Gibbs, Hamish; Sanchez, Sarah; Yabe, Takahiro; Klein, Brennan; O'Brien, Daniel; Moro, Esteban
nodes.abstract:
Urban residents inhabit the same physical city yet experience profoundly different opportunity structures. Traditional social science theory attributes these disparities to income-based access to resources and amenities [1]. However, we uncover an important empirical puzzle: individuals with comparable incomes utilize the same amenities at systematically different times of day. These temporal divergences are poorly explained by socioeconomic status alone. We argue that this reflects a deeper, unmeasured dimension of inequality, time-richness, capturing individuals’ capacity to flexibly structure and allocate their time across the day.

While social scientists have long recognized that the unequal distribution of time generates downstream forms of inequality [2], prevailing approaches operationalize time through coarse aggregates such as discretionary hours after work. Such measures cannot explain why some individuals can reconfigure their daily rhythms (e.g., visiting parks in the early afternoon) while others cannot. To address this gap, we introduce a mobility-derived measure of time-richness based on two orthogonal components extracted from large-scale smartphone geolocation data: self-similarity, capturing how predictable an individual’s daily movement patterns are, and peer-similarity, capturing how synchronized those patterns are with one’s socioeconomic peers.

Using smartphone geolocation data across 11 U.S. metropolitan areas, we show that these dimensions of time-richness are statistically orthogonal to income. Further, incorporating time-richness into behavioral models substantially improves our ability to predict when and where individuals engage in discretionary activities. Most critically, we demonstrate that time-richness explains variation in experienced segregation far better than income or traditional measures of discretionary time. Our findings reveal that time constitutes an independent axis of inequality, shaping how individuals access the social and spatial dimensions of the city. 

[1] Chetty, R., Hendren, N., Kline, P., & Saez, E. (2014). Where is the land of Opportunity? The Geography of Intergenerational Mobility in the United States. The Quarterly Journal of Economics, 129(4), 1553–1623. https://doi.org/10.1093/qje/qju022

[2] Vickery, C. (1977). The Time-Poor: A New Look at Poverty. The Journal of Human Resources, 12(1), 27–48. https://doi.org/10.2307/145597

nodes.title: Cost and preferences in bipartite complex systems: inverting the (sub-)optimal transport problem
nodes.authors: Mazzilli, Dario*; Patelli, Aurelio; Buffa, Lorenzo; Piombo, Riccardo
nodes.abstract:
Optimal Transport (OT) provides a foundational framework for modeling resource allocation between heterogeneous sources and destinations by minimizing a global transportation cost under conservation constraints. Classical OT solutions exhibit a sparse, tree-like network structure, a property that is rarely observed in empirical transport systems. To investigate deviations from strict optimality, we introduce a stochastic transport model that interpolates between fully optimized and purely random transport regimes. The model enforces global resource conservation while modulating the influence of transportation costs through a control parameter 𝛽: in the limit β→∞ the classical OT solution is recovered, whereas  β=0 yields cost-indifferent transport with flat preferences.

This framework provides a natural setting to address the inverse OT problem, namely the inference of the underlying cost structure from observed transport flows. In classical OT, this problem is severely ill-posed due to the extreme sparsity of optimal transport plans and the nonlinear dependence on both costs and constraints. By contrast, sub-optimal transport plans at finite  β retain substantially richer information. Exploiting the geometric properties of the solution space in this regime, we show that the inverse problem becomes tractable without imposing restrictive assumptions on the cost matrix.

We apply our approach to empirical systems, including international trade networks and ecological bipartite systems such as plant–pollinator interactions, framing both as resource allocation problems driven by gain functions. This unified perspective enables the quantification of OT-like mechanisms in real data, highlights systematic deviations from optimality, and sheds light on how optimization and randomness jointly shape observed transport structures.

nodes.title: Disentangling Spatial and Noise Structures in Network Processes
nodes.authors: Nocet-Binois, Esteban*; Hackl, Jürgen
nodes.abstract:
Real-world dynamical processes exhibit variability that is poorly captured by deterministic models. In practice, fitting such models directly to data often risks conflating unmodeled stochasticity with structural components of the model, leading to parameter estimates that absorb noise and lose physical meaning. Instead, stochastic process models provide a principled alternative by explicitly separating deterministic dynamics from (structured) random forcing.

Our work aims to untangle the effects of system topology, the dynamics governing evolution on that topology, and the statistical properties of external perturbations. In particular, we fill the gap between network inference from heat-diffusion trajectories and SDE-based graph kernels, to address the identifiability of spatial operators and noise structure from spatio-temporal observations.

nodes.title: The urban impact of AI: modelling feedback loops in location-based recommender systems
nodes.authors: Mauro, Giovanni*
nodes.abstract:
Location-based recommender systems are increasingly embedded in location-based services, shaping individual mobility decisions in urban environments. 
While their predictive accuracy has been extensively studied, less attention has been paid to their systemic impact on urban dynamics. In this work, we introduce a simulation framework to model the human–AI feedback loop underpinning next-venue recommendation, capturing how algorithmic suggestions influence individual behaviour, which in turn reshapes the data used to retrain the models. 
Our simulations, grounded in real-world mobility data, systematically explore the effects of algorithmic adoption across a range of recommendation strategies.
We find that while recommender systems consistently increase individual-level diversity in visited venues, 
they may simultaneously amplify collective inequality by concentrating visits on a limited subset of popular places. This divergence extends to the structure of social co-location networks, revealing broader implications for urban accessibility and spatial segregation.
Our framework operationalizes the feedback loop in next-venue recommendation and offers a novel lens through which to assess the societal impact of AI-assisted mobility—providing a computational tool to anticipate future risks, evaluate regulatory interventions, and inform the design of ethic algorithmic systems.

nodes.title: The urban impact of algorithmic navigation
nodes.authors: Cornacchia, Giuliano*; Nanni, Mirco; Pedreschi, Dino; Pappalardo, Luca
nodes.abstract:
The collective impact of navigation services is unclear: while widely used in urban mobility, they can also unintentionally reshape traffic flows and amplify emission inequalities. In this work, we introduce a simulation-based framework for evaluating the impact of navigation services, integrating real-world GPS data, road networks, and route recommendations offered by leading navigation services' APIs. The results demonstrate a universal pattern of amplified conformity: increasing adoption rates of navigation services cause a reduction of route diversity. Although navigation services recommendations can help reduce CO2 emissions when their adoption rate is low, these benefits diminish or even disappear when the adoption rate is high and exceeds a certain city- and service-dependent threshold.
To isolate the core dynamics of traffic and emissions concentration, we replicate our experiments in simplified mobility settings, confirming that these patterns emerge independently of real-world urban complexity.
Overall, our findings reveal how widespread algorithmic routing can induce emergent network-level concentration, with important implications for the efficiency and equity of urban mobility systems.

nodes.title: Unsupervised Embedding of Mobility Networks Reveals Invisible Barriers in US Cities
nodes.authors: Weng, Guangyuan*; Kim, Minsuk; Ahn, Yong-Yeol; Moro, Esteban
nodes.abstract:
Many visible borders are present within our cities, such as highways, railways, rivers, and other administrative bound-
aries. These boundaries can unintentionally act as physical barriers, hindering social interaction. These tangible
divisions are complemented by invisible barriers arising from demographic stratification, institutional gatekeeping, and
socioeconomic disparities, which collectively intensify social segregation. Traditional methods of identifying and an-
alyzing these invisible boundaries have relied primarily on qualitative observations, anecdotal evidence, or static census
data, limiting our understanding of how residents navigate and experience urban spaces. This study examines mobility
networks across 11 U.S. cities to identify both visible and invisible borders that generate unexpectedly low movement
patterns, utilizing unsupervised embedding of human mobility trajectories to uncover their underlying characteristics.

nodes.title: Unveiling temporal patterns in the maritime tanker network
nodes.authors: Teo, Kevin*; Kiss, Istvan ; Arnold, Naomi; Hone, Andrew ; Coulon, Michael; Santillana, Mauricio
nodes.abstract:
The global maritime oil tanker network is a critical infrastructure for the world's energy and fuel sector, handling an estimated 70% of global oil trade. Unlike other maritime industries, crude oil market volatility drives uncertainty in the tanker shipping sector, resulting in complex traversal patterns as ships respond to changing market conditions. Traditional complex systems analysis of maritime trade predominantly employs static network representations that mask the rich temporal information embedded in tanker movements. We introduce a dual-scale framework utilizing sequential motifs and Dynamic Mode Decomposition (DMD) to explore dynamical patterns at both individual vessel and fleet levels.  

Analyzing 192,000 voyage segments across four tanker classes representing 3,026 vessels from Jan 2016 to Feb 2020, our findings indicate that short-term route diversification---captured through sequential motifs---correlates significantly with the laden-ballast ratio, a routing performance measure defined as the proportion of time carrying cargo versus total travel time. High-performing vessels favor ABC motifs (visiting three distinct regions consecutively) over AAA motifs (remaining within single regions), achieving laden-ballast ratios approximately 50% higher. At the system level, we reconstruct directional cargo flows in and out of regional sectors over time. DMD of these flows reveals global seasonalities with a dominant period of 51.5 weeks, corresponding to an annual cycle. Phase analysis shows that major importing regions (East Asia, Europe) display synchronous annual demand, while export regions (Middle East, South America) exhibit complementary anti-synchronous cycles relative to the import regions. These temporal patterns, invisible to static analysis, reveal how complex temporality operates across multiple network scales, offering practical insights for operational optimization in maritime infrastructure.

nodes.title: Disease-induced immunization is efficient in dense geometric networks
nodes.authors: Balogh, Sámuel; Ódor, Gergő; Karsai, Marton*
nodes.abstract:
A critical aspect of infectious disease control lies in the achievement of population-level herd immunity, which also determines how the disease can spread upon a potential future outbreak among the residual set of non-immunized individuals. The effectiveness of herd immunity can be quantified by the size of the largest residual connected subgraph consisting of the remaining susceptible individuals after the population has been immunized. Importantly, the effectiveness of herd immunity is strongly influenced by the topology of the underlying contact network and, crucially, by the immunization process. Recently, Hiraoka et al. [1] compared two fundamentally different immunization strategies. First, vaccine-induced \emph{random immunization}, which involves a f fraction of randomly selected network nodes who receive immunity, reflecting mass vaccination campaigns (see Fig.1a). Second, disease-induced \emph{natural immunization}, which appears as individuals acquiring immunity after recovering from an earlier wave of an epidemic (see Fig.1b). Although both immunization strategies share the common goal of providing protection against an epidemic, their effects are often influenced by non-pharmaceutical interventions (NPIs). Such interventions typically aim to reduce interactions between individuals, thereby lowering the probability of pathogen transmission across the contact network. 

In this work, we explore the interplay between interventions and immunization outcomes using two distinct approaches. First, we model the contact networks as synthetic Geometric Inhomogeneous Random Graphs (GIRGs) [2], in which the average degree and degree heterogeneity can be implicitly controlled through model parameters. Second, we use the SafeGraph data [3] to analyze empirical state-level mobility networks from the U.S. before and during the COVID-19 pandemic. In this case, weekly origin-destination flows recorded in April 2019 and 2020 provide a natural experimental setup to reflects lockdown-induced reductions in the average degree.

Interestingly, both in synthetic and empirical geometric networks, we discover a trend-reversal phenomenon: in sparse regimes, random immunization outperforms natural immunity, whereas in dense regimes the opposite is observed (see Fig.1c). This effect is entirely absent in maximally random networks, highlighting the critical role of spatial embeddedness. We also observe similar behavior in other geometric structures, and real geometric networks (see Fig.1d-e).

In summary, we demonstrate that the average number of contacts in geometric networks is not just a scaling parameter but a structural determinant that can qualitatively alter the balance between vaccination- and disease-driven immunity. Dense networks favor natural immunization, while vaccination may become optimal when combined with contact-reducing interventions. These results highlight the importance of including network geometry and structural changes in epidemic models and might provide guidance for designing interventions that combine NPIs with vaccination strategies.

[1] T. Hiraoka, et al., Strength and weakness of disease-induced herd immunity in networks, PNAS 122, 28, e2421460122, 2025.
[2] K. Bringmann, R. Keusch, and J. Lengler, Geometric inhomogeneous random graphs, Theo. Comp. Sci., 760, 35–54, 2019.
[3] Y. Kang et al., Multiscale dynamic human mobility flow dataset in the U.S. during the COVID-19 epidemic, Scientific Data 7, 390, 2020.

nodes.title: Empirical Sexual Contact Networks: A Systematic Review Reveals Methodology Shapes Observed Structure
nodes.authors: Arfaoui, Heger*
nodes.abstract:
Sexual contact networks are fundamental to understanding sexually transmitted infection dynamics. Network structure determines epidemic trajectories and intervention effectiveness in ways that aggregate partnership measures cannot capture. Yet the empirical basis for parameterizing network models has not been systematically evaluated.

We conducted a scoping review, systematically identifying 17 sociocentric studies (1984–2022) employing four methodological approaches: contact tracing from disease cases (6 studies), chain-link sampling from community seeds (4), saturated sampling within bounded populations (4), and online data mining (4).

Our central finding is that observed structure varies systematically with methodology (Figure 1). Contact tracing studies report fragmented networks: largest components typically contain less than 20% of nodes. Saturated sampling and online studies find giant components spanning 50–95%. This divergence likely reflects methodological artifacts: contact tracing truncates at recruitment failure and disease-negative individuals, mechanically severing paths.

We also identify convergent findings. Adolescent networks show striking absence of short cycles, consistent with norms against dating friends' former partners. Adult networks show substantial cyclicity. The Likoma Island study demonstrates high connectivity through broad concurrency rather than hubs, challenging a common modeling assumption. Degree distributions vary by population: heavy-tailed in commercial networks, uniform in adolescent and rural populations.

These findings have direct implications for epidemic modeling: network connectivity is likely higher than contact tracing suggests, and commercial network patterns should not be generalized. Future work should prioritize saturated sampling of adult general populations, explicit attention to temporal ordering of partnerships, and standardized metric reporting.

nodes.title: Network Structure Breaks Homogeneous Mixing: Why Spatial Aggregation Distorts Epidemic Inference
nodes.authors: Santillana, Mauricio*; Bhora, Yash; Das, Haridas; Pant, Binod; Kiss, Istvan; Parag, Kris; Moro, Esteban; Stolerman, Lucas
nodes.abstract:
Simple compartmental epidemic models deployed at state and national scales during COVID-19 systematically violated
their core assumption of homogeneous mixing, yet the specific consequences for parameter inference and forecasting
remained poorly quantified. We demonstrate through canonical network simulations, mobility-informed metapopulation
models, and comprehensive COVID-19 empirical validation that spatial aggregation creates fundamental distortions in
epidemiological inference that depend critically on network connectivity structure.
Using metapopulation SIR models on canonical topologies (fully connected, star-shaped, unidirectional cycles), we show
that aggregate epidemic curves can deviate dramatically from local dynamics even when all nodes follow identical trans-
mission parameters. Large, sparsely connected networks produce spurious temporal patterns—including sustained plateaus
in effective reproduction numbers (Rt) where none exist locally—that fundamentally mislead policy responses. Standard
SIR models fitted to aggregated data systematically underestimate transmission rates (up to 55% bias), with error mag-
nitude determined by the interplay between network topology, size, and mobility levels.
Mobility-informed simulations using empirical SafeGraph data for Texas, Colorado, and Massachusetts confirm these
artifacts emerge in realistic connectivity structures. State-level aggregation in large, sparse states produces multimodal
epidemic curves violating homogeneous mixing assumptions, while Metropolitan Statistical Areas (MSAs) consistently
preserve epidemic coherence across diverse network structures.
COVID-19 validation across two years of US county-level data reveals systematic patterns predicted by network theory.
MSAs exhibit 2.5-fold higher epidemic synchrony than states (county-aggregate correlations >0.8 vs <0.6, p < 0.001).
Independent mobility community detection confirms MSAs align remarkably with transmission networks (NMI=0.965 for
large MSAs vs 0.865 for states), providing mechanistic explanation for their epidemiological performance. This conver-
gence demonstrates that functional connectivity, not administrative convenience, determines aggregation validity.
We present quantitative criteria that enable the prospective selection of appropriate spatial-scale data aggregation where
well-mixed SIR models may be reasonably used to characterize the dynamics of disease outbreaks, using a data-driven
strategy that leverages human mobility data from mobile phones.
Our findings reveal network connectivity as the fundamental determinant of whether spatial aggregation illuminates or
obscures epidemic reality, with direct implications for the operational practices of agencies like CDC and ECDC that
continue deploying compartmental models at state and national scales. We provide algorithmic protocols transforming
spatial aggregation from ad hoc administrative convenience to epidemiologically grounded decision-making for future
pandemic preparedness.

nodes.title: Community structure affects $R_0$ in the presence of infection heterogeneity
nodes.authors: Gupta, Abhay*; Landry, Nicholas
nodes.abstract: See attached

nodes.title: The impact of household physical distancing on the transmission of SARS-CoV-2
nodes.authors: Coletti, Pietro*
nodes.abstract:
Households constitute a fundamental mesoscale unit of epidemic spreading, where transmission dynamics are governed
by highly structured and adaptive contact networks. How these contact patterns are spontaneously reshaped by behavioral
responses following the introduction of an infection, however, remains poorly quantified and is often neglected in epidemic
models.
To address this issue, we analyzed a large prospective household transmission study of SARS-CoV-2 in the United
States (April 2020–April 2021), combining detailed behavioral data and longitudinal testing data to quantify how contact
network topology and its temporal reconfiguration following infection awareness shape epidemic outcomes (Figure 1).
Using Exponential Random Graph Models (ERGMs), we infer physical contact networks within 280 households at multiple
time points, capturing effects of household size, age-specific mixing, contacts clustering, and role asymmetry associated
with index cases. We find that awareness of infection induces a sharp, non-uniform rewiring of household networks,
