characterized by a 77% reduction in contact density and selective avoidance of index cases, while preserving age-
structured interaction patterns and higher-order network motifs.
We embed the inferred networks into an individual-based, two-level mixing epidemic model that explicitly couples
within-household transmission along network edges with community-level exposure. Calibrating the model via Bayesian
inference to longitudinal infection data collected in the study households, we quantify how the timing of behavioral
network adaptation modulates secondary attack risk. Counterfactual simulations show that initiating contact reductions
at symptom onset, rather than after test confirmation, can reduce secondary household infections by over 25% in
medium-sized households, with pronounced age-specific effects.
Our results highlight the central role of adaptive, small-scale contact networks in epidemic dynamics and demonstrate
how network inference combined with mechanistic spreading models can bridge behavioral responses and transmission
outcomes. Beyond COVID-19, this framework provides a generalizable approach for incorporating empirically grounded,
time-varying network structure into epidemic models.

nodes.title: To trace or not to trace: analytical insights from network-based contact-tracing models
nodes.authors: de Meijere, Giulia; Pugliese, Andrea; Gonzalez, Gerardo; Simon, Peter; Kiss, Istvan*
nodes.abstract:
Contact tracing is one of the most important control measures deployed during epidemics and pandemics, relying on
the identification of contacts of known infected individuals [1,2]. A network-based description is therefore essential for
its accurate modeling [3,4]. Pairwise models have been used extensively to study contact tracing, but existing analytical
results typically rely on a strong decoupling assumption—most commonly that contact tracing operates on a much faster
timescale than disease transmission [3].
Here, we relax this assumption and provide a full analytical characterization of the epidemic threshold without requiring
fast tracing. Our analysis employs a fast-variables approach [5], capturing the rapid early stabilization of key network
quantities, including the average number of susceptible neighbors per infected individual and the average number of
infected neighbors of a typical tracing-triggering node. Inspired by social adoption dynamics, we further introduce an
extended tracing mechanism in which infected individuals may be traced not only via direct contact with a tracingtriggering neighbor, but also indirectly as part of a triple containing two such nodes.
Using the resulting pairwise model and analytical threshold condition, we map out the interplay between disease
dynamics and the critical contact tracing efficiency required to prevent epidemic invasion. We additionally show that this
critical tracing rate is strongly model dependent: in particular, contact tracing vanishes from the outbreak threshold in
a classical mean-field formulation that neglects network structure. Together, these results yield a rigorous and tractable
threshold condition for contact tracing on networks, extending the applicability of pairwise models and providing new
insight into the interplay between disease progression and tracing processes.

nodes.title: Social immunity in ants: a network model of adaptive grooming.
nodes.authors: Dilisante, Francesca*
nodes.abstract:
Social insects, like ants, exhibit colony-level disease protection through social immunity, behaviors such as dynamic collective sanitary care that respond to pathogen load [1,2]. We present a probabilistic, continuous-time model of a fungal pathogen in an ant colony analyzing the core mechanisms behind risk-dependent collective grooming.

The model captures a bidirectional feedback loop between disease dynamics and network structure. An individual's pathogen load evolves through intrinsic growth, grooming reduction, and potential infection from caregiving. Grooming intensity, represented as weighted links in the colony's interaction network, is dynamically modulated by the infection status of interacting ants.

In the non-infectious limit, the system exhibits bistability for intermediate connectivity and grooming rates, where initial load determines a pathogen-free or endemic outcome. With transmission, we find a non-monotonic effect of social connectivity: increased connectivity initially promotes a disease-free state via amplified collective care, but beyond a critical threshold, it accelerates pathogen spread, causing collapse. Furthermore, dynamic grooming reshapes the network, hierarchizing it based on topological proximity to the infection source, a finding that aligns with experimental findings [3]. Varying network modularity yields outcomes from full containment to colony-wide epidemic, with intermediate modular structures enabling stable partial containment.

This minimal model, grounded in empirical assumptions of risk-dependent grooming and caregiving transmission, captures the essential feedback between adaptive behavior and disease that shapes social immunity. Its recapitulation of experimental findings supports its future use as a predictive tool for exploring scenarios that are challenging to implement in real colonies. Beyond social insects, it provides a controlled theoretical framework to explore adaptive network dynamics as outbreak defense, offering a pathway to address limitations inherent in outbreak-focused epidemiological models.

[1] Cremer, S., Social immunity in insects, Current Biology 29(11), R458-R463, 2019.
[2] Casillas-Pérez, B., Dynamic pathogen detection and social feedback shape collective hygiene in ants, Nature Communications 14, 3232, 2023.
[3] Stroeymeyt, N., Social network plasticity decreases disease transmission in a eusocial insect, Science, 362(6417), 941–945. 2018.

nodes.title: An analytical method to calculate epidemics threshold within spatial structure
nodes.authors: WANG, Boxuan*; Valdano, Eugenio
nodes.abstract:
Epidemic thresholds are a central tool in network epidemiology, commonly used to assess outbreak risk and guide interventions at national and global scales. On spatial and metapopulation networks, analytic thresholds are typically obtained from the spectral radius of a next-generation/reproduction operator. However, a single system-wide threshold compresses heterogeneous communities into one number and can be misleading for realistic seeding: even in a globally supercritical system, introductions in weakly connected or low-contact areas may still go extinct due to early-stage stochasticity.

We develop an analytic framework that extends the notion of threshold into a spectrum of community- and structure-specific critical points, derived from the fixed-point equations of multi-type branching-process outbreak probabilities. Beyond the global singularity, we show that local “activation” points emerge and are detectable via the minor outbreak size (the expected size of finite outbreaks conditional on extinction). Using a Lyapunov–Schmidt reduction, we prove that weak coupling generically shifts these singularities off the real axis into complex-conjugate pairs: the real part sets the effective local activation threshold for a given community or multi-community mode, while the imaginary part quantifies the smoothing (“smearness”) of the transition, i.e., how sharply the mode turns on under coupling.

The method is analytic and data-parsimonious, requiring only an estimated reproduction operator from surveillance and recurrent mobility data. Applied to real colocation-based mixing in Italy, it recovers local thresholds missed by the system-wide value and reveals dynamically cohesive versus fragmented regional structures. On high-resolution Texas data combining mobility with measles vaccination coverage, thresholds self-organize into clusters that align with metropolitan commuter-sheds rather than administrative borders, highlighting epidemiological units as dynamic mobility-defined regions.

nodes.title: Balancing Disease Model Accuracy and Privacy Protection in Mobility Networks
nodes.authors: Pullano, Giulia*
nodes.abstract: Human mobility data have become central to predicting the spread of infectious diseases, yet their use raises substantial privacy concerns. Individual movement trajectories are highly distinctive and can enable re-identification. This study assesses whether commonly used pseudo-anonymization and aggregation provide sufficient protection and develops privacy-preserving alternatives that retain epidemiological utility. We analyzed daily, individual-level location data to construct inter-county mobility networks. Despite aggregation at the US county level, we found that singling out individual movement patterns remained possible. To address this risk, we applied differential privacy mechanisms with varying protection levels (ε), which add calibrated noise to network edges. Informing a metapopulation model with these perturbed mobility networks, we quantified trade-offs between privacy protection and the preservation of critical network properties that determine epidemic spread. Networks with moderate privacy protection (ε = 0.5) maintained network topology sufficient for accurately predicting epidemic onset and peak timing, with minimal loss in predictive performance relative to unperturbed networks. Stronger protection (ε < 0.5) introduced noise that fragmented long-range links, degrading disease model accuracy, whereas weaker protection (ε > 1) offered limited privacy safeguards while preserving network structure. Our differential privacy framework provides an approach to protecting sensitive data while maintaining the network properties essential for disease modeling. These findings provide actionable guidance for researchers seeking to leverage and openly publish mobility data while meeting ethical data use standards, enabling broader data sharing that advances public health research without compromising individual privacy.

nodes.title: From human mobility networks to future pandemic preparedness: how mobility can inform public health policies
nodes.authors: Trevisin, Cristiano*; Parino, Francesco; Sabbatini, Chiara E; Rubrichi, Stefania; Bansal, Shwet; Colizza, Vittoria
nodes.abstract:
Countries have historically adopted both nationwide and targeted containment strategies to control epidemics, reflecting persistent uncertainty about how policies translate into health and societal outcomes. The COVID-19 pandemic provides a unique opportunity to revisit a central preparedness question: under which conditions do local restrictions outperform nationwide interventions in balancing epidemiological burden and social costs?
We analyze France between summer 2020 and summer 2021, a period characterized by substantial epidemic activity, partial vaccination coverage, and multiple variants. We use a stochastic metapopulation epidemic model calibrated to regional hospitalization data via a Spatial Partially Observed Markov Process algorithm and informed by vaccination data, variant prevalence data, and time-resolved mobility networks derived from mobile phone data. We simulate counterfactual nationwide and local lockdown strategies across a wide range of trigger and release thresholds, and assess their impact on cumulative hospitalizations and effective days under restrictions. We further examine how outcomes depend on contextual factors, including vaccination pace, spatial heterogeneity, and spatial scale of intervention.
Local strategies can outperform nationwide interventions, yielding simultaneous reductions in hospitalizations and social burden, but only within specific regions of the trigger–release parameter space. These regimes are highly sensitive to timing: small deviations in thresholds can reverse outcomes. In addition, they also depend on elements specific to the epidemic context, such as vaccination rate, spatial resolution, spatial heterogeneity.
Our results highlight that targeted interventions are not universally superior, but can be effective when carefully timed and adapted to epidemic context and spatial structure. Pandemic preparedness should therefore rely on a portfolio of strategies, supported by real-time data on transmission, mobility, and healthcare pressure, rather than a one-size-fits-all approach.

nodes.title: Generating function framework for spatiotemporal outbreak analytics
nodes.authors: Boudreau, Mariah*; St-Onge, Guillaume
nodes.abstract:
Branching processes and their solutions via probability generating functions (PGFs) are a cornerstone of network
science and network epidemiology. They enable the calculation of various quantities like extinction probabilities and
outbreak size distributions for transmission networks [1]. However, these efficient analytical approaches have yet to be
extended to operational public health tools essential for real-time forecasting and public health decision-making.
Here, we develop a broadly applicable, computationally efficient framework for spatiotemporal outbreak prediction and
inference leveraging generating functions. Building on Ref. [2], we formalize a novel generating function methodology to
encode and extract joint distributions of arbitrary observables, for example, cases and hospitalizations, across space and
time (Fig 1, panel a). To make this framework practical and efficient in computational epidemiology settings, we leverage
the cumulant generating function to characterize the first-order cumulants (mean, covariance) of observables, and use
moment-matching to model high-dimensional joint distributions. The resulting approach enables the principled Bayesian
inference of important disease parameters for structured epidemic models, obtaining similar posterior distributions as
state-of-the-art approximate Bayesian computation (ABC), but at a fraction of the computational cost (Fig 1, panel
b). We further demonstrate accurate predictions for complex metapopulation networks, extending the framework’s
applicability to the spatiotemporal spread of epidemics (Fig 1, panel c). By transforming generating functions into a
computational and statistical inference engine, we provide a pragmatic bridge between theoretical network models and operational public health tools, enabling rapid, data-driven outbreak response.

nodes.title: Vulnerability of the French hospital network to nosocomial infections
nodes.authors: Calmon, Lucille*; Bhuria, Shrichand; Chaturvedi, Madhav; Goseberg, Daniel; Karch, André; Donker, Tjibbe; Temime, Laura; Crepey, Pascal; Colizza, Vittoria
nodes.abstract:
Hospitals within a healthcare system are interconnected through patient transfers in a temporal network. This network facilitates the spread of nosocomial infections—such as influenza, COVID-19, and antibiotic-resistant bacteria—between hospitals. Consequently, the hospital vulnerability to these pathogens does not depend solely on within-hospital control measures and local community prevalence, but also on conditions in other hospitals and on the connectivity patterns linking them. 

To quantify this vulnerability, we reconstructed the daily temporal network of patient transfers between hospitals over 2023 in France using the PMSI (Programme de médicalisation des systèmes d’information) database. We used the infection propagator framework [1], which provides an analytical expression of the epidemic threshold for epidemic processes on temporal networks. The vulnerability index [2] is then defined as the inverse of this threshold, i.e. the transmissibility above which sustained epidemic spread becomes possible. 

Using data on hospital stays associated with c-difficile infections (CDI), a pathogen commonly transmitted in hospitals, we modelled the duration of CDI activity periods in hospitals accounting for hospital characteristics (size, patient characteristics, admission rates). Integrating the results of this model into the infection propagator framework [1], we parametrised this framework to assess the epidemic vulnerability of regional hospital networks in France to c-difficile infection. Comparing the resulting vulnerability index to network characteristics and regional case burden, we identified regions of higher risk, which are also those higher within region mean in-strength, and number of ICU beds per hospital.

Our findings enable a regional comparison of vulnerability, which can inform targeted interventions in the most vulnerable regions, ultimately benefiting the entire hospital network.

[1] Valdano et al. Phys Rev Lett (2018)
[2] Darbon et al. Prev Vet Med (2018)

nodes.title: Inferring spatial transmission matrices from Austrian wastewater sequence data
nodes.authors: Odor, Gergely*; Amman, Fabian; Bergthaler, Andreas
nodes.abstract:
Comprehensive wastewater surveillance programs enable continuous monitoring of the geographic distribution of infectious diseases. Beyond tracking prevalence, sequencing approaches quantify circulating variants and provide early warning signals for emerging ones [1]. However, current analyses largely ignore most mutations in wastewater sequence datasets, which are typically rare, often biologically neutral, yet still encode information about transmission dynamics.

Here, we introduce a method that leverages this overlooked signal to infer Estimated Transmission Matrices (ETMs) between wastewater catchments, which yield symmetrized transmission weights expected to correlate with true transmission counts. Using network-based epidemic simulations, we evaluate multiple statistical methods and identify a robust approach based on weekly counts of shared mutations, inversely weighted by their spatial ubiquity. Applied to the Austrian SARS-CoV-2 wastewater dataset [1], time-aggregated ETMs reveal transmission network structures consistent with expected transmission patterns (Fig. 1(a)). Seasonal analyses show longer-range connections between major cities in summer and more localized transmission in winter (Fig. 1(b,c)). This seasonal pattern is further quantified by fitting a gravity model of the form $\log (ETM_{ij})= \log((P_iP_j)^\alpha) - \log(d_{ij}^\beta) + \gamma$, with population counts $P_i$ and geographic distances $d_{ij}$. The model fit shows higher spatial exponents $\beta$ during winter and higher population exponents $\alpha$ during summer (Fig. 1(d)). Finally, we compare time-varying ETMs to Austrian census commuting data [2] and to a gravity model, with $\alpha$ and $\beta$ fitted to the entire dataset. We find that commuting data correlate more strongly with ETMs until early 2022, around the time when most Austrian leisure time restrictions were lifted, while the gravity model exhibits higher correlation thereafter (Fig. 1(e)). These results provide a new framework for benchmarking mobility datasets used in operational epidemic models, and for extracting transmission patterns from large-scale genetic sequence data [3,4].

Amman, F., et al. \textit{Nature Biotechnology} 40, 1814--1822 (2022).
Statistik Austria. \textit{Abgestimmte Erwerbsstatistik} (2025).
Ódor, G., et al. \textit{Nature Communications} 16, 4758 (2025).
Tran-Kiem, C., et al. \textit{Nature} 640, 176--185 (2025).

nodes.title: Field-Specific Networks: How Disciplines Shape the Geography of Global Scientific Recognition
nodes.authors: Gao, Jianjian*; Gates, Alexander J.
nodes.abstract:
Global science is an interconnected system of knowledge production, recognition, and exchange that underpins innovation, collaboration, and the diffusion of ideas across national boundaries. Mapping the structure of this system is essential for understanding participation dynamics, structural barriers to equity, pathways of knowledge flow, and pathways through which scientific influence travels across countries and fields. However, much of the existing literature maps global science using volume-based indicators (e.g. publication counts, co-authorships, or raw citation totals) that conflate scale with relational importance. This  "volume problem" causes high-output countries to appear artificially central, obscuring the structure of scientific relationships and masking meaningful patterns of recognition, dependence, and exclusion [1]. Recent scholarship suggests that the structure of global science exhibits increasing fragmentation rather than conforming to a canonical core-periphery structure [2,3,4]. To address this limitation, recent work has shifted toward relational measures of scientific recognition. In particular, citation preference networks capture realized patterns of recognition and reveal which countries systematically value whose contributions, independent of production scale. Using this approach, prior research demonstrates that the global scientific recognition network exhibits increasing fragmentation over time, rather than converging toward a stable core–periphery structure [2,3,4]. Yet national scientific recognition is not uniform across disciplines.  Aggregate, cross-field analyses obscure substantial disciplinary heterogeneity: countries may occupy divergent positions across fields, emerging areas reorganize international hierarchies, and mechanisms of geographic stratification operate differently across scientific domains. Understanding the fragmentation of global science and the diffusion of ideas therefore requires mapping citation preference networks at the field level, where the structure and dynamics of scientific recognition are most directly expressed.


Here, we use a probabilistic, rank-based measure to quantify whether one country systematically over- or under-cites another within each disciplinary domain [4], 
We analyze 76,080,360 OpenAlex publications spanning 26 fields from 1990–2022. Field-level citation preference networks exhibit pronounced heterogeneity. Network size ranges from ~70 countries in infrastructure-intensive fields (e.g., physics, chemistry) to over 100 in applied domains (e.g., medicine, engineering), while density varies from 0.054 to 0.119, with physical sciences most dense and social sciences and humanities least dense. Field-level analysis reveals many citation preferences absent from aggregate networks, particularly in computer science and physics & astronomy (30–40%), indicating recognition structures that depart from canonical core–periphery patterns; arts & humanities largely remain a subgraph (15%). Despite overall stability in community structure, field-specific deviations are substantial: China is markedly more central in computer science, physics is highly centralized, and medicine exhibits a polycentric topology.


Understanding disciplinary variation in global network structure enables countries to identify strategic niches---such as regional specialization in medicine or infrastructure development in computer science---where barriers to international recognition are lower.

nodes.title: Following the Laplacian Trail:  A Journey in Time Through Scientific Network Evolution
nodes.authors: Gallo, Paola*; Venturini, Sara; Urbinati, Alessandra; Davis, Jessica; Vespignani, Alessandro
nodes.abstract:
Scientific progress is deeply intertwined with  the story of humankind. By analyzing the global evolution of science in time, we can understand how strongly global events can influence its growth rate. These events span from technological big discoveries to conflicts and economic cycles [1,2].  In this work we use OpenAlex, a large-scale bibliographic database, to map the research space by creating a bipartite network between scientific works and the topics associated with them, and by projecting on the topics space. We obtain in this way a weighted and undirected co-occurrence network. We study the evolution of the structure of the network through an analysis of the Laplacian Spectrum [3].  Several indicators are analyzed, like eigenvalues variance distribution, effective dimension evolution and community detection from eigenvectors. Our results show some robust structural signatures that can be found in practically all indicators that come from the Laplacian analysis, and that are clearly connected to big global events. But, especially focusing on the last decades, more subtle features emerge from the combined analysis of the indicators. If from one side they delineate a network that has stabilized its dimension, from the other they show a very complex reciprocal relationship between science growth and science stagnation, and global events [4].
 
[1] L. Bornmann and R. Mutz, J. Ass. Inf. Sci Tech. 66, 2215 (2015).
[2] L. Bornmann, R. Hanuschild and R. Mutz, Hum. Soc. Sci. Comm. 8, 224 (2021).
[3] P.N. McGraw and M. Menzinger, Phys. Rev. E 77, 031102 (2008).
[4] Paola Gallo, Sara Venturini, Alessandra Urbinati, Jessica Davis and Alessandro Vespignani, Following the Laplacian Trail: A Journey in Time Through Scientific Network Evolution, in preparation (2026).

nodes.title: Publication preferences reveal structural diversity of academic fields
nodes.authors: Landgren, Ekaterina*
nodes.abstract: Publication decisions shape scholarly communities, yet the value of publishing in a particular venue is far from universal: a journal widely viewed as “top-tier” in one field may be peripheral in another, and researchers differ in how they balance prestige, audience, cost, and potential impact. Studying these preferences directly has been difficult because bibliometric data conflate aspiration with the ability to realize those aspirations. To address this, we developed an online survey platform where respondents select venues they aspire to publish in and then make pairwise comparisons among them. Using data from 3,600 participants across 13 academic fields, we obtained consideration sets (venues participants hope or want to publish in) and combined them with comparison data to generate quantitative preference rankings. We examine how these rankings co-vary with career stage, gender, publication records, and institutional prestige to characterize individual- and field-level patterns in publication preferences. Our results reveal substantial structural diversity: some fields exhibit strong consensus around a few central venues (e.g., 96% of economists selected American Economic Review), whereas others, such as Engineering and Computer Science, display broad and fragmented consideration sets. Fig. 1 illustrates field heterogeneity through venue accumulation curves (Fig. 1A), within-field overlap between top venues (Fig. 1B and C), and ranking network visualization (Fig. 1D). Regression analyses further show that researchers at more prestigious institutions both publish in and prefer higher-ranked venues at the field and aggregate levels, suggesting alignment between institutional position and preference formation. By allowing preference structures to arise directly from individual judgments, our approach extends prior work on journal rankings and disciplinary structures and provides a richer view of how academic hierarchies, field cultures, and researcher attributes jointly shape the publishing landscape.

nodes.title: Quantifying Ageing Effect in Scientific and Innovative Careers
nodes.authors: Yang, Yangfanyu*; Yang, Alex Jie; Yin, Yian
nodes.abstract: Recent advances in science and innovation have accumulated growing empirical evidence that old innovators continue to exert a lasting impact on science and technology. Citations, funding and awards are increasingly directed towards senior innovators, resulting in newcomers experiencing greater delays before achieving recognition. Despite the broad implications of this ageing phenomenon for scientific production, communication, and evaluation, our quantitative understanding of the underlying mechanisms remains limited. Here, we systematically quantify the ageing phenomenon across multiple innovative domains, covering seven decades of scientific careers and four decades of patent inventors. We find a marked increase in the average age of innovators who receive recognition, measured by incoming citations. We tested three commonly proposed explanations and found them insufficient. Instead, we identify preferential attachment and network densification as the causal drivers of ageing, and introduce a simple mechanistic model that not only reproduces the observed patterns but also reveals how structural feedback shapes the dynamics of career ageing. Analysis of the model demonstrates a direct link between ageing and network densification, providing a basis for forecasting future recognition patterns once the magnitude of densification is known. These findings contribute to a new empirical and theoretical framework for understanding career dynamics and inform policy design to support early career innovators.

nodes.title: The hidden structure of innovation networks
nodes.authors: Emer, Lorenzo; Gallo, Anna; Marzi, Mattia; Mina, Andrea; Squartini, Tiziano*; Vandin, Andrea
nodes.abstract:
Understanding how innovation emerges requires uncovering the structure of collaborations among inventors, firms and institutions. Here, we employ the ORBIS Intellectual Property data~\citep{orbis} across the years 2020-2024 to construct the \emph{co-inventorship} and \emph{co-ownership} networks of three, strategic, technological domains, i.e. \emph{artificial intelligence}, \emph{biotechnology} and \emph{semiconductors}. For each domain and level of analysis, we focus on the 500 most cited actors.

At the microscale, inventor networks appear as denser and more cohesive than organization networks. In order to investigate their mesoscale organization, we compare the results coming from modularity maximization with those coming from the minimization of the Bayesian Information Criterion, instantiated with both the Stochastic Block Model (SBM) and its degree-corrected variant (dcSBM)~\citep{karrer2011} - although the SBM is `agnostic' about the underlying structure to be detected, it is still block-wise homogeneous: the dcSBM, instead, refines the picture provided by the SBM by incorporating the degree heterogeneity. While modularity, consistently with its assortative bias, tends to cluster collaborations across all domains, the SBM and the dcSBM uncover a wider range of configurations, including non-assortative structures such as locally tree-like patterns.

As our results clearly indicate, innovation networks exhibit different mesoscale structures at different levels: while inventor networks self-organize in modules mirroring small, recurrent teams of collaborations, organization networks appear as combinations of core-periphery structures, where few, dominating actors coordinate the most peripheral ones. Moreover, Lorenz curves of forward citations show a pervasive inequality in technological influence: inventor networks display a steadily high concentration; for what concerns organization networks, instead, the steepest inequality is observed in the domain of artificial intelligence - driven by a handful of corporate-academic alliances.

nodes.title: The Sources and Impacts of Idiosyncrasy in Peer Review
nodes.authors: Barnes, Kathryn (Kate)*; Clauset, Aaron; Larremore, Daniel
nodes.abstract:
A common folk theory holds that success in peer review is down to the luck of the draw, that is, that outcomes depend heavily on which reviewers and editors a manuscript happens to receive. We call this editorial idiosyncrasy: the variation in how different editors respond to the same work, reflecting individual preferences for certain topics, methods, or interpretations. If such idiosyncrasies substantially influence publication decisions, they also shape scientific careers and the trajectory of research itself. However, the empirical literature on peer review has focused primarily on social biases and replicability [1], leaving idiosyncrasy largely unmeasured—partly because confidentiality makes detailed data scarce, and partly because measuring it requires a specific structure of editor-manuscript assignments.

This work investigates editorial idiosyncrasy at Science by leveraging the interconnected structure of the journal’s initial evaluation stage. These data, acquired through a data use agreement with Science’s publishers, enabled us to create a bipartite network connecting 48,800 manuscripts with only 443 eminent, interdisciplinary scholars (Fig. 1a), called the Board of Reviewing Editors (BoRE). In the initial review stage, BoRE members provide a rapid and shallow review of manuscripts, returning ratings from 1 to 10 to the presiding editors. Most submissions are sent to one BoRE (62%), but 18,444 articles are sent to two or more, allowing us to quantify systematic differences in how different reviewers rate the same manuscript. We examine whether such idiosyncrasy is present in the initial evaluation stage at Science, how this matters to later paper outcomes, and if decision makers appear to compensate for idiosyncratic rating tendencies.

By modeling BoRE ratings as a mixture of fixed paper qualities, BoRE members’ idiosyncratic rating baselines and noise, we show that more than one third (34%) of the BoRE exhibit idiosyncrasy, meaning their baseline is significantly above or below the population mean. In total, 134 reviewers rate papers consistently higher than average and only 15 reviewers exhibit lower-than-average rating tendencies (p < 0.01). To investigate whether these idiosyncracies matter, we use matching procedures from causal inference. Manuscripts sent to BoRE with negative baselines (n = 3, 675) are 4% less likely to go out to review (p = 0.000045) and 1.3% less likely to be accepted (p = 0.017) than papers which received only neutral advisors. By comparison, manuscripts sent to BoRE with positive baselines (n = 28, 666) are 1.7% more likely to go out to review (p = 0.00041) and have a 0.9% advantage for acceptance (p = 0.0014). Using matching, we reveal evidence that editors partially compensate for BoRE rating idiosyncrasies, estimating that the 1.7% advantage and 4% penalty would balloon to 7.3% and 7.7% respectively without the influence of the presiding editor (Fig. 1b). Editor compensation can also be seen in the predictive power of paper-level fixed effects from our model, representing the rating each paper would receive if BoRE idiosyncrasies were accounted for. These ”true” paper quality coefficients better model papers’ probabilities of being sent out to peer review, and later acceptance, than the raw BoRE ratings (Fig. 1c), indicating that presiding editors adjust ratings toward these values when making their decisions.

This work reveals evidence of idiosyncrasy in the initial evaluation stage at the journal Science, and shows that while rating idiosyncrasies impact later paper outcomes, editors compensate for a substantial portion of this effect. The kind of idiosyncrasy revealed here is likely a general phenomenon present in other review-based systems such as conference and grant review. However, in systems without a high density of ratings per reviewer, the compensatory behavior we describe is not possible, suggesting that idiosyncrasy substantially shapes outcomes in scientific peer review at large.

nodes.title: Dataset re-purposing fuels AI breakthroughs
nodes.authors: Yu, Yulin*; Ahn, Yong-Yeol ; Romero, Daniel
nodes.abstract:
Data is the primary driving force of AI research. New datasets enable not only the development of models with greater capacity, stronger performance, and broader generalization, but also deeper understanding of how models behave and learn. Sometimes, however, existing datasets are creatively repurposed to trigger innovation—much like old, familiar drugs are repurposed for unexpected treatments. We ask whether this form of creative reuse drives scientific innovation: Can it spark the emergence of new research areas, or does it instead result in isolated, low-impact work?
We measure dataset repurposing through a network-science lens by integrating (i) a bipartite dataset–paper network with (ii) a semantic embedding space of paper content. This framework allows us to quantify how far a focal paper moves a shared dataset into new topical regions relative to earlier dataset neighbors. 


To operationalize this study, we construct a large-scale bipartite dataset–paper network using data from Papers With Code. We derive a paper–paper network in which nodes represent papers and an edge is formed when two papers use the same dataset. The resulting network covers over 10,000 AI papers and 1,689 datasets. To quantify \emph{dataset repurposing}, we embed each paper into a semantic space derived
from large-scale language-model embeddings of paper abstracts.
Repurposing is then defined as the semantic distance between a focal paper and prior papers that used the same datasets.
Intuitively, repurposing measures how far a paper pushes a shared dataset into new topical
regions relative to its existing usage neighborhood.
Across this network, we find that higher repurposing is strongly associated with
\emph{disruptive} scientific impact.
A one-standard-deviation increase in repurposing corresponds to a 10.2\% increase 
in 3-year disruption, even after controlling for dataset frequency,
dataset-combination atypicality, topical novelty, venue, and team characteristics.
In contrast, repurposing shows no robust short-term citation advantage, highlighting its
high-risk, high-reward nature. We further study \emph{repurposing propagation} by asking whether subsequent papers that reuse
the same datasets move toward the focal paper in semantic space, effectively rewiring the
local paper--paper network neighborhood.
Propagation is relatively rare, but when it occurs it is consequential:
papers that successfully pull later work toward their semantic position receive both higher
citations and greater disruption.
Team-level analysis suggests that institutional research strength and
academia-industry collaboration increase both repurposing and propagation success. Our findings reveal new mechanisms through which specific forms of data utilization drive scientific impact, offering valuable insights for researchers, policymakers, and data curators.

nodes.title: Faculty mid-career moves among US PhD granting universities
nodes.authors: Prasanna, Chethan*; Zhang, Sam; Clauset, Aaron; Larremore, Daniel B.
nodes.abstract: Despite numerous enlightening studies of the hiring and retention of academic faculty, relatively little is known about the rates, patterns, and causes of mid-career moves (MCMs). Here, we analyze 10 years (2011-2020) of an annual U.S. faculty census to characterize MCMs. MCMs identified from this data induce flows between universities that naturally form a network such that there is a directed edge from university i to university j for every MCM from i to j. Certain analyses that depend strongly on the underlying network structure cannot be done with typical regression methods, and naturally require network null/reference models. In this study, we mainly investigate who moves and why, where faculty go when they move and the consequences of these moves for faculty careers and academia as a whole. Although MCMs are rare in academia (1.03% annually), we find a rich heterogeneity in the annual MCM rate by field and domain. Younger faculty, faculty at elite institutions and rural institutions are at a higher risk of moving, self-hires are at a much lower risk, while we do not find a significant gender effect on MCM risk. MCMs tend to move faculty to institutions closer in prestige to their origin institution more often than we expect, exhibit significant assortativity in public and private institutions and effectively increase the number of self-hires and faculty at private and urban institutions. Faculty experience prestige advantages when they move to their alma mater, to private and urban institutions, and women gain slightly more prestige than men over MCMs. Finally, we find that faculty tend to trade prestige for a promotion when they move, prompting us to develop a utility theory of faculty MCMs. These findings quantify large-scale patterns in MCMs in tenure-track/tenured faculty in PhD granting universities in the US at an unprecedented scale, enriching the grounds for further research to determine causal mechanisms that drive these patterns.

nodes.title: Idea Timing: Structure as the Hidden Variable in Innovation
nodes.authors: Yang, Seolmin*; Jeong, Dawoon; Ahn, Yong-Yeol; Youn, Hyejin
nodes.abstract:
Technological evolution is often punctuated by disruptive events [1]. To understand this punctuated evolution, we examine how community structure evolves in networks of U.S. patent technology codes. We construct technology networks from U.S. patent data between 1865 and 2014, where nodes represent technology codes and edges capture their co-occurrences within patents. We find that densely interconnected codes self-organize into modules whose life cycles exhibit birth, growth, division, and recombination [2]. Coarse-graining these communities reveals system-level reconfigurations, including war-driven recombinations and the emergence of semiconductor clusters, corresponding to disruptive events in the technological space. We observe that macro-level structures undergo punctuated reconfiguration, even as micro-level structures evolve more continuously (Fig. 1a–c).
To quantify these structural shifts, we measure the stability of community structure across time using Normalized Mutual Information (NMI). By aggregating forward- and backward-looking NMI, we define a measure of structural stability. Declines in this stability mark discrete transitions between stable regimes, often with forward-backward asymmetries (Fig. 1d–e). We apply this framework to rolling five-year patent networks from 1976 to 2014 and analyze these transition impacts on innovation. Tracking NMI-based stability over time, we classify years into transition- and stable-regime reference points: end-point years are local minima where declining stability reverses to recovery within the z < 0 range; start-point years are the immediately following years; and peak years are all local maxima within positive-stability intervals between transitions (Fig. 1f–g). These macro-level reconfigurations follow a punctuated pattern with episodic shifts [3].
Linking these transition periods to innovation outcomes, we find that patents granted at transition start points outperform those at end points in both deviation and annualized change of annual average five-year forward citations (Fig. 1h–i) as well as in market value (Fig. 1j). In contrast, peak years outperform other years not within transitions. These findings show that innovation advantage depends not only on cumulative processes, but on entry into episodes of network reorganization. Our framework reveals how innovation impact varies across phases of structural transition.
[1] Dosi, G. Technological paradigms and technological trajectories. Research Policy 11, 147–162 (1982).
[2] Palla, G., Barab´asi, A.-L. & Vicsek, T. Quantifying social group evolution. Nature 446, 664–667 (2007).
[3] Gould, S. J. & Eldredge, N. Punctuated equilibria. Paleobiology 3, 115–151 (1977).

nodes.title: Interdisciplinarity Revealed by Transitive Reduction of Citation Networks
nodes.authors: AlMuhanna, Haya*
nodes.abstract: We investigate the impact of transitive reduction on citation networks. Our hypothesis is that documents which lose fewer citations under transitive reduction are likely to be interdisciplinary, while a large loss of citations suggests a document is primarily cited within a single discipline. We test this hypothesis by using an artificial model of a citation network and by using data on citations from three sources: academic papers, court decisions and patents. Where needed, we applied modularity-based clustering techniques on a network defined using bibliographic coupling to classify documents by topic. A cluster-dependent measure was then used to classify the nodes as interdisciplinary or intradisciplinary. Our results provide strong support for our hypothesis in three of the four cases, with somewhat weaker but still positive support in the case of patents.

nodes.title: Second Acts in Scientific and Creative Careers
nodes.authors: Erkol, Sirag*; Tripodi, Giorgio; Wang, Dashun
nodes.abstract:
Careers are often defined by periods of concentrated achievement, when high-impact work occurs in close succession. Yet behind every celebrated first act lies a deeper question: what happens next? Understanding how a second act unfolds is essential to explaining the long-term dynamics of success. Competing theories offer divergent predictions. The Matthew Effect emphasizes “success-breeds-success” dynamics. Yet the same success also reshapes identity, expectations, and networks in ways that narrow subsequent search. These competing forces may operate at different levels, but their net effect is unified: success re-parameterizes the explore–exploit problem. It tilts the payoff surface toward exploitation, raises the perceived and realized costs of pivoting, and constrains feasible exploration through reputational and structural path dependence.

Here, we reconstruct the career histories of 21,470 individual scientists, 3,046 film directors, 2,243 artists, and 4,278 authors, including domain-specific impact measures. We assign topics to each work using methods from network science and machine learning. To robustly identify high-impact periods within individual careers while minimizing noise from single lucky hits, we use the hot-streak framework. This allows us to distinguish individuals who achieve repeated success from those who have a single high-impact period. We quantify topical dynamics using cosine similarity between topics in the first hot streak and the remainder of the career and exploration after the first hot streak using the entropy measure. 

Across all four domains, we find a consistent pattern: the probability of a second hot streak decreases as the impact of the first hot streak increases. This mechanism operates separately from the Matthew effect, which preserves long-standing status differences by amplifying the consequences of early achievements. Mechanistically, early success is associated with a lock-in effect: the larger the impact of the first hot streak, the more an individual will continue to exploit the same direction afterwards. This lock-in effect, in turn, limits exploration and decreases the chances of achieving another period of highly successful work. Crucially, lock-in is not inevitable. Mid-career exploration significantly increases the likelihood of experiencing a second act. This exploration, however, is costly. The larger the pivot, the longer the interval between the two streaks. Interestingly, the seeds of a second act, when different from the first, are planted in the early stages of the career. Even when pivoting, however, individuals do not abandon the first main topic of interest but rather embrace ambidexterity and a balanced portfolio approach. 

Taken together, our findings suggest that the traditional emphasis on early-career promise may be too narrow. In many domains, and particularly in science, the age of peak achievement is rising. This shift reflects a growing burden of knowledge and the lengthening learning curve required to reach the frontier of innovation. As a result, the widespread emphasis on early accomplishments may not reliably predict long-term impact. Our novel perspective is potentially informative for funding agencies, investors, and organizations that need to identify and support long and impactful careers, as well as support more forward-looking hiring strategies. The capacity to continuously recognize opportunities for exploitation, remain open to novelty, and adapt over time may be as important as early signals of potential.

nodes.title: Tenure and Research Trajectories
nodes.authors: Tripodi, Giorgio*
nodes.abstract: Tenure is a cornerstone of the US academic system, yet its relationship to faculty research trajectories remains poorly understood. Conceptually, tenure systems may act as a selection mechanism, screening in high-output researchers; a dynamic incentive mechanism, encouraging high output prior to tenure but low output after tenure; and a creative search mechanism, encouraging tenured individuals to undertake high-risk work. Here, we integrate data from seven different sources to trace US tenure-line faculty and their research outputs at a remarkable scale and scope, covering over 12,000 researchers across 15 disciplines. Our analysis reveals that faculty publication rates typically increase sharply during the tenure track and peak just before obtaining tenure. Post-tenure trends, however, vary across disciplines: In lab-based fields, such as biology and chemistry, research output typically remains high post-tenure, whereas in non-lab-based fields, such as mathematics and sociology, research output typically declines substantially post-tenure (Figure 1). Turning to creative search, faculty increasingly produce novel, high-risk research after securing tenure. However, this shift toward novelty and risk-taking comes with a decline in impact, with post-tenure research yielding fewer highly cited papers. Comparing outcomes across common career ages but different tenure years or comparing research trajectories in tenure-based and non-tenure-based research settings underscores that breaks in the research trajectories are sharply tied to the individual’s tenure year. Overall, these findings provide an empirical basis for understanding the tenure system, individual research trajectories, and the shape of scientific output.

nodes.title: The Network of Interdisciplinary US Faculty Hiring
nodes.authors: Aoki-Sherwood, Ben*; Larremore, Daniel
nodes.abstract:
Hiring a faculty member is a high-stakes investment and represents an academic department’s belief about the future
of innovation in their field. Faculty hired across disciplinary boundaries, with a PhD in one field and a professorship in
another, provide a window into the fungibility of skills and credentials between fields. They also reveal how the labor
market for academic faculty responds to the increasing emphasis on interdisciplinary research as a driver of innovation.
However, a major hurdle to mapping interdisciplinary faculty hiring is that existing faculty hiring networks draw institution-
to-institution edges [1], but interdisciplinary hiring is only detectable at the department-to-department level. This lack
of data leaves numerous questions unanswerable, including: How does interdisciplinary faculty hiring shape the academic
labor force? How does the system of interdisciplinary hiring affect the career outcomes of individual faculty members?
We transformed existing institution-level faculty hiring networks to the department level by systematically annotating
a large-scale faculty hiring census from the Academic Analytics Research Center with the PhD department and field for
each faculty member (from OpenAlex, ProQuest, and ORCID). The result is a novel dataset of department-to-department
faculty hiring in the US, which naturally forms a directed multigraph where each directed edge (i, j) represents a professor
in department j who received their PhD in department i. This network tracks 107,812 hiring flows between 12,833 US
academic departments and can be coarse-grained to represent flows between fields.
Although interdisciplinary hiring is common, the field-to-field hiring network is sparse. 38% of faculty are hired into
a different field than that of their PhD, but only 11% of field pairs exchange substantial flows of faculty (which account
for 26% of interdisciplinary hires). Some fields such as Economics exhibit unreciprocated outflows of faculty, while others
such as Computer Science and its computational neighbors are mutually connected by faculty hiring (Fig. 1a).
Our results shed light not just the structure of the system, but the consequences for the people in it. Despite
ubiquitous downward prestige mobility in faculty hiring [1], interdisciplinary hires experience smaller prestige drops from
degree to hiring department—19% versus 23% for their peers hired from the same field. At the same time, interdisciplinary
hires take about one year longer to secure faculty positions than disciplinary hires (Fig. 1b). Interdisciplinary hiring thus
represents a trade-off between higher prestige but longer training times for individual faculty. The sparsity and asymmetry
of the network suggest that interdisciplinary hiring, despite its prevalence, flows through narrow channels shaped by limited
training fungibility between fields.

nodes.title: Funding the Frontier: Visualizing the Broad Impact of Science and Science Funding
nodes.authors: Wang, Yifang*; Qian, Yifan; Qi, Xiaoyu; Yin, Yian; Dang, Shengqi; Qian, Ziqing; Jones, Benjamin; Cao, Nan; Wang, Dashun
nodes.abstract: Understanding the broad impact of science and science funding is critical to ensuring that science investments and policies align with societal needs. Existing research links science funding to the output of scientific publications but largely leaves out the downstream uses of science and the myriad ways in which investing in science may impact human society. As funders seek to allocate scarce funding resources across a complex research landscape, there is an urgent need for informative and transparent tools that allow for comprehensive assessments and visualization of the impact of funding. Here we present Funding the Frontier (FtF ), a visual analysis system for researchers, funders, policymakers, university leaders, and the broad public to analyze multidimensional impacts of funding and make informed decisions regarding research investments and opportunities. The system is built on a massive data collection that connects 7M research grants to 140M scientific publications, 160M patents, 10.9M policy documents, 800K clinical trials, and 5.8M newsfeeds, with 1.8B citation linkages among these entities, systematically linking science funding to its downstream impacts. As such, Funding the Frontier is distinguished by its multifaceted impact analysis framework. The system incorporates diverse impact metrics and predictive models that forecast future investment opportunities into an array of coordinated views, allowing for easy exploration of funding and its outcomes. We evaluate the effectiveness and usability of the system using case studies and expert interviews. Feedback suggests that our system not only fulfills the primary analysis needs of its target users, but the rich datasets of the complex science ecosystem and the proposed analysis framework also open new avenues for both visualization and the science of science research.

nodes.title: SciSciGPT: Advancing Human-AI Collaboration in the Science of Science
nodes.authors: Shao, Erzhuo*; Wang, Yifang; Qian, Yifan; Pan, Zhenyu; Liu, Han; Wang, Dashun
nodes.abstract:
Recent advances in the Science of Science increasingly rely on large-scale data describing scholarly production, collaboration, and impact, which are naturally represented as complex heterogeneous networks. While network-based approaches have yielded fundamental insights into the structure and dynamics of scientific systems, the growing scale of data and methodological complexity has raised substantial barriers to efficient, reproducible, and exploratory research.

To tackle this challenge, here we introduce SciSciGPT, an open-source, prototype artificial intelligence (AI) collaborator that uses the domain of Science of Science as a testbed to explore the potential of large language model (LLM)-powered research tools. SciSciGPT is a multi-agent AI system structured by a collaboration network of specialized agents, which automates complex workflows, supports diverse analytical approaches, accelerates research prototyping and iteration, and facilitates reproducibility. 

Through case studies, we demonstrate its ability to streamline a wide range of empirical and analytical research tasks, including network construction, visualization, statistical analysis, and regression, while highlighting its broader potential to advance research. We further propose an LLM agent capability maturity model for human-AI collaboration, envisioning a roadmap to further improve and expand upon frameworks like SciSciGPT. As AI capabilities continue to evolve, frameworks like SciSciGPT may play increasingly pivotal roles in scientific research and discovery. At the same time, these new advances also raise critical challenges, from ensuring transparency and ethical use to balancing human and AI contributions. Addressing these issues may shape the future of scientific inquiry and inform how we train the next generation of scientists to thrive in an increasingly AI-integrated research ecosystem. [SciSciGPT Full paper https://www.nature.com/articles/s43588-025-00906-6] [SciSciGPT Demo Video https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=cRA2G5HOL-w]

nodes.title: The Murmuration of Scientists
nodes.authors: Kim, Munjung; Ahn, Yong-Yeol *
nodes.abstract:
Scientists shift their research interest, or “move” through the space of knowledge, to explore new ideas and topics. Can this collective behavior of scientists resemble that of animals foraging in physical space?
Here, we create a continuous mapping of authors' positions in a topic space to examine how scientists "move" through the knowledge space in relations with their peers (Fig. 1a). Drawing inspiration from the boids model of collective animal behaviors [1], we model research topic dynamics through a set of simple peer-interaction rules that shape how scientists move through the knowledge space (Fig. 1b). 
We then analyze how scientific prominence as measured by the h-index modulates these basic rules. Our empirical analysis shows that scientists are more likely to align with the movement of, or cohere around, more highly prominent scientists within their local peer neighborhood (Fig. 1c). In contrast, highly proficient scientists exhibit a lower propensity to follow their peers’ movements overall (Fig. 1d). Furthermore, we find that the propensity to align with peers exhibits an U-shaped relationship with subsequent h-index growth (Fig. 1e), suggesting that scientific impact may benefit either from pursuing a more independent research trajectory or from closely following prevailing trends within the local peer community. Taken together, our framework treats scientists’ research trajectories as a form of collective motion, offering a useful framework for quantifying how peer dynamics shape the evolution of research interests and impact.

nodes.title: The Rise of Large Language Models and the Direction and Impact of US Federal Research Funding
nodes.authors: Qian, Yifan*; Wen, Zhe; Furnas, Alexander; Bai, Yue; Shao, Erzhuo; Wang, Dashun
nodes.abstract: Federal research funding shapes the direction, diversity, and impact of the US scientific enterprise. At the same time, large language models (LLMs) are rapidly diffusing into scientific practice, holding immense promise while raising widespread concerns. Despite growing attention to AI use in scientific writing and evaluation, little is known about how the rise of LLMs is reshaping the public funding landscape. Here, we combine two complementary levels of analyses to examine the LLM involvement at key stages where scientific ideas compete for public support: confidential NSF and NIH proposal submissions from two large US R1 universities, including funded, unfunded, and pending proposals, and the full population of publicly released NSF and NIH awards. We apply an established detection approach to estimate LLM involvement and relate it to the semantic distinctiveness of proposed ideas, proposal success, and downstream publication outcomes. We find that the use of LLMs rises sharply beginning in 2023 and exhibits a bimodal distribution, indicating a clear split between minimal and substantive use. Across both private submissions and public awards, higher LLM involvement is consistently associated with lower semantic distinctiveness, as measured using network-based embeddings, positioning projects closer to recently funded work within the same agency. The consequences of this shift are strongly agency-dependent. In both proposal selection and downstream outcomes, LLM use disproportionately affects NIH-funded research: it is positively associated with proposal success and with higher subsequent publication output at NIH, whereas no comparable associations are observed at NSF. Notably, the amplification in output at NIH is concentrated in non-hit papers rather than in the most highly cited work. Together, these findings provide large-scale evidence that the rise of LLMs is already reshaping how scientific ideas are positioned, selected, and translated into publicly funded research. More broadly, they highlight how generative AI can subtly alter the composition and consequences of public investment in science, with implications for portfolio governance, research diversity, the stewardship of public funding, and the long-run impact of science.

nodes.title: The social structure of scientific disagreement
nodes.authors: Murray, Dakota*; Liu, Yixuan
nodes.abstract:
Healthy disagreement among peers is vital to the production of scientific knowledge. Disagreement is also social. Whether a scientist disagrees with a peer requires being aware of the target (visibility ), sharing domain expertise to deeply engage (topical similarity ), and confidence to do so without sacrificing social position (social forces). 

We present a data-driven investigation into the social structure of debate in the scientific literature. Two forms of disagreement are examined. The first are 2,000 critical letters [1] that appear in journals of the American Physical Society (APS) and other journals. The second is more than 23,000 “contrasting” (and 350,000 “supporting”) citations sourced from Scite.ai. We use these data to represent severe and mild disagreement, respectively. 

Disagreement in science tends to be local. “Contrasting” citations are more often issued within the same venue and field, and to more topically similar papers than neutral ones (Fig 1.a). Notably, the most highly-cited (visible) papers are less likely than expected to receive a contrasting citation, suggesting that visibility is not the sole driver of disagreement (Fig 1.b). This extends past work which observed that critical letters target visible and topically-similar papers [1]. 

Social forces shape disagreement. Exploratory analysis finds that senior authors are disproportionately involved compared to other authors published in the same journal (Fig 1.c), and are more often men and from Europe (results not shown). Focusing on the case of APS, the “social distance” spanned by authors writing and receiving critical letters is greater than the baseline of neutral citations (Fig 1.d); no difference is observed for “contrasting” citations (not shown).

Disagreement is structurally distinct from neutral citation. We build a journal citation network and contrast the weighted degree and eigenvector centrality of journals using only edges of each citation type. “Contrasting” citations are more dissimilar to mentioning citations than are supporting across both node centrality metrics (Fig 1.e).

Collectively, these results suggest that disagreement in science is directed locally against similar papers, but not against direct or near collaborators. Further, disagreement is a structurally distinct form of scholarly communication

nodes.title: The software space of science
nodes.authors: Wu, Zhouming*; Murray, Dakota
nodes.abstract:
Science advances not only through the accumulation of facts but also the evolution of tools. Yet tool use is difficult to study: tools are tacitly transmitted and their adoption is not systematically tracked. The widespread use of software in research offers a rare opportunity, as software mentions in scientific articles can be identified and traced over time.
Here we map the software space of science by analyzing 1.3 million publications across 170 disciplines from 2004 to 2021. We construct a network of 520 software tools linked by disciplinary co-usage, with link strength weighted by proximity based on Revealed Comparative Advantage (RCA). The resulting network reveals a structured landscape where tools cluster into functional communities, from computing and statistics to wet lab and dry lab applications. Each discipline carries a distinct signature of used tools reflecting its investigative character.
Tracking disciplinary tool ecologies over time uncovers divergent patterns. Data-intensive fields show progressive consolidation around dominant platforms, while laboratory-based sciences retain varied, task-specific toolkits. Yet across most domains, software usage has grown increasingly stable, suggesting a broad trend toward instrumental settlement. This stability could indicate field maturity or technological lock-in. Disentangling these interpretations requires linking tool patterns to scientific outcomes, a question our software space now makes accessible.

nodes.title: AIProxiMed: AI-Powered Network Proximity Modeling for Therapeutic Discovery across Pharmacological and Nutritional Chemical Spaces
nodes.authors: Xu, Hao*; Menichetti, Giulia; Loscalzo, Joseph
nodes.abstract:
Drug–target interaction (DTI) prediction is a central challenge in drug discovery and has traditionally relied on experimental assays. Although highly reliable, wet-lab experiments are costly, labor-intensive, and difficult to scale. To overcome these limitations, a variety of computational approaches have been developed. Classical computational strategies, such as hand-crafted similarity measures and descriptor-based QSAR models, typically rely on fixed feature representations and predominantly local inferences in the chemical or target space, limiting their capacity to incorporate modern representation learning or leverage global interaction context. Recent advances in deep learning have enabled end-to-end DTI prediction directly from molecular and protein representations; yet, many existing models depend on the availability of large labeled datasets, overlook the global structure of interaction networks, and struggle to generalize to unseen molecules under data sparsity and sampling bias. 

To bridge these gaps, we introduce AIProxiMed, an AI-powered, network-based framework for estimating ligand–target proximity within drug–target interaction networks. Drawing on network science, drugs and targets are represented as nodes in an interaction graph, enabling DTI prediction to be naturally formulated as a distance-prediction problem, where distances are learned directly from molecular representations rather than computed from observed interactions. AIProxiMed encodes molecular structures using a chemical language model to produce high-dimensional semantic representations, which are then mapped to predict discrete, ordered graph distances between compounds and biological targets. These distances serve as proxies for relative ligand–protein binding propensity.
By casting proximity prediction as an ordinal regression problem rather than an unordered multiclass classification task, the framework aligns learning objectives with the hierarchical structure of interaction networks and avoids modeling adjacent proximity levels as mutually independent classes, thereby mitigating the effects of sparse and noisy supervision.

We then apply AIProxiMed for therapeutic target discovery in heart failure. The trained model is used to screen both approved drugs and food-derived small molecules, prioritizing candidates that are proximal in the drug–target network to disease-associated proteins such as the clinically relevant AT2T1, ACE and HMG-CoA reductase. By integrating molecular semantics, network topology, and ordinal regression, AIProxiMed provides a scalable and data-efficient framework for compound prioritization, enabling network-informed discovery across pharmacological and nutritional chemical spaces.

nodes.title: Brain network science modelling of sparse neural networks enables Transformers and LLMs to perform as fully connected
nodes.authors: Zhang, Yingtao; Cerretti ,  Diego ; Zhao, Jialin ; Liao, Ziheng; Wu, Wenjing; Micheli, Umberto; Cannistraci, Carlo*
nodes.abstract: This study aims to enlarge our current knowledge on the application of brain-inspired network science principles for training artificial neural networks (ANNs) with sparse connectivity. Dynamic sparse training (DST) emulates the synaptic turnover of real brain networks, reducing the computational demands of training and inference in ANNs. However, existing DST methods face difficulties in maintaining peak performance at high connectivity sparsity levels. The Cannistraci-Hebb training (CHT) is a brain-inspired method that is used in DST for growing synaptic connectivity in sparse neural networks. CHT leverages a gradient-free, topology-driven link regrowth mechanism, which has been shown to achieve ultrasparse (1% connectivity or lower) advantage across various tasks compared to fully connected networks. Yet, CHT suffers two main drawbacks: (i) its time complexity is O(N · d^3)- N node network size, d node degree - hence it can be efficiently applied only to ultra-sparse networks. (ii) it rigidly selects top link prediction scores, which is inappropriate for the early training epochs, when the network topology presents many unreliable connections. Here, we design the first brain-inspired network model - termed bipartite receptive field (BRF) - to initialize the connectivity of sparse artificial neural networks. Then, we propose a matrix multiplication GPU-friendly approximation of the CH link predictor, which reduces the computational complexity to O(N^3), enabling a fast implementation of link prediction in large-scale models. Moreover, we introduce the Cannistraci-Hebb training soft rule (CHTs), which adopts a flexible strategy for sampling connections in both link removal and regrowth, balancing the exploration and exploitation of network topology. Additionally, we propose a sigmoid-based gradual density decay strategy, leading to an advanced framework referred to as CHTss. Empirical results show that BRF offers performance advantages over previous network science models. Using 1% of connections, CHTs outperforms fully connected networks in MLP architectures on visual classification tasks, compressing some networks to less than 30% of the nodes. Using 5% of the connections, CHTss outperforms fully connected networks in two Transformer-based machine translation tasks. Finally, with only 30% of the connections, both CHTs and CHTss achieve superior performance over other dynamic sparse training methods, and perform on par with—or even surpass—their fully connected counterparts in language modeling across various sparsity levels within the LLaMA model family.

nodes.title: Hyperedge Classification for Predicting High-Order Drug Interactions in Pharmacological Networks
nodes.authors: Shirmohammady, Naeem*; Kafaie, Somayeh
nodes.abstract:
High-order networks exhibit emergent relations that cannot be reduced to dyadic interactions or modeled through traditional graph-based techniques. Pharmacological networks represent a notable example of such high-order complexity, where adverse outcomes frequently arise when multiple drugs are co-administered. These complex drug-drug interactions impose a significant burden on healthcare systems, contributing to annual costs exceeding $177 billion in the United States.
In this study, we model the pharmacological system as a hypergraph, where nodes represent drugs and hyperedges encode interacting drug combinations of size two to five. We cast the prediction of myopathy-inducing combinations as a hyperedge prediction task, allowing direct modeling of multi-drug interactions beyond pairwise relationships. Each drug is characterized using Morgan fingerprints to capture structural features and multi-hot vectors of known protein targets to represent biological mechanisms. We employ a hybrid propagation strategy that integrates feature-based similarity with co-occurrence information from observed hyperedges, allowing the learned embeddings to reflect both molecular similarity and real-world co-administration patterns.
Building on these embeddings, we introduce an intra-hyperedge self-attention mechanism to model context-dependent interactions within each drug combination. Drug features are first projected into a latent space, after which the attention module assigns importance weights to individual drugs by jointly considering their intrinsic features and the surrounding hypergraph structure, including co-occurrence and neighborhood information. This enables the model to identify influential drugs within a combination while capturing higher-order interaction effects. A gated fusion layer then integrates intrinsic and contextual representations, and a permutation-invariant pooling operation produces interpretable hyperedge-level embeddings that reflect the relative contribution of each drug. The results demonstrate stable predictive performance across folds (accuracy ≈ 0.89$, F1 ≈ 0.89$, AUC ≈ 0.96$), providing evidence that the model can effectively capture high-order pharmacological risk and generate reliable estimates for previously unseen drug combinations. Although only a limited number of studies have addressed high-order drug interactions, our model achieves a performance that is among the strongest reported for this prediction task.

nodes.title: Pre-Trained Graph Neural Network for Universal Network Dynamics
nodes.authors: Riedl, Christoph*; Smead, Rory
nodes.abstract: A longstanding question in network science is to connect the dynamics of network formation with the dynamical processes that unfold on those networks. We develop a generative model that connects the dynamics of interactions and graph structure on a fundamental level. Our model applies to all agents and organism that rely on reproduction, imitation, or learning and which then experience functional benefits or ``payoffs'' from their interactions. This model encodes topological structures of cooperative interactions from generic mutual benefit such as enhanced stability, enhanced functionality, or lower overall system's free energy. It specifically captures the tension between cooperative and competitive dynamics such as competition for binding sites or pathological states. Building on this new theory model, we develop a physics-aware Graph Neural Network (GNN) which we then apply in a transductive way to real network datasets like Protein-Protein interaction. For this, we derive a differentiable loss function that captures the fundamental connection between structure and dynamics developed in our theory. We then use self-supervised learning on synthetic data to train a GNN. Once pre-trained, the GNN can be used for common graph prediction tasks like link prediction and node classification from graph structure as its only input. The GNN thus encodes behavioral information about competitive and cooperative dynamics that occur in cellular or human social networks. This physics-aware GNN has full interpretability built in from the ground up. This pre-trained model archives state of the art performance on link prediction in protein-protein interaction networks. Given its unique structure, our model supports entirely new prediction tasks such as predicting the dominant payoff structure faced by agents, provides interpretable labels labels for edges and entire graphs, and distance from equilibria (dynamic balance). Since the model is pretrained on synthetic data it can be productively used in settings with few known data points---both small networks or settings with few graphs---and with very low inference latency given its small size. Our physics-informed model acts as a rigorous motif detector while rejecting the statistical noise of standard link predictor models. Our findings suggest that coevolutionary systems are information compressors. Agents, through their local interactions and rewiring, ``wash out'' the fine-grained details of the incentive structure, collapsing a continuous space of incentives into a discrete set of stable topologies (Universality Classes). This suggests that the standard game theory assumption that small shifts in payoff parameters matters is wrong; instead, different payoff parameters collapse onto the same network structure and our GNN is performing ``thermodynamic denoising.''

nodes.title: Social Influence in Large Language Models (LLMs) Groups During Cognitive Tasks
nodes.authors: Borges, Henrique M.; Yeung, Wang Ngai*; Deshpande, Raj; Di Clemente, Riccardo; Petri, Giovanni
nodes.abstract:
With an increasing prevalence of LLMs in our society, it has been a growing interest in understanding the emergence of collective intelligence (CI) behavior [1]. Albeit CI has been studied widely across human and non-human animal groups (e.g., honeybees, birds), it is rather unclear as to whether the dynamics of collective decision-making can also be observed in LLMs.

We sought to examine how social interaction shapes the performance of the collective and the individual using previously approaches to studying CI behaviors in human groups [2].This was done by using a one-shot cognitive assessment: the Number Line Estimation (NLE) task, a widely used instrument for evaluating mathematical competence in children and adolescents [3]. We first carried out simulations on the ego-network associated with LLMs of varying model families and sizes exposed to synthetically generated neighbours’ answers from a Gaussian distribution. We then tested numerous combinations of the parameters, neighbours’ variance σ and neighbours’ mean μ. Following this, we derived a functional form that captures how different configurations of neighbourhood’s performance constraint the ego’s performance.

We found that agents integrate social information with a tendency to either maintain complete conviction in their initial answer or fully adherence to the social consensus, with the switch probability being determined by a predictable combination of size of neighbours, neighbours’ variance and errors. This phenomenon can be understood as a critical transition point from stubbornness to possible adoption. Intuitively, agents appear more willing to abandon their initial estimates when surrounded by many neighbors who largely agree with one another, treating such convergence as a signal of reliable collective information.

In addition, we repeated the experiment but allowed multiple LLM agents interacting together instead of providing synthetically generated answers, and discovered a significant compensatory effects of social influence. For larger models, social learning via information exchange harms initially accurate agents, but it reduces the errors of the originally inaccurate ones, with a transition point that is invariant to network topology. This finding is contrastive to our expectation that larger-sized models can more resistant against external opinions. 

All in all, our findings capture the mechanisms and conditions that characterize how networked LLM agents may learn to become better at cognitive tasks.
