Specifically, this work contributes to the large body of scholarly work on the potential of social learning between LLMs. Our analytic characterization will subsequently enable us to formulate predictions of macroscopic performance in larger networked populations during multi-shot cognitive tasks, encompassing both homogeneous (identically parameterized agents) and heterogeneous ensembles (agents instantiated from distinct model classes).

[1] Schoenegger, P. et al., Wisdom of the silicon crowd: LLM ensemble prediction capabilities rival human crowd accuracy. Sci. Adv. 10, eadp1528 (2024). https://doi.org/10.1126/sciadv.adp1528

[2] Wall, J. L. et al. (2016). Children can accurately monitor and control their number-line estimation performance. Developmental
Psychology, 52(10), 1493–1502. https://doi.org/10.1037/dev0000180

[3] Woolley, A. W., Chabris, C. F., Pentland, A., Hashmi, N., & Malone, T. W. (2010). Evidence for a collective intelligence factor in the performance of human groups. Science, 330(6004), 686 688. https://doi.org/10.1126/science.1193147

nodes.title: Testing autonomous LLM agents as surrogates for humans in networked social dilemmas
nodes.authors: Ferraz de Arruda, Henrique*; Gracia-Lázaro, Carlos; Aleta, Alberto; Moreno, Yamir
nodes.abstract:
Large language models (LLMs) hold great promise as artificial agents for simulating social and economic systems, and their use in generative agent-based models (GABMs) has rapidly expanded. Yet, it remains unclear what collective behavior emerges when multiple LLMs interact fully autonomously, and, crucially, whether such interactions can reproduce human behavior in controlled networked experiments. This question is particularly relevant in settings where network structure shapes strategic outcomes, and in light of recent concerns regarding LLM evaluation and reliability. Here, we address this gap using a canonical benchmark from network science: the iterated Prisoner's Dilemma played on networks. We construct a GABM that closely replicates a well-known laboratory experiment originally conducted with human participants, in which network structure was shown not to promote cooperation. Using the empirical results of this experiment as a reference, we systematically test whether populations of LLM-based agents reproduce human collective behavior when placed on the same networks and subjected to the same game rules and instructions. We simulate nine different LLMs interacting on regular and heterogeneous networks, explore the role of prompt design, and consider mixed populations combining LLM agents with random decision-makers to account for experimentally observed behavioral noise. Our main findings can be summarized as follows:
    - We successfully reproduce the original human experiment at the collective level using fully autonomous interacting LLM agents, under carefully controlled modeling and prompting assumptions.
    - In agreement with the human data, we confirm the absence of network reciprocity: network structure does not promote cooperation in this setting.
    - Once validated, the generative framework opens the door to systematic exploration of social dilemmas across payoff matrices and parameter regimes that would be impractical or impossible to study with human subjects alone.
Taken together, our results show that GABMs can approximate collective human behavior in networked social dilemmas, while clarifying the conditions under which LLM agents can -and cannot- serve as reliable surrogates for humans in network experiments, thus enabling systematic exploration of fully autonomous and interacting generative agents.

nodes.title: A Review of Coarse-Graining Methods: Insights from Physics, Network Science, and Computer Science
nodes.authors: Fernández Da Silva, Marco*
nodes.abstract:
Coarse-graining methods have become essential in the analysis of large and complex networks, offering ways to reduce dimensionality while preserving key structural and dynamical properties. These methods span across multiple disciplines, each with distinct goals and methodologies. In Physics, coarse-graining often focuses on preserving eigenvalues and is typically grounded in robust theoretical frameworks [1, 2]. In contrast, Computer Science prioritizes scalability, developing methods that handle larger graphs efficiently for storage and exploration. Recently, there has also been significant interest in adapting these methods to Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) to reduce training costs while maintaining performance. Meanwhile, Network Science emphasizes preserving topological features such as degree distributions, motifs, and community structures, frequently linking these properties to dynamical processes like diffusion or synchronization.

Despite the extensive body of work in this area, selecting the most suitable coarse-graining method for a given problem remains challenging. Many methods lack theoretical guarantees, raising concerns about their applicability to systems beyond their original domains. To address these gaps, a group of young researchers joined in 2023 and 2025 in the AccelNet-Multinet collabathons held in Zaragoza and Boston to tackle this problem. This collaboration resulted in a comprehensive review of coarse-graining methods and the creation of a Python package—CoarseNet—implementing over 25 algorithms.

In this work, we present the results of our review, offering a detailed comparison of coarse-graining algorithms based on structural, topological, and dynamical properties. Specifically, we evaluate the ability of these methods to preserve features such as modularity, degree distribution, and spectral properties across diverse networks. We also examine their impact on dynamical processes, including diffusion models, random walks, and epidemic spreading. Our findings reveal trade-offs between computational efficiency and feature preservation, with certain methods excelling in capturing global structural patterns while others perform better in retaining local properties.

nodes.title: Link Prediction with Untrained Message Passing Layers
nodes.authors: Qarkaxhija, Lisi*; Wegner, Anatol; Scholtes, Ingo
nodes.abstract: In this work, we explore the use of untrained message passing layers in graph neural networks for link prediction. The untrained message passing layers we consider are derived from widely used graph neural network architectures by removing trainable parameters and nonlinearities in their respective message passing layers. Experimentally, we find that untrained message passing layers can lead to competitive and even superior link prediction performance compared to fully trained message passing layers while being more efficient, especially in the presence of high-dimensional features. We also provide a theoretical analysis of untrained message passing layers in the context of link prediction and show that the inner product of features produced by untrained message passing layers relate to common neighbour and path-based topological measures which are widely used for link prediction. As such, untrained message passing layers offer a more efficient alternative to trained message passing layers in link prediction tasks, with clearer theoretical links to classical path-based heuristics.

nodes.title: Quantum Network-Based Prediction of Cancer Driver Genes
nodes.authors: Coutinho, Bruno*
nodes.abstract: Identification of cancer driver genes is fundamental for the development of targeted therapeutic interventions. The integration of mutational profiles with protein-protein interaction (PPI) networks offers a promising avenue for their detection, but scaling to large network datasets is computationally demanding. Quantum computing offers compact representations and potential complexity reductions. Motivated by the classical method of Gumpinger *et al.*, in this work we introduce a supervised quantum framework that combines mutation scores with network topology via a novel state preparation scheme, *Quantum Multi-order Moment Embedding* (QMME). QMME encodes low-order statistical moments over the mutation scores of a node’s immediate and second-order neighbors, and encodes this information into quantum states. These are used as inputs to a kernel-based quantum binary classifier that discriminates known driver genes from others. Simulations on an empirical PPI network demonstrate competitive performance, with a 12.6% recall gain over a classical baseline. The pipeline performs explicit quantum state preparation and requires no classical training, enabling an efficient, nearly end-to-end quantum workflow. A brief complexity analysis suggests the approach could achieve a quantum speedup in network-based cancer gene prediction. This work underscores the potential of supervised quantum graph learning frameworks to advance biological discovery.

nodes.title: Scalable Construction of Spectrally Near-Optimal Networks via Reinforcement Learning
nodes.authors: Hwang, Junsung*; Liu, Ji
nodes.abstract:
The algebraic connectivity (lambda_2) of a simple graph, G(V,E) with N = |V| nodes and M = |E| edges, fundamentally governs the robustness of networked systems, controlling synchronization speeds in power grids, consensus convergence in distributed computing, and information diffusion rates [1]. While maximizing lambda_2 is NP-hard [2], greedy spectral heuristics, such as maximizing Effective Resistance (ER) or Fiedler Vector (FV) separation, can produce high-quality topologies. However, these methods require expensive eigendecompositions or matrix inversions (O(N^3)), resulting in a prohibitive total complexity of O(M  N^3) or O(N^5) for dense graphs. Conversely, scalable O(N^2) constructive models like the Watts-Strogatz Small World (SW) often fail to avoid spectral bottlenecks, yielding suboptimal robustness.


We introduce a Two-Phase Hybrid Framework that delivers high algebraic connectivity with O(N^2) inference, bridging the gap between spectral quality and computational scalability. 

Phase I (Deterministic Skeleton): We propose a deterministic strategy that approximates high spectral quality via local geometric proxies. By prioritizing edges with maximal chordal distance and disjoint neighborhoods, we facilitate isotropic expansion, promoting robust, monotonic connectivity scaling across arbitrary edge densities.

Phase II (RL Refinement): To surpass deterministic limits, we employ a Graph Convolutional Network (GCN) agent trained via Reinforcement Learning to add residual edges. Crucially, we introduce a Top-K Guided Policy: the deterministic heuristic from Phase I prunes the search space to the top K candidates, allowing the RL agent to focus on learning subtle topological optimizations within a high-quality subspace. This imparts guided flexibility to the pipeline without sacrificing scalability.

We benchmark our method across validation scales N in {32,64,128,256}, where greedy spectral baselines remain tractable for direct comparison, since ER and FV heuristics recompute a Laplacian eigensolver at each greedy edge-addition step. Results show that our O(N^2) hybrid pipeline achieves 95--97\% of the greedy ER baseline's lambda_2 while consistently outperforming 
Small-World graphs by 10--20%. We further evaluate scalability up to N = 2,048, observing quadratic runtime scaling and sustained lambda_2 gains of approximately 15% over Small-World baselines.

nodes.title: Semi-supervised Classification in an Undirected Multilayer Networks with Groupwise Subspace Structure
nodes.authors: Toleugazin, Abdulla*
nodes.abstract:
We study inference in multilayer undirected networks where layers share common latent structure only
within unknown groups. Each layer connects the same set of nodes to possibly different sets of target
nodes and follows a generalized random dot product graph model. While layers within the same group
share a common left latent subspace, all other structural components may vary, and individual layers can
be too sparse or heterogeneous for reliable standalone analysis.
To address these challenges, we propose a two-stage semi-supervised classification framework. In the
first stage, the method automatically identifies a subset of informative layers and clusters them using
spectral methods applied to projection-based embeddings. In the second stage, group-level subspace
estimates are constructed by aggregating information across clustered layers and are subsequently used
to classify the remaining low-signal layers through projection-based similarity scores.
We establish non-asymptotic theoretical guarantees showing that, under mild balance and sparsity
conditions, the proposed procedure achieves perfect layer classification with high probability and consis-
tent recovery of shared latent subspaces. Simulation results demonstrate strong empirical performance
and robustness to heterogeneous layer sizes and noise levels.
The proposed semi-supervised approach provides a computationally efficient and statistically reliable
alternative to fully unsupervised clustering methods and enables principled analysis of multilayer networks
in regimes where many layers are individually uninformative.

nodes.title: Epidemic Source Detection as a New Benchmark Task for Evaluating GNNs
nodes.authors: Sterchi, Martin*; Brack, Nathan; Hilfiker, Lorenz
nodes.abstract:
Epidemic source detection on networks has been an active research area since its introduction in 2010 [1]. The task is
to infer the true source given the network and a snapshot of the epidemic states of the nodes. The field has experienced renewed interest with the emergence of Graph Neural Networks (GNNs), leading to promising results but also raising questions regarding benchmarking practices and experimental reproducibility. For instance, several recent studies do not compare against well-established source detection methods such as the Soft Margin Estimator (SME) [2], making it difficult to assess the relative advantage of GNN-based approaches.

Motivated by these observations, we review existing GNN-based solutions to source detection in a new paper [3] and present a clear and reproducible experimental evaluation of GNNs and relevant benchmarks. Building on our prior work on traditional source detection methods [4], we initially set out to challenge the effectiveness of GNNs for source detection. However, our results indicate that, with appropriate architectural choices, GNN-based models outperform traditional benchmarks, including SME and our Monte Carlo mean-field (MCMF) approach [4], across a range of network topologies [3].

Based on these findings, our primary objective is to propose epidemic source detection as a benchmark task for evaluating GNN architectures. As a node-selection problem, source detection differs from both node classification and standard graph classification. In particular, single-source detection can be formulated as a multi-class prediction problem, with model outputs representing a probability distribution over nodes. A notable feature of this task is that training and test data can be generated synthetically, enabling controlled and scalable experimental setups. This flexibility opens the door to new research questions, such as how model performance scales with training data size.

Establishing epidemic source detection as a benchmark task could benefit everyone: GNN researchers would gain an additional and practically relevant evaluation task, while source detection research could benefit from increased methodological scrutiny and unified benchmarks enabling more structured progress. As an illustration of real-world relevance, we report results on identifying the source country of the 2009 H1N1 pandemic using the global airline network and simulated epidemic data from a simple susceptible–infectious (SI) model (Figure 1).

nodes.title: Beyond Demographics: Creating Interpretable Lifestyle Embeddings Across US Cities
nodes.authors: Gibbs, Hamish*; Moro, Esteban
nodes.abstract:
The dynamics of urban life arise from complex interactions between individuals and the built environment, which shape networks of social interaction, economic production, consumption, and civic participation. Traditionally, urban scientists have relied on the residential demographics of neighborhoods to study complex phenomena such as the structure of social networks, inequalities in access to amenities, or individuals' economic opportunities in cities. Behavioral data, including GPS traces from mobile phones, offer insights into "what people do away from home", providing novel perspectives on urban dynamics not available from traditional demographic data. 

Despite opportunities to improve urban decision-making, behavioral data can be challenging to use, limited by restrictions on data sharing, and can risk individual privacy. Rather than using behavioral data directly, there is increasing interest in providing access to low-dimensional representations of behavioral data in the form of embeddings, which capture the structure of behavioral datasets while increasing ease of use and reducing risks to individual privacy. Though promising, many embedding methods suffer from a lack of explainability, a crucial requirement to support decision-making using behavioral data. 

In this study, we present a set of interpretable behavioral features derived from GPS mobility data which reveal latent regularities in mobility behavior across neighborhoods and over time. Using non-negative matrix factorization (NMF) applied to a mobility dataset of ~30B visits across 20 US cities between 2019 and 2022, we identify 12 latent behavioral features capturing regularities in urban mobility behavior such as Weekend Shopping, Morning Commute, or Campus Life. Importantly, we show that these behavioral features offer stable, interpretable behavioral dimensions which can be incorporated in models of urban phenomena to complement existing demographic data. Finally, we show that adding lifestyle embeddings to demographic baselines improves prediction for selected outcomes in health, employment, civic engagement, and access to opportunity.

nodes.title: Generative Modelling of Traffic Distributions in Sparse Communication Networks
nodes.authors: Ahuja, Akanksha *; N. Herzberg, Sam; Rafel, Albert; Wright, Paul ; Lord, Andrew; J. Savory, Seb
nodes.abstract:
Traffic on sparse infrastructure networks is governed by a small number of long-tailed flows that concentrate load on a few links, increasing both cost and failure risk. National optical backbones, consisting of hundreds of low-degree nodes, are a key example. However, their traffic matrices are extremely sparse, proprietary, and usually available at most as a single static snapshot. In this data-limited setting, researchers rely on simple distributional assumptions that underestimate extreme flows and distort estimates of capacity, congestion, and robustness. To address this, we show that (i) traffic on sparse backbones is multimodal, (ii) modelling the bulk and the tail separately is both necessary and sufficient, and (iii) additional node features (geolocation, population, data-centre locations) only marginally improve performance.

Can a generative model produce realistic synthetic traffic samples in this data-scarce regime? In this work, we develop a hybrid Gaussian Mixture–Extreme Value Theory (GMM–EVT) model [1, 2]. The bulk distribution of origin–destination flows is learned using a finite mixture of Gaussians, while extreme upper-tail behaviour is modelled via peaks-over-threshold Generalised Pareto distributions. The model is evaluated on a single traffic snapshot from the 106-node British Telecommunications (BT) backbone, with traffic defined as the sum of inflows and outflows across non-zero, asymmetric source–destination (SD) pairs. We assess model performance by comparing the observed and synthetic traffic distributions using RMSE (root mean squared error). Five components minimise error and are selected as optimal.

How does GMM-EVT compare to existing traffic models? GMM–EVT achieves the lowest error on UK backbone traffic (RMSE = 0.02), even outperforming the lognormal distribution (0.03), the standard assumption and state-of-the-art model for Internet traffic [3]. Domain-specific heuristics perform substantially worse as gravity models (0.31), data-centre and peering heuristics (0.28), and uniform distributions (0.25) incur errors an order of magnitude higher [4, 5]. We further investigate whether contextual features improve distributional fidelity and evaluate supervised baselines that are widely adopted by operators (linear regression, random forests, neural networks, gradient boosting [6]) using geolocation, population, data-centre and Internet Exchange Point features, and node embeddings. While these methods outperform heuristics (0.16), they still fail to match GMM–EVT’s performance. Finally, we demonstrate that the unified GMM–EVT formulation is necessary, as it reduces error relative to GMM-only (0.03) or EVT-only (0.13) variants.


Does the 5-component GMM-EVT structure generalise to other sparse backbones? We validate on a 105-node Telefonica backbone with substantially different traffic characteristics [7]. The GMM-EVT model generalises to this network with operator-specific parameters and achieves an equally low error (0.02). Overall, this approach (i) challenges single-family distributional assumptions that underestimate traffic extremes and consequently infrastructure costs, (ii) indicates a recurrent stratification of traffic into distinct statistical regimes across operators, and (iii) enables privacy-preserving sharing by allowing operators to release model parameters without exposing source–destination flows.


[1] C. M. Bishop, “Pattern Recognition and Machine Learning,” Springer, (2006).
[2] S. J. Savory, “Extreme values in optical fiber communication systems,” OFC, (2020).
[3] M. Alasmar et al., “Internet traffic volumes are not Gaussian—They are log-normal,” IEEE/ACM Trans. Netw., (2021).
[4] A. Dwivedi et al., “Traffic model for USA long-distance optical network,” OFC, (2000).
[5] S. K. Patri et al., “Planning optical networks for unexpected traffic growth,” ECOC, (2020).
[6] D. Szostak et al., “ML classification and regression approaches for optical network traffic prediction,” Electronics, (2021).
[7] J. M. R. Moscoso et al., “TEFNET24: reference packet optical network topology for edge to core transport,” JOCN, (2024).

nodes.title: Network theory and machine learning perspectives in the quest of new materials
nodes.authors: Caldarelli, Guido*
nodes.abstract:
Understanding and predicting the emergence of novel materials is a fundamental challenge in condensed matter physics, materials science, and technology. With the rapid growth of materials databases in both size and reliability, the challenge shifts from data collection to efficient exploration of this vast and complex space. A key
strategy lies in the smart use of descriptors at multiple scales, ranging from atomic arrangements to macroscopic properties, to represent materials in high-dimensional abstract spaces. Network theory provides a powerful framework to structure and analyze these relationships, capturing hidden patterns and guiding discovery. Ma-
chine learning complements this approach by enabling predictive modeling, dimensionality reduction, and the identification of promising material candidates. By integrating network-based methods with machine learning (ML) techniques, researchers can construct, analyze, and efficiently navigate the material space, uncovering novel materials with tailored properties. This review explores the synergy between network theory and ML, highlighting their role in accelerating materials discovery through a systematic and interpretable approach.

nodes.title: Nanoscale networks for information processing
nodes.authors: Cipollini, Davide*
nodes.abstract:
Most of today's activities rely on hardware and algorithms designed to process information. Artificial Intelligence has become so pervasive that it is now applied to even the simplest tasks. Yet this ubiquity brings with it serious concerns about its cost in terms of resources. At the same time chip manufacturing is proximate to reach its physical limits.
For these reasons, unconventional computing strategies that rely on a physics-based approach are receiving significant research attention. Among the most promising are those leveraging devices built from disordered networks of nano-materials [1]. Such systems compute directly at the matter level, with information processing emerging from the material’s own physical structure and dynamic behavior under electrical stimulation. 
Indeed, these computational substrates are complex networked systems at the nanoscale. While structural properties vary across distinct resolution scales, functional behavior emerges from the collective dynamics of many interacting elements, rather than from the sum of individual properties.
Despite rapid progress in the development of novel disordered functional nanomaterials and related devices [1,2,3,4,5], network science has so far played only a marginal role in their analysis and design, even though it offers substantial potential benefits.
In this talk, we show how the convergence of network science and nanotechnology enables new strategies for the analysis, modeling, and design of complex nanoscale systems within a network-centered theoretical framework [1,3,4].
Such an approach allows the realization of functions through structure, supporting novel physics-based concepts of information processing in unconventional computing architectures.

nodes.title: Trapped by Personalization: LLM-Assisted Recommendations and Their Hidden Impact on Human-AI Ecosystems
nodes.authors: Hosseinmardi, Homa*
nodes.abstract:
Over the years, recommender systems have evolved from basic tools for organizing content into sophisticated systems that now play a significant role in our daily lives, even subtly shaping our behavior.  By controlling what information we see, they can influence the version of the world we perceive---a dynamic that has raised concerns about their role in creating filter bubbles, radicalization, polarization, and social inequality. 
 With the rapid advancement of technologies such as large language models (LLMs), more powerful personalizations have become possible, where these effects may become even more pronounced. 
 Consequently, it is imperative to examine LLM-powered recommender systems within the broader context of human-AI ecosystems. 
 In this study, we audit an LLM-assisted YouTube recommender system to analyze how LLMs can enhance personalization while also assessing their potential to amplify societal polarization. In our setting, the initial candidate videos are drawn directly from YouTube's sidebar recommendations based on a real user's watch history. These candidates are then reranked using a zero-shot, in-context LLM strategy to further personalize the recommendations. First, we find that integrating LLMs into recommender systems can unintentionally increase exposure to conspiratorial and extremist material. Second, we demonstrate that prompt-level regularization, applied within an in-context learning setup, can reduce the likelihood of promoting extreme content and improve the diversity of recommended videos, with only modest losses in relevance to user history. Our findings highlight both the promise and the risks of integrating LLMs into ranking pipelines, underscoring the importance of explicit design choices---such as instruction-level regularization---that can balance personalization against societal responsibility.

nodes.title: Learning by Surprise: Surplexity for Mitigating Model Collapse in Generative AI
nodes.authors: Gambetta , Daniele *; Gezici, Gizem; Giannotti, Fosca ; Pedreschi, Dino ; Knott, Alistair ; Pappalardo, Luca
nodes.abstract:
Recent research has investigated the risks associated with a self-consuming loop, often referred to as autophagy process, where Large Generative Models (LGMs) are recursively fine-tuned on their own outputs. The process can lead to a phenomenon called model collapse, characterised by a significant loss of linguistic diversity. However, as noted by Schaeffer et al., there are many alternative definitions of collapse as well as many open questions. 

We focus on three issues: (i) Model collapse is typically detected through metrics defined on generated content, but we lack a method to characterise a collapsed model as a probability model; (ii) while it is known that fine-tuning a model on synthetic data causes collapse, we lack a principled understanding of which properties of training documents contribute to collapse; (iii) methods for mitigating model collapse assume knowledge about whether training documents are synthetic or human-authored: an assumption that is difficult to validate in real-world settings. 

Using three textual datasets, we consider different fine-tuning scenarios based on the composition of the training set, simulating the autophagy process over 10 steps. 
In response to issue (i), we propose a metric for a collapsed LGM based on the probability distributions it generates. 
Then we address issue (ii) by proposing a new definition of the class of documents responsible for collapse related to the model's surprise: documents that are not surprising lead to collapse, while documents that are surprising do not. 
Given a model and a document, we define surplexity as the extent to which the model is surprised by the document. We then use surplexity to address issue (iii), mitigating collapse by filtering training items by the level of surprise they elicit (see Figure a), finding that the strategy is as effective in mitigating model collapse while requiring no knowledge of whether documents are human-authored or AI-generated. We extend our characterisation based on next-token probabilities into a network representation (see Figure b), obtaining a network that captures the growing inequality in the probability distribution of a single prompt. This approach enables us to characterise model collapse in terms of network metrics and, additionally, provides a framework for comparing the phenomenon of autophagy feedback loops in generative AI with other ecosystems.

nodes.title: A Guide to Higher-Order Homophily
nodes.authors: Laber, Moritz*; Klein, Brennan
nodes.abstract:
Homophily—the tendency for similar agents to interact—is a foundational mechanism shaping network structure and
formation. Rooted in the social sciences [1], homophily has shaped network science broadly, motivating measures like
assortativity and modularity, informing stochastic block and latent-space models, and more recently influencing graph
machine learning. However, as network data increasingly record higher-order interactions—teams, group chats, collaborations,
and collective problem solving—our understanding of homophily beyond pairwise ties remains underdeveloped. In
higher-order representations (e.g., hypergraphs and simplicial complexes), dyadic homophily neither determines whether
groups are attribute-homogeneous nor captures changes in homophily with group size.
We organize this review around three questions. First, how should higher-order homophily be measured (Fig. 1(a))?
We survey approaches that generalize homophily and related metrics (e.g., modularity) from pairwise networks to the case
of hypergraphs and/or simplicial complexes (e.g., [2,3]), as well as novel metrics unique to the higher-order setting (e.g.,
[4,5]). We clarify what each measure captures, when different measures agree, and when they diverge. Second, how can
one generate higher-order networks with homophily (Fig. 1(b))? We review generative models of higher-order networks
that incorporate different notions of homophily, including higher-order stochastic block models, generalized configuration
models with node attributes, and growth mechanisms with attribute-dependent attachment. Third, how does higher-order
homophily affect dynamical processes (Fig. 1(c))? Focusing on spreading and social contagion (e.g., [6,7]), but including
other dynamics such as message passing [5], we synthesize evidence that higher-order mixing can shift thresholds and
change which interventions are effective. Taken together, we hope this guide provides a common vocabulary, a map of
existing methods, and practical recommendations for measuring and modeling homophily in higher-order networks.

[1] McPherson et al. Birds of a feather: Homophily in social networks Ann. Rev. Soc. 27:415-444 (2001)
[2] Sarker et al. Higher-order homophily on simplicial complexes PNAS 121 (12) e2315931121 (2024)
[3] Veldt et al. Combinatorial characterizations and impossibilities for higher-order homophily Sci. Adv. 9 eabq3200 (2023)
[4] Telyatnikov et al. Hypergraph neural networks through the lens of message passing TMLR (2025)
[5] Kumar et al. Perplexity-homophily index: Homophily through diversity in hypergraphs arXiv:2511.19170 (2025)
[6] Laber et al. Effects of higher-order interactions and homophily on information access inequality Comm. Phys. (2025)
[7] Rizi et al. Homophily within and across groups Nat. Comm. 16 11351 (2025)

nodes.title: Higher-order interactions enhance dynamic range in networks of excitable systems
nodes.authors: Restrepo, Juan*; Taylor, Dane; Kritpracha, Poom
nodes.abstract:
We study the effect of cooperative higher-order interactions on the response of a network of excitable systems to an
external stimulus. In various applications, such as in models of functional brain networks, the effectiveness of such a
network is measured with the dynamic range, the range of stimuli over which the response can be inferred. In models
of functional brain networks in which nodes are coupled with pairwise interactions, it has been found that the dynamic
range is maximized when the strength of the interactions is such that the network is at the cusp of a phase transition
between quiescence and self-sustained activity [1]. The brain has been hypothesized to operate in this so-called critical
regime, in which other measures of information processing are also optimized [2].
However, these previous analyses assume pairwise non-cooperative interactions, while cooperative or higher-order
interactions are prevalent in the brain. In order to explore the effect of higher-order interactions on the dynamic range
of networks of excitable systems, we extend the Kinouchi-Copelli model [1] to include higher-order (triadic) interactions,
where two nodes need to be simultaneously active in order to excite another via a triadic interaction. We conduct
numerical simulations on randomly generated networks that include both pairwise and triadic interactions with strengths
K2 and K3. We find that the dynamic range becomes the largest when the strength of higher order interactions K3 is
such that the network response is close to a subcritical pitchfork bifurcation. Moreover, even away from this bifurcation,
the dynamic range attains values that are higher than the maximum value attained with pairwise interactions alone.
We validate our numerical results by developing a mean-field theory, from which we obtain semi-analytical results
for the dynamic range. The results of this theory are shown in Fig. 1, which shows the dynamic range as a function of
K2 and K3. To explore the generality of our results, we validate them on another model where each node is excited
stochastically with a probability that depends on their input, which is a combination of pairwise and triadic interaction
terms. The results of this different model agree qualitatively with those of the Kinouchi-Copelli model. We also extend
our analyses for random networks to networks with structured higher-order interactions, and determine the factors that
increase the dynamic range.
Our results show that higher-order interactions in networks of excitable systems can enhance their information pro-
cessing capabilities, both by increasing the dynamic range and by enlarging the region of parameter space where large
values of the dynamic range can be found.

nodes.title: Information theory for hypergraph similarity
nodes.authors: Felippe, Helcio*; Kirkley, Alec; Battiston, Federico
nodes.abstract: Comparing networks is essential for a number of downstream tasks, from clustering to anomaly detection. Despite higher-order interactions being critical for understanding the dynamics of complex systems, traditional approaches for network comparison are limited to pairwise interactions only. Here we construct a general information theoretic framework for hypergraph similarity, capturing meaningful correspondence among higher-order interactions while correcting for spurious correlations. Our method operationalizes any notion of structural overlap among hypergraphs as a principled normalized mutual information measure, allowing us to derive a hierarchy of increasingly granular formulations of similarity among hypergraphs within and across orders of interactions, and at multiple scales. We validate these measures through extensive experiments on synthetic hypergraphs and apply the framework to reveal meaningful patterns in a variety of empirical higher-order networks. Our work provides foundational tools for the principled comparison of higher-order networks, shedding light on the structural organization of networked systems with non-dyadic interactions.

nodes.title: Irreducible higher-order interactions in complex systems
nodes.authors: Neri, Matteo*; Mediano, Pedro; Luppi, Andrea; Marinazzo, Daniele; Petri, Giovanni; Brovelli, Andrea
nodes.abstract:
Many phenomena of current scientific and societal interest, such as climate change, epidemics spreading, or the quest to understand human and artificial intelligence, share a common thread: rich collective behavior emerging from many interacting parts. Network science allows us to represent and study such interactions, offering tools from information theory and graph theory to comprehend their complexity. Recently, many contributions in this field have highlighted the importance of higher-order interactions (HOI), i.e. interactions involving more than two units at once. These interactions can be studied in informational terms, identifying groups of variables dominated by synergy, information present in the whole but not in the single variables, and redundancy, information shared across all variables. While many works have studied these properties and highlighted their relevance across a broad range of complex systems, it remains unclear how to disentangle the contributions of a group of variables from those of its subgroups. This issue is central for understanding group interactions and must be addressed to clarify how complex systems balance contributions across different orders. For example, when analyzing the interaction between four variables, one must distinguish information that is genuinely specific to the joint distribution of all four (order 4) from that arising trivially from lower-order interactions. 

To this end, we introduce a new framework based on the O-information (Ω), an information-theoretic measure that quantifies the dominance of synergy (negative values) or redundancy (positive values). Specifically, our framework enables the decomposition of the O-information into two distinct components: irreducible O-information and reducible O-information. These components respectively capture the portion of Ω that cannot be reduced, or can be reduced, to a sum of contributions from isolated subsystems. We first validate the metric on synthetic systems with controlled higher-order structure, including XOR logic-gate between four variables and canonical generative models (Gaussian, Ornstein–Uhlenbeck, and Ising networks), Fig.A. Across models, irreducible Ω increases selectively with genuine order-4 coupling, while remaining insensitive to changes in total synergy driven by lower-order interactions. This demonstrates that the metric captures the order of collective structure rather than its overall strength. Applying this framework to human brain activity (resting-state fMRI time series from the Human Connectome Project), we map the ratio between order-4 irreducible Ω and Ω (focusing on synergy-dominated multiplets with negative Ω). The resulting organization is highly structured: transmodal association networks, including default mode, frontoparietal control, and dorsal attention systems, show the strongest irreducible HOI. We next consider social phenomena, analysing U.S. Supreme Court voting data, where irreducible Ω is computed directly from the joint voting patterns of the justices across cases. Specifically, we compute irreducible Ω for all quadruplets of justices (n = 9).  To assess the role of political alignment in shaping HOI, we quantified left–right variance for each quadruplet as the variance of the justices’ left-right scores (ranging from −3.036, left, to +3.767, right, following previous literature). Lower ideological variance corresponds to higher irreducible Ω in voting patterns, an effect not observed for reducible Ω. We then apply the same measure to epidemic time series across U.S. states, computing irreducible Ω and reducible Ω for each group of four states using infection density time series. Decomposing Ω into irreducible Ω and reducible Ω allows us to distinguish different diseases. Overall, this work provides a principled information-theoretic framework to isolate order-specific interactions in complex systems, clarifying how collective behavior emerges across interaction orders in complex systems.

nodes.title: Pairwise representation of higher-order interactions
nodes.authors: Lee, Jongshin*; Motter, Adilson
nodes.abstract:
Comparing higher-order and pairwise network dynamics is often ambiguous due to their use of fundamentally different building blocks: hyperedges versus edges. These building blocks lack natural correspondence for allocating coupling strengths and matching interaction structures. To enable fair comparison between these two frameworks, we introduce a transformation using auxiliary variables to construct equivalent pairwise systems that reproduce higher-order dynamics exactly (Fig. 1A and 1B). 

Our approach can be interpreted as a converse of dimensionality reduction: while eliminating degrees of freedom in pairwise networks generates higher-order interactions as a byproduct [Thibeault et al., Nat Phys 2024], introducing suitable auxiliary variables can lower the order of the interactions. We prove that for systems with nonlinear coupling, the auxiliary variables must be linear combinations of state variables. Otherwise, infinite recursion occurs because partial derivatives couple variables across interactions. This establishes a sharp boundary: higher-order systems violating this condition cannot be reduced to pairwise form. For transformable systems, we present an optimal construction minimizing auxiliary variable count and prove that alternative strategies (multilevel composition, symmetry reduction) offer no advantage. To understand the network size dependence of representation cost, we analyze uniform random hypergraphs. For Kuramoto-type interactions, the required auxiliary variable count grows linearly with system size $n$ (Fig 1C). For product-type interactions, it exhibits a sparse regime scaling $\sim (nz/d)(2^d-1-d)$, revealing exponential growth with the interaction order $d$.

This framework enables principled comparison between higher-order and pairwise representations using identical metrics (node and edge count) and provides rigorous grounds for assessing when higher-order structure is genuinely irreducible versus reducible through dimensional expansion. Exact transformation also unlocks analytical tools developed for pairwise networks, including spectral methods and the master stability formalism, providing rigorous foundations for stability analysis of higher-order systems. Finally, our work raises an important question on whether observed phenomena attributed to higher-order structure reflect genuine higher-order effects or could emerge from pairwise dynamics.

nodes.title: When Can We Detect Higher-Order Contagion? A Likelihood-Based Approach
nodes.authors: Lalovic, Marko*; Kiss, Istvan; Georgiou, Nicos; Malizia, Federico
nodes.abstract:
Contagion dynamics on higher-order networks are increasingly recognized as essential for modelling group interactions in social and biological systems. However, distinguishing these higher-order mechanisms from classical pairwise interactions using observational data remains a significant challenge. We present a rigorous likelihood-based framework for birth-death processes (BDPs) to investigate if and when the disentanglement of pairwise and higher-order rates from a single observed trajectory is possible.

We formulate the inference problem for finite populations where extinction is inevitable by conditioning on non-extinction. This allows us to construct the Doob h-transformed Q-process and derive a consistent Quasi-Maximum Likelihood Estimator (QMLE) validated via martingale central limit theory. We focus on the realistic unlabelled scheme where only aggregate population counts are available. We treat the specific infection mechanism as missing data and derive a Conditional Expectation-Maximization (Q-EM) algorithm, adapting existing EM framework for BDPs to the survival-conditioned regime. Finally, we provide a boundary likelihood-ratio test to rigorously detect the presence of higher-order interactions accounting for non-standard asymptotics.

With fully observed data, we recover rates with high precision. When the data is unlabelled, the pairwise and higher-order rate functions can become locally collinear near the quasi-steady state. In this "hard regime", parameters become practically exchangeable, confining most distinguishing information primarily to the transient growth phase. We quantify this information loss via the asymptotic covariance matrix. Crucially, we demonstrate that despite this challenge, the derived QMLE converges to the true parameters, validating that our robust Q-EM algorithm successfully disentangles the composite dynamics and recovers the underlying pairwise and higher-order rates as the observation window increases.

nodes.title: A geometric model for the emergence of nestedness in higher-order networked systems.
nodes.authors: Nortier, Berné*
nodes.abstract:
Since its inception, a central objective of network science has been to characterise emergent phenomena. In turn, this requires an understanding of how collective behaviours arise from the microscopic organisation of interactions. When systems encode interactions involving more than two entities, completely novel local features arise. A notable example of such a truly higher-order feature is hyperedge overlap. Nestedness, the extent to which smaller hyperedges are completely contained within larger ones, is a specific instance of hyperedge overlap that has been observed in numerous real-world networks. It is also known to strongly influence collective dynamics across a range of higher-order systems[1]. Despite the importance of nestedness, existing generative models for higher-order networks do not allow the principled generation of higher-order overlap. Typically, it is inserted into the system in an ad hoc fashion[2] or emerges only indirectly but cannot be controlled[3].

Our work introduces the first generative model of higher-order interactions in which nestedness both emerges naturally and is controllable. By assuming a latent geometric structure, our model allows nestedness to be directly tuned via a well-defined geometric parameter beta. The model draws on previous work to generalise the well-known network geometry model[4,5] which is derived from maximum-entropy principles.

The use of a hidden-variable framework with a latent geometry has several advantages: the assumption of independence at the latent level allows the model to circumvent the issues of interdependence that have previously hindered analytical treatment higher-order overlap. Here we derive exact constraints imposed by geometry on hyperedge organisation and in the large network limit we show that overlap undergoes a smooth phase transition controlled by the system’s coupling to the underlying geometry: one regime in which nestedness remains finite and persists with system size, and another in which it vanishes asymptotically. Furthermore, our framework is highly general. While we focus here on unweighted hypergraphs, the model naturally extends to different notions of overlap for more general hypergraphs containing for example weights or double edges. Finally, our model provides a principled null model for empirical higher-order networks, providing the wider community with a tool to analyse systems with diverse overlap profiles.

nodes.title: Entropy-based models to randomise real-world hypergraphs
nodes.authors: Saracco, Fabio*; Petri, Giovanni; Lambiotte, Renaud; Squartini, Tiziano
nodes.abstract:
Many real-world systems are characterised by interactions involving more than two entities at a time [1, 2]. While hypergraphs provide a natural representation of such higher-order structures, the literature shows a limited presence of
principled and analytically tractable null models. Here we introduce the framework of Exponential Random Hypergraphs
(ERH), a direct extension of entropy-based random graph models to hypergraphs [3], formulated in terms of the incidence
matrix.
Within this framework, we derive a family of canonical and microcanonical null models that randomise higher-order
connectivity while preserving selected structural constraints. In particular, the Hypergraph Configuration Model (HCM)
preserves both node degrees and hyperedge sizes, generalising the configuration model to polyadic interactions [4]. The
resulting ensembles are analytically tractable, scalable to large systems, and enables exact expressions for expected
observables.
We characterise the behaviour of ERH ensembles across sparse and dense regimes, identifying filling, resolution, and percolation thresholds that control the emergence of empty, parallel, and mutually connected hyperedges.
Finally, we show how ERH models provide a natural benchmark for hypergraph-to-graph projections, see Fig. 1. By computing analytical expectations for projected observables, such as node overlap and assortativity, the HCM enables the detection of non-random higher-order organization in empirical data. Taken together, ERH models establish a unifying and practical null-model framework for higher-order network analysis.

References
[1] Federico Battiston et al., Physics Reports (2020).
[2] Renaud Lambiotte et al., Nature Physics (2019).
[3] Giulio Cimini et al., Nature Reviews Physics (2018).
[4] Fabio Saracco et al., Communications Physics (2025).

nodes.title: Erdős-Rényi Random Hypergraphs
nodes.authors: Sabhahit, Narayan*; Laber, Moritz; Hartle, Harrison ; Pieleanu, Luca; Schepers, Markus; Petri, Giovanni; Krioukov, Dmitri
nodes.abstract:
Modeling networks with higher-order interactions has recently received increased attention in network science. Higher-order interactions have been shown to induce non-trivial dynamical phenomena and are crucial for understanding the functioning of real-world complex systems [1]. Despite this surge of interest, the network science literature still lacks an in-depth study of the arguably simplest random hypergraph model: the Erdős-Rényi Random Hypergraphs (ERH) [2].

Here, we treat the ERH in complete generality and present it from a maximum entropy perspective [3]. Sampling from a canonical ERH model involves walking over all of the $\binom{n}{l}$ hyperedges of size $l \in [n] \equiv\{1,2\cdots, n\}$---$n$ is the number of nodes---and choosing to place hyperedges independently with probability $p_{n,l}$ $\forall l\in[n]$. The microcanonical ERH, on the other hand, is the uniform distribution over the set of all hypergraphs satisfying the sharp constraint that the number of hyperedges for every $l$ is $m_{n,l}$. Our main contributions include obtaining the asymptotic scaling of the entropy of the model, studying the ensemble equivalence between the microcanonical and canonical ERH, the degree distribution, small-worldness, percolation (the emergence of the giant connected component (GCC)), and component size distribution. We additionally show that the ERH in the sparse regime is locally hypertree-like with vanishing hyper-edge overlaps, and derive further key structural results. We provide extensive numerical evidence for our theoretical derivations. To this end, we generalize the fast Erdős-Rényi sampling algorithm [4] and the Newman-Ziff percolation algorithm [5] to the case of non-uniform hypergraphs. Finally, motivated by the use of ERH as a null model, we propose an ansatz for the sequences $\{p_{n,l}\}$ and $\{m_{n,l}\}$, inferred from empirical data, and use it to compare trends observed in real-world hypergraphs with those produced by the ERH.

[1] Battiston, F., et al. (2020). Networks beyond pairwise interactions: Structure and dynamics. Physics Reports, 874, 1–92.

[2] Schmidt-Pruzan, J., et al. (1985). Component structure in the evolution of random hypergraphs. Combinatorica, 5(1), 81–94.

[3] Jaynes, E. T. (1957). Information theory and statistical mechanics. Physical review, 106(4), 620.

[4] Batagelj, V. and Brandes, U. (2005) Efficient generation of large random networks., Phys. Rev. E, 71, 036113.

[5] Newman M. and Ziff R. M. (2000) Efficient Monte Carlo Algorithm and High-Precision Results for Percolation. Phys. Rev. Lett. 85, 4104.

nodes.title: Interplay of size and degree heterogeneities in the simplicial Ising model on hypergraphs
nodes.authors: Son, Gangmin*; Battiston, Federico; Lee, Deok-Sun; Goh, Kwang-Il
nodes.abstract:
Higher-order interaction networks~[1] in the real world are often heterogeneous in various aspects; yet, understanding how such heterogeneities shape collective phenomena remains limited. Here, we study the simplicial Ising model (SIM)~[2,3,4] on hypergraphs with heterogeneous distributions of hyperedge size and node degree. The SIM is a prototypical equilibrium model that describes beyond-pairwise nonlinear ordering via the so-called simplicial rule~[5], defined by the Hamiltonian: $\mathcal{H} = -\sum_{e\in\mathcal{E}} J_{|e|} \delta_{\{S_i|i \in e\}},$ where the Ising spins $S_i\in\{\pm1\}$ within the same hyperedge $e$ interact via the size-dependent ferromagnetic coupling $J_{|e|}\geq0$, and $\delta_{\{S_i\}}$ is the higher-order Kronecker delta, equal to 1 if all variables in the set are identical and 0 otherwise (Fig.~1a). Previous works have shown that the SIM~[3,4] supports a rich variety of phase transitions. Here, we systematically extend these studies to general hypergraphs with arbitrary size and degree distributions by developing a cavity-method framework, focusing on the roles of size and degree heterogeneities as well as inter-size degree correlation (ISDC).
The main results are as follows:
(i) In regular uniform hypergraphs, transitions shift from continuous to discontinuous as either hyperedge size or node degree increases (Fig.~1b), confirming and extending the results of Ref.~[3].
(ii) When pairs and groups coexist, continuous--discontinuous double transitions can occur as temperature decreases (Fig.~1c); the continuous transition at higher temperature is primarily driven by pairs, while the discontinuous one is driven by groups (Fig.~1d).
(iii) In contrast, with the coexistence of hubs and non-hubs, the transition nature can shift from discontinuous to continuous and back (Fig.~1e); notably, this suggests that the introduction of hubs can promote continuous transitions, distinct from the effect of large groups (Fig.~1c).
(iv) Under power-law distributions, this contrast intensifies: size heterogeneity sharpens the transition (Fig.~1f), while degree heterogeneity softens it (Fig.~1g). In addition, degree heterogeneity alone can induce double transitions (Fig.~1g, $\gamma_k=4.5$).
(v) Finally, with negative ISDCs, where group-hubs are unlikely to be pair-hubs, group-driven transitions are delayed, broadening the intermediate partially-ordered phase (Fig.~1h).
Our results illustrate the intricacies of the interplay between size and degree heterogeneities on collective properties of higher-order interactions.

[1] F. Battiston \textit{et al.}, Nat. Phys. \textbf{17}, 1093 (2021).
[2] T. Robiglio \textit{et al.}, Phys. Rev. Lett. \textbf{134}, 137401 (2025).
[3] G. Son \textit{et al.}, arXiv:2411.19080 (2024).
[4] T. Robiglio \textit{et al.}, Phys. Rev. E \textbf{112}, L022301 (2025).
[5] I. Iacopini \textit{et al.}, Nat. Commun. \textbf{10}, 2485 (2019).

nodes.title: Reducibility of higher-order to pairwise interactions: Social impact models on hypergraphs
nodes.authors: Llabrés, Jaume*; Toral, Raúl; San Miguel, Maxi; Vázquez, Federico
nodes.abstract: We show that general social impact models with higher-order interactions on hypergraphs can be exactly reduced to a pairwise interactions model on a weighted projected network. This reduction is made by a mapping that preserves the microscopic flipping probabilities. For hypergraph-voter models, we compute the weights of the projected network both analytically and numerically across several hypergraph ensembles, and we characterize their ordering dynamics through simulations of both higher-order and reduced dynamics. For a linear social impact function, the weights of the projected network are time independent, allowing us to develop a Pair Approximation that predicts macroscopic observables independent of those weights. The macroscopic dynamics is thus equivalent to that of the classical voter model on the unweighted projected network. For a nonlinear social impact function, the weights of the projected network depend on the instantaneous system configuration. Nevertheless, the classical nonlinear voter model on the unweighted projected network still reproduces the main macroscopic trends for enough connected hypergraphs.

nodes.title: Stochastic block model reveals multi-order block structure of higher-order networks
nodes.authors: Nakajima, Kazuki*; Sasaki, Yuya; Uno, Takeaki; Aida, Masaki
nodes.abstract:
Higher-order networks, naturally described as hypergraphs, are essential for modeling real-world systems involving interactions among three or more entities. 
Stochastic block models are principled frameworks for characterizing their mesoscale organization, yet these models for hypergraphs face a trade-off between expressive power and computational complexity. 
A recent simplification, Hy-MMSBM model [1], mitigates this complexity by assuming a single affinity pattern governs any interaction order. 
The assumption of such universal affinity patterns, however, may obscure higher-order structural details. 
Here we propose a multi-order stochastic block model, HyperMOSBM, that relaxes this assumption by introducing a multi-order block structure, in which different affinity patterns govern distinct subsets of interaction orders.

nodes.title: Creating multilayer networks with controlled overlap and degree distributions
nodes.authors: Fosch, Ariadna*; Traversa, Pietro; Aleta, Alberto; Moreno, Yamir
nodes.abstract:
Prior research has found that non-negligible edge-overlap between the layers of a multiplex can impact the propagation of dynamical processes [1,2]. Yet, the generation of overlapping multiplexes from empirical data is still an open problem. Available methods often involve random edge rewiring [2], without control over the degree distribution, or ensemble generation [1], constraining the overlap only on average. However, non of these strategies are applicable when both layers are required to reproduce degree distributions inferred from empirical data.

To this end, we propose a method that, starting from two known degree distributions ($P(k_1)$, $P(k_2)$) and a target level of overlap $\alpha$, generates a biplex satisfying simultaneously all constraints. This is achieved through the combination of a classical configurational model, for layer generation, and a simulated annealing routine that performs random degree-preserving edge-swaps, until $\alpha$ is reached. In contrast with prior approaches, our method ensures that the original degree-sequence of each layer is preserved, making it highly suitable for the creation of realistic multilayer network models.

We demonstrated the performance of our algorithm by generating biplexes with different degree distributions in both layers, exploring how their heterogeneity conditions the maximum overlap achievable ($\alpha_{\text{max}}$). We further support this analytically, showing its dependence on each layer's degree distribution and inter-layer degree correlations. 

Finally, as a validation, we applied our algorithm to generate overlapping networks for epidemic modeling. Based on empirically collected mobility data reflecting in-person and non-physical interactions [3], we built multiple biplexes with different levels of overlap and explored the impact of $\alpha$ on different dynamical processes, like percolation and Behaviour and Disease models (BaD).

nodes.title: Low-Dimensional Phase Diagram of Higher-Order Networked Systems
nodes.authors: Qin, Jia-Jie*; Yan, Gang
nodes.abstract: Tipping points in nonlinear cooperative systems are fundamental to understanding — and potentially controlling — critical transitions, such as the shift from survival to extinction. However, analytically estimating these tipping points remains a major challenge due to the high dimensionality, complex interaction topologies, and diverse dynamical rules of real-world networks, particularly those with higher-order interactions. In this work, we introduce a general dimension reduction framework that captures arbitrary higher-order interactions by projecting their influence onto a low-dimensional state representation. This method enables an intuitive yet rigorous characterization of phase transitions and yields accurate predictions of tipping points in complex, high-dimensional networked systems.

nodes.title: Modeling Homophilic Hypergraph Growth Using Edge Copying
nodes.authors: Ross, Violet*; Chodrow, Phil
nodes.abstract:
In many real-world networked systems, growth depends on both multi-way relationships and homophily (the tendency of individuals to connect with others similar to them). Hypergraphs, and the growth models associated with them, allow for explicit representation of multi-way connections in the evolution of a complex system.

We propose a hyperedge-copying growth model for hypergraphs with node labels that parametrizes the level of homophily, extending the hyperedge-copy model for unlabeled networks presented in [1]. The model proceeds by iteratively sampling an edge from the hypergraph, selecting a node within the edge, and making a noisy copy of the sampled edge with some amount of preference (set by the parameters) for nodes with the same label as the selected node. We analyze the degree and edge size distributions of hypergraphs grown using our model, perform dynamical analysis on the mean number of nodes of each label within an edge, and derive and implement a Stochastic Expectation Maximization (SEM) algorithm to infer the model's parameters. We perform experiments on synthetic data to test the ability of the algorithm to recover true parameters and, finally, apply the SEM algorithm to the hypergraph of United States Senate bills.

The SEM algorithm performs well on synthetic data, estimating each of the parameters of a 2000-edge hypergraph within 7\% of their true value when the algorithm is run for 2000 iterations. On the Senate data, SEM-estimated parameter value describes patterns in homophily according to political party, in the level of retention of sponsors between bills, and in the presence of brand new sponsors on bills in the senate. Our approach accounts for the influence of two key mechanisms (multi-way relationships and homophily) in the generation of new edges. In doing so, the model allows for more accurate and explainable modeling, prediction, and inference of network growth and homophily.

[1] He, Xie, Philip S. Chodrow, and Peter J. Mucha. "Edge correlations and link prediction in growing hypergraphs." Physical Review E 112.2 (2025): 024305.

nodes.title: Nested hyperedges promote the onset of collective transitions but suppress explosive behavior
nodes.authors: Malizia, Federico*; Guzmán, Andrés; Battiston, Federico; Kiss, Istvan Zoltan
nodes.abstract:
Higher-order networks often exhibit striking nonlinear behaviors, such as explosive transitions and bistability, yet how these effects arise depends strongly on the microscopic organization of interactions. Here, we study an SIS process in which pairwise and three-body interactions coexist, focusing on inter-order hyperedge overlap, which is defined as the fraction of links that are embedded in three-node groups. This structural measure captures how tightly the two contagion channels are nested within each other.

Using a mean-field model that explicitly tracks node, pair, and group states, we derive analytical conditions for the epidemic threshold and for the onset of backward bifurcation, corresponding to the emergence of bistability. We show that increasing inter-order overlap smooths the transition: it lowers the epidemic threshold while suppressing explosive behavior. Simulations on random regular hypergraphs confirm these results and reveal that overlap suppresses discontinuous transitions by reducing the structural independence between pairwise and group transmission pathways. Additionally, we provide a microscopic, mechanistic explanation for this effect.

Furthermore, we show that these findings remain consistent for higher-order Kuramoto dynamics. Overall, our results reveal a general topological mechanism through which higher-order structure controls the emergence of nonlinear collective behavior.

nodes.title: Random walk on Simplicial Complexes
nodes.authors: Febbe, Diego*
nodes.abstract:
In the last few decades, the paradigm of Complex Networks (CNs) has proven essential in understanding a wide range
of natural and technological problems by modeling the interactions among different agents, leading to the emergence of
new, complex phenomena.
A further step in understanding complex systems composed of multiple entities can be achieved by modeling structures
that encapsulate the higher-order interactions present in the system, and in this context, Simplicial Complexes (SCs) can
play a crucial role as their regular structure allows the achievement of various types of results.
In this work, we present a natural extension of the Random Walk (RW) phenomenon, which is well known and studied
in Complex Networks, to higher-order structures such as an SC. A natural application of this approach can
be found in studying the propagation of ideas, originating from interactions within a clique of people and spreading to
individual components, and vice versa.
This is done by introducing a novel operator describing a RW process that enables transitions across the various
dimensions of an SC. Its stationary solution characterizes a natural ranking of the structures, allowing for a comparison
of their importance across mixed dimensions.In reverse, such an operator can also induce an adjacency meaning, useful
for SC generators characterized by a preferential attachment mechanism.
When restricting the starting and ending states solely to the 0th order structures (the nodes), we can compare the
results with those emerging on standard undirected networks, identifying differences in global measures for structure
explorability such as the First Passage Time (FPT) and Optimal Search Strategy (OSS). This leads to a description of
diffusion dynamics among nodes whose interactions are governed by higher-order structures.
Finally, leveraging the same approach used in the case of CNs, the RW has been applied as a community discovery
method for SCs. By considering different time scales, the walker’s diffusion on the SC can reveal various mesoscale
structures, where the inclusion of higher-order interactions can lead to new types of community organization.

nodes.title: Unveiling the impact of cross-order hyperdegree correlations in contagion processes on hypergraphs
nodes.authors: Guzman ,  Andres*; Malizia, Federico; Kiss, Istvan Z.
nodes.abstract:
Recent years have seen growing interest in spreading phenomena that extend beyond pairwise interactions to include group interactions [1]. These interactions among groups of more than two individuals give rise to non-trivial phenomena including discontinuous phase transitions, hysteresis, explosive spreading, and bistability. Within higher-order network frameworks, nodes participate in hyperedges of different sizes, resulting in a vector of hyperdegrees rather than a single degree. Previous studies have shown that hyperdegree distributions strongly influence spreading dynamics; for example, heterogeneity in pairwise interactions can suppress bistability and explosive transitions [2]. However, hyperdegree distributions across different interaction orders are typically assumed to be independent. The correlations arising from joint hyperdegree distributions in heterogeneous systems remain largely unexplored, despite their potential to fundamentally alter which nodes act as hubs at different interaction orders.

We address this gap by proposing a configuration model with joint hyperdegree distributions that explicitly accounts for cross-order degree correlations. We consider a higher-order network in which each node i participates in two orders of interactions: pairwise interactions associated with hyperdegree ki1 and three-body interactions associated with hyperdegree ki2. Correlations are quantified by the Pearson coefficient σ between a pairwise hyperdegrees and three-body hyperdegree. We develop an effective-degree model for the Susceptible–Infected–Susceptible (SIS) process that accurately captures dynamics on heterogeneous hypergraphs while accounting for cross-order correlations.

Our model reproduces stochastic simulations across diverse correlation regimes, enabling systematic analysis of how cross-order correlations reshape epidemic spreading. We identify three fundamental mechanisms: (i) positive correlations lower epidemic thresholds by concentrating multiple interaction orders in the same hub nodes, creating transmission pathways that amplify outbreak potential; (ii) negative correlations temporally desynchronize pairwise and group transmission, with pairwise spreading establishing an initial infection base that precedes subsequent group amplification; and (iii) negative correlations invert the canonical hierarchical infection pattern where high-degree nodes are typically infected first and contagion cascades to lower-degree classes [3] instead causing high-degree group hubs to become infected later, reversing the expected temporal hierarchy for group interactions. These mechanisms have direct implications for intervention design. Optimal control strategies depend on both correlation structure and the relative strength of pairwise versus group transmission, with negatively correlated systems requiring adaptive strategies that shift between pairwise and group targeting. More broadly, our results show that the structure of higher-order interactions, beyond their mere existence, critically determines dynamics in hypernetwork, making inter-order correlations essential for accurate modelling and effective intervention across real-world systems.

nodes.title: The Role of Interlayer Directionality in Diffusion and Jamming on Multiplex Networks
nodes.authors: Bouchet, Mateo; Tejedor Cubero, Alejandro*; Wang, Xiangrong; Moreno, Yamir
nodes.abstract:
Diffusion on multiplex networks exhibits a rich phenomenology arising from the interplay between network topology and interlayer coupling. While previous studies have shown that directionality within layers can induce superdiffusion and the prime regime (optimal coupling), the role of directionality in the interlayer connections themselves has remained largely unexplored. In this work [1], we investigate diffusion dynamics in multiplex networks with directed interlayer couplings. We demonstrate, both numerically and analytically, that interlayer directionality alone, even when all individual layers are undirected, can reproduce a rich diffusion phenomenology including both superdiffusion and the prime regime. We also provide an analytical formulation for the convergence of eigenvalue families at different interlayer diffusivity intensities (e.g. see Fig. 1a).
More importantly, we uncover a previously unreported dynamical phase, which we term the jamming regime. In this regime, strong asymmetric interlayer couplings hinder diffusion, dynamically fragmenting the system into effectively disconnected components and preventing convergence to the steady state. We characterize this transition through the spectral properties of the supra-Laplacian, demonstrating that the smallest nonzero eigenvalue $\Lambda_2$ vanishes asymptotically as the interlayer coupling increases ($\Lambda_2 \sim D_X^{-1}). As shown in Figure 1, this phenomenon is attainable in both synthetic toy models (panel b) and real-world systems, such as the London transport network (Panel b). Our results identify interlayer directionality as a fundamental control parameter governing transport, relaxation, and connectivity in multiplex systems, with significant implications for the design and optimization of interconnected infrastructures.

[1] Bouchet, Mateo, Alejandro Tejedor, Xiangrong Wang, and Yamir Moreno. Directionality-Induced Jamming in Multiplex Networks. arXiv:2510.22634, 2025. https://doi.org/10.48550/arXiv.2510.22634

nodes.title: ``Reducing'' transfer entropy for nonparametric network reconstruction from dynamics
nodes.authors: Kirkley, Alec*
nodes.abstract:
The task of network reconstruction from dynamics, in which edges indicating functional dependence are inferred from time series observed on a set of nodes, has wide reaching applications across various scientific fields. Bayesian network reconstruction provides a principled means to reconstruct networks from dynamics, allowing for model selection and quantification of uncertainties. However, applying the standard Bayesian paradigm is challenging when the observed process is highly nonlinear (e.g. chaotic) and the underlying dynamics are unknown. For such dynamics, it is helpful to incorporate autoregressive dependencies through time delay embeddings, which are difficult to reproduce in inference objectives through explicit Bayesian generative models. With its capacity for capturing flexible nonparametric dependencies among time series, transfer entropy has become widely used for network reconstruction when the underlying dynamics are highly nonlinear, gaining popularity in research focusing on neural, climate, and other complex systems.

However, when applied to discretized data, transfer entropy in its standard form exhibits two major shortcomings for network reconstruction: (a) It provides substantially inflated values for completely uncorrelated time series when the data is sparsely sampled; (b) It has no clear means of assessing statistical significance, requiring expensive simulations and the choice of an arbitrary significance level (which should be corrected for multiple comparisons and large sample sizes). These issues are fundamentally a result of treating the time series as instances of random variables---Shannon entropy is non-increasing under additional conditioning, so it is always ``worth'' (in an entropy sense) including the past time series values at a node i to predict the future time series values at a node j, regardless of how correlated these series actually are and how much information is already accounted for in j's past time series. 

By considering a combinatorial formulation of transfer entropy in which information is shared only during a single finite transmission event rather than among underlying stochastic processes, here we utilize the Minimum Description Length (MDL) principle to alleviate the above issues and automatically reconstruct networks from discrete dynamics. Our approach results in a ``reduced'' transfer entropy which is asymptotically equivalent to the standard maximum likelihood plug-in estimator but introduces a finite-size correction that allows for negative transfer entropy values when it is more compressive to transmit the future time series values at node j using only its own time delay embedding than to also include i's time delay embedding. Our measure also allows for automatic selection of the optimal temporal lags for constructing the time delay embeddings, and can be generalized to compute a multivariate transfer entropy measure for reconstructing networks with non-redundant information sharing among nodes.

nodes.title: Community Detection Beyond Network Structure
nodes.authors: Somin, Shahar*; Pentland, Alex ; Cohen, Tom; Kepner, Jeremy
nodes.abstract:
Community detection is a central problem in network science and is traditionally approached through explicit connectivity patterns. 
However, coordination does not always manifest as dense structure, and groups may act in a coordinated manner even when observable networks remain sparse. 
In such regimes, topology alone provides limited information for identifying communities.
Here, we show that latent community structure can be inferred from bursty dynamics, without relying on observed network connections.
In particular, we demonstrate that members of the same latent community exhibit similar inter-event time distributions, extending earlier results on the informativeness of bursty temporal fingerprints [1].
This alignment persists even when explicit connectivity is weak or absent.
Building on this observation, we introduce a community-detection framework based on bursty inter-event time dynamics. 
We evaluate the framework both within single platforms, where explicit structure may be available, and across platforms, where explicit links are absent by definition. In both settings, over four distinct datasets, our bursty framework outperforms structure-based community detection and temporal embedding baselines, including time-series foundation models.
To explain the origin of similar bursty patterns arising among community members, we introduce a generative model. The latent community structure is represented by a weighted stochastic block model $A_w \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$. 
At each iteration, a source node $v_{source}$ is chosen with probability proportional to $A_w \cdot z$, capturing how a member's recent activity stimulates other community members into action. 
$z[v_{source}]$ holds the time since $v_{source}$ last activity.  
A target node $ v_{target} $ is chosen according to: 
    \[
    P(v_{target}) \propto \lambda A_w \cdot d + (1 - \lambda) A_{rand} \cdot d
    \]
following [2].
$\lambda \in [0,1]$ controls the strength of community-based ($A_w$) vs. random selection ($A_{rand}$).
Preferential attachment is applied to favor nodes with higher existing degree ($d$).
The model reproduces the observed alignment of inter-event time distributions within communities, an effect that is not captured by former individual-level queuing-based models [3].
The model also predicts a detectability transition.
