Namely, unless latent communities induce highly dense observable sub-networks ($~70\%$ intra-community connectivity), bursty inter-event time statistics enable more accurate community detection compared to structure-based methods and temporal embedding models.
Simulations reproduce this transition and are consistent with the empirical results.
Together, these findings identify bursty dynamics as a signature of community structure in regimes where topology alone is insufficient.

nodes.title: Community Detection for Directed Graphs Reveals Interdisciplinarity in Computer Science
nodes.authors: Cionca, Alexandre*; Chan, Chun Hei Michael; Van De Ville, Dimitri
nodes.abstract:
Network modeling can be applied to many real-world data and phenomena. One important application is the discovery of community structure: sets of nodes that are more densely connected to one another than to the rest of the graph [1]. However, conventional community detection does not fully fully account for the information contained in directed graphs. To address this shortcoming, we recently introduced bicommunities, which define a community in terms of its sending and receiving parts [2]. We developed a new spectral framework tailored to directed graphs, which reverts to edge clustering in an embedding space.

Here, we apply the new framework to the Wiki-cs dataset [3], which is often used as a benchmark for machine learning architectures. The Wiki-cs network captures directed links between Wikipedia pages in computer science (CS) that have been classified into 10 subcategories. We first investigate the directed communities and assess how the ground truth categories are represented. Second, we evaluate the specificity of bicommunities for these classes and compare the retrieved patterns with those from randomized edge clusters.

Bicommunities of Wiki-cs exploit directed edge information within the network, hinting at a hierarchical and interdisciplinary organization of the CS subfields (Fig. 1.B). The unsupervised detection of bicommunity captures a meaningful representation with respect to the labeling of the nodes (Fig. 1.C). Using Wiki-cs as a case study, we demonstrate that bicommunity detection effectively highlights the directed organization of networks. The perspective of bimodularity extends the analytical toolkit of network science.

[1] Newman, Mark EJ. ”Modularity and community structure in networks.” Proceedings of the national academy of sciences 103,
no. 23 (2006).
[2] Cionca, Alexandre, Chun Hei Michael Chan, and Dimitri Van De Ville. ”Community detection for directed networks revisited
using bimodularity.” Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences 122, no. 35 (2025).
[3] Mernyei, P´eter, and C˘at˘alina
Cangea. ”Wiki-cs: A wikipedia-based benchmark for graph neural networks.” arXiv preprint arXiv:2007.02901 (2020).

nodes.title: Community Detection in Psychometric Subject Networks
nodes.authors: Armanetti, Arianna*; Ibáñez-Berganza, Miguel; Garlaschelli, Diego
nodes.abstract:
Psychometrics studies psychological constructs through questionnaire data. Traditional factor analysis describes responses via latent variables representing common causes (e.g., personality traits or pathological symptoms). 
Network psychometrics offers an alternative paradigm, modeling items or symptoms as interacting nodes in a network. Within this paradigm, questionnaire datasets can be viewed as bipartite networks, projectable onto either item or subject space. 
While item-space networks have been extensively studied [1], the subject space remains largely unexplored, particularly regarding community structure that could reveal clinically or theoretically meaningful subgroups.
We here propose and compare a set of modularity-based approaches for community detection in subject networks, differing in their choice of null model and adjacency matrix type.
We construct subject similarity networks using two complementary measures: (i) a positive weighted similarity metric based on item-wise distance, ranging from 0 to 1, and (ii) cosine similarity of response vectors, yielding signed weights in [-1,1].
For the positive similarity matrix, we maximise Newman modularity on a binarized network, hence using the configuration model as a null model. For the cosine similarity, we consider various possible null models that, based on random matrix theory, filter noise-consistent eigenvalues, extending the Garlaschelli-MacMahon framework[2] on correlation networks. 
In both cases, maximising the modularity amounts to finding the ground state of a Potts model with disordered couplings, that we sample via simulated annealing. The whole procedure eventually provides us with a significance assessment of the obtained communities. 
We test these methods on psychometric datasets including the Big Five personality inventory, DASS-42 depression-anxiety-stress scale, and Empathy Quotient. 
Preliminary results show that all the approaches consistently detect similar well-defined community structures, identifying a characteristic number of communities for each dataset. This work provides methodological tools for exploring population heterogeneity in psychometric data through subject-space network analysis.

nodes.title: Detectability threshold in weighted modular networks
nodes.authors: Radicchi, Filippo; N. Silva, Filipi ; Flammini, Alessandro ; Fortunato, Santo; Kojaku, Sadamori*
nodes.abstract: We study the necessary condition to detect, by means of spectral modularity optimization, the ground-truth partition in networks generated according to the weighted planted-partition model with two equally sized communities. We analytically derive a general expression for the maximum level of mixing tolerated by the algorithm to retrieve community structure, showing that the value of this detectability threshold depends on the first two moments of the distributions of node degree and edge weight. We focus on the standard case of Poisson-distributed node degrees and compare the detectability thresholds of five edge-weight distributions: Dirac, Poisson, exponential, geometric, and signed Bernoulli. We show that Dirac distributed weights yield the smallest detectability threshold, while exponentially distributed weights increase the threshold by a factor 2‾√, with other distributions exhibiting distinct behaviors that depend, either or both, on the average values of the degree and weight distributions. Our results indicate that larger variability in edge weights can make communities less detectable. In cases where edge weights carry no information about community structure, incorporating weights in community detection is detrimental.

nodes.title: Task-Focused Dynamic Network Inference from Node Attribute Time Series
nodes.authors: Banerji, Namrata*; Berger-Wolf, Tanya
nodes.abstract:
Many systems of scientific and societal importance, ranging from brain connectivity to ecological interactions and financial trust networks, are inherently dynamic. Both the attributes of individual entities and the patterns of interaction between them evolve over time, leading to complex dependencies that are most naturally represented as dynamic networks. Yet in most real-world settings, the underlying network structure is not observed directly, and must instead be inferred from time series of node-level measurements \cite{HolmeSaramaki2012,KolarXing2010}. Network reconstruction has a long history in neuroscience, biology, and economics, but most existing approaches assume static topologies or require past observations of edges. These assumptions limit their ability to capture the transient, heterogeneous, and evolving relationships that shape real-world dynamics. We present \textbf{DyNRI}, a framework for \emph{task-focused dynamic network inference} that reconstructs evolving interaction networks directly from node attribute time series. DyNRI builds on Neural Relational Inference (NRI) \cite{Kipf2018} by extending it to time-varying settings. The model employs an encoder–decoder architecture in which the encoder infers distributions over latent edges at each time step using Gumbel-Softmax sampling, and the decoder autoregressively predicts future node attributes conditioned on the inferred dynamic graphs. By aligning network inference with the task of multi-step forecasting, DyNRI learns structures that are not only plausible explanations of past data but also useful for prediction. This distinguishes our approach from purely unsupervised structure discovery and grounds inference in predictive utility.

A major challenge in this domain is evaluation: ground-truth dynamic graphs are rarely available. To address this, we introduce a co-evolutionary simulation framework that generates coupled dynamics of node attributes and connectivity. In our simulator, nodes move in a latent space according to a stochastic policy that balances attraction to the centroid of their local neighborhood with independent goal-directed movement. Edges are formed based on $k$-nearest neighbors subject to a distance threshold, ensuring that attribute similarity drives connectivity while connectivity, in turn, influences subsequent attribute updates. This bi-directional coupling produces realistic, non-trivial dynamics where attributes and structure mutually influence each other, providing a principled testbed for dynamic inference methods.


Experiments on synthetic data suggest that DyNRI can recover meaningful dynamic structures without access to ground-truth edges, while maintaining prediction accuracy comparable to or exceeding baselines. Ongoing work is extending these experiments to ecological datasets involving animal movement and implicit interactions within groups, and building a reinforcement learning framework that uses DyNRI as a base policy to infer networks in settings with little training data.

nodes.title: Decoupling the scaling of the Fiedler eigenvalue from the spectral dimension
nodes.authors: Grimaldi, Lorenzo*; Villegas, Pablo; Vezzani, Alessandro; Burioni, Raffaella; Cassi, Davide; Gabrielli, Andrea
nodes.abstract:
The Laplacian Renormalization Group (LRG) has been introduced to extend the coarse-graining method to heterogeneous networks. Notably, it allows for generalizations of entropy and heat capacity, which can be used to recognize the scale-invariant regions and the spectral dimension $d_s$ of the network.\\
It has also been argued that the Fiedler eigenvalue $\lambda_F$ scales with the system size $N$ as $\lambda_F\sim N^{-2/d_s}$, such that the index of the scaling serves as an estimate of the spectral dimension of the graph. We have checked the consistency of these methods for two specific fractal networks, namely the Sierpinski gasket and carpet.

However, once we look at the compositions of networks (specifically bundled networks, like the Dirac comb and extensions), we find evidence of a decoupling between the scaling exponent $d_g$ of the Fiedler eigenvalue and the spectral dimension. The heat capacity derived from the LRG machinery correctly isolates the different regions of the system and characterizes them with the related spectral dimension, capturing the multidimensional nature of complex networks. The dimension $d_g$ can be analytically computed from the fractal and spectral dimensions of the underlying sectors.\\
We examine the behavior of $d_g$ also for bundled structures built on Barabasi-Albert graphs, as well as random and $(u,v)$-trees, showing that the procedure remains reliable even when random graphs are considered.

nodes.title: Functional Graph Spectra
nodes.authors: Scott, Cory*
nodes.abstract: In this work I present a functional perspective on normalized graph spectra. By viewing the symmetric normalized eigenvalues of a graph as pointwise samples of an unknown spectral function, we can evaluate the spectrum at values other than 1,2,..n. This interpretation lets us 1) compare graphs of different sizes with the L2 norms of their respective functions; 2) derive objective functions for spectral graph reduction/editing; and 3) examine the distributions of spectral functions that arise from distributions of graphs. I will detail each of these, and present some data science applications as well as further questions that arise from treating graph spectra this way.

nodes.title: UniqueRank: Identifying Important and Difficult-to-Replace Nodes in Attributed Graphs
nodes.authors: Cai, Erica; Miller, Benjamin*; Simek, Olga; Smith, Christopher
nodes.abstract:
Identifying important nodes within a graph is a critical task across a wide range of real-world applications. A persistent challenge is that the removal of important nodes can greatly compromise a network’s ability to perform its intended function. However, many methods for identifying critical nodes overlook the fact that networks are often resilient and can adapt to disruptions by substituting the removed nodes with alternative nodes located nearby. For example, in a criminal organization, if a central leader is removed, another operative with similar capabilities may step into the leadership role, with network connections subsequently rerouting to preserve the operational structure. In such cases, while the initial removal may temporarily destabilize the system, the long-term impact on overall effectiveness may be minimal if a suitable replacement is readily available.

To address this gap, we focus on identifying nodes whose removal and subsequent attempted replacement result in a significant decrease in the network’s overall efficiency. Numerous methods have been developed to rank the importance of nodes, with PageRank being one of the most widely used. More recent approaches, such as AttriRank, extend these methods by integrating node attributes—such as organizational membership—into the ranking process, which captures richer contextual information. We introduce UniqueRank, an efficient method that builds on AttriRank. Like PageRank and AttriRank, we adopt a random walk perspective. Unlike prior methods, which assign equal transition probabilities to all neighbors, UniqueRank increases the likelihood of transitioning to neighbors with more distinct attributes. Each edge (i, j) receives a weight defined as the maximum attribute difference (minimum inverse similarity, defined by a radial basis function applied to attribute differences) between node j and its neighbors, capturing a local neighborhood at most two hops from i. Therefore, movement from node i to a neighbor j is proportional to how different j’s attributes are, relative to other neighbors. The top UniqueRank nodes form a robust set of candidates that are both critical and hard to replace. A secondary refinement step is then applied to select the final set of top-ranked nodes that maximize both structural importance and attribute uniqueness.

We rigorously evaluate efficiency reduction of top UniqueRank nodes across diverse domains—real criminal networks, social networks, and supply chain networks—and find that removing and then attempting to replace these top-ranked nodes consistently leads to larger efficiency reduction in the neighborhood of the removed node when compared to other widely used and traditional ranking methods. We measure efficiency reduction by quantifying the relative decrease in shortest path distances between node pairs after a top-ranked node is removed and replaced. Our results hold across different attribute-similarity and node-distance criteria for assessing a node’s suitability as a replacement for a removed node. The presentation will also include results with a novel baseline method, an ablation study, and observations on an extension to biomolecule analysis.

nodes.title: Simultaneous Inference of Network Structure and Dynamics: How Much Data is Needed?
nodes.authors: Sun, Yiting*; Gao, Tingting; Yan, Gang
nodes.abstract: Understanding complex systems requires knowledge of both their network structure, which captures the interactions among components, and their dynamical mechanisms, which govern system evolution. While recent advances have enabled data-driven inference of either network topology or governing equations, an approach capable of recovering both simultaneously has remained elusive. Equally unresolved is the fundamental question of how much data is minimally required to achieve reliable inference. Here we present a unified framework that jointly infers both network topology and governing equations directly from time-series data, combining interpretable symbolic representations with computational adaptability. By applying this framework to coupled systems, we reveal the fundamental data limits for accurate inference and identify a phase transition from practically inferable to non-inferable regimes as system size increases. Our analysis further quantifies the information content of observational data for different time windows and sampling rates, linking it to the achievable inferability. These findings lay down both the theoretical foundations and practical guidelines for data-driven inference of interconnected dynamical systems across diverse scientific domains.

nodes.title: Spectral Decomposition of Feed-Forward Networks: Identifying Task-Essential Structure via Eigen-Projectors
nodes.authors: Chicchi, Lorenzo*
nodes.abstract: In this work, we introduce an innovative analytical framework that grants access to the spectral properties of Feed-forward neural networks (FFNNs) .By introducing specific topological elements, we map the layered architecture into a global, square matrix representation. This formulation allows for the extraction of a complete set of eigenvalues and eigenvectors that characterize the network as a single dynamical system rather than a sequence of isolated layers. Building on the ranking of network components via post-trained eigenvalues, we investigate the geometric and functional structure of the projectors derived from the eigenvectors. We find that the projectors associated with the most dominant eigenvalues capture the "backbone" of the network’s decision-making process. These projectors exhibit global topological properties that are not localized to individual neurons but are distributed across the network’s depth. Experimental validation across multiple datasets reveals a striking phenomenon: the network can be reconstructed using only a small fraction of the dominant projectors without significant loss in task performance. This work offers a bridge between deep learning and spectral graph theory. By viewing artificial neural networks through the lens of complex systems, our framework opens new avenues for understanding how information is distilled within hierarchical architectures.

nodes.title: The rich are loopy: spectral signatures of hub structure
nodes.authors: Torres, Leo*
nodes.abstract:
The rich club coefficient ϕ(k) quantifies whether high-degree nodes interconnect more densely than expected [1].
While widely used, this measure captures only edge density and is blind to the topology of hub interconnections. A
star of hubs and a loop or cycle of hubs can yield similar ϕ(k) values despite having fundamentally different structural
properties.
We introduce a family of linear operators that provide spectral signatures for rich club structure. For each degree
threshold k, we define the cycle core Hk = 2-core(G[deg ≥ k]): the maximal subgraph of high-degree vertices that
forms cycles among themselves. The nested sequence H2 ⊇ H3 ⊇ H4 ⊇ · · · defines a filtration different from the
standard k-core filtration that identifies vertices by how deeply they participate in the hierarchical core (Fig. 1A).
The escape walk operator Mk is a matrix indexed by states (u, v, W), where v is a vertex with deg(v) ≥ k, u is
a neighbor of v (the “arrival” direction), and W is a set of k−2 other neighbors (the “escape” directions). A transition
(u, v, W) → (v, x, W′
) occurs when x is a neighbor of v distinct from u and from all vertices in W. This generalizes
the non-backtracking matrix [2]: for k = 2 there are no escapes and we recover the standard construction; for k ≥ 3 the
additional blocked directions create a sparser transition structure sensitive to high-degree cycles.
The key spectral property: Mk has non-zero eigenvalues if and only if Hk is non-empty. When hubs form acyclic
structures, the spectrum vanishes—but the operator’s state space still encodes information. We show that the state count
equals P
v
(d
high
v
)
2
, the second moment of the hub degree distribution, capturing heterogeneity invisible to density-based
measures. We define normalized pre-spectral measures ψstates and ψtrans that quantify how the hub subgraph compares
to a clique of equal size (Fig. 1C). Row-normalizing Mk yields a spectral radius ρ ∈ [0, 1] that characterizes how random
walks spread among hubs when forced through multiple escape routes—networks with identical |Hk| can have different
ρ, revealing dynamical structure beyond size (Fig. 1D). A collapsed representation via exact lumping [3] reduces state
space by up to 99%, enabling tractable computation.

nodes.title: Equivalences between modularity and diverse low-dimensional representations
nodes.authors: Rubinov , Mikail *
nodes.abstract: This work equates normalized and generalized versions of the modularity with variants of the k-means objective, spectral manifold learning objectives, and UMAP. These equivalences naturally lead to definitions of new representation objectives. As an example, we show that one of these objectives embeds brain-imaging data better than UMAP. Together, our results unify outwardly distinct representations across unsupervised learning, network science, and imaging neuroscience.

nodes.title: A Bayesian approach to out-of-sample network reconstruction
nodes.authors: Marzi, Mattia*; Squartini, Tiziano
nodes.abstract:
Networks underpin systems from finance to biology, yet their structure is often only partially observed. Exponential Random Graph Models (ERGMs)~\citep{fronczak2014,squartini2017} reconstruct network topologies from constrained statistics, still they are typically fitted independently to each snapshot. Designed to carry out network reconstruction in an \emph{in-sample} fashion, such an inference scheme cannot be employed to predict future configurations. 

To overcome such a limitation, here we introduce a Bayesian framework that integrates past information into an empirical, time-aware prior and uses it to predict subsequent snapshots, thus enabling genuine \emph{out-of-sample} reconstruction. More specifically, our approach employs the fitness-based ERGM named \emph{density-corrected Gravity Model} (dcGM)~\citep{cimini2015} to calculate the posterior predictive distribution $P(\mathbf{A}_{t+1}|\mathbf{A}_t)$, i.e. the probability of observing a given configuration at time $t+1$, on the basis of the previous snapshot.

Our framework turns the dcGM into a Bayesian Fitness Model (BFM) where the link probability $p_{ij}=zs_is_j/(1+zs_is_j)$ depends on the strengths of nodes, $\{s_i\}$, and a prior, $\pi(z)$, calibrated over a rolling window gathering three years of historical data: this prior, compatible with a Gamma distribution, evolves dynamically, as the estimate of the link density at time $t$ enriches it to carry out the prediction exercise at time $t+1$.

When applied to the Electronic Market for Interbank Deposit (eMID) at the weekly time scale~\citep{cimini2015}, the BFM accurately reproduces both global and local properties. More detailedly, the BFM achieves a predictive accuracy of $\simeq80\%$ and a link-level precision that is roughly twice the one achieved in~\citep{parisi2018} by the Directed Binary Configuration Model, implemented to carry out the in-sample version of the same exercise.

Most strikingly, each predicted configuration serves as a reliable prior for the next one: in symbols, $P(\mathbf{A}_{t+1}|\langle\mathbf{A}_t\rangle)$ can be employed to carry out a fully `self-sustained', out-of-sample reconstruction of ten years of the eMID evolution - an exercise requiring the knowledge of the strength sequence, the prior at $t=0$ and just one, empirical snapshot (i.e. $A_0$).

nodes.title: Bayesian Resolution of Discrepant Self-Reported Network Ties
nodes.authors: Gile, Krista*
nodes.abstract:
Most social network analysis assumes an objective network of shared social ties, typically measured as self-reports from research subjects. Subjects often give discrepant reports on ties, however, and there is no standard way to resolve these discrepancies. We develop a method to address this pervasive and important problem. Building on work by Butts, 2003, we develop a Bayesian model that leverages patterns of agreement among respondents across multiple relations on the same set of subjects.  The multiple relations allow a natural context for pseudo-replication, allowing us to estimate false positive and false negative rates for each individual and to find a posterior distribution for the objective network. Our work is motivated by the pervasive problem of discrepant reports in social network research and developed in the context of an exemplary set of six undirected social relations among middle school students in the Food Activity Screens and Teens (FAST) study (Sirard et al, 2024).

The FAST study investigates multiple social relations among U.S. middle school students in several schools. Observed reporting patterns including small numbers of dramatic under-reporters and an even smaller number of dramatic over-reporters motivates our use of a novel class of flexible priors to allow for these realistic aberrant reporting behaviors.

In simulations and model checks, we find that our method improves on both the extreme heuristic methods commonly used to resolve discordant reports, and on previous statistical methods (see Figure 1).  We also provide posterior predictive checks which illustrate the strength of model performance; comparison across 6 relations in 4 datasets allows reflection on circumstances when the models perform more or less well.

nodes.title: Category similarity fitness models and medium entropy for network reconstruction
nodes.authors: Duong, Duc*; Garlaschelli, Diego
nodes.abstract: The problem of network reconstruction when only a few published information are known is a challenge in network science. Solving this problem well brings great benefits because the information provided can help policy makers make strategic decisions. The traditional fitness model method achieves relatively good results, however, this method only uses one variable: GDP in case of countries or total asset in case of firms to reconstruct the network. This method often ignores additional structural or contextual features. In this paper, we propose improvements with the similarity of category features. Two different methods have been proposed. The first method uses maximum likelihood with additional constraint to find a solution. The second method, used under limited input information conditions, proposes using medium entropy to find the suitable solution among all the solutions that satisfy the constraint. Our approach provides a principled and scalable strategy for building networks, which can exploit maximize potential of the available published information. Three different datasets were applied: ELEnet16, BACI (CPEII) and UN Comtrade to demonstrate the effectiveness of the method. The results are measured by ROC AUC, PR AUC, AIC and BIC. We find that economic regions are the variable that play an important role in network reconstruction for the above dataset.

nodes.title: Hypergraph inference from network data via the minimum description length principle
nodes.authors: Zang, Yingbang*; Zhang, Yanting; Kirkley, Alec
nodes.abstract: Reconstructing higher-order interactions from network data is important for understanding and predicting the behavior of complex systems, yet in most applications only a static pairwise projection of the underlying structure is available. Currently, many approaches either rely on supervision or side information, or adopt fully Bayesian formulations with Markov chain Monte Carlo sampling, which limits scalability and makes it difficult to deploy such methods on large real-world systems. Here, we present an unsupervised recovery scheme that combines a Minimum Description Length objective with a greedy merge-split algorithm to infer latent hyperedges solely from static pairwise edge data, making the trade-off between model complexity and explanatory power explicit while avoiding MCMC. On synthetic benchmarks that are globally sparse but locally dense, our method achieves high accuracy in recovering higher-order structure.

nodes.title: NetPair: Structural motifs for physical network distance
nodes.authors: Brennan, Daniel*; Rogers, Timothy; Worden, Keith; Klein, Brennan
nodes.abstract:
Network motifs are commonly used to find repeating patterns within a network; however, in some fields, topology alone provides inadequate granularity to distinguish patterns. One of these such fields is Population-based Structural Health Monitoring (PBSHM) [1,2]. The aspiration of PBSHM is to acquire greater knowledge on the health of an individual structure by accumulating data across a range of similar structures. The belief is that by taking learned knowledge from one structure, this can be transferred [3] to another similar structure and improve the overall knowledge.

To establish which structures are similar, a unified language - an Irreducible Element model - is first used to describe the geometrical, material, contextual, and interactional properties of the structure in graph form. Comparisons are then drawn between these representations in a network space where the links between the structures are a weight of how similar the associated structures are. Despite there being numerous network based similarity measures, they often view attributes as second-class data. To overcome these limitations, this paper proposes a new algorithm which promotes attributes to first-class data and facilitates partial attributing matching in motifs; NetPair. The NetPair algorithm is evaluated against the Population-based SHM Engineered Asset Resource (PEAR) dataset.

nodes.title: Toward Fairness in Network Algorithms: Rankings by Biased Random Walks
nodes.authors: Salvai, Elisabetta*; Aarup Dalsgaard, Jacob; Gallo, Luca; Petri, Giovanni; Sinatra, Roberta
nodes.abstract: Ranking algorithms such as PageRank play a central role in ordering information and identifying influential nodes in networks, but they can also perpetuate existing biases, reducing the visibility of minority groups. While prior work has examined structural network features of unfair rankings, fewer studies have proposed concrete interventions to improve fairness. We address this gap by introducing a biased variant of PageRank that modifies the random walk transition probabilities based on node in-degree. By tuning a bias exponent, the algorithm can favour transitions toward either high- or low-degree nodes, producing different rankings on the same network. Focusing on networks with binary attributes, we evaluate group fairness by comparing the representation of minority nodes across ranking segments to their expected proportion. Experiments on 15 real-world networks show that standard PageRank consistently performs worse than the proposed Biased-PageRank in terms of fairness. Analysis using null models suggests that unfairness largely arises from degree distribution differences between groups. Overall, our results demonstrate that selecting an appropriate bias exponent can substantially improve ranking fairness, highlighting the importance of explicitly fairness-aware ranking algorithms.

nodes.title: Emergence of stability in social and informational temporal networks
nodes.authors: de Meijere, Giulia*; Karsai, Marton; Iniguez, Gerardo
nodes.abstract:
When studying temporal networks, the analysis of empirical datasets often starts from the processing of raw data into a series of time-aggregated snapshots. 
While dynamical changes prevail at narrower aggregation scales, connectivity emerges at wider scales. 
However, both dynamical changes and connectivity are crucial for our purpose of studying the evolution of connectivity in real networks. 
Most often in the literature, temporal data is aggregated at timescales of interest. 
Although this is a valid way to treat the evolution of temporal networks, we argue that specific fully-aggregated statistics of the network, including burstiness, determine an aggregation scale where dynamicity and global connectivity actually coexist. 

After optimally aggregating the data, we are able to move to the study of the evolution of connectivity in several (39) social and informational temporal networks. Temporal networks of social contact and information transfer often exhibit striking levels of stability at the system level, which emerge from bursty and highly variable interactions between individuals characterized by heterogeneous activity patterns. Here, we investigate the conditions under which seemingly erratic behavior at the individual level gives rise to stable collective dynamics in temporal networks.
Across datasets, we identify robust patterns in the evolution of network connectivity over time. 
We find that node degrees self-regulate in such a way that when nodes are poorly connected they tend to gain links in the next observation window, while when they are highly connected they tend to lose links. At the population level, this translates into a surprising degree of balance between positive and negative degree changes that signals the presence of system-wide stability.

nodes.title: Engagement steady states and volatility in temporal interaction networks
nodes.authors: Miller, Hadar; Mokryn, Osnat*
nodes.abstract:
Temporal networks provide a natural framework for studying how interactions evolve in complex systems, from organizations and online communities to transportation and communication infrastructures~\cite{science}. While much work has focused on structural properties or diffusion processes, less attention has been given to low-dimensional engagement summaries that remain comparable as network size and activity fluctuate.

This work proposes two temporal engagement indices for weighted interaction networks: a Temporal Network Intensity index that captures average edge activity, and a Temporal Dominance Inequality index that captures how unevenly activity is distributed across nodes. Interaction networks are modeled as sequences of weekly snapshots with edge weights capturing the volume of one-to-one communication. The dominance index applies a Gini-style inequality measure to nodes’ weighted degrees, quantifying how concentrated engagement is among a few actors.

Using six real-world temporal interaction networks (organizational email, online forums, and Wikipedia communication), the study shows that both indices are remarkably stationary over time. For each network, more than 80\% of week-to-week changes in intensity fall below 10\% despite large swings in the number of active nodes and edges, and probability distributions place each network in a narrow region of the (intensity, inequality) plane, suggesting engagement ``steady states''~\cite{miller2019constant}.

Weekly index pairs also serve as a compact fingerprint: a simple multi-class classifier infers the underlying network from (intensity, inequality) with F1 scores around 0.85–0.95, even when time-series lengths differ.

An important exception is the Enron managers’ email network before the company’s collapse. There, intensity is higher and both indices show persistent large week-to-week changes, so neither can be treated as stationary. This contrast suggests that departures from a network’s typical engagement steady state may provide early signals of organizational stress or structural change, complementing change-point detection methods that operate directly on network topology.

Viewed together, these results show that temporal interaction networks often self-organize into stable engagement regimes that are robust to size fluctuations, and that simple intensity–inequality indices can both characterize these regimes and highlight rare periods of instability.

nodes.title: Evolution of Conditional Entropy for Diffusion Dynamics on Graphs
nodes.authors: Koovely, Samuel*; Bovet, Alexandre
nodes.abstract:
Diffusion processes on graphs underpin widely-used tools in network science and machine learning. Various information-
theoretic have been used to study these processes: recent works in network science include studies of the entropy
production, a measure of the divergence of a process from detailed balance, and Von Neumann
entropy, originally introduced in the field of quantum information. While the steady-state behavior of diffusion processes is well understood based on Markov chain theory, comparatively few exact results describe their time
evolution. Here [3], we characterized this evolution through conditional entropy.
Let {Z(t)} denote the heat diffusion on a graph, modeled as a continuous-time Markov chain. We consider the conditional entropy curve H(Z(t) | Z(0)), i.e., an average of row-wise Shannon entropies of the heat kernel. We show that this quantity admits a natural thermodynamic interpretation: mass conservation yields a first-law analogue, and, on connected graphs, H(Z(t) | Z(0))
is monotone non-decreasing in time (a second-law analogue). We then obtain explicit spectral results for complete
graphs, path graphs, and families of circulant graphs. These formulas confirm the thermodynamic intuition and yield
interpretable structural comparisons. In particular, the entropy growth is strongly affected by how local or long-range the
available steps are in circulant graphs: enlarging the step-set accelerates mixing, while holding degree fixed but
changing step lengths still produces distinct transient regimes. Additionally, we show that conditional entropy
remains informative on random graph ensembles. For ER graphs, fully analytical expressions are not available; we propose a mean-field approximation that captures the qualitative dependence on edge density and improves as density
increases. Finally, we discuss asymptotic behavior and heuristically using path-graph and complete-graph curves
as entropy growth bounds.
Overall our work highlights that, from the perspective of conditional entropy, heat diffusion on graphs behaves similarly
to heat diffusion on a solid medium in the real world. This work paves the way for the development and understanding
of physics-inspired methods in applications such as detecting change points and periodic patterns in temporal networks.

nodes.title: Generating realistic temporal networks with periodic RWIG
nodes.authors: Shvydun, Sergey*
nodes.abstract:
Many complex systems, such as social, transportation and biological systems, are inherently dynamic, with interactions that change over time. Representing these systems as temporal networks through sequences of time-ordered graphs $G_0, \dots, G_{K-1}$ encodes the timing and order of interactions, which directly affects the spread of dynamical processes such as epidemics and information. This representation allows us to track how connectivity patterns change over time, detect recurring interaction structures and predict both future network changes and the outcomes of dynamical processes.
Developing simple and interpretable \emph{generative models} for temporal networks remains an open challenge. Ideally, such models should (i) reproduce realistic contact sequences, (ii) allow analytical treatment, and (iii) shed light on the mechanisms shaping both the topological and temporal structure of networks. In this work, we study the \emph{Random Walkers Induced Temporal Graph (RWIG) model} [1], in which $M$ independent walkers traverse a fixed underlying graph $G$ with $N$ nodes in discrete time steps. Each walker $w_i$ moves according to a Markov process defined by an $N {\times} N$ transition matrix $P_i$ (see Figure 1a). At each time step $k$, walkers occupying the same node generate links according to the \emph{co-location principle}, forming the temporal contact graph $G_k$ (see Figure 1b-c).  A physical interpretation of RWIG is a collection of individuals moving through space (e.g. city ) where each node of the underlying graph is a physical location. 
We focus on the \emph{inverse problem}: 
given only a single $K$-length contact sequence $G_0, \dots, G_{K-1}$ and without access to the actual movements of the walkers, our goal is to infer the initial transition matrices $P_1, \dots, P_M$ that can reproduce the observed contacts.  Motivated by empirical observations that many real-world interactions exhibit bursts, daily or weekly cycles and quasi-periodic patterns, we model the transition matrices of the walkers as \emph{$p$-periodic}, assigning each walker $i$ a sequence of steady-state distributions $\tilde{s}_i[0], \dots, \tilde{s}_i[p-1]$ satisfying 
\[
\tilde{s}_i[(k+1) \bmod p] = \tilde{s}_i[k] P_i, \quad k = 0, \dots, p-1,
\]
with each vector $\tilde{s}_i[l]$ being a steady state of $P_i^p$. This formulation ensures that the inferred transition matrices $P_i$ capture the quasi-periodic patterns observed in real temporal networks. Building on our previous results in [2], we prove that when all walkers share the same transition matrix $P_i {=} P$ and $M {\ge} N$, both the number of states $N$ and the steady-state distributions at each period $k$ can be derived as the roots of an $M$-degree polynomial $p_M(z)$ in the complex variable $z$:
\[
p_M(z) = \sum_{r=0}^{M} a_r z^r.
\]
The coefficients $a_0, \dots, a_M$ are defined recursively with $a_M = 1$ and $a_r = - \frac{1}{M-r} \sum_{l=r+1}^{M} a_l \, \sigma_{l,k}$ for $r = 0, \dots, M-1$,
where $\sigma_{l,k}$ is the empirical probability of a contact involving $l$ walkers at times $k, k+p, k+2p, ...$. When each walker $w_i$ has its own transition matrix $P_i$, we show that the steady-state distributions and matrices $P_1, \dots, P_M$ can be recovered numerically from the observed probabilities of contacts between walkers. As a result, we propose a \textit{periodic RWIG model} that infers both the period $p$ and the corresponding $p$-periodic transition matrices from observed temporal contact data.

We validate the periodic RWIG model on both artificial and empirical datasets, including school, conference and hospital face-to-face interaction networks (e.g. see Figure 1d). The results demonstrate that RWIG can reproduce realistic temporal networks while preserving the observed contact probabilities between walkers, suggesting that many networks can be approximated by a small number of periodic graph sequences reflecting quasi-periodic activity patterns.

nodes.title: Partial coalescence of random walks on networks
nodes.authors: Mattsson, Carolina*; Coutinho, Bruno; Starnini, Michele
nodes.abstract: This work introduces a model of multiple random walkers whose dynamics interpolate between independent and coalescing random walks on networks. Links activate stochastically; walkers at a node independently choose to follow an available temporal link with jump probability p. Co-located walkers see the same activation events, meaning the steps they take can coincide. While individual node occupancies fluctuate, the distribution of occupancies is stationary and depends on p. We derive a master equation for occupancy and identify a new dynamical regime (“partial coalescence”) where nodes regularly find themselves empty of walkers no matter how many walkers there are.

nodes.title: Weighted Digraph Dowker Persistence and Applications in Dynamical Systems
nodes.authors: Timofeyev, Tobias*; Potvin, Christopher; Jones, Benjamin; Kurianski, Kristin; Lopez, Miguel; Tanweer, Sunia
nodes.abstract:
By their nature it is difficult to differentiate chaotic dynamical systems through measurement. In recent years, work has begun on using methods of Topological Data Analysis (TDA) to qualitatively type dynamical data by approximating the topology of the underlying attracting set. This comes with the additional challenges of high dimensionality incurring computational complexity along with the lack of directional information encoded in the approximated topology. Due to the latter fact, standard methods of TDA for this dynamical data do not differentiate between periodic cycles and non-periodic cycles in the attractor. We present a framework to address both of these challenges. We begin by binning the dynamical data, and capturing the sequential information in the form of a coarse-grained weighted and directed network. We then calculate the persistent Dowker homology of the asymmetric network, encoding spatial and temporal information. Analytically, we highlight the differences in periodic and non-periodic cycles by providing a full characterization of their one-dimensional Dowker persistences. We prove how the homologies of graph wedge sums can be described in terms of the wedge component homologies. Finally, we generalize our characterization to cactus graphs with arbitrary edge weights and orientations. 
Our analytical results give insight into how our method captures temporal information in its asymmetry, producing a persistence framework robust to noise and sensitive to dynamical structure.

nodes.title: A broad class of network motifs enables constants of motion in phase dynamics
nodes.authors: Thibeault, Vincent*; Claveau, Benjamin; Allard, Antoine; Desrosiers, Patrick
nodes.abstract: Partial integrability in phase-oscillator dynamics is typically examined for identically connected oscillators or groups thereof [1,2,3]. Yet, the precise connectivity conditions that ensure conserved quantities on general networks remain unclear. Using Koopman theory [4], we rigorously derive conditions for the existence of distinct conserved quantities in the Kuramoto model with heterogeneous phase lags on any weighted, directed, and signed graph [5]. In the process, we establish a criterion for Lie symmetries in general dynamical systems in terms of the Koopman generator, allowing us to identify symmetries that generate new conserved quantities. These results reveal a broad class of network motifs that support conserved quantities and we detect these motifs in hundreds of complex empirical networks. Our results provide a rigorous theoretical application of Koopman's framework to nonlinear dynamics on complex networks and new possibilities for preserving phase relationships over time in complex systems through a careful design of their structure.

nodes.title: A Koopmanian approach to spectral and geometric properties of dynamics on networks
nodes.authors: Claveau, Benjamin*; Thibeault, Vincent; Allard, Antoine; Desrosiers, Patrick
nodes.abstract: Initially developed by Koopman and von Neumann in the early 1930s, Koopman theory is a mathematical framework representing any dynamical system in an operator formalism similar to quantum mechanics. Despite prominent data-driven applications in soft robotics and fluid mechanics, Koopman theory has only begun to show its full potential for dynamics of complex systems. For example, recent work on phase oscillators linked their constants of motion and symmetries to network motifs via properties of the Koopman operator, which governs the time evolution of functions on the state space. This operator is linear, albeit infinite-dimensional, thereby allowing the use of spectral methods. In this project, we adopt a more general perspective and seek the families of dynamical systems that admit specific Koopman eigenfunctions. First, we show that each Koopman eigenfunction constrains the component of the vector field normal to the corresponding level sets. Hence, the components of the vector field tangent to the intersection of the level sets of all eigenfunctions remain unrestricted. From this geometric perspective, we identified two general families of dynamics on networks where the rank deficiency of the weighted adjacency matrix plays a central role. These families encompass widely used models such as recurrent neural networks (RNNs) with affine eigenfunctions and the Lotka-Volterra model with monomial eigenfunctions. In all cases, each left singular vector of the weight matrix of null singular value implies a specific Koopman eigenfunction. Given a weighted adjacency matrix of rank r, these eigenfunctions define a generally nonlinear change of variables suitable for the exact dimension reduction of the system to r non-autonomous equations. The reductions also hold approximately for dynamics on complex networks whose weight matrix has low effective rank, a feature that is widespread in empirical networks. Our results link structural, spectral, and geometrical properties of dynamics on networks, and highlight the relevance of operator-theoretic approaches in the study of complex systems.

nodes.title: Analytical prediction of structure-function relationships in networks of Kuramoto oscillators
nodes.authors: Légaré, Arthur*; Desrosiers, Patrick; Doyon, Nicolas
nodes.abstract:
Mapping structural to functional connectivity remains a central challenge in the study of networked dynamical systems, particularly in computational neuroscience. Although the Kuramoto model provides a standard framework for relating network structure to function, most theoretical work has focused on how coarse network features affect collective synchronization, relying either on mean-field approaches in the thermodynamic limit or on low-dimensional reduction techniques. These approaches impose assumptions on system size or topology, leaving open how general network structure shapes pairwise correlations between oscillators. Here, we show that in the weak-coupling regime, pairwise correlations driven by local resonances provide a direct signature of topology that is valid for any number of oscillators. We consider a network of N Kuramoto oscillators with phases evolving in time, coupled through a weighted adjacency matrix K with global coupling and heterogeneous natural frequencies drawn from a Cauchy-Lorentz distribution. Using a second-order perturbative expansion in the rescaled coupling, we extract the stationary contributions to the correlation matrix defined over an integration time, in the long-time limit, by averaging over natural frequencies drawn from this distribution. This averaging weights the local resonant modes that generate stationary correlations.
As illustrated in Figure 1a, stationary correlations in the weak-coupling regime arise solely from second-order indirect interactions, while first-order direct interactions vanish. As a result, structurally disconnected nodes (j,k) become functionally correlated through shared neighbors (l). This yields a closed-form prediction. The analytical predictions are validated by extensive numerical simulations across multiple network topologies. Figure 1b illustrates an application to the zebrafish structural connectome. At weak coupling, the analytical expression accurately reproduces the structure-function relationship observed in simulations. Up to the stall coupling strength, beyond which the approximation breaks down, the cosine similarity between the analytical prediction and the correlation matrix obtained from numerical integration of the Kuramoto dynamics remains high (0.977 ± 0.004). This robust correspondence suggests that the approach can be extended to other statistics, such as phase locking, and to broader classes of network dynamics.

nodes.title: Disassortative community structure can induce oscillations and travelling waves in discrete excitable systems
nodes.authors: Kritpracha, Poom*; Taylor, Dane; Restrepo, Juan
nodes.abstract: Networks of discrete-state excitable systems can be used as simple models of activity in functional brain networks, epidemic spreading, and the propagation of social behavior like protest activity or the adoption of innovations. The macroscopic dynamics of such networks has received much attention due to their ability to reproduce many of the experimental observations that support the hypothesis that the brain operates at criticality, the onset of self-sustained activity that occurs as the strength of the coupling between the excitable systems increases. Previous work studying the bifurcations that occur as the excitability of the network is increased has focused mostly on networks without community structure. We study a stochastic discrete-state model with a refractory period on a network with community structure, and study how the dynamics depends on both the structure of the coupling of the communities, and on the network’s assortativity, which we quantify by the ratio of intra- to inter-community coupling. Using a mean-field description of the system, we show that additional bifurcations occur as the community structure of the network turns from assortative to disassortative. Depending on the structure of the network of communities, various bifurcations can occur, including period-doubling bifurcations, Hopf bifurcations, and a heteroclinic bifurcation as complete disassortativity is approached. Some of these bifurcations can lead to traveling waves when the network of communities is coupled on a ring structure. We illustrate our theoretical results with numerical simulations on different networks.

nodes.title: Distributed Self-Control of Dynamical Networks by Adaptive Link Weight Adjustments
nodes.authors: Sayama, Hiroki*
nodes.abstract: Conventional control theory considers controlling the behavior of a dynamical system toward a desired state by injecting externally designed inputs into the system. Meanwhile, most complex systems exhibit self-organization through local information exchanges among individual dynamical components that are embedded within a complex network of interactions. The self-organizing dynamics of those systems are realized in a highly distributed manner using locally available information only, and therefore, their behaviors have not been discussed much from a control theoretic viewpoint. Meanwhile, adaptive networks, i.e., dynamical networks whose states and topologies coevolve at similar time scales, can offer a promising theoretical framework in which a distributed form of ``self-control'' can be formulated as adaptive link weight adjustments. Here we propose and study a set of models of self-controlling dynamical networks that can self-organize their own states into a desired one through distributed adaptive link weight adjustments. We demonstrate the feasibility of both of the first-order and second-order approaches. Numerical experiments show that the second-order approach works significantly better than the first-order one, achieving a substantially faster and more robust convergence with less transient behaviors. The performance difference is more manifested for systems with faster adaptive dynamics. However, we also found that the second-order approach was highly sensitive to noise added to the system's states, illustrating a non-trivial trade-off between the two self-control approaches.

nodes.title: Early Predictor for the Onset of Critical Transitions in Networked Dynamical Systems
nodes.authors: Liu, Zijia; Zhang, Xiaozhu; Ru, Xiaolei; Gao, Ting-Ting; Moore, Jack*; Yan, Gang
nodes.abstract:
Numerous natural and human-made systems exhibit critical transitions whereby slow changes in environmental conditions spark abrupt shifts to a qualitatively distinct state, entailing severe consequences [1]. Examples include rapid global warming, extinction events, and power outages [2]. Real-world complex systems often comprise hundreds or thousands of interacting entities, and prevention or management strategies for critical transitions would benefit from knowledge of the point at which such transitions occur. However, most established methods [2] focus on low-dimensional systems, do not estimate the point of onset of transitions, or rely on prior knowledge of network connectivity or governing dynamics.

We address this gap using a machine-learning framework that combines graph isomorphism networks for highdimensional interacting systems with gated recurrent units for time series analysis [3]. The resulting model is trained to predict the parameter value at which a critical transition occurs using only short time series from observed nodes, and without knowledge of connectivity or dynamics.

We benchmark the method on a diverse set of model systems exhibiting both smooth (second-order) and explosive (first-order) transitions: synchronization in coupled Kuramoto oscillators, abrupt collapse of resource biomass in ecosystems, and failure of Wilson-Cowan neuronal networks. The model accurately anticipates critical transitions well in advance of their onset and remains robust to noise, nonstationarity, and partial observability [3]. Finally, applying the method to empirical vegetation and rainfall data using transfer learning, we predict forest-to-savannah transitions in African ecosystems (Fig. 1).

[1]	M. Scheffer, J. Bascompte, W. A. Brock, V. Brovkin, S. R. Carpenter, V. Dakos, H. Held, E. H. van Nes, M. Rietkerk, and G. Sugihara, Early-warning signals for critical transitions, Nature 461, 53-59 (2009).
[2]	I. Franovi´c, S. Eydam, and D. Eroglu, Regime switching in coupled nonlinear systems: Sources, prediction, and control—Minireview and perspective on the Focus Issue, Chaos 34, 120401 (2024).
[3]	Z. Liu, X. Zhang, X. Ru, T.-T. Gao, J. M. Moore, and G. Yan, Early Predictor for the Onset of Critical Transitions in Networked Dynamical Systems, Physical Review X 14, 031009 (2024).
 
Figure 1: Predicting abrupt transitions in empirical vegetated ecosystems. (a) Empirical vegetation data used to fine-tune a model after training on synthetic systems (transfer learning), showing average tree cover as a function of annual rainfall $\varepsilon$. (b,c) Predicted transition points for two areas from a previously unseen district at different lead distances. Horizontal lines span the interval from the data window used for prediction (blue dots) to the predicted transition point. Colour represents prediction error. Insets show magnified views. In (a-c), dashed vertical lines mark critical transitions $\varepsilon_C$.

nodes.title: Adaptive Weight Control of Power Grid Networks via Reinforcement Learning
nodes.authors: Park, Sangjoon*; Choi, Hoyun; Lee, Yongsun; Jo, Seungchan; Kahng, Byungnam
nodes.abstract:
Transport processes in weighted networks are strongly affected by the distribution of link weights. When a link fails—effectively reducing its weight to zero—it may trigger further failures and lead to an unstable system. Such cascading breakdowns are observed in power grids [1] and other infrastructures. While changing the network topology can enhance stability, it cannot respond dynamically once instability arises. Consequently, adapting the weights of the remaining links is a more practical approach. Yet, determining the optimal weight configuration over all links is computationally demanding.

Here, we introduce the Adaptive Weight Controller (AWC) algorithm within a reinforcement learning framework, which autonomously tunes link weights to drive the transport dynamics back to stable states after network attacks or spontaneous failures. We apply the AWC algorithm to a realistic representation of the UK power grid [2]. Frequency synchronization in power grids is described by the swing equation, a modified second-order Kuramoto model [3,4], given by $m_i\ddot{\theta}_{i} = P_{i} - \gamma_i \dot{\theta}_{i} + \sum_{j\neq i}^N{K_{ij}\sin(\theta_{j} - \theta_{i})}$, where $m_i$ denotes inertia, $\gamma_i$ the damping coefficient, $P_i$ the power and $K_{ij}$ the coupling strength (link weight). When a link $\ell$ is removed, it causes quantified frequency fluctuations.
{\small $$\Xi^{(\ell)} \equiv \frac{1}{T} \int_0^T dt \left[ \frac{1}{\sum_i m_i} \sum_i m_i \dot{\theta}_i^2(t) - \left( \frac{1}{\sum_i m_i} \sum_i m_i \dot{\theta}_i(t) \right)^2 \right],$$}
where $T$ is the total simulation time, set to 10 seconds. The AWC algorithm aims to minimize $\Xi^{(\ell)}$ by continuously adapting the weights of the links. 

Figure 1(a) presents the frequency deviations $\Xi^{(\ell)}$ resulting from the failure of individual links, highlighting a strongly heterogeneous response pattern. Across 105 failure cases, the AWC algorithm yields an average 53\% reduction in $\Xi^{(\ell)}$, selectively targeting high-impact failures while avoiding unnecessary interventions for small disturbances [Figure 1(b)]. In the illustrative example (failure of the link between buses 46 and 47, indicated by red arrows), the algorithm quickly damps oscillations within a few seconds [Figure 1(c)] by adaptively and bidirectionally tuning the link weights [Figure 1(d)].

These findings demonstrate that reinforcement learning can successfully handle the exponentially large search space of weight configurations to recover target dynamics following topological disturbances. We anticipate that this approach generalizes beyond power grids to other networked systems that demand fast stabilization after disruptions, such as transportation networks and communication infrastructures.

nodes.title: Discovering Communities in Continuous-Time Temporal Networks by Optimizing L-Modularity
nodes.authors: Brabant, Victor*
nodes.abstract: Community detection is a fundamental problem in network analysis, with many applications in various fields. Extending community detection to the temporal setting with exact temporal accuracy, as required by real-world dynamic data, necessitates methods specifically adapted to the temporal nature of interactions. We introduce LAGO, a novel method for uncovering dynamic communities by greedy optimization of Longitudinal Modularity, a specific adaptation of Modularity for continuous-time networks. Unlike prior approaches that rely on time discretization or assume rigid community evolution, LAGO captures the precise moments when nodes enter and exit communities. We evaluate LAGO on synthetic benchmarks and real-world datasets, demonstrating its ability to efficiently uncover temporally and topologically coherent communities.

nodes.title: Inferring Transient Behavior Changes During the 2022 Mpox Outbreak Using Adaptive Stochastic Temporal Networks
nodes.authors: Gubela, Nils*
nodes.abstract:
Mathematical modeling of the spread of infectious diseases on temporal networks has gained popularity in complex systems science to understand the intricate interplay between social dynamics and epidemics. In these models, the spread of the pathogen and the dynamics of the contact networks are described by stochastic processes. The spreading process depends on the state of the network, since transmission is only possible between connected agents. Importantly, the evolution of the network also depends on the state of the infection process. For example, diagnosed agents may isolate themselves and meet fewer people until they recover. Similarly, a critical number of infected agents may induce risk-averse behavior in susceptible agents. It was hypothesized that exactly this mechanism shaped the epidemic curve of mpox clade IIb in non-endemic regions during the summer of 2022. Mpox denotes a viral zoonosis caused by the Orthopoxvirus monkeypox (MPXV), which is endemic in West and Central Africa. In spring 2022, notable outbreaks of MPXV clade IIb were recorded around the world, predominantly affecting men who have sex with men (MSM). Within the same year, the outbreak significantly declined, and it is unclear which factors contributed to this rapid decrease [1,2]. To investigate the concomitant effects of sexual contact networks, transient contact reductions and the effect of infection- vs. vaccine-derived immunity on the 2022 mpox outbreak in Berlin, we calibrated an adaptive temporal network model with epidemic, vaccination, contact- and behavioral data [3]. The simulation of the model was done using high acceptance sampling [4], a technique especially tailored to efficiently sample pathogen spread on stochastic temporal network that adaptively evolve with the pathogen. The method is multiple orders of magnitude faster than the Gillespie algorithm, while it maintains to be exact.
Our results indicate that transient behavior changes played a major role in the decline of mpox cases in Berlin (see Figure 1). Mpox shredding kinetics are relatively fast compared to the speed of the contact network (or other sexually transmissible diseases) and hence, people with many contacts are most at risk of mpox infection. This group at risk either got vaccinated, infected or transiently changed their behavior and hence were not at highest risk for infection.
Our findings, supported by serological studies conducted in Berlin, highlight that, in addition to vaccination, timely and clear communication of transmission routes may trigger spontaneous protective behavior within key populations; underscoring the importance of targeted sexual health education as a core component of outbreak response.

nodes.title: Multistable active phases in complex contagion with group plasticity
nodes.authors: Lamata Otín, Santiago*; Malizia, Federico; Keating, Leah A.; St-Onge, Guillaume ; Latora, Vito; Gómez Gardeñes, Jesús; Hébert-Dufresne, Laurent
nodes.abstract: Higher-order contagion models show that group-based reinforcement can trigger discontinuous transitions, yet these results are typically derived on static hypergraphs that neglect temporal group dynamics. Here, we demonstrate that temporality is essential for correctly characterizing complex contagion by developing an analytically solvable approximate master equation framework that incorporates group switching. Our theory interpolates continuously between quenched and mean-field limits and reveals that increasing group switching rates can induce a discontinuous transition, enlarge the bistable region, and, under sufficiently strong nonlinearities, parallel exposures, or large group sizes, reverse its influence on the invasion threshold, thereby hindering contagion. Mapping empirical group switching rates and group-size heterogeneity to theory reveals the emergence of four distinct dynamical phases, combining continuous or discontinuous transitions with localized or delocalized activity. Remarkably, in localized regimes, we uncover multistable active phases in which multiple endemic states coexist, extending classical bistability. Furthermore, the emergence of discontinuous transitions requires stronger nonlinear reinforcement than previously thought, highlighting that both static-network simulations and mean-field analyses can yield qualitatively misleading predictions.

nodes.title: Networked Quantum–Classical Interdependency
nodes.authors: Meng, Xiangyi*
nodes.abstract: Interdependency---the mutual reliance and interaction between interconnected systems---is a fundamental factor determining performance and functionality of complex networked systems. In quantum networks, the quantum hardware intrinsically depends on classical communication for signaling, feedback and resource allocation, yielding a tightly coupled architecture. While this tight quantum–classical coupling is recognized as a critical bottleneck limiting the reliable distribution of high-quality entanglement, prevailing analytical models typically oversimplify this interaction, leaving performance boundaries of quantum networks largely unexplored. Here, we introduce a quantitative framework revealing that this interdependency is not merely a bottleneck but a fundamental driver of emergent, network-wide collective phenomena. We show that the quantum–classical interdependency induces phase transitions in entanglement distribution—either mixed-order (discontinuous) or second-order (continuous)—depending critically on the specific quantum control protocol and noise levels. To reveal the underlying mechanism, we introduce a novel metric—the linear gain coefficient—which analytically captures the role of the quantum protocol and accurately predicts both the transition type and network robustness, as validated by quantum computer simulations. Our framework provides fundamental insights into quantum–classical interdependency and offers a predictive tool to guide the design of resilient quantum architectures capable of reliable, long-distance entanglement distribution.

nodes.title: Synergistic and redundant signatures of temporal higher-order interactions.
nodes.authors: Ceria, Alberto*
nodes.abstract:
Temporal higher-order networks provide an effective representation for studying social systems in which time-varying interactions involve groups larger than dyads. While recent work has characterized
cross-order temporal correlations and group dynamics, the mechanisms by
which larger gatherings form remain unclear: do they emerge from the spontaneous addition of
individuals to any active sub-group, or do they require the presence of specific "core" subsets?
We introduce a multivariate information-theoretic framework to discriminate between these
group-formation pathways from data. Leveraging dynamic O-information, we assess whether the
past activity of sub-hyperlinks predicts the activity of a target hyperlink primarily through
synergy, i.e., predictive information that arises only when multiple sub-hyperlinks are considered
jointly and cannot be recovered from any single sub-hyperlink, or through redundancy, where
different sub-hyperlinks carry largely overlapping predictive information about the target. For each hyperlink of order d, we compute the dynamic O-information between its activity time series and the lagged activity of its sub-hyperlinks of order d' < d. Statistical significance is evaluated via the comparison with a randomized null model that preserves key temporal properties of individual time series (e.g., event-duration and inter-event-time distributions) while disrupting temporal correlation across hyperlinks.
We apply the method to five datasets of face-to-face interactions obtained from different social settings, such as schools and workplaces. Across all datasets, we observe both synergy- and redundancy-dominated group interactions, with the strongest effects mostly not reproduced by the null model. The most synergistic cases typically involve
target activations following the activity of multiple alternative sub-groups, supporting a
"many-roots" assembly mechanism rather than growth from a single initiating subset. In contrast,
strong redundancy is associated with target activations that reliably follow the activity of a
specific sub-group, consistent with "core-driven" formation. 
Our framework provides a principled way to classify higher-order temporal group dynamics and constrain generative models of human social gatherings.

nodes.title: "Dark information" in complex networks and systems: revealing the synergistic shadow structure.
nodes.authors: Varley, Thomas*
nodes.abstract:
There has been an explosion of interest recently in the problem of ``higher-order" interactions in complex systems.
In classic, network-based approaches, higher-order interactions between elements can only be inferred obliquely by observing patterns of low-order edges (as in community detection, or transitivity).
In contrast, information-theoretic analyses can directly assess high-dimensional interactions of many types, including redundant information (information duplicated over many elements) and synergistic information (information only disclosed by the joint state of multiple elements when observed simultaneously). 
A natural question is how well the classic, oblique approaches represent the genuine higher-order structures: how much (if any) ``structure" have classical complex systems and network science been missing? 

This presentation shows that statistical (``functional") connectivity networks are systematically biased in favor of redundant information and insensitive to the higher-order synergistic information. 
Drawing on analogy with ``dark matter" in cosmology, we argue that the scaffold of synergistic dependencies forms a kind of ``dark information" - a potentially vast set of possible interactions that are largely inaccessible to tools commonly used in network science and complexity science. 
Exploring this structure will require the development of novel mathematical machinery in information theory, and has the potential to reveal new vistas of discovery.
Finally, we will argue that synergistic dependencies should be of broad interest. 
Far from being a mere statistical curiosity, synergistic information is closely related to the phenomena of causal ``colliders" - when a variable's state is an integrative function of multiple colliding influences, the dependency structure is synergy-dominated. 
This suggests that the ``dark information" in complex systems is significant, as it reflects that ``integrated" information produced when multiple elements interact in non-trivial ways.

nodes.title: Emergent Topology of Optimal Networks for Synchrony
nodes.authors: Mikaberidze, Guram*; Taylor, Dane
nodes.abstract:
Real-world networks, whether shaped by evolution or intelligent design, are typically optimized for functionality while adhering to physical, geometric, or budget constraints. Yet tools to identify such structures remain limited [1]. Unlike classical synchronization studies that vary a global coupling constant on a fixed graph [2], our framework co-optimizes topology and weights under a hard coupling budget, revealing fundamentally different synchronization phenomenology.

This presentation will cover our recent work [3] in which we develop a gradient-based optimization framework to identify synchrony-optimal weighted networks under a constrained coupling budget. As shown in Fig. 1A, the resulting networks exhibit counterintuitive features: they are sparse, bipartite, elongated, and extremely monophilic (i.e., the neighbors of any node are similar to one another while differing from the node itself) [3]. 

Dynamics unfolding over optimal networks provably lack a synchronization threshold; arbitrarily small budgets lead to non-zero synchronization (see the top panel of Fig. 1B), even without diverging degrees or dense connectivity. Instead, we observe a sharp onset of global phase-locking as the budget exceeds a calculable critical value (see the black dash in Fig. 1B), exhibiting critical scaling at the transition.

These findings are matched by ``constructive'' theory: a nonlinear differential equation identifies which pairs of nodes are coupled (the black curve in Fig. 1C), while variational analysis prescribes the budget allocated to each node (the areas under the blue curves in Fig. 1C). This theory reveals two admissible pairing branches—reconciling prior conflicting numerical results—and predicts which is globally optimal, consistent with our gradient-based optimization. Our results have implications for power grids, neuromorphic computing, and other coupled oscillator technologies.


[1] Arenas, A., Díaz-Guilera, A., Kurths, J., Moreno, Y. and Zhou, C., 2008. Synchronization in Complex Networks. Physics Reports, 469(3), pp.93-153.
[2] Skardal, P.S., Taylor, D. and Sun, J. (2014) Optimal synchronization of complex networks, Physical Review Letters, 113(14), p. 144101.
[3] Mikaberidze, G. and Taylor, D., 2025. Emergent Topology of Optimal Networks for Synchrony. arXiv preprint arXiv:2509.18279.

nodes.title: Observing network dynamics by a small number of sentinel nodes
nodes.authors: MacLaren, Neil; Barzel, Baruch; Masuda, Naoki*
nodes.abstract: See the pdf abstract.

nodes.title: Quantifying Transient Dynamics in Heterogeneous Networks under Various Inputs
nodes.authors: Bao, Xiaoge*; Dai, Wei; Jan, Nagler; Lin, Wei
nodes.abstract:
Understanding how transient dynamics respond to localized inputs is crucial for predicting and controlling signal propagation in network systems, including neural processing, epidemic control, and power-grid stability. However, most existing theories assume homogeneous network structures and constant or pulse-like inputs, obscuring how structural heterogeneity and various input types shape transient dynamics and limiting agreement between theory and observation.
To establish a quantitative framework linking input properties to transient responses in heterogeneous networks, we focus on two core metrics: strength (amplification and peak response) and timing (time constant and response time). Beyond standard spectral and matrix-inverse approaches, we use a Neumann series expansion to separate the roles of intrinsic node dynamics and network structure. This method reframes propagation between nodes as the sum of all directed walks. Each walk’s influence is recursively weighted by the self-dynamics of nodes it passes through. This leads to a core principle: along any single path, strength multiplies at each step, and timing increases by increments. Specifically, strength from node $d$ to $d+1$ is set by the ratio of coupling strength to downstream nodal intrinsic decay, while the added time constant depends only on the next node's intrinsic decay, 
\begin{align*}
\frac{R(d+1)}{R(d)} &= \frac{A_{d\to d+1}}{\beta + D_{d+1}}, \quad 
\tau(d+1) - \tau(d) = \frac{1}{\beta + D_{d+1}}.
\end{align*} 
These rules clarify how structural heterogeneity, such as degree variance, amplifies both strength and timing. The framework also extends to explain effects from other network motifs. Together, these advances provide a general and interpretable theory of transient dynamics across network structures and input types, offering practical guidance for predicting and optimizing signal propagation.

nodes.title: Sampling-Free Uncertainty Propagation for Mean-Field Approximations
nodes.authors: Thompson, William*; Young, Jean-Gabriel; Hébert-Dufresne, Laurent
nodes.abstract: Network dynamics are intrinsically high-dimensional, necessitating mean-field approximations. In mean field approximations, a vector of order parameters $\mathbf{u}$ is defined as the fixed point of a mapping $\mathbf{u} = F (\mathbf{u}; \mathbf{p})$, where $\mathbf{p}$ represents structural and dynamical control parameters. While these approximations are standard, $\mathbf{p}$ is often subject to significant uncertainty from measurement noise or finite-sample effects. Propagating this uncertainty to $\mathbf{u}$ is particularly difficult near critical points, where susceptibility diverges, and traditional sampling methods suffer from critical slowing down. We present a general, sampling-free framework for uncertainty propagation based on the Implicit Function Theorem (IFT). The Jacobian of the map between parameters p and fixed points u is, according to the IFT, given by $(I - \delta_\mathbf{u} F)^{-1} \delta_\mathbf{p} F$. This allows for the deterministic mapping of probability densities via a change-of-variables formula. Analytically, we derive closed-form expressions for the sensitivity of outbreak sizes in branching processes with negative binomial offspring distributions, providing a theoretical basis for numerical observations in [1]. We demonstrate that our framework recovers universal scaling exponents and critical susceptibilities for the Curie-Weiss model and percolation on Erdős–Rényi and power-law graphs. Computationally, we use automatic differentiation (AD) to propagate uncertainty in systems where self-consistency is defined via integrals, such as the Sherrington–Kirkpatrick model.
