Finally, we show that our approach is equally valid in high-dimensional settings where sampling becomes expensive by quantifying the uncertainty of the Thouless–Anderson–Palmer equations. Our approach offers a high-performance, deterministic alternative to Monte Carlo simulation and polynomial chaos expansions [2], particularly where critical slowing down or divergent variances render sampling-based methods intractable.

nodes.title: Tempological Control of Network Dynamics
nodes.authors: Zhang, Yuanzhao; Cornelius, Sean*
nodes.abstract: The normal functioning of many complex systems requires them to operate near a prescribed dynamical state. To this end, built and natural systems alike–from flocking birds to power grids–employ a strategy of feedback control to stabilize desirable (but otherwise unstable) dynamical states. Unfortunately, this strategy requires continuous control input to counteract deviations from the functional state, which can be invasive and demand considerable resources. Here, for networked systems whose function relies on the synchronization of their components, we introduce tempological (temporal + topological) control–a noninvasive feedback control scheme that stabilizes synchronization without any control input. Instead, our strategy works by making sporadic but deliberate changes to the coupling network on-the-fly, based on the dynamical states of the nodes. We show that by implementing this control strategy, a temporal network can achieve synchronization even if all subnetworks (snapshots) are individually unstable. We demonstrate the utility of our approach numerically using two canonical nonlinear models for synchronization: the Kuramoto and Stuart-Landau systems. We also establish probabilistic guarantees on the success rate of “tempological” control. In particular, we develop a statistical theory that shows how—in the limit of large temporal networks, success is virtually guaranteed—with diversity of network snapshots overcoming the deficiencies of any snapshot in isolation.

nodes.title: Collective Noise Filtering in Complex Networks
nodes.authors: Zhao, Tingyu*
nodes.abstract:
Networks are powerful representations of complex systems across scales and domains. However, real-world network data are often corrupted by noise in the edge weights, incomplete observations, and sampling biases, all of which undermine the reliability of downstream analyses. Standard approaches that filter noise independently at the individual edge level are fundamentally flawed, as both signal and noise often exhibit dependencies across many edges. As a solution, we introduce the Network Wiener Filter, a principled method for collective edge-level noise filtering that leverages network topology together with explicit noise characterization. Our framework reduces noise in observed edge weights and also predicts weights for missing edges. It applies seamlessly to binary, weighted, signed, and directed networks.

We demonstrate the broad efficacy of the Network Wiener Filter in two applications from distinct domains: the Enron email communication network as a social setting and the pairwise genetic interaction network of the yeast \textit{Saccharomyces cerevisiae} as a biological setting, noting compelling evidence of enhanced signal-to-noise ratio in both. In the Enron network, our method effectively mitigates sampling noise arising from short temporal windows, while in the genetic interaction network, the filtered result shows improved agreement with established biological benchmarks. By enabling principled, error-aware reconstruction of network structure, the Network Wiener Filter advances a shift toward network science that embraces data imperfection as an inherent feature---and learns to navigate it effectively.

nodes.title: Analyzing corporate exports with network science tools to predict firm growth
nodes.authors: De Stefano, Valerio; Mula, Maddalena; Mariani, Manuel Sebastian; Zaccaria, Andrea*
nodes.abstract:
Network science methods have been extensively applied to various areas of economics, from finance to the development and industrialization of countries. Firm level investigations, however, are lagging behind, mostly because of the difficulties of obtaining data for micro units.  
From an economic viewpoint, however, a rich theoretical and empirical literature investigated the link between export diversification and firm performance. While prior theoretical works hinted at the key role of capability accumulation in shaping production activities and performance, product-level indicators able to forecast corporate growth are still lacking. In our work [1], we borrow tools from the economic complexity framework, which usually analyses country level capabilities and growth starting from the bipartite network which connects countries with the products they export. In particular, we examine which characteristics of a firm's export basket predict future performance. We analyze a unique longitudinal dataset that covers export and financial data for 12,852 Italian firms, and which allows us to define a bipartite firm-product network (see Figure 1, left). We find that firms exporting products typically exported by wealthier countries -- a proxy for greater product sophistication and market value [2] -- tend to experience higher growth and profit per employee (see Figure 1, right). Moreover, we find that diversification outside of a firm's core production area is positively associated with future growth, whereas diversification within the core is negatively associated. This is revealed by introducing novel measures of in-block and out-of-block diversification, based on algorithmically-detected production blocks. A key point is the use of suitable community detection algorithms, as the use of the blocks defined by the UN classifications does not lead to statistically relevant results. In other words, the data-driven methods based on network science are key to identifying the proper ecosystem of each company, defined by the set of products mainly exported by it and its competitors - their "production core".
Our findings suggest that growth is driven not simply by how many products a firm exports, but also by where these products lie within the production ecosystem, at both local and global scales.

nodes.title: Expanding the policy toolbox: A global industry space captures regional diversification paths
nodes.authors: Mazzarino, Laura*; Landman, Mattie ; O'Clery, Neave
nodes.abstract:
It is well established that places (countries, cities, and regions) tend to diversify into new products, industries, and technologies that are related to their existing activities. In other words, this diversification and growth are driven by a path-dependent process through which regions evolve by entering new economic sectors that share many capabilities with those already present. To model this process, a range of country-specific industry networks has been deployed to predict the entry of new industries into regions. In this paper, we focus on inter-industry labour flow networks, where nodes represent industries and edges correspond to the number of workers transitioning between them. Specifically, we analyse the Skill-Relatedness Network as proposed by Neffke et al. (2017), where edges measure the degree of skill overlap (relatedness) between two industries, inferred from the number of workers moving between them. The underlying idea is that when two industries share a high degree of knowledge, workers are more likely to transition between them, as the skills acquired in one industry are highly valued in the other. This type of model has been highly successful in the policy sphere as it provides quite specific recommendations on which new industries might be feasible in a region based on its current industrial base. However, to date, these models are country-specific, and require detailed longitudinal individual-level data that is only available in a few countries. Here, we propose to build a ‘global network’ which combines data on inter-industry job transitions from multiple countries. This network can then be used to predict potential diversification routes for regions in any country. As a first step, we collect and analyse several country-specific networks, including those of Germany, Sweden, the Netherlands, Ireland, and the UK. For all networks, the industry classification follows the 4-digit NACE 2 system. 
Building on the insights from these country-specific analyses, we then construct the global network by utilising a combination of network science techniques, ranging from community detection to link prediction. In particular, we implemented a machine learning-based link prediction framework, where a set of topological and similarity-based features was extracted from the country-specific networks to train supervised classification algorithms.
To validate the model, we use an econometric approach and demonstrate that it can predict diversification patterns for a set of countries not included in the network construction, including both middle- and high-income countries. We believe that this model is an important step towards expanding the current state-of-the art methodological approaches to the policy toolkit beyond a small set of data-rich countries.

nodes.title: Inferring firm-level supply chain networks with realistic systemic risk from industry sector-level data
nodes.authors: Fessina, Massimiliano; Cimini, Giulio*; Squartini, Tiziano; Astudillo-Estevez, Pablo; Thurner, Stephan; Garlaschelli, Diego
nodes.abstract: Production networks form the structural backbone of modern economies, yet recent crises have exposed their intrinsic vulnerability. Quantifying the system-wide impact of local disruptions—i.e., systemic risk—requires detailed knowledge of supply chain networks (SCNs), since such risks depend sensitively on their underlying topological patterns. In practice, however, microscopic SCN data are rarely available [1], forcing systemic-risk estimates to rely on coarse sector-level information, such as input–output tables, and aggregate production data at the firm-level [2]. Here we assess the ability of maximum-entropy approaches [3] to reconstruct SCNs with realistic level of systemic risk, when only sector- and firm-level constraints are provided. Leveraging the unique availability of the complete production network of Ecuador, we benchmark four reconstruction models with different formulations and data requirements, by comparing the inferred structural and systemic-risk properties (particularly, the Economic Systemic Risk Index [4]) with those of the real network. We find that the model incorporating firm-specific input requirements disaggregated by sector [5] most accurately reproduces the systemic-risk content of the empirical network, underscoring the importance of capturing heterogeneity in firms’ input profiles. We further show how this information can be inferred from aggregate firm-level data and input-output tables. Our results identify the minimal sector-level information needed to statistically generate synthetic SCNs that faithfully encode firm-level systemic risk. The proposed methodology is model-based and fully interpretable, reconstructing the network from aggregate information without ever training on real network instances.

nodes.title: Revisiting Industry Adjacency: from Organizational to Production
nodes.authors: Kwon, Hee Youn*; Lee, Joon Mahn; Park, Hyunwoo; Youn, Hyejin
nodes.abstract:
As the economy becomes more complex and innovation continually reshapes industrial boundaries, firms increasingly operate across multiple industries rather than within a single, well-defined domain. This raises an organizational question: how are industries combined within firms? From a classic perspective, a firm can be understood as an organizational entity that coordinates multiple economic activities under a common managerial and financial boundary. At the same time, new activities often emerge through the recombination of existing capabilities, and established firms frequently internalize these combinations through diversified organizational structures [1]. 

Here, we examine how industries are jointly organized within firms by analyzing operating segments—managerially defined units used for internal oversight and external reporting (10-K disclosures)—across 7,159 publicly listed firms between 2017 and 2022 in Compustat. Firms may report multiple segments when they conduct distinct business activities that are reviewed separately by top management. From this, we construct an industry co-occurrence network in which two industries are connected when the same firm operates segments in both. Figure 1 shows the resulting network at the 2-digit NAICS level (Fig.1A–B), how such bundling scales with firm size and sales (Fig.1C–D), and how diversification is distributed across the economy (Fig.1E). 

Several stylized facts emerge. (1) The network does not separate cleanly into distinct clusters as segment definitions follow managerial organization rather than industrial classification [2]. Segment labels such as “Platforms,” “Solutions,” or “Corporate,” for example, bundle heterogeneous activities that do not map one-to-one onto NAICS industries. (2) Larger firms operate across more segments (Fig. 1D) with highly diversified holding companies and conglomerates, such as Compass Diversified Holdings and Berkshire Hathaway, and technology firms such as Alphabet. At the industry level, knowledge-intensive sectors—particularly (51: Information) and (54: Professional, Scientific, and Technical Services)—frequently co-occur within the same firm, indicating redeployment of capabilities across adjacent domains. 

Additional stylized facts are explored in the remainder of the paper. Do more diversified firms transition more frequently across industries (entries and exits)? We quantify them using two standard measures — the Shannon entropy and the Herfindahl–Hirschman Index given the sales share of firm in industry and the number of industries that a firm belongs to (Fig. 1(E)). Conceptually, segment-based industry networks capture organizational scope — how firms bundle activities, allocate managerial attention, and redeploy capabilities — rather than production or technological similarity [2]. Simply put, segment NAICS encode organizational boundaries, whereas establishment-level NAICS encode production boundaries. Revisiting the measure will complement existing relatedness measures in regional science and economic complexity, and provides a structural view of cross-industry organization.

nodes.title: Supplier network connections to multinational enterprises enhance export performance of domestic firms
nodes.authors: Sebestyén, Tamás*; Braun, Erik; Juhász, Sándor
nodes.abstract:
Multinational enterprises (MNEs) play a key role in modern economies, connecting production processes across countries and acting as powerful economic actors [1]. MNEs often possess more efficient production technologies and superior export capabilities compared to domestic firms, serving as crucial gatekeepers that enable local economies to enter global value chains [2]. While connecting with these MNEs offer opportunities for domestic firms to participate in GVCs, long-term economic development eventually depends on domestic firms becoming globally competitive [3].

This study investigates whether domestic firms learn from their supplier relationships with MNEs, improving their export performance as a result. Using firm-level data on national buyer-supplier ties and international trade patterns in Hungary (2016–2019), we show that firms supplying to MNEs are more likely to start exporting and achieve greater export growth compared to firms without such connections. From a policy perspective, these findings suggest that domestic firms can indeed benefit from MNEs through interfirm learning and access to international markets. However, the number of domestic firms capable of becoming MNE suppliers remains limited. Figure 1 illustrates the empirical framework (on the left) and provides early results from multiple regressions with export outcomes as the dependent variable (on the right), highlighting the positive effect of MNE connections on export performance.

nodes.title: The structure of domestic production networks and production efficiency: the dual economy paradox
nodes.authors: Braun, Erik*; Sebestyén, Tamás; Gyimesi, András; Longauer, Dóra; Iloskics, Zita
nodes.abstract:
European economies exhibit substantial heterogeneity in the economic role played by foreign‑owned firms [1]. While these firms may introduce advanced technologies to less developed regions, multinational enterprises (MNEs) often rely on pre-existing supplier networks, which are frequently foreign-owned, rather than sourcing inputs from local firms. This limits the positive external effects of MNEs on productivity and growth [2].

Using the OECD Analytical AMNE database, this study examines the degree of integration between domestic and foreign-owned firms within individual economies at the sectoral level, focusing on supplier and sales linkages.  As illustrated by the contrasting cases of Slovakia and Germany in Panels a) and b) of Figure 1, there is significant cross-country variation, with less developed economies displaying markedly weaker connections between the two groups of firms.

We construct a measure of economic duality that captures the strength of these domestic-foreign linkages within domestic production networks, and demonstrate its relevance for economic policy. In the short run, higher duality -- reflecting weaker domestic-foreign connections -- is associated with investment patterns dominated by manufacturing activities, where efficiency gains from established international supplier networks and export‑oriented production play a central role. In the long run, however, limited supplier and sales relationships hinder the diffusion of positive externalities from foreign to domestic firms. Econometric analysis confirms this paradox, which is illustrated with a descriptive example in Panels c) and d) of Figure 1: while increasing duality is linked to improved resource efficiency in the short term, it is negatively associated with efficient resource allocation over longer horizons. These findings underscore the importance of strengthening domestic-foreign linkages to promote sustainable, endogenous economic growth [3].

[1] Nölke, A., and Vliegenthart, A. (2009). Enlarging the varieties of capitalism: The emergence of dependent market economies in East Central Europe. World Politics, 61(4):670-702.

[2] Wang, J.-Y., and Blomström, M. (1992). Foreign investment and technology transfer: A simple model. European Economic Review, 36(1):137–155.

[3] Romer, P. M. (1990). Endogenous technological change. Journal of Political Economy, 98(5/2):71-102.

nodes.title: Firm diversification in public procurement markets
nodes.authors: Sturm, Niclas Frederic*; Candia, Cristian; Hartmann, Dominik; Damásio, Bruno; Pinheiro, Flávio L.
nodes.abstract: Public procurement, the acquisition of goods and services by public sector organizations, accounts for nearly 13% of GDP in OECD countries, but has only recently moved back into the academic mainstream. The data sources made available through government transparency portals enable researchers to cast a new light on questions such as firm diversification. Public procurement activities can be considered a reflective measure of firm knowledge and through an analysis of public sector markets, we can reframe these research issues in the light of methods derived from economic complexity.

nodes.title: Global Propagation of Economic Policy Uncertainty and Geopolitical Risk: A Complex Network Perspective via Complex Hilbert PCA
nodes.authors: Souma, Wataru*
nodes.abstract:
Economic policy uncertainty (EPU) and geopolitical risk (GPR) propagate across countries through complex, time-lag, and asymmetric interactions. Traditional approaches based on real-valued correlations or causality tests are limited in their ability to capture such lead–lag structures at a global scale, as they typically rely on pairwise analysis and explicit model assumptions. In this study, we propose a complex-network-based framework that embeds temporal precedence directly into the network representation. By employing Complex Hilbert Principal Component Analysis (CHPCA), we construct weighted and directed networks that jointly encode the strength and phase of interdependencies among countries. This approach enables a global, model-free characterization of uncertainty propagation in the international system.

We analyze monthly newspaper-based EPU and GPR indices for 20 countries from January 1997 to December 2024 (Fig. 1, left). Each time series is transformed into its analytic signal using the Hilbert transform and the complex correlation matrix. Complex Hilbert Principal Component Analysis is applied to the resulting complex correlation matrix. Unlike standard PCA or causality-based methods, CHPCA preserves phase information and embeds lead–lag relationships directly into a multivariate correlation structure, enabling simultaneous analysis of all countries without pairwise model specification. Based on complex correlations, we construct fully weighted, directed networks in which nodes represent countries and edges encode both the magnitude of interdependence and the direction implied by phase differences. Directionality in this context reflects systematic temporal precedence and directional influence, rather than causal identification. To further characterize the structure of uncertainty propagation, we apply the Helmholtz–Hodge (HH) decomposition to the directed networks, separating potential-driven source–sink structures from cyclic (rotational) flows.

The constructed networks exhibit pronounced asymmetries and dynamic reconfigurations over time. Periods associated with major global shocks—most notably the September 11 terrorist attacks in 2001 and the Russia–Ukraine conflict in 2022—coincide with endogenous structural changes in network topology and edge weights, rather than being imposed ex post. The HH potential highlights dominant sources of uncertainty transmission within the global system (Fig. 1, right). In particular, China’s EPU emerges as a significant upstream driver of the GPR in Egypt and Israel, while Russia’s EPU plays a leading role in shaping the GPR dynamics in Taiwan, Japan, and China. These source–sink patterns are obscured in raw directed networks but become explicit through the HH decomposition.

From a network science perspective, our results suggest that global uncertainty behaves as a directed flow system, with a small number of nodes acting as influential sources that depend on geopolitical conditions and economic uncertainty. By embedding lead–lag information into complex-valued networks, the proposed framework reveals structural features that are invisible in real-valued or symmetric analyses. More broadly, the methodology provides a general approach for analyzing delayed and directional interactions in multivariate dynamical systems, with potential applications beyond economic and geopolitical contexts.

nodes.title: Labor Market Networks and Policy Failure: Why Opportunity Zones Didn’t Help Residents
nodes.authors: NARAYAN, KISHAN*
nodes.abstract:
The Opportunity Zone program offered tax incentives for $75 billion invested in 8,762 designated low-income census
tracts. Despite substantial capital deployment, evaluations till the year 2020 find null effects on resident employment
and poverty Freedman et al. (2023). We first extend he evaluation till 2022 with extended results and further explain
the null effect through network analysis of metropolitan labor markets Busso et al. (2013).
Using LODES commuting flow data (21 million annual tract-pair observations, 2013–2022), we construct labor market
networks where nodes are census tracts and edges are worker flows. Replicating Freedman et al.’s inverse probability
weighting methodology extended to 2022, we confirm null effects on resident employment (+0.00%, p > 0.90), poverty
(-0.13%), income (+0.21%), and—newly documented—workplace employment in designated tracts (+0.14%). We then
apply six network diagnostic methods to explain these null results.
Designated tracts occupy severely disadvantaged network positions: (1) Only 6.7% are in the network core versus
32.2% of non-designated tracts (k-core decomposition); (2) In-degree centrality is 75% lower (8.9 vs 34.6, p < 0.001),
indicating few workers commute to these areas; (3) Community detection reveals 71% concentrate in two segregated, low-
income communities with only 15% of flows crossing boundaries (vs 29% for non-designated); (4) Structural constraint
is 10% higher (Burt’s measure, p < 0.001); (5) ERGMs confirm strong income homophily (β = −0.21, p < 0.001) that
designation does not disrupt (interaction β = 0.08, p = 0.28); (6) Network exposure to OZ predicts worse employment
(β = −0.045, p < 0.001), indicating negative spillovers.
Figure 1 visualizes community segregation in Chicago. Designated tracts cluster in peripheral, low-income communities
despite geographic dispersion. This network-based segregation—distinct from spatial clustering—prevents investment
from integrating disadvantaged residents into mainstream labor markets.
Our findings demonstrate that network position determines policy effectiveness. Investment in peripheral, constrained,
segregated tracts cannot overcome structural barriers to benefit diffusion. Future place-based policies must map network
structure and target bridging positions, not just geographic distress.

nodes.title: Networks and Corporate Board Appointments
nodes.authors: Raddant, Matthias*
nodes.abstract:
Diversity in leadership positions, including corporate boards, is an important aspect of equality. It is important because it is the key to better decision-making and innovation and, above all, it paves the way for future generations to participate and shape our society. Many studies emphasize the importance of visibility of role models and the effect that connectivity has on the success of minorities in leadership. However, the connectivity of firms, the dynamics of the adoption of minorities into leadership positions, and the long-term effects in terms of group dynamics and visibility are not well understood.

In the CBRC project we analyze large datasets of firms and their corporate board members in the EU and Japan over time. We analyze how connectivity on the organizational and personal level influences how board composition changes, how legislation on board composition impacts this process, and we analyze how  the composition of the board influences decision making, firm profitability, as well as governance. Preliminary results for the EU suggest that firm characteristics together with the firms' networks provide a good indicator for a board's appointment decisions. 

In addition to performing an empirical analysis, we use observed appointment decisions, networks, and personal characteristics to build network models that describe the dynamics of diversity on corporate boards. These models are used to analyze the influence of connectivity, visibility, as well as homophily on the dynamics of board appointments. They are also used to analyze the effectiveness of counterfactual intervention mechanisms as well as structural changes to the system. In practice, we often observe a bias with respect to firm size for women. The results of our simulation illustrate how such bias that typically translates to visibility can prolong the process towards a more equal composition on corporate boards.

nodes.title: Reconstructing temporal multi-relational firm networks at scale using LLMs
nodes.authors: Köse, Seyda*
nodes.abstract:
The semiconductor industry is foundational to nearly every modern technology, yet its global firm-level network remains
only partially observable. Any reconstruction of such an economic system is necessarily incomplete, as available data
sources provide biased and fragmentary views of underlying supplier, partnership, and ownership relations. This poses
persistent challenges for both scientific analysis and policy design. Existing studies rely heavily on proprietary databases,
which are costly, slow to update, and ill-suited to capturing rapidly evolving dependencies. Here, we demonstrate that
Large Language Models (LLMs), applied to open, first-party web data, can reconstruct the semiconductor firm network
at scale while explicitly accounting for partial observability. By analyzing 170 million webpages from semiconductor
companies, we reconstruct a temporal network (2015–2025) comprising over 1,300 consistently linked firms connected
by more than 3,300 directed edges. Our framework targets a multi-relational structure, using LLMs to distinguish
supplier–buyer flows, strategic partnerships, and ownership links. Validation against human-annotated ground truth yields
high classification performance (F1-score: 0.78 for link existence and 0.76 for link type). Rather than treating either the
web-derived network or commercial datasets (e.g., S&P Capital IQ) as ground truth, we address the more fundamental
problem of inferring the full, unobserved network from overlapping but incomplete data sources. We model the intersection
of these independent partial views within a probabilistic framework and apply Maximum Likelihood Estimation to estimate
the true scale of the latent global semiconductor network. This provides a novel network-theoretic approach to quantifying
unobserved economic activity and assessing the completeness of digital footprint data. The reconstructed network enables
the study of salient industry-wide dynamics. We observe a temporary 9% decline in network edges during the 2022 chip
shortage. Most notably, NVIDIA exhibits a sharp increase across all considered network centrality measures after 2021,
coinciding with the inflection point of the generative AI boom (Fig. 1a), confirming its emerging role as a nexus supplier.
Beyond technological shifts, we detect clear geopolitical signals: by 2025, the share of new links connecting Chinese
firms to European partners declines sharply, consistent with intensifying trade frictions. Finally, we trace the evolving
regional alignment of lead firms (Fig. 1b), illustrating how web-derived economic networks can capture rapid structural
adjustments in high-stakes industries.

nodes.title: Green building blocks reveal the complex anatomy of climate change mitigation technologies
nodes.authors: LI, YANG*; Neffke, Frank
nodes.abstract:
Decarbonization is often framed as “deploying green technologies”, but the transition is not a monolithic shift. Instead, many climate-change mitigation technologies emerge when an existing nongreen technology (the problem/source domain) is augmented with modular components that reduce its carbon footprint. Building on theories of combinatorial innovation, we introduce a network representation of this process by identifying Green Building Blocks (GBBs)—reusable mitigation modules that can be recombined across domains.

Using 8.4 million USPTO patents (1976–2022) and defining CCMT patents via the Y02/Y04 tagging scheme, we pair each green patent with functionally similar nongreen patents using semantic text embeddings, and validate the functional-similarity ranking against expert human judgments (normalized correlation ~ 0.81). We then extract “extra” CPC codes that appear on the green patent but not on its nongreen match, denoise these signals with a CPC2vec similarity model, and cluster the resulting additions (UMAP + HDBSCAN) into 82 GBBs; clustering shared codes yields 193 nongreen source fields. The resulting bipartite source–GBB network maps which mitigation modules can be applied to which incumbent technologies, illustrating cases where broadly applicable GBBs (e.g., fuel cells) contrast with narrowly specialized solutions. We find that the source–GBB adjacency matrix is nested: a small set of general-purpose GBBs connects to many sources, while most GBBs are specialized, and some sources have only a short “menu” of decarbonization options. 

This structure is not merely descriptive. At the micro level, a firm’s prior activity in GBBs relevant to a CCMT class predicts entry into that class, with the GBB signal over three times stronger than source-field experience, and with strong complementarity between the two. At the meso level, we treat alliance formation as link prediction: for 5,947 high-patenting firms, we compute “missing-GBB” complementarity against 7,190 potential partner institutions and show that green co-assignment alliances concentrate among the most complementary pairs. Finally, the most complementary partners are frequently cross-border—optimal partners for ~84% of US, 87% of German, and 92% of Chinese firms are foreign—suggesting that frictions in international collaboration may materially narrow the feasible option set for rapid mitigation innovation.

nodes.title: The impact of self-interactions on the interactome
nodes.authors: Kovacs, Istvan*; Zhao, Tingyu
nodes.abstract:
Proteins that assemble into complexes with identical copies of themselves (homomerizing proteins) play a central role on the structure and function of organisms across all domains of life. The resulting homomers shape network structure and evolution and form the basis of a wide range of protein complexes by gene duplication. Yet, we lack network models of gene duplication that incorporate the role of homomerizing proteins. Here, we present and analytically solve a family of gene duplication models that explicitly follow the evolution of homomerizing proteins. Our results demonstrate that homomerizing proteins have a profound impact on the network patterns through network evolution, diversifying the network structure at all scales, starting from degree sequence and clustering to higher-order network patterns of all sizes. As a result, homomerizing proteins can be efficiently inferred from the rest of the interactome, outperforming alternative methods such as AlphaFold [1], as evidenced by our large-scale experimental validations for human proteins, HuRI [2].
Our methods are applicable to any organism and serve as a starting point towards a more complete understanding of the evolution of the structure and function of the interactome.

nodes.title: Minimal model of spatial growth approaches biological multi-objective optimality
nodes.authors: Lucas, Maxime*; Bisot, Corentin; Petri, Giovanni; Declerck, Declerck; Carletti, Timoteo
nodes.abstract: Many biological networks grow by elongation of filaments that can branch and fuse---typical examples include fungal mycelium or slime mold. These networks must simultaneously perform multiple tasks such as transport, exploration, and robustness under finite resources. Yet, how such multi-task architectures emerge from local growth processes remains poorly understood. Here, we introduce a minimal model of spatial network morphogenesis based solely on stochastic branching, fusion, and stopping, during elongation. Despite the absence of global optimization or feedback, the model generates a broad morphospace from tree-like, to loopy, as well as hybrid architectures. By quantifying multiple functional objectives, we show that (i) these synthetic structures occupy similar regions of performance space than evolved empirical fungal networks, and (ii) that their Pareto front of optimal trade-offs lies close to that of these same fungal networks. Our results show that biological architectures approaching multi-objective optimality can arise from simple local growth rules, and identify branching and fusion as fundamental ingredients shaping the architecture of living transport networks.

nodes.title: Subject-specific networks reveal disease-specific regulatory changes
nodes.authors: Dalldorf, Christopher*
nodes.abstract:
Most biological network research efforts in humans focus on generating and analyzing population-level network models.
However, these models struggle to capture the intrinsic variability between individual humans. In this project, we capture subject-specific contributions to network structures using LIONESS[1], and combine with the message-passing algorithm PUMA[2] to estimate subject-specific miRNA-mRNA regulatory networks (Figure 1A).

Using a similar pipeline, Gentili, 2025[3] previously identified differential regulatory network patterns between chronic
obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD) and control subjects based on data collected from whole blood. We performed a
complementary analysis using a lung tissue data set containing individuals with COPD and idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis (IPF), another lung disease which shares many common clinical features and causes with COPD. In this study, we further expand the analysis to explore differences in the community structure between COPD and IPF subject networks.

Linear regression on the edge-weights in subject-specific networks as a function of subject diagnoses identifies edges strongly associated with either COPD or IPF. These identified edges connected to several well known COPD and IPF associated regulators and genes and formed core networks that vary greatly between healthy, COPD, and IPF subjects. We additionally used a bipartite network community detection algorithm (CONDOR[4]) to identify communities in each subject-specific network and used these communities to identify structural commonalities across the subjects. We found that certain structural motifs are more commonly shared among diseased subjects compared to control subjects (Figure 1B). These findings show the importance of generating subject-specific network models and of systematically evaluating the structure of these networks.

nodes.title: Asynchronous Boolean Network Modeling of the Neuro–Immune System to uncover Autoimmune Disease Emergence
nodes.authors: Urmi, Tamanna*
nodes.abstract:
Autoimmune diseases such as rheumatoid arthritis (RA), systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE), multiple sclerosis
(MS), and type-1 diabetes (T1D) arise from dysregulation of neuro–immune–endocrine (NEI) homeostasis. We model
this system using a curated Boolean network comprising immune cells, cytokines, hormones, tissues, and glands, and
study how perturbations drive transitions between healthy and disease states. While prior work has examined autoimmune
processes at the genomic level or within isolated immune subsystems, our approach integrates heterogeneous biological
components into a unified dynamical framework. Pairwise regulatory interactions were extracted through extensive
literature mining using LLM-assisted prompting and validated against established biological interaction databases.
We develop an asynchronous Boolean network framework to explore disease-reachable dynamical regimes in a NEI
regulatory network consisting of 47 nodes and 214 directed edges encoding activating and inhibitory interactions
[1,3]. Nodes integrate upstream signals using biologically motivated aggregation rules, including AND, OR, activation-
dominant, inhibition-dominant, and majority-vote logic, enabling the representation of diverse regulatory mechanisms
observed across immune and endocrine pathways [2, 4]. Network dynamics are simulated using asynchronous updates,
which better capture stochasticity and heterogeneous signaling timescales in biological systems. Disease phenotypes are
defined by canonical cytokine activation patterns associated with RA, SLE, MS, and T1D.
Through systematic perturbation of healthy initial conditions, we identify minimal sets of node activations that repro-
ducibly drive the system into disease-specific dynamical states, demonstrating that autoimmune phenotypes are emergent
and mechanistically grounded in network structure. Re-simulating full asynchronous trajectories reveals intermediate
components that mediate the propagation and stabilization of disease signals. These intermediates, often distinct from
disease markers, represent key control points for intervention. Together, this perturbation-to-disease mapping uncov-
ers causal pathways underlying autoimmune emergence and provides a principled foundation for identifying therapeutic
strategies that disrupt disease trajectories upstream of irreversible pathological states.

nodes.title: Reproducible Assembly of Complex Networks with Designed Building Blocks
nodes.authors: van der Kolk, Jasper*
nodes.abstract: Many real systems assemble networks reproducibly from building blocks with local interaction rules. In contrast, traditional stochastic network models produce widely varying connectivity patterns. Here, we derive exact conditions on the local rules between node types that guarantee a unique target network. We demonstrate this framework across diverse systems, from protein complexes to electronic circuits, highlighting how specific design principles can ensure robust network formation.

nodes.title: Molecular networks elucidate the epigenetic landscape of food
nodes.authors: Lombardo, Salvo Danilo*; Sebek, Michael; Menichetti, Giulia
nodes.abstract:
The Human Genome Project showed that genetic variation alone cannot explain heterogeneity across individuals and
populations. Instead, gene expression is dynamically regulated by epigenetic processes, through which many environmental
factors act—most notably diet. Historical evidence, such as the Dutch famine, demonstrates that nutritional exposure
can induce persistent, even transgenerational, epigenetic effects [1]. Yet, despite extensive epidemiological links between
diet and epigenetic regulation, including the role of folate and B vitamins in one-carbon metabolism, a unifying, holistic
framework linking food-derived molecules to epigenetic mechanisms is still lacking.
Here, we introduce EpiNet, a curated epigenetic interactome built by integrating public databases, validated through
specialized Large Language Models (LLMs) (Fig. 1a). EpiNet comprises 1,240 proteins organized in ten epigenetic
hallmarks, forming a dense submodule of the protein-protein interaction (PPI) network. Moving beyond expert-defined
associations, we apply a network-based reclassification that offers a finer-grained modular organization of epigenetic
mechanisms and their intrinsic interdependencies (Fig. 1b).
We embedded nutrition onto this epigenetic landscape by reconstructing the chemical and molecular profiles of 1,003
ingredients across 92,246 recipes worldwide. Using network proximity analysis, we identified over 1,000 food-derived compounds
with predicted epigenetic activity, enabling the ranking of compounds, ingredients, recipes, and cuisines according
to their potential to modulate epigenetic activity (Fig. 1c). Finally, we show that flaxseed consumption—classified as
epigenetically active by our framework—is associated with increased survival across ten NHANES IV cycles (1998–2018)
(Fig. 1d).
Together, these results establish the first network-based framework for the epigenetic landscape of food, linking
molecular insights to epidemiological outcomes. By moving beyond isolated nutrient–pathway associations, our approach
highlights diet as a central modulator of epigenetic regulation, providing a foundation for precision nutrition in disease
prevention and healthy aging.
[1] Heijmans, B. T. et al. Persistent epigenetic differences associated with prenatal exposure to famine in humans. Proc. Natl. Acad. Sci. U. S. A. 105, 17046–17049 (2008).

nodes.title: Assessment of methods for network analysis of single-time-point microbial samples
nodes.authors: Calinsky, Yehonatan; Goldental, Meital Mili; Bashan, Amir*
nodes.abstract:
Harnessing information from a single-time-point microbial sample holds transformative potential for advancing personalized medicine. 
While the microbiome ecosystem is shaped by inter-species interactions, traditional methods for single-time-point analysis mainly focus on the abundance of the individual species rather than their interrelationships.
Recently, two novel approaches have been proposed, that, instead of assessing abundance of the individual species, focus on the inter-species relationships in the test sample, the `network-impact' (NI) approach and the individualized dissimilarity-overlap analysis (IDOA).
The NI is a measure of the contribution of the test sample to the calculated inter-species correlations in a `bottom-up' approach, while the IDOA analyzes the relationships between species assemblages and their abundances in a `top-down' approach.

In this research, we comprehensively evaluate and compare the traditional and the new approaches.
Employing the Generalized Lotka-Volterra (GLV) model, we create synthetic samples to rigorously test the classification capabilities of each measure in both supervised and semi-supervised setups. 
Our findings reveal that when the species of the test sample share similar self-dynamics as the reference cohort but distinct inter-species interactions, they typically can not be classified by conventional dissimilarity-based measures.
In contrast, IDOA and NI parameters emerge as successful tools in assessing microbial relationships within a single-time-point sample, while a neural network is highly dependent on the training set size.

This study makes two key contributions: First, it provides a thorough performance comparison of methods aimed at analyzing microbial interactions from a single sample. Second, it contrasts two approaches for analyzing complex microbial systems: `bottom-up' approach that starts with the detailed structure of the interaction network and `top-down' approach that avoids the details and analyzes the system as a whole. It underscores the potential of extracting information from the intricate inter-species relationships based on individual microbial snapshots, paving the way for improving microbiome-based personalized medicine.

nodes.title: An exactly solvable model of highly clustered random networks
nodes.authors: Timár, Gábor*; Cirigliano, Lorenzo; Baxter, Gareth; Castellano, Claudio
nodes.abstract:
An important feature of many observed real-world networks is a high density of short loops, particularly triangles, whose prevalence is quantified by the clustering coefficient, sometimes also referred to as transitivity. Designing mathematically tractable network models that account for these properties in a realistic manner has been an interesting challenge in recent decades. Various generalizations of the standard configuration model have been proposed, where small blocks (clustered subgraphs, generally cliques or partial cliques) are randomly connected to form a network. These models, while containing many short loops by virtue of the individual ”microscopic“ constituents, still maintain a large-scale locally treelike structure, allowing for the use of standard generating function techniques in studying percolation-type processes. Despite the mathematical benefits, such models tend to have a somewhat artificial flavour, as they do not attempt to give a realistic account of how these clustered network structures arise. Some more recent network models incorporate the mechanism of ”triadic closure“ whereby open triads (three nodes sitting on two adjacent links) are closed with a certain probability. In many contexts – particularly social networks – this may be a realistic micromechanism of network formation, since two nodes that share a common neighbour are often more likely to become neighbours themselves.
Here we study a static version of a triadic closure model, whereby we start with a configuration model network of given degree distribution P(k) and we close each triad in the network independently with probability f. We derive exact expressions for the clustering coefficient and for the densities of various small, loopy motifs. With some reasonable assumptions about the degree distribution of the original configuration model network, we can establish approximate relationships between these motif densities, which we test in a large dataset of real-world networks. We find good agreement with predictions indicating that some form of triadic closure mechanism may be responsible for the generation of a variety of small motifs. We define a sequence of quantities called s-clique clustering coefficients, generalizations of the standard global clustering coefficient, which, in scale-free networks, undergo discontinuous transitions as a function of the degree distribution exponent gamma, due to the competition between triads in topologically different positions in the network. We also derive an exact expression for the Pearson degree correlation coefficient and show that degree correlations are always assortative, irrespective of the original degree distribution P(k). We discuss how degree assortativity is connected to clustering in this model, providing further evidence – at least in networks where triadic closure is a viable evolution mechanism – that the two phenomena are closely related. We are also able to provide exact equations for site percolation in these clustered networks, via a mapping to extended-range percolation, revealing interesting insights into the question of universality of percolation on complex networks.
In this talk we aim to give an overview of our results regarding the Static Triadic Closure model and discuss promising avenues for future work.

nodes.title: Beyond positive and negative: enriching balance theory with neutral interactions
nodes.authors: Gallo, Anna*; Squartini, Tiziano; Teixeira, Andreia Sofia
nodes.abstract: Network theory offers a powerful framework for modelling interacting systems, representing units as nodes and their interactions as links. Signed networks, capturing positive and negative social interactions, have recently gained a renewed attention, but little attention has been paid to neutral relationships. In many real-world settings we encounter a broader spectrum of relational attitudes: individuals may hold conflicting attitudes towards another person or simply feel neutral. Crucially, when representing this diversity, \textit{neutrality is not the mere absence of a relationship} as it represents a distinct relational state with its own structural and cognitive implications. Reducing interactions to a positive/negative dichotomy is overly limiting and fails to reflect the inherent complexity of human attitudes and decision-making, thus resulting in an oversimplified representation~[1,2]. Since neutral ties arise from relational mechanisms differing from the ones driving positive or negative connections, their presence leads to the definition of patterns falling outside what classical conceptual frameworks were intended to describe, such as the one of Balance Theory~[3]. When considering triads composed entirely of neutral ties there is no cognitive tension, exerting no pressure for relational change. By contrast, triads containing a mix of both neutral and signed edges may create instabilities, arising from asymmetric engagements or ambiguous expectations; moreover, one may argue that neutral ties may evolve, `absorbing' the sign of adjacent relationships, thus pushing the system towards more familiar configurations. These considerations call for a novel formulation of (im)balance, capable of capturing structural and dynamical nuances that are absent in the traditional Balance Theory framework. To this aim, we extend state-of-the-art probabilistic models to binary, undirected, neutral-enriched signed networks, imposing both global constraints (total number of positive, negative and neutral ties) and local ones (node-specific degree sequences)~[4]: from a modelling perspective, our goal is to develop the most appropriate tools to study these structures within a statistical framework. We, then, employ these neutral-inclusive models to study the emergence of microscopic and mesoscopic patterns (triads and communities), evaluate their stability under alternative theoretical assumptions, and test whether neutral-aware formulations provide more expressive, cognitively-grounded instruments for analysing and predicting social dynamics. In Figure~1 we exemplify synthetic configurations with a variety of modular structures. To retrieve the planted partitions, we optimize the Bayesian Information Criterion (BIC), instantiated with the Neutral-Enriched Signed Stochastic Block Model (NE-SSBM): as we can see, blocks are correctly recovered, independently from the kind of links constituting the majority of connections. Application to both synthetic and empirical networks demonstrates that neutral-inclusive models reveal mesoscale structures invisible to binary frameworks.

nodes.title: Choosing the right stochastic block model
nodes.authors: Jerdee, Maximilian*
nodes.abstract: Many types of stochastic block models (SBMs) have been proposed and used to model community structure in networks. Canonical and microcanonical, degree-corrected and non-degree corrected, assortative and non-assortative. When applied to the same network, variants of the model often yield markedly different groupings of nodes and so produce competing interpretations and predictions. We introduce a parametric model that directly generalizes many of these forms, allowing us to for instance interpolate between a non degree-corrected and a degree-corrected SBM. We discuss how the posterior distribution of the parameter that bridges these models not only reveals which endpoint better represents the network, but also itself measures something meaningful about the network, in this case the inequality of degrees within communities. While individual SBMs can identify interpretable groups of nodes under restricted assumptions, we demonstrate that in an unsupervised, purely data-driven sense (model evidence and predictive power), our generalized model routinely adjudicates between and out-performs existing SBM variants on real-world networks.

nodes.title: Modeling Continuous-time Interval Graphs using Marked Hawkes Process Network Models
nodes.authors: Huang, Zhipeng*; Soliman, Hadeel; Xu, Kevin
nodes.abstract:
In many application settings involving temporal networks, edges denote quantities that have durations, such as lengths of phone calls between two people. Such temporal networks with edge durations have often been referred to as interval graphs [1], as edges are active over time intervals. In continuous-time interval graphs, each edge is observed through timestamped events represented by a quadruplet (𝑢,𝑣,𝑡,𝑚), indicating a directed interaction from node 𝑢 to 𝑣 that starts at time 𝑡 with a duration of length 𝑚, as shown in Figure 1.

Most existing continuous-time network models are based on relational event models, which only model instantaneous events of the form (𝑢,𝑣,𝑡) ignoring their duration. For many types of interactions, including phone calls and meetings, modeling temporal networks requires capturing not only when interactions occur but also how long they last. These edge durations can be viewed as marks accompanying the timestamped relational events.

We propose a general approach for modeling continuous-time interval graphs by using marked Hawkes processes to jointly model edge start times (using the Hawkes process event times) and edge durations (using the marks). We apply our modeling framework to extend two recently proposed continuous-time network models, the Multivariate Community Hawkes (MULCH) model [2] and the Latent Space Hawkes (LSH) model [3], to interval graphs. MULCH is based on a stochastic block model, where each node belongs to one of 𝐾 latent blocks, and the probability of forming edges between nodes depends only on their latent blocks. LSH is based on the latent space model, where each node is represented by a vector in a continuous latent space, and the probability of forming edges between nodes depends on the distance between their latent vectors. For both MULCH and LSH, we consider two different latent modeling approaches: a single shared latent representation, where edge start times and durations are modeled by the same latent block or vector, and a dual latent representation, where edge start times and durations are each modeled by separate latent blocks or vectors.

We apply our proposed MULCH-Interval and LSH-Interval models to a variety of real network data sets with different types of edges with durations, including phone calls and bike share rentals. Our models achieve competitive prediction accuracy for both edge start times and durations, with the dual latent representations usually resulting in higher prediction accuracy. Moreover, our models offer strong interpretability for model-based exploratory analysis. For example, in the network of bike share rentals, we find that pairs of bike stations with longer durations of rentals are generally placed further apart in the latent space for LSH-Interval with dual latent spaces, which reveals some of the geographical structure of the bike stations, as it takes longer to bike between further stations. Thus, our models are useful both for prediction and yielding meaningful insights when used for exploratory analysis on real-world data.

References

[1] Holme, Petter, and Jari Saramäki. Temporal networks. Physics Reports 519.3 (2012): 97–125.
[2] Soliman, Hadeel et al. The Multivariate Community Hawkes model for dependent relational events in continuous-time networks. International Conference on Machine Learning. PMLR, 2022.
[3] Huang, Zhipeng et al. A mutually exciting latent space Hawkes process model for continuous-time networks. International Conference on Uncertainty in Artificial Intelligence. PMLR, 2022.

nodes.title: Network Field Theory
nodes.authors: Motter, Adilson*
nodes.abstract: The multidisciplinary study of complex systems has been greatly facilitated by the simplicity and flexibility of networks as a modeling paradigm. However, traditional network models often focus on scalar interactions of similar types, limiting their ability to fully capture systems characterized by multifaceted, multidimensional, and/or complex interactions. Here, I will introduce Network Field Theory (NFT), a novel and general framework that extends field theory to systems structured as networks, incorporating higher-order fields to model interactions spanning multiple dimensions. Within this framework, I will present edge decomposition, edge expansion, and node fixation as fundamental operations in NFT for modifying topology, modeling adaptive systems, and coarse-graining across multiple scales (Fig. 1). By integrating network topology and geometry as central components of system behavior, NFT offers the power and versatility to enable a unified description of diverse interactions across a wide range of physical, biological, and social networks.

nodes.title: Parameter Stability as a Diagnostic of Network Model Misspecification
nodes.authors: Volkmann, Henry*; Rubinov, Mikail
nodes.abstract:
Much of scientific modeling is concerned with accurate and reproducible estimation of model parameters. In explanatory modeling of complex systems, for example, such estimation helps to understand the importance of the basic building blocks of these systems. Much of network science has adopted a similar explanatory outlook, although studies have traditionally focused on the network statistics (values of constraints) rather than the underlying parameters in generative models. This has partially reflected a practical challenge: in many cases, the generative model of individual networks has remained implicit, which has made such parameter values difficult to estimate.

Seminal work by Squartini and Garlaschelli has adopted the formalism of maximum-entropy (exponential-random-graph) models to explicate the parametric generative models of canonical network features (including in- and out-degrees), primarily to generate null models of real networks [1]. Here, we adopt this work to explore the parametric aspect of this formalism. We specifically examine the emergence of a phenomenon analogous to collinearity in classic linear regression. Collinearity (or multicollinearity) denotes a situation when the presence of correlated regressors results in high variance of parameter values, across multiple estimates. Though this phenomenon is well-established in classical statistics, it has been less explored in network science. Here, we consider a nonlinear version of this phenomenon within the maximum entropy framework.

We adopt maximum entropy models that maximize entropy over network ensembles subject to specified constraints, specifically node degrees. Each constraint is assigned a Lagrange multiplier that encodes its contribution to reproducing desired network statistics. Our central aim is to quantify the effects of constraint similarity  on parameter stability. We generated ensembles of perturbed networks by adding Gaussian noise to edge weights, fit models to each perturbed sample, and computed the coefficient of variation for each fitted parameter across all network samples. We quantified similarity of individual constraints using normalized mutual information (NMI). Across pairs of constraints, we observed a consistent relationship: increased constraint overlap was associated with increased mean parameter CV, suggesting that constraint similarity induces parameter instability. 
Here, we focus on in- and out-degree constraints across three example networks from neuroscience. The NMI between in-degree and out-degree constraints is 0, and correspondingly, the coefficient of variation of their fitted parameters is quite small, demonstrating that co-non-linearity is minimal in these data. However, as parameters become more similar to those of the out-degree constraint, we observe a super-exponential rise in parameter variation. This relationship highlights how parameter stability sensitively detects even subtle constraint overlap.

In sum, we developed a practical diagnostic for estimating the stability of parameters, and used it to investigate the effects on constraint overlap on the reproducibility of individual parameters. We found a robust and stereotyped increase in parameter instability with constraint similarity, indicating that this instability can serve as a quantitative signature of misspecified models. Ultimately, this approach complements existing model-selection and goodness-of-fit practices by shifting attention from constraint values to the estimation of the parameters that generate them. In this way, it offers a principled approach for advance principled specification and estimation of explanatory models.

nodes.title: Antagonistic communities heterogeneities lead to imbalances in species fitness
nodes.authors: Gallarta, Pablo*
nodes.abstract:
Antagonistic interactions, such as predation, parasitism, and herbivory, are fundamental to the structure and function
of ecological communities. These exploitative relationships generate strong reciprocal selective pressures that shape the
evolution of traits and the organization of ecological networks. However, despite their central role in natural ecosystems,
the coevolutionary dynamics of antagonistic communities remain far less understood than those of mutualistic systems,
which have been more extensively studied through trait-based and fitness-centered models. In this work, we develop
a novel coevolutionary framework that integrates network structure, trait evolution and species fitness within a unified
formalism.

nodes.title: Message flux imbalance under copy-spread-annihilate dynamics predicts edge weight asymmetries on the mammal connectome
nodes.authors: Hong, Randy; Hao, Yan; Graham, Daniel*
nodes.abstract:
Regions of the mammal brain form nodes in a densely interconnected communication network linked by white matter axons. However, the network is not symmetric: it possesses asymmetries in edge weights between node pairs (as well as non-reciprocal edges). Here we investigate white matter connection strength asymmetries, which have so far not been predicted by a simple generative model. While the exponential distance rule [1] predicts mean edge weight for connected pairs based on empirical tract lengths, this rule does not explain weight asymmetries. Using a dynamic copy-spread-annihilate (CSA) message-passing model, we predict weight asymmetries from simulated message flux over bi-directional edges. Our CSA model is a synchronous Markovian process where messages arriving coincidentally at a node are deleted, while surviving messages spread to all nearest neighbors [2]. We simulated message-passing under the model on the pairwise-complete mouse brain connectome with all weights set to 1 and all spatial and tract length information removed. Message flow generates consistent flux differences across edges that predict empirical weight differences of those edges. For the largest ≈ 5% of weight differences, sign (Spearman’s ρ_avg = 0.30, p_avg < 0.001) and signed magnitude (ρ_avg = 0.22, p_avg < 0.005) are well predicted. These results hold over a range of values of the sole model parameter, message load (i.e., the number of new messages injected at randomly-chosen open nodes each timestep). Adding Gaussian- or exponential-distributed noise to weight values eliminates the effect. No significant prediction of weight asymmetries is achieved by static measures of network structure such as search information [3] and hub-set-orientation prevalence [4] nor by measures of message flux under alternative dynamic models including a random-walk-annihilate (RWA) model, the susceptible-excited-refractory (SER) model [5], and mean first-passage time. We suggest that weight asymmetries are due in part to the ways in which the brain’s evolved topology biases the spread of messages across the network, though other factors such as differences in regional function surely also contribute to these asymmetries.
[1] M. Ercsey-Ravasz, N. Markov, C. Lamy, D.C. Van Essen, K. Knoblauch, Z. Toroczkai, and H. Kennedy, Neuron 80, 184 (2013).
[2] Y. Hao and D.J. Graham, Network Neuroscience 4, 1055 (2020).
[3] C. Seguin, Y. Tian, and A. Zalesky, Network Neuroscience 4, 980 (2020).
[4] P. Moretti and M.-T. Hütt, Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences USA 117, 18332 (2020).
[5] M. Müller-Linow, C.C. Hilgetag, and M.-T. Hütt, PLoS Computational Biology 4, e1000190 (2008).

nodes.title: The multiscale self-similarity of the weighted human brain connectome
nodes.authors: Barjuan Ballabriga, Laia*; Zheng, Muhua; Serrano, M. Ángeles
nodes.abstract: Anatomical connectivity between different brain regions can be mapped to a network representation, the connectome, where the intensities of the links, the weights, influence resilience and functional processes. Yet, many features associated with these weights are not fully understood, particularly their multiscale organization. In this paper, we elucidate the architecture of weights, including weak ties, in multiscale human brain connectomes reconstructed from empirical data. Our findings reveal multiscale self-similarity, including the ordering of weak ties, in every individual connectome and group representative. This phenomenon is captured by a renormalization technique based on a geometric network model that replicates the observed structure of connectomes across all length scales, using the same connectivity law and weighting function for both weak and strong ties. The observed symmetry represents a signature of criticality in the weighted connectivity of the human brain and raises important questions for future research, such as the existence of symmetry breaking at some scale or whether it is preserved in cases of neurodegeneration or psychiatric disorder.

nodes.title: Glioblastoma invasion reorganizes network structure and information flow in in vitro neuronal networks
nodes.authors: Amos, Giulia; Jordi, Luc; Miccoli, Mariangela; Ahuja, Karan; Gerber, Alexandra; Hutter, Gregor; Voros, Janos; Tringides, Christina M.; Vasiliauskaite, Vaiva*
nodes.abstract:
Glioblastoma (GBM) is the most aggressive primary brain tumor and is frequently accompanied by severe neurological symptoms, including epilepsy and cognitive impairment. Although hyperexcitability associated with glioblastoma is often attributed to increased excitatory drive or local disinhibition, the neurological symptoms often persist following surgical resection~[1], indicating that glioblastoma induces durable and self-sustaining changes in the surrounding neural circuitry. However, mechanistic understanding of how glioblastoma reshapes neuronal network structure in the tumor periphery remains limited.

In this work, we investigated how invasion by glioblastoma reorganizes the structure and dynamics of the neuronal network using a compartmentalized \textit{in vitro} platform illustrated in Fig.\ 1A. Neuron somata and glioblastoma were cultured in separate wells connected by axon-guiding microchannels, allowing long-term observation of tumor–neuron interactions. The system was placed on top of high-density microelectrode arrays to permit simultaneous electrophysiological recordings from the entire network. Leveraging this platform, we performed effective connectivity inference [2] at multiple stages of glioblastoma invasion.

We found that glioblastoma infiltration drives a collapse of modular network organization and the emergence of broadcasting hub neurons (Fig.\ 1B). These hubs played a distinct functional role during population bursts: they were largely silent at burst onset and became selectively active at later stages of the burst. The reorganization of network structure was also accompanied by changes in information dynamics: glioblastoma confined information transfer predominantly to population bursts, while information integration shifted toward predominantly redundant processing.

To mechanistically interpret these observations, we developed an \textit{in silico} spiking neural network model coupled to a glioblastoma-driven global modulatory variable. We showed that the experimentally observed population dynamics can be recapitulated, as illustrated in Fig.\ 1C, when hub neurons possess distinct intrinsic properties—specifically, reduced baseline excitability combined with stronger coupling to glioblastoma-mediated signals. Together, our results suggest that glioblastoma reshapes neural activity not merely by homogeneously elevating excitability, but by reorganizing the network structure and redistributing computational roles across neurons.

nodes.title: Learning-Driven Reorganization of Neural Tissue Networks
nodes.authors: Clawson, Wesley*; Grasso, Caitlin; Palmer, Sydney; Steadman, Danielle; Levin, Michael
nodes.abstract:
Neural systems exhibit spontaneous bursting activity that reflects structured, collective dynamics rather than independent neuronal firing [1]. Across brain regions and preparations, bursts have been implicated in communication, plasticity, and learning, yet how burst structure reorganizes during learning remains poorly understood. In particular, it is unclear whether learning modifies bursts merely in rate and timing, or whether it reshapes the underlying network states that bursts express.

Here, we investigate learning-induced changes in bursting dynamics using dynamic functional connectivity (DFC) as a state-based representation of population activity [2]. We study dissociated cortical cultures embodied in our closed-loop training framework (Holistic Agential Learning, HAL), in which ongoing electrophysiological activity is monitored in real time and stimulation is adaptively applied to reinforce task-relevant behavior. Cultures are trained on a  task in which bursts are reinforced to initiate on a specific side of the array. To probe how bursting reorganizes with learning, we represent the culture as a network whose nodes correspond to recording channels on the multielectrode array. Dynamic functional connectivity is estimated within and across bursts, allowing us to track how patterns of coordination between channels evolve as learning progresses. Bursts are treated as temporally localized network states, enabling comparisons of functional connectivity structure both within individual bursts and between successive bursts over training.

Using this framework, we examine whether learning induces systematic changes in the spatial organization of functional connectivity, alters the internal structure of bursts associated with different initiation locations, and reshapes how activity propagates across the network. We further extend this analysis using directed measures of interaction to assess how information flow during bursts differs across learning stages and task conditions, consistent with state-dependent reorganization of network hubs and information processing [4]. Beyond the specific task studied here, this work demonstrates HAL as a general platform for probing learning, plasticity, and state-dependent information processing in adaptive biological networks under closed-loop control.

nodes.title: Multi-State Bootstrap Percolation for Neuronal Networks
nodes.authors: Spencer, Owen; Rahimian, Amin*; Streipert, Sabrina; Rubin, Jonathan
nodes.abstract:
Multi-state activation cascades are ubiquitous in neuronal and social systems. Neurons can remain quiescent, emit isolated spikes, or enter high-intensity bursting states whose synaptic outputs are disproportionately strong. Analogously, collective action online and offline often proceeds through intermediate low-commitment participation (viewing, liking, sharing, small donations) before a subset of individuals transitions to high-commitment actions (organizing, protesting, sustained contribution). Classical bootstrap percolation captures only a single active state and therefore cannot represent how multi-level activity spreads or how intermediate activity can catalyze a global transition.

We introduce multi-state bootstrap percolation (MSBP), a minimal extension of bootstrap percolation in which nodes can be inactive, weakly active, or strongly active, and we analyze MSBP on Erdos-Renyi random graphs. Each node sends a signal to its neighbors whose strength depends on its current state: zero if inactive, sigma between zero and one if weakly active, and one if strongly active. At each discrete update, a node becomes weakly active if the weighted sum of incoming signals from currently weak and strong neighbors exceeds a threshold k1, and becomes strongly active if that same weighted sum exceeds a higher threshold k2 greater than k1. The weak threshold induces an effective integer parameter kappa1, defined as the ceiling of k1 divided by sigma, which governs the minimal number of weak neighbors needed (in the absence of strong neighbors) to activate a node.
Starting from a constant number of weak seeds, we establish two sharp connectivity regimes corresponding to distinct mechanisms for global activation. First, we prove a weak-driven percolation threshold at connection probability on the order of n to the power minus one over kappa1. If the connection probability is much smaller than this scale, then with high probability weak activation cannot expand beyond a vanishing neighborhood and the cascade dies out. If the connection probability is much larger than this scale, then weak activation expands, reaches a macroscopic fraction of the network, and ultimately triggers global strong activation, even though the seed set is constant size. This result formalizes the intuition that an intermediate weak state can lower the connectivity required for a global cascade: weak activation forms a sparse but extensive scaffold that eventually pushes many nodes over the stronger threshold.

Second, we prove a strong-only threshold at connection probability on the order of n to the power minus one over k2. When the connection probability is much larger than this scale, strong activation becomes self-sustaining and can produce global strong activation without relying on any contribution from weak nodes. This recovers the classical bootstrap percolation scaling for the single-state model and clarifies how MSBP interpolates between two propagation mechanisms: cascades catalyzed by the weak level (controlled by kappa1 and thus by sigma and k1) versus cascades driven purely by strong activation (controlled by k2).

Beyond threshold identification, we characterize transient behavior and non-asymptotic (finite size) trends via simulation. Once activation begins in the supercritical weak-driven regime, the number of active nodes grows approximately exponentially under mild conditions, consistent with a branching-like amplification of weak activation during early stages. As connectivity increases beyond the weak-driven threshold, three robust dynamical regimes emerge: (i) weak self-sustaining, in which weak activation proliferates while strong activation remains negligible until late in the process; (ii) mixed propagation, in which weak and strong activation spread in parallel and many nodes transition through the weak state before becoming strong; and (iii) strong self-sustaining, in which strong activation rapidly dominates and the role of weak nodes becomes secondary.

We also quantify phase-transition signatures across network sizes for two events: full percolation (global strong activation) and self-sustaining strong contagion (strong percolation without weak assistance). In both cases, event probabilities transition sharply from near zero to near one as connectivity crosses a critical value, and the transition becomes steeper and shifts to smaller connection probabilities as network size increases. Importantly, the scaling of the critical probability depends on the event: weak-driven percolation scales with kappa1, whereas strong-only percolation scales with k2.
Interpreted for neuronal circuits, weak activation can be seen as transient spiking that contributes subthreshold synaptic drive, while strong activation corresponds to bursting that delivers sustained drive. The parameter sigma captures the relative efficacy of a spike compared with a burst, and kappa1 expresses how many weak inputs are needed to compensate for one strong input. The weak-driven threshold therefore predicts when sparse spiking activity can percolate through a circuit and create the conditions for widespread bursting, even when the circuit is too sparse to support bursting directly.

More broadly, MSBP provides a parsimonious mechanism by which intermediate engagement can reduce the connectivity required for large-scale cascades. The model and analysis naturally extend to other random-graph ensembles (such as configuration models with heavy-tailed degrees) and to heterogeneous thresholds or signal strengths across nodes, where intermediate activation may interact with degree heterogeneity to create multiple competing nucleation pathways. These directions connect MSBP to questions of robustness and fragility in networked dynamical systems and offer a bridge between mathematically tractable percolation theory and data-driven studies of multi-level activation in real networks.

nodes.title: Modeling neurodegenerative disease as a spreading process on brain networks
nodes.authors: Alexandersen, Christoffer*
nodes.abstract:
Neurodegenerative diseases---such as Parkinson’s and Alzheimer’s disease---are characterized by the progressive appearance of pathological proteins in new brain regions over time.
