Experimental evidence suggests that these proteins propagate along anatomical connections between neurons, motivating the idea that disease progression can be understood as a dynamical process on a brain network. However, network-based models of neurodegeneration have remained relatively simple and have rarely been tested against longitudinal data in a principled way.

We formulate network-based dynamical systems to capture the spatiotemporal progression of protein pathology in the brain. Using high–temporal-resolution histopathology data from mouse models, we fit these models to data using Bayesian Markov chain Monte Carlo. We compare a hierarchy of models of increasing complexity: (i) diffusive spreading along network connections, (ii) spreading with independent local growth dynamics, and (iii) spreading with growth and an additional slower \emph{decay} process, leading to a reduction in pathology at later stages of the disease.

We find that diffusion-only and diffusion–growth models fail to reproduce observed regional trajectories. In contrast, the full model incorporating both growth and decay accurately captures the spatiotemporal dynamics of disease progression, achieving an R^2 = 0.84 at the maximum a posteriori estimate. The Bayesian framework allows tractable inference of 2$N$ region-specific parameters and direct comparison of competing transport mechanisms and network null models.

Analysis of inferred parameters reveals strong regional heterogeneity. Growth and decay rates vary independently across brain regions. Correlating these parameters with regional gene expression shows that decay rates, but not growth rates, align with dopaminergic pathways central to Parkinson’s disease. This result suggests that disease-relevant vulnerability may be more strongly associated with the loss or clearance of pathology than with its initial accumulation.

Together, these findings demonstrate that neurodegenerative progression is governed by multiple timescales on brain networks, and that incorporating decay is essential for explaining longitudinal data. More broadly, this work positions neurodegeneration as a concrete inference problem in network dynamical systems and highlights an underutilized application domain for network science.

nodes.title: Geometric Criticality: Laplacian Renormalization flows and the breakdown of dimensionality in complex networks
nodes.authors: Lucarini, Lorenzo*
nodes.abstract: The concept of scale-invariant networks generalizes the idea of system dimensionality—a fundamental feature determining the critical properties of physical systems. From the perspective of the Laplacian Renormalization Group (LRG), these networks represent the fixed points of renormalization transformations, extending universality classes to the structural domain of complex networks. In this work, we address the challenge of mapping the basins of attraction for these fixed points, proposing a framework to classify complex networks based on their critical stability. We analyze the response of scale-invariant topologies to structural disorder introduced via rewiring and dilution. Our results uncover a hitherto unknown structural phase transition, termed Geometric Criticality, where disorder beyond a critical threshold causes the network to lose its well-defined dimensionality. These topological perturbations drive the system into a heterogeneous, lacunar phase characterized by non-trivial fractal properties, also revealing hidden LRG flows toward unstable or trivial fixed points. By characterizing this geometric breakdown, our work provides a novel mechanism for the emergence of "rare regions" and non-ergodic dynamics, offering a geometric origin for Griffiths phases in disordered systems.

nodes.title: Harmonic morphisms and dynamical invariants in network renormalization
nodes.authors: Guadagnuolo, Francesco*; Nurisso, Marco; Petri, Giovanni; Allard, Antoine
nodes.abstract:
Renormalizing complex networks while preserving their dynamics remains a central open problem in network science.
Although many renormalization schemes successfully compress structure, when and how coarse-graining preserves dy-
namical invariants remains unclear, even for the simplest diffusion processes. Principled criteria to assess the effect of
most existing approaches on dynamics are still wanting.
Here we address this outstanding challenge: defining a probabilistic renormalization scheme for networks that preserves
random-walk dynamics across scales. We show that this problem admits a precise solution by leveraging harmonic
morphisms, a concept from algebraic graph theory bridging discrete probability and combinatorial geometry. We prove
that harmonic morphisms represent the minimal condition under which a coarse-graining maps random walks on the
original network to random walks on the renormalized one, up to a scale-dependent random time change. This establishes
harmonic morphisms as the discrete analogue of conformal maps in field theory, providing a principled notion of dynamical
equivalence for network renormalization.
Building on this theoretical result, we introduce the harmonic degree, a quantitative diagnostic that measures how
closely an arbitrary coarse-graining approximates a harmonic morphism. We apply this framework to major renormalization
schemes—geometric, Laplacian-based, and graph-neural-network-based methods—across dozens of real-world networks.
Our results show that harmonic-degree curves along renormalization flows act as dynamical fingerprints revealing when
dynamical invariance is preserved, when it breaks, and whether it is recovered at larger scales. Doing so allows the
characterisation of coarse-graining mechanisms into top-down, bottom-up, and learning-based categories. Remarkably,
Laplacian renormalization spontaneously generates exact harmonic morphisms at specific scales in real networks, yielding
perfect preservation of random-walk dynamics despite strong structural compression. Finally, we show that our framework
naturally extends to higher-order networks by replacing graph Laplacians with Hodge Laplacians on simplicial complexes,
where harmonicity becomes sensitive to intrinsic topological dimension.
Together, our results provide a unified, dynamical perspective on network renormalization and offer practical tools to
compare and design multi-scale representations of complex systems.

nodes.title: Geometric alignment and inference of shortest paths in Soft random geometric graphs
nodes.authors: Qiu, Zhihao; Balogh, Sámuel*; Liu, Xinhan; Van Mieghem, Piet; Kitsak, Maksim
nodes.abstract:
The study of shortest paths is central to many dynamical processes on networks, from communication routing to biological signaling. Although shortest paths can be computed exactly in fully known networks in polynomial time, this task becomes challenging when the network structure is incomplete or noisy, as is often the case in real systems. Motivated by this challenge, we focus on understanding the geometric organization and inferability of shortest paths in Euclidean Soft random geometric graphs.

We demonstrate that shortest paths display a pronounced geometric alignment, closely following the geodesic curves as measured by both node deviations and path stretch. This alignment strengthens with increasing system size but shows a surprising non-monotonic dependence on network connectivity. At intermediate average degrees, shortest paths are optimally aligned, whereas both sparse and highly connected regimes yield larger deviations. We explain this behavior through the competition between two opposing mechanisms: reduced hop counts improve alignment, while increasing edge lengths weaken it, and induce geometric detours.

Building on this sufficiently strong alignment, we demonstrate that shortest path nodes can be directly inferred from their proximity to geodesics, even without full knowledge of the network. This geometry-based approach remains robust under substantial coordinate noise and can outperform traditional Dijkstra-based methods in partially observable networks. Our results provide insights into how geometry impacts the organization of shortest paths and provides an efficient framework for path inference in imperfectly known systems.

nodes.title: Multiscale Network Renormalization
nodes.authors: Garlaschelli, Diego*
nodes.abstract:
The representation of the internal architecture of all physical systems depends on the resolution at which they are observed and described. 
In spatially embedded systems, geometric coordinates provide a natural way to change the resolution level, indicating how to coarse-grain both the real system and any model of the latter, allowing consistent mappings across scales that lie at the foundation of the renormalization group (RG). 
By contrast, for complex networks with no explicit spatial embedding, multiple renormalization schemes exist [1], resulting in non-unique representations of the same system across different scales. 
The Multiscale Network Renormalization approach [2,3] has been recently introduced as a way to design random graph models that can represent the same network consistently under arbitrary aggregations of nodes. It seeks a probability distribution over graphs that is invariant under node aggregation (see Fig. 1), thereby representing a fixed point of a nontrivially generalized RG flow.
The model comes with node variables that are additive upon node aggregation.
It successfully replicates, at multiple hierarchical levels, the properties of several real-world networks [2,4,5] and lends itself naturally to the renormalization of directed graphs [3], which is  otherwise problematic. 
It also allows one to infer the structural properties of a network at a hierarchical level that is different from the one at which empirical observations are available, opening new avenues for cross-scale network reconstruction [4].
Moreover, the approach can be applied to Machine Learning algorithms that take a graph as input and encode its structure onto output vectors that represent nodes in an abstract space [5,6]. In particular, under arbitrary coarse-grainings of the input graph, the multiscale method ensures statistical consistency of the embedding vector of a block-node with the sum of the embedding vectors of its constituent nodes. 
This guarantee enables the interpretable application of the basic properties of vector spaces (i.e. sum of vectors and multiplication of a vector by a scalar) to the latent space where node embeddings are identified.  
It turns out that several key network properties, including a large number of triangles, are successfully replicated already from embeddings of very low dimensionality, allowing for the generation of faithful replicas of the original networks at arbitrary resolution levels [5,6].
Finally, a purely abstract, annealed version of the model  leads to infinite-mean node variables and  spontaneously replicates, without any fitting parameters, several real-world network properties such as power-law degree distribution, finite local clustering coefficient, and disassortativity profiles [2,7].
Moreover, since node aggregation invariance is a form of discrete scale invariance, several unique properties emerge in the spectrum of the adjancency matrix, such as log-periodicity and complex scaling exponents [8].

References:
[1] A. Gabrielli, D. Garlaschelli, S. P. Patil, M. A ́. Serrano, Nature Reviews Physics 7, 203-219 (2025). [2] E. Garuccio, M. Lalli, D. Garlaschelli, Physical Review Research 5(4), 043101 (2023).
[3] M. Lalli and D. Garlaschelli, arXiv:2403.00235 (2024).
[4] L. N. Ialongo, S. Bangma, F. Jansen, D. Garlaschelli, arXiv:2412.16122 (2024).
[5] R. Milocco, F. Jansen, D. Garlaschelli, arXiv:2412.04354 (2024).
[6] R. Milocco, F. Jansen, D. Garlaschelli, arXiv:2508.20706 (2025).
[7] L. Avena, D. Garlaschelli., R. S. Hazra, M. Lalli, Journal of Applied Probability, https://doi.org/10.1017/jpr.2025.10037 (2025). [8] A. Catanzaro, R. S. Hazra, D. Garlaschelli, arXiv:2509.12407 (2025).

nodes.title: Scaling and multiscaling invariance in the Laplacian Renormalization Transformation
nodes.authors: Kahng, Byungnam; Kim, Cook Hyun*
nodes.abstract:
Complex networks are a simple way to illustrate diverse phenomena in biology, society, and engineering, yet their vast sizes and intricate connectivity are often obstacles to capturing essential structural features and organizing principles. Extracting insight from such systems requires systematic coarse-graining methods that preserve key features. The renormalization group (RG) approach, which reduces complexity by aggregating nodes and links, has been a potential candidate as a powerful method. Despite its promise, renormalization in complex networks faces challenges absent in regular or fractal lattices. Real-world networks, especially scale-free networks with power-law degree distributions $P(k) \sim k^{-\gamma_d}$, exhibit small-world properties that obscure spatial scales for coarse-graining. Their diameters grow as $\ln N$ or $\ln\ln N$, making characteristic length scales ill-defined in the RG transformation.

To address these challenges, two main approaches have been developed, each targeting a different aspect of complex networks. Real-space approaches, such as box-covering methods, partition networks into subgraphs, treating each as a renormalized supernode. Although this method yields structural scaling relations,
they neglect dynamical correlations. Recently, the Laplacian renormalization group (LRG) method has been proposed~[1]. This method analyzes Laplacian eigenmodes under diffusion dynamics to extract dynamical observables such as the spectral dimension~$d_s$, but it lacks a systematic framework for understanding the scaling relations between exponents regarding structural and dynamic features. This separation poses a fundamental limitation: in complex systems, spatial organization and dynamical behavior are intrinsically coupled. 

Recently, we extended the previous LRG framework to simultaneously capture structural and dynamical features within a unified spectral formalism [2]. The central idea is to embed the LRG framework into the standard RG framework via the Gaussian model defined in Laplacian eigen-space. This theoretical construction yields scaling relations that relate the thermal and magnetic exponents~($y_t$, $y_h$) to the spectral, fractal, random walk dimensions, and degree scaling exponent~($d_s$, $d_f$, $d_w$, $d_k$). These scaling relations establish the theoretical framework for the LRG in a mathematically explicit manner. The fractal and spectral dimensions, and degree scaling exponents are indispensable and complementary to characterizing the renormalization of complex networks.

Next, based on the Gaussian model formalism, we construct a meta-graph algorithm that maps spectral modes back to real-space topology, enabling direct extraction of random walk dimensions. We apply this meta-graph algorithm to real-world networks, for instance, the Internet topologies at the AS level over several years, yeast regulatory networks, and the European power grid. The algorithm yields the scaling exponents that satisfy the theoretically predicted scaling relations while preserving structural features such as degree distributions across scales. These results support the soundness of our refined RG framework. Moreover, the framework unveils previously inaccessible features, including multiple-scaling behaviors in evolving networks and long-range coherence emerging from spectral renormalization. Our RG framework provides a powerful lens for understanding mesoscopic organization and dynamical coherence in heterogeneous networks.

nodes.title: Equilibrium-preserving Laplacian Renormalization Group
nodes.authors: Lee, Deok-Sun*; Yi, Sudo; Yang, Seong-Gyu; Goh, Kwang-Il
nodes.abstract:
Coarse graining is central to understanding large-scale organization in complex systems, yet a rigorous real-space renormalization group (RG) framework for networks and hypergraphs remains incomplete. Laplacian renormalization group (LRG) approaches [1] use diffusion time as a scaling parameter and yield a coarse-grained network as an effective structure governing diffusion dynamics, thereby overcoming limitations of earlier methods. However, LRG procedures are largely heuristic, and the RG transformations of the Laplacian and adjacency matrix have yet to be formulated.

We introduce the equilibrium-preserving Laplacian renormalization group (EqLRG) [2], a principled RG framework that preserves the equilibrium state while accurately capturing late-time diffusion. Supernodes are defined as locally equilibrated node sets, formalized through a reduced basis spanning the slow Laplacian eigenmodes. This construction yields a unique basis transformation that preserves the zero-eigenvalue left and right eigenvectors of the Laplacian, ensuring equilibrium invariance under renormalization. Using this basis, we derive a renormalized Laplacian that reproduces the small-eigenvalue spectrum. Equilibrium-state flows further define a renormalized adjacency matrix, producing a consistent coarse-grained network with conserved mean connectivity. These results establish a principled real-space LRG scheme.

We apply EqLRG to diffusion on hypergraphs in bipartite vertex–hyperedge form, enabling simultaneous renormalization of elements and interactions. Numerical validation shows that EqLRG accurately reproduces long-time diffusion and outperforms heuristic coarse-graining schemes [Fig. 1 (a,b,c)]. Recursive application reveals distinct large-scale flows: hypergraphs with finite spectral dimension, such as random (RN) and Barab\'{a}si-Albert (BA) hypertrees,  flow toward effective Poissonian structures [Fig. 1 (d,e,f)], whereas Erd\H{o}s–R\'{e}nyi (ER) hypergraphs develop increasingly heterogeneous, power-law-like connectivity [Fig. 1 (g,h,i)]. These results clarify the roles of informational, structural, and dynamical scale invariance in higher-order networks. More broadly, EqLRG provides a rigorous foundation for multiscale analysis, enabling systematic complexity reduction while preserving essential dynamical properties.

[1] P. Villegas, T. Gili, G. Caldarelli, and A. Gabrielli, Nat. Phys. 19, 445 (2023).
[2] S. Yi, S.-G. Yang, K.-I. Goh, and D.-S. Lee, arXiv:2507.04977 (2025).

nodes.title: Unveiling the importance of nonshortest paths in quantum networks
nodes.authors: Sun, Hanlin*
nodes.abstract: Quantum networks (QNs) exhibit stronger connectivity than predicted by classical percolation, yet the origin of this phenomenon remains unexplored. We apply a statistical physics model—concurrence percolation—to uncover the origin of stronger connectivity on hierarchical scale-free networks, the (U, V) flowers. These networks allow full analytical control over path connectivity through two adjustable path-length parameters, ≤V. This precise control enables us to determine critical exponents well beyond current simulation limits, revealing that classical and concurrence percolations, while both satisfying the hyperscaling relation, fall into distinct universality classes. This distinction arises from how they “superpose” parallel, nonshortest path contributions into overall connectivity. Concurrence percolation, unlike its classical counterpart, is sensitive to nonshortest paths and shows higher resilience to detours as these paths lengthen. This enhanced resilience is also observed in real-world hierarchical, scale-free internet networks. Our findings highlight a crucial principle for QN design: When nonshortest paths are abundant, they notably enhance QN connectivity beyond what is achievable with classical percolation.

nodes.title: Shortest-path percolation on scale-free networks
nodes.authors: Kim, Minsuk*; Cirigliano, Lorenzo; Castellano, Claudio; Sun, Hanlin; Jankowski, Robert; Poggialini, Anna; Radicchi, Filippo
nodes.abstract:
The shortest-path percolation (SPP) model aims at describing the consumption, and eventual exhaustion, of resources
on networks [1]. Starting from a network with a macroscopic connected component, random pairs of nodes are sequentially
selected, and, if the shortest-path length connecting the node pairs is smaller than a tunable budget parameter, then
all edges along such a path are removed from the network. As edges are progressively removed, the network eventually
breaks into multiple microscopic components, undergoing a percolation-like transition. It is known that SPP transition
on Erdős–Rényi networks (ERNs) belongs to same universality class as of the ordinary bond percolation if the budget
parameter is finite; for unbounded budget instead, the SPP transition becomes more abrupt than the ordinary percolation
transition. By means of extensive numerical simulations and finite-size scaling analysis, we study the SPP transition on
random scale-free networks (SFNs) characterized by power-law degree distributions. We find, in contrast with ordinary
percolation, that the transition is identical to the one observed on ERNs, denoting independence from the degree exponent
[2]. Still, we distinguish finite- and infinite-budget SPP universality classes. Our findings follow from the fact that the
SPP process drastically homogenizes the heterogeneous structure of SFNs before the SPP transition occurs.

nodes.title: Robustness of Directed Networks to Generalized Edge Set Removal
nodes.authors: LaRock, Timothy*; Hackl, Jürgen
nodes.abstract:
The robustness of networks to the failure of nodes and edges is a cornerstone of network analysis. Shortest path percolation (SPP) has recently been proposed to model the simultaneous failure of edges along shortest paths. At each time step in SPP, a random origin and destination node are chosen and all of the edges along one shortest path between them are removed if the path length is smaller than a parameter C [1]. Studies of this model have found that with finite C, SPP is in the same universality class as traditional edge percolation on both random Erdos-Renyi and scale-free networks, while with infinite C the transition is more abrupt [1, 2]. Further, an application to a weighted, directed, temporal, and multi-layer line graph representation of a flight network found a transition from a phase where passengers can successfully navigate the network to a disconnected phase where many itineraries are not satisfiable [3].

Inspired by this line of research, here we investigate generalized failure scenarios involving the removal of sets of edges of varying sizes from a network. We develop a general framework where we first define a universe of possible sets of edges, then a (dynamic) ranking function that gives a probability for simultaneous failure at a given time in the process. Recast in this framework, the universe of SPP is all of the available shortest paths, and the probability of choosing a path is that of first selecting its origin and destination nodes, then selecting the specific path among all shortest paths between that pair. In our framework, however, sets of edges may be defined not only by the network topology, but also via other mechanisms, such as dynamical processes (e.g., random walks), or based on data (e.g., observed walk statistics). Figure 1 shows preliminary results on a directed network built from the Wikispeedia game dataset, where users are placed on a random Wikipedia page and tasked with navigating internal hyperlinks to find a target page [4]. We use all of the approximately 51k observed walks in the dataset corresponding to successful games and construct a directed graph with approximately 4k nodes and 60k edges. Figure 1 shows results attacking this network using 5 attack strategies that sample a walk or path through the graph. The first 3 strategies attack the network by removing whole walks from the input data, essentially iteratively downsampling the observed walks. Observed walks are sampled for removal either (i) uniformly at random, (ii) based on their static length (ignoring removed edges), or (iii) based on dynamic length (accounting for removed edges). In contrast, the last 2 strategies attack the network based on (iv) random walks and (v) shortest paths. The random walk attack chooses a random node, then simulates a random walk from that node with length up to the length of a randomly sampled observed walk. The shortest path strategy chooses a random node and a random BFS descendant of that node, then removes a shortest path from the source to the descendant node (different from SPP only because the sampled target node depends on the sampled source node).

Our preliminary results indicate that the random walk and shortest path based approaches are more effective at breaking up the largest weakly connected component of the network than removing observed walks (c-d). Of the 3 strategies based on downsampling the observed walks, only the dynamic length-based sampling is effective. The uniform walk and static-length-based approaches are qualitatively worse than all other methods, suggesting that substantial redundancy in the observed walks can lead to the same edges being sampled multiple times despite already having been removed from the network.

In future work we plan to explore more sophisticated edge set sampling functions based on higher-order network science representations such as de Bruijn graphs. Further, since the size of the largest weakly connected component may not always be the most important factor in robustness of directed and weighted networks, we will consider alternative metrics. Finally, we will apply this framework to infrastructure and mobility systems, especially transportation.

[1] Kim, & Radicchi, Phys. Rev. Lett., 133(4), 2024. https://doi.org/10.1103/PhysRevLett.133.047402
[2]  Kim et al., arXiv preprint arXiv:2509.09142}, 2025. https://doi.org/10.48550/arXiv.2509.09142
[3] Kim, Tyler, & Radicchi, Nat. Comm., 16(1), 2025. https://doi.org/10.1038/s41467-025-63489-w
[4] West, Pineau, & Precup, IJCAI'09, 2009. https://dl.acm.org/doi/abs/10.5555/1661445.1661702

nodes.title: Feedback percolation on networks
nodes.authors: Jang, Hoseung*; Min, Byungjoon
nodes.abstract:
Percolation models provide a fundamental framework for understanding connectivity, robustness, and phase transitions in complex networks. Most studies of percolation assume fixed activation probabilities for each node [1]. In many real-world systems, however, the activation probability of nodes and the size of the giant component often mutually influence each other. This feedback mechanism captures situations where local activation is influenced by global connectivity such as Hebbian plasticity in neural systems and the interplay between the environment and interactions in social and ecological systems. In this work, we introduce a feedback percolation model, in which the probability of activating sites dynamically depends on the size of the giant component. 

We study both positive and negative feedback in percolation models by varying the feedback strength $a$. We analyze the impact of the feedback on the size of the giant component both numerically and analytically. Under positive feedback, the system shows a continuous percolation transition independent of the value of $a$, followed by an additional discontinuous jump in the size of the giant component, whose location approaches the percolation threshold $p_c$ as the feedback strength increases. In contrast, negative feedback leads to a discontinuous transition with an increasing percolation threshold as the feedback strength $a$ increases. We also discuss how the feedback mechanism in percolation models provides insight into several variants of percolation models, including cascading failures in interdependent networks, bootstrap percolation, and $k$-core percolation.

nodes.title: Matching and mixing: Matchability of graphs under Markovian error
nodes.authors: Li, Zhirui; Levin, Keith; Zhao, Zhiang; Lyzinski, Vince*
nodes.abstract: We consider the problem of graph matching for a sequence of graphs generated under a time-dependent Markov chain noise model.  Our edgelighter error model, a variant of the classical lamplighter random walk, iteratively corrupts the graph $G_0$ with edge-dependent noise, creating a sequence of noisy graph copies $(G_t)$.  Much of the graph matching literature is focused on anonymization thresholds in edge-independent noise settings, and we establish novel anonymization thresholds in this edge-dependent noise setting when matching $G_0$ and $G_t$. Moreover, we also compare this anonymization threshold with the mixing properties of the Markov chain noise model.  We show that when $G_0$ is drawn from a sufficiently flat graph model (e.g., the Erdos–Renyi model), the graph matching anonymization threshold and the mixing time of the edgelighter walk are both of order $\Theta(n^2\log n)$.  We further demonstrate that for more structured model for $G_0$ (e.g., the Stochastic Block Model), graph matching anonymization can occur in $O(n^\alpha\log n)$ time for some $\alpha<2$, indicating that anonymization can occur before the Markov chain noise model globally mixes.  Through extensive simulations, we verify our theoretical bounds in the settings of Erdos–Renyi random graphs and stochastic block model random graphs, and explore our findings on real-world datasets derived from a Facebook friendship network and a European research institution email communication network.

nodes.title: Beyond Direct Links: Structural Mediation in Multipartite Networks
nodes.authors: Duarte, Pedro*; Durazzi, Francesco; Remondini, Daniel
nodes.abstract:
Many complex systems are naturally represented by entities interacting within different layers encoding different types of relationships. Yet, network analyses often focus on direct connections in each single layer, ignoring how these interactions are impacted by information contained in the other ones. Here, we introduce a network methodology to quantify how additional information coming from another layer mediates direct relationships and reshapes node relevance.
Some systems can be  represented as networks with three levels of information: an attribute level, whose internal relationships are our object of interest; a mediator level, whose structure gives us the context in which the relationships are shaped; and an entity level, that connects to attributes and mediators. For example: a network with species (entities) connected to the habitats they live in (mediators) and labeled with their phenotypes (attributes). This formulation allows us to explicitly examine how the roles of nodes at the attribute level change when the mediator level structure is included. We propose a framework that identifies the most relevant nodes, both considering direct relationships and those that emerge through the mediator level. 
We begin by characterizing the direct relationships. Starting from the bipartite network linking entities to attributes, we build the attribute-attribute network through contraction, obtaining direct relationships between attributes. For the mediated relationships, we start from the entity-mediator bipartite network and we build its entity-entity contraction, where two entities are linked if they share a mediator, such that proximity now reflects a shared embedding in the mediating structure. Each entity is then annotated with its attributes, forming overlapping subsets within the network. We compute average shortest-path distances within and between these attribute subsets to quantify how close they are in the entity network, when paths are constrained by the mediator-imposed structure. This yields a distance matrix, from which we derive an attribute-attribute network whose links are weighted according to its inter-attribute distance. Finally, we compute centrality measures for all nodes in both networks to see how important they are, considering direct or indirect connections. This allows us to isolate how structural mediation alters relational patterns and node relevance. These effects are captured in the metric Delta C, defined as the difference between indirect and direct centrality, and in a two-dimensional space defined by direct versus indirect centrality, which better describes the information gained through mediation.
We have applied this framework to three different cases: microbial phenotypes structured by the ecological context, scientific impact mediated by collaboration networks and musical artist popularity shaped by social interactions. For all three, we show that the indirect structural context systematically reorganizes  attribute relationships and centrality rankings. For the music data set, and using eigenvector centrality, the social structure mediation highlights popular artists at the time of data extraction, such as Bruno Mars, while direct associations capture canonical artists, such as the Beatles. Similar shifts can be observed in the biological and literature datasets, showing that these mediating layers can fundamentally alter how central a node is. Our results show that the inclusion of indirect structural mediation reveals information that is inaccessible when we only consider direct relationships, offering an approach to help clarify how multi level interactions shape relevance and organization in complex networks.

nodes.title: The Western Record Industry Band Collaboration Network
nodes.authors: Coscia, Michele*
nodes.abstract:
Cultural data is valuable for network science because it can provide rich attributed data for nodes and edges of complex networks representing various aspects of cultural production. In this abstract, we present a novel dataset focused on Western music. We build a band collaboration network using data from Discogs (https://www.discogs.com/). Discogs provides credit information for over 18 million records. In our network, we connect two bands -- including solo artists -- if they have a significant number of shared credits -- artists credited in their released records. We focus on Western music -- plus Russia and Japan -- as these are the countries which are well covered in the Discogs data. We perform a number of data cleaning steps to increase the data quality from Discogs -- disambiguating aliases, using only musical roles, identifying correct years of release/recording, adding country information, etc.

At the moment of writing, the network includes 41,207 nodes and 193,980 edges, but the data coding and cleaning is still underway and these counts are posed to greatly increase once completed. The data will be ready by the time the conference happens. This work is a significant improvement over previous efforts focused on a single country [1]. The nodes of the network have rich attributes, including their country of origin, genres played, labels publishing their records, and years of cultural relevance (when the band publishes records). The dataset is valuable both for network and cultural analysis on the one hand, and for machine learning on graphs on the other. We show in Fig 1 two examples of such analyses.

For cultural and network analysis, the network supports a number of research questions. For instance, by calculating the country entropy of the connections of a band in the network -- by estimating the probabilities of their links to go to bands of different countries, we can discuss the difference between nationally- vs internationally-important bands. One case is the one of Björk: the band is in the top 5 most central internationally, even if it does not appear in the top 10 most central bands in a network including only bands from Iceland -- its country of origin. Fig 1(a) also shows how we can use network distances to analyze and visualize relationships between music genres. We treat the number of releases a band makes in a given genre as a node attribute, and perform dimensionality reduction on the network to highlight how genres played by the same bands co-locate in this complex manifold. This can be used as the basis of a novel classification of musical genres. In a final relevant analysis at the intersection of network science and cultural data analytics, we can fit exponential random graphs to see which node attributes of the bands influence edge creation. We discover that the most important factor leading two bands to have significant artist-sharing relationship is having records produced by the same label (explaining 20% of the variance in the edge existence variable), followed by playing similar genres and hailing from the same country (both explaining 11% of the variance each).

For machine learning on graphs, this dataset provides an ideal test scenario to perform Graph Neural Network (GNN) tasks. We can use the nodes attributes to predict a target one -- i.e. using label information to predict country of origin. By performing message-passing on the network, a GNN can outperform a Neural Network (NN) classifier which would only use the node attributes without topological information -- as Fig 1(b) shows. The precision-recall curves shows a clear superiority of GNN approaches over NN ones, providing a clean dataset for testing new GNN architectures.

In summary, we are providing a valuable resources for network science, cultural data analytics, and machine learning, showing only a few of the potential interesting analyses that this dataset supports. The data will be made available on Zenodo once the process of data coding and cleaning is complete.

nodes.title: Dynamics of Gender Representation in Academic Mathematics
nodes.authors: Chodrow, Philip*; Brooks, Heather; Lee, Harlin; Porter, Mason; Restrepo, Juan Gabriel; Haensch, Anna
nodes.abstract:
In 2024, women remain highly underrepresented in academic mathematics. We study the mechanisms that drive
gender representation in academic mathematics, learn these mechanisms from individual-level data, and analyze networked
mathematical genealogies to understand the roles of long-range inﬂuence in the diversiﬁcation of the ﬁeld. We study
a data set derived from the Mathematics Genealogy Project1, which logs advisor-advisee relationships for 300,000 PhD
mathematicians, as well as graduation years, institutions, and mathematical subﬁeld classiﬁcations. We used a name-
based gender inference package to estimate binary (female/male) gender for 93% of records.

We propose a stochastic model of the data that explicitly models several unobserved mechanisms in the data gener-
ating process, including ﬁeld-speciﬁc productivity eﬀects, gender homophily by mathematical subﬁeld, and production of
graduate students by advisors. This model supports a view of the data as a nonstationary multitype branching process
with parameters to be inferred from incomplete data through the lens of the model’s unobserved mechanisms. We
perform inference in this model using maximum-likelihood estimation. We ﬁnd gendered disparities in both hiring rates
and career lengths, as consistent with recent studies. We also ﬁnd ﬁeld-speciﬁc eﬀects related to faculty production of
graduate students and gender homophily. We simulate our model forward in time, ﬁnding that interventions focused
towards equalizing faculty attrition and production power of PhDs can accelerate the pace of gender diversiﬁcation by
10-15 years.

We also study the network structure of academic genealogy trees (Figure 1) with the goal of identifying inﬂuential
individuals and groups that contribute to the diversiﬁcation of a subﬁeld, institution, or lineage. We develop a novel
measure of centrality-like inﬂuence quantifying the impact of an individual node on downstream production of female
PhD students, validating our measure via a link prediction task.

nodes.title: Resilience of Labor Markets Under the COVID-19 Shock: The Role of Job Connectivity
nodes.authors: Li, Wendi*; Moro, Esteban; Frank, Morgan; Ahn, Yong-Yeol
nodes.abstract: Economic complementarities shape resilience of labor market systems, cities with greater job connectivity experience markedly lower unemployment when encountering shocks. However, most studies on labor market resilience focus on occupation-homogeneous networks without disaggregating different types of occupations, which may bias the assessment of resilience dynamics. In this study, we expand the research framework by moving beyond treating all occupations as homogeneous and instead analyzing structural job connectivity across broad occupational categories defined by two-digit Standard Occupational Classification (SOC) codes. By constructing occupation networks for 45 U.S. metropolitan areas, we explore how job connectivity shapes resilience mechanisms in response to the Covid-19 shock. The preliminary results show that the role of job connectivity in labor market resilience is context-dependent—mitigating unemployment in stable environments but amplifying vulnerability during severe disruptions. Finally, our research enhances the richness of granular unemployment data.

nodes.title: Upward mobility in the green sector for frontline fossil fuel workers
nodes.authors: CHANG, QING*
nodes.abstract: The green sector is often viewed as a source of new jobs for frontline fossil fuel (FFF) workers, yet concerns persist about uncompetitive salaries. This view focuses narrowly on immediate horizontal transitions into similar green jobs, overlooking the sector’s potential to enable long-term career mobility. As an emerging industry, the green sector may offer greater opportunities for advancement into management and engineering roles than mature fossil fuel sectors. Wage comparisons, therefore, should account for differences in upward mobility. Using data on 190,648 FFF workers in the U.S., we analyze career trajectories after leaving their first fossil fuel job. Over their career, FFF workers who, at any point, transition into the green sector (green switchers) earn 4\% more than those who remain in fossil fuel jobs. This is partly driven by higher upward mobility in the green sector. Green switchers are twice as likely to hold managerial jobs and 2.5 times as likely to hold science and engineering (SE) jobs compared to peers who remain in the fossil fuel sector. Within these occupational categories, green sector workers earn 10\% and 30\% more, respectively, than their fossil fuel counterparts. To secure these roles, workers adopt distinct reskilling strategies: skill diversification raises the odds of managerial advancement, while skill deepening into engineering-relevant skills increases the likelihood of entering SE occupations. These findings inform reskilling policies that promote both upward mobility and horizontal flexibility for FFF workers.

nodes.title: Structural Diversity and Network Versatility are Higher in  Informal Structures of Collaboration
nodes.authors: Danus, Lluis*; Dinneen, William; Torreblanca, Carolina; Grossman, Guy; Gonzalez-Bailon, Sandra
nodes.abstract:
Most scholarly exchange occurs outside formal co-authorship, through informal collaborations that provide advice, feedback, and guidance but remain largely invisible in publication-based records. We make this “invisible college” visible by extracting informal ties from acknowledgment sections of published papers and compare their importance vis-à-vis with the more formal coauthorship ties.

Using all articles published in Political Science journals between 2003 and 2024 (129,750 articles and 85,653 authors), we reconstruct co-authorship and acknowledgment networks and compare their structural diversity. Following past research on how the structure of the network might influence the diversity of information one has access to, we operationalize structural diversity using ego-network fragmentation after ego removal, the number of long ties that the ego enables, and the number of stochastic block model partitions of the network that it connects. Additionally, to assess whether structural diversity corresponds to substantive diversity, we measure topical diversity in authors’ research as measured through structural topic modeling.

We find systematic differences between formal and informal collaboration networks. Informal networks expose researchers in comparable meso-scale structural positions (e.g., K-core or SBM communities) to significantly more topic-diverse environments. Moreover, we also observe how the ego-centered structural diversity positions is associated with greater integration of diverse knowledge, an effect that is stronger for informal collaborations. These ties allow scholars to draw on advice and feedback from a broader and more topically diverse set of colleagues, while also revealing unequal access to informal networking opportunities. Finally, our results show that structural diversity in informal collaboration networks captures meaningful variation in access to diverse knowledge and may help explain subsequent academic outcomes.

nodes.title: Beyond Skills and Similarity: Multilayer Networks and Relational Mechanisms in Team Formation
nodes.authors: Xia, Jiarui; Contractor, Noshir; Gomez-Zara, Diego*
nodes.abstract:
Understanding how teams form in networked environments is a foundational problem in network science, organizational research, and computational social science [1]. Prior work has often explained team assembly through individual attributes—such as competence, expertise, or demographic similarity—while treating relational structure as background context. However, teams emerge within evolving social networks, where prior relationships, trust, and interaction history may play a decisive role. This raises a core question: Which network mechanisms most strongly shape collaborator selection when individuals assemble teams over time?

This paper examines team formation by integrating multi-theoretical, multi-level (MTML) frameworks [2] with data-driven network modeling. We analyze team assembly processes across four distinct empirical settings, where individuals form teams while embedded in pre-existing collaboration networks: student projects, software development teams, scientific collaborations, and space crews. The resulting consolidated, open-access database captures individual attributes, dyadic relationship quality, evolving network structure, and downstream team outcomes, enabling comparative analysis across contexts.

To model team formation as an endogenous network process, we employ Exponential Random Graph Models (ERGMs) and Multi-Level ERGMs (MLERGMs) [3]. These models allow us to simultaneously account for individual-level attributes (e.g., competence, experience, similarity), dyadic relational features (e.g., prior collaboration quality), and higher-order structural dependencies. Rather than treating team membership as a purely rational or attribute-driven choice, this approach models collaborator selection as a probabilistic process shaped by embedded social relations.

Across all four settings, we find a consistent and robust pattern: the quality of prior relationships is a significantly stronger predictor of collaborator selection than either individual competence or attribute similarity. Individuals are substantially more likely to choose collaborators with whom they have high-quality prior ties, even when alternative candidates exhibit greater expertise or closer similarity. These effects persist across model specifications and levels of analysis, highlighting relational embeddedness as a central mechanism of team assembly.

This work demonstrates the value of MLERGM frameworks for studying team formation as a multilevel network phenomenon, contributing to a more network-centered understanding of how collaborative groups form and persist.

[1] Contractor, N. (2013). Some assembly required: leveraging Web science to understand and enable team assembly. Philosophical Transactions of the Royal Society A: Mathematical, Physical and Engineering Sciences, 371(1987), 20120385.
[2] Contractor, N. S., Wasserman, S., & Faust, K. (2006). Testing multitheoretical, multilevel hypotheses about organizational networks: An analytic framework and empirical example. Academy of Management Review, 31(3), 681-703.
[3] Krivitsky, P. N., Koehly, L. M., & Marcum, C. S. (2020). Exponential-family random graph models for multi-layer networks. psychometrika, 85(3), 630-659.

nodes.title: Towards GPU-accelerated Temporal Graph Learning with Higher-Order De Bruijn Graphs
nodes.authors: Scholtes, Ingo*; Hackl, Jürgen; Heeg, Franziska; Lampert, Moritz; Qarkaxhija, Lisi; Perri, Vincenzo
nodes.abstract:
The growing polularity of deep learning for graph-structured data generates a number of opportunities as well as interesting challenges for the network science community. The lack of tools that make it easy to bridge between network science methods and data structures and computational techniques used in deep learning hinder a much needed exchange that could benefit both communities.

Addressing this gap, we introduce pathpyG, a python-based Open Source package for network analysis and learning that leverages data structures provided by the geometric deep learning framework pytorch-geometric. It particularly allows to model time-respecting paths in temporal networks, which are the basis to model temporal networks with k-th order De Bruijn graphs and to apply causality-aware GNNs. However, computing time-respecting paths in large temporal networks can be challenging. To address this, we represent (temporal) networks as multi-dimensional pytorch tensors. This facilitates massively parallel tensor operations to efficiently calculate time-respecting paths, which can be transparently moved to the GPU, Here, we study whether the modelling of time-respecting paths benefits from GPU operations for two exemplary data sets. Our preliminary results show a complex picture, where potential speed-ups depend on characteristics like size, density, number of unique timestamps and temporal distribution of edges.

pathpyG also provides comprehensive methods for GPU-based operations on static networks and interfaces to established tools like networkx and graphtool. To facilitate approaches that bridge network science and deep graph learning, we implemented random graph models and (temporal) network randomization techniques, added methods to plot and analyse generating functions, and included an interface to netzschleuder, which makes it easy to apply GNNs in thousands of attributed and temporal networks.
nodes.session: Graph Learning, Generative Models & Information Dynamics

nodes.title: Deep Graph Generation for Microcanonical Ensemble with Arbitrary Topological Constraints
nodes.authors: Choi, Hoyun*; Jo, Junghyo; Lee, Deok-Sun
nodes.abstract:
Randomized graph ensembles serve as essential null models in network science for discerning statistically significant structural features of real-world networks. These ensembles are typically constructed either as \textit{canonical}, where a topological observable $M(G)$ is constrained on average ($\langle M(G) \rangle = m$), or as \textit{microcanonical}, where the constraint is satisfied exactly for every realization ($M(G) = m$).

Exponential Random Graph (ERG) model is the standard framework for generating canonical ensembles, utilizing a control parameter $\alpha$ conjugate to $M(G)$ to bias the sampling probability $P(G) \sim \exp[-\alpha M(G)]$ [1]. In practice, sampling from ERGs relies on Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) methods, such as Metropolis-Hastings algorithms with degree-preserving rewiring. However, the mapping between target $m$ and parameter $\alpha$ is often analytically intractable, requiring exhaustive search. Moreover, the resulting ensembles exhibit finite variance in $M(G)$, and mixing time requires large number of rewiring steps.

Recent studies have begun to explore deep reinforcement learning for graph generation [2]. In this work, we introduce a deep graph generation (DGG) sampling, a reinforcement learning framework designed to generate microcanonical ensembles directly, bypassing the limitations of MCMC sampling. Unlike MCMC, which relies on stochastic accept and reject, we formulate the graph generation as a sequential rewiring selection process as shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:fig1}(a). A graph neural network (GNN) agent, trained via Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO), takes the adjacency matrix and the target $m$ as input and autoregressively selects the optimal edge swaps to drive the graph to the exact target state $M(G)=m$.

We demonstrate the efficacy of the DGG by focusing on degree-degree correlation, or assortativity $r$. The trained agent successfully generates microcanonical ensembles across a broad spectrum of network sizes ($N \in [10^2, 10^4]$), sparsity (mean degree $\langle k \rangle \in [3, 10]$), and degree distributions (Poisson and power-law) over the full range of assortativity $r \in [-0.8, 0.8]$. Crucially, DGG maintains topological randomness, exhibiting entropy~[3] comparable to MCMC sampling (Fig.~\ref{fig:fig1}(b)), while requiring an order of magnitude fewer rewiring steps. Our results suggest that deep reinforcement learning offers a scalable, flexible, and efficient alternative for constructing rigorous null models in network science.

\textbf{(a) Schematic comparison of ensemble generation methods.} ERG sampling requires proper conjugate parameter $\alpha^*$ and perform random rewiring for canonical ensemble. DGG sampling directly utilizes the target assortativity $r^*$ and a GNN agent performs optimal rewiring, resulting in microcanonical ensemble. \textbf{(b) Entropy and efficiency.} The entropy of DGG generated microcanonical ensembles (blue) closely matches that of MCMC canonical ensembles (orange). The inset highlights that DGG satisfies more strict constraints with fewer rewirings compared to the ERG.

\noindent[1] Park, J., \& Newman, M. E. (2004). Statistical mechanics of networks. Phys. Rev. E. 70(6), 066117. \\
\noindent[2] Darvariu, V.-A., Hailes, S., \& Musolesi, M. (2024). Graph Reinforcement Learning for Combinatorial Optimization: A Survey and Unifying Perspective. Transact. Mach. Learn. Res. \\
\noindent[3] Bianconi, G. (2007). The entropy of randomized network ensembles. Europhys. Lett., 81(2), 28005.

nodes.title: The Self-Loop Paradox: Investigating the Impact of Self-loops on GNNs
nodes.authors: Lampert, Moritz*; Scholtes, Ingo
nodes.abstract: Artificial neural networks make predictions for samples based on their features. Message Passing Neural Networks (MPNNs) extend standard neural networks by incorporating the graph topology that connects multiple samples in addition to each sample’s features. When making a prediction for each node, many MPNN models include the features of each node for its own prediction by adding a self-loop to each node, i.e. by modifying the graph topology. A one-layer MPNN can pass messages from each node back to itself and, thereby, use each node’s own features for its prediction because of this added self-loop. However, if the MPNN consists of more than one layer, messages about each node’s own feature can also return to its origin via cycles in the graph topology. Intuition suggests that this “backflow” of messages should be larger in graphs with self-loops compared to graphs without. In this work, we counter this intuition and show that for certain MPNN architectures, each node’s information gain from itself can be smaller in graphs with self-loops compared to the same graphs without. We call this phenomenon the self-loop paradox and explain its cause analytically with the changes in graph topology introduced by adding self-loops to all nodes.
nodes.session: Graph Learning, Generative Models & Information Dynamics

nodes.title: A Stochastic Model for Understanding the Volatility of the Infodemic Risk Index
nodes.authors: Bertani, Anna*; Gallotti, Riccardo
nodes.abstract:
Gallotti et al.[1] proposed three metrics to quantify this phenomenon of infodemic in social media, including the Infodemic Risk Index (IRI). This index measures the extent to which social media accounts are exposed to unreliable content, considering the number of followers as a key factor in amplifying such content. While the IRI is a practical tool, it exhibits significant volatility over time, especially in certain countries where it may spike one day and sharply decline the next. In this work, we aim to investigate the causes behind these rapid fluctuations, identifying key metrics that contribute to the instability of the index. We analyzed Twitter data collected by the COVID-19 Infodemic Platform from over 50 countries, covering the period from February 2020 to the end of May 2022. To evaluate the instability of the IRI (also referred to as \emph{volatility}), we computed its standard deviation over time for each country. As expected, we found a strong anti-correlation between the number of daily tweets and the volatility of the IRI: countries with a high volume of tweets tend to have a more stable Infodemic Risk Index over time. In principle, this finding should align with the expected scaling of standard deviation with the number of daily messages, which suggests a volatility scaling of \( \sigma \approx N^{0.5} \). However, our fitted model instead reveals a scaling exponent of -0.24, indicating an unexpected dependence of volatility on tweet volume.

To understand this discrepancy, we developed a stochastic model that accounts for key factors influencing the IRI's fluctuations. Specifically, our model incorporates:
\begin{itemize}
    \item The number of followers for users sharing both reliable and unreliable sources, particularly the slope \( \alpha \) of the follower distribution.
    \item The disinformation rate, defined as the fraction of news domains classified as unreliable over the total number of news domains shared per day.
\end{itemize} We extracted the \( \alpha \) values of the follower distribution for a set of 51 countries, finding that users sharing reliable content had an \( \alpha \) value of 2.10, while users sharing unreliable content had an \( \alpha \) value of 2.12. These extracted values were then applied to our simulation, where we conducted simulations for different sets of users, with user groups of sizes 20, 40, 80, 160, 320, and 2000. In each case, we measured the standard deviation of the IRI and plotted the results in a log-log scale. The simulation revealed a scaling exponent of 0.11, which contrasts with the traditional \( N^{0.5} \) scaling.

To further test that the long tail character of the distribution of followers was the source of this smaller scaling exponent, we examined the relationship between the number of messages and the standard deviation of the IRI with different follower distributions: exponential, uniform, and normal. We found that for these distributions, the scaling exponent \( \alpha \) was 0.5, as expected according to the Central Limit Theorem. Our goal is to compare nations of different sizes in terms of daily tweet volume while capturing the mechanisms driving IRI volatility. The model, despite its simplicity, already reproduces a power-law relationship with an exponent lower than 0.5, consistently closer to the empirical observations.
nodes.session: Graph Learning, Generative Models & Information Dynamics

nodes.title: Reinforcement Learning for Respondent-Driven Sampling
nodes.authors: Weltz, Justin*; Yoon, Angela; Zhang, Yichi; Volfovsky, Alexander; Laber, Eric
nodes.abstract: Respondent-driven sampling (RDS) is widely used to study hidden or hard-to-reach populations by incentivizing study participants to recruit their social connections. The success and efficiency of RDS can depend critically on the nature of the incentives, including their number, value, call to action, etc. Standard RDS uses an incentive structure that is set a priori and held fixed throughout the study. Thus, it does not make use of accumulating information on which incentives are effective and for whom. We propose a reinforcement learning (RL) based adaptive RDS study design in which the incentives are tailored over time to maximize cumulative utility during the study. We show that these designs are more efficient, cost-effective, and can generate new insights into the social structure of hidden populations. In addition, we develop methods for valid post-study inference.
